The Star of David, also known as the Magen David (Shield of David), is a six pointed symbol composed of two overlapping equilateral triangles. Today, it is the primary symbol of Jewish identity and the central feature of the flag of the State of Israel. While it is deeply meaningful to many people today, it is important to understand its history and its relationship to the Bible.

The Bible does not mention the Star of David. There is no description of a six pointed star being used as a religious or national symbol for King David or the people of Israel in the Old or New Testaments. Historically, the symbol began appearing in Jewish contexts during the Middle Ages, and it did not become a widespread, exclusive symbol of Judaism until the 17th century and later. In the ancient world, the hexagram was often used as a decorative motif by many different cultures and religions.

While the geometric symbol itself is not found in Scripture, the Bible does use star imagery in a prophetic sense. For example, Numbers 24:17 says, "A star shall come out of Jacob, and a scepter shall rise out of Israel." Christians believe this prophecy was fulfilled in Jesus Christ, who is the promised Messiah and the descendant of King David. In the New Testament, Jesus refers to Himself as "the bright morning star" in Revelation 22:16.

The Bible teaches that while symbols and heritage are significant, our ultimate identity and salvation are found in the person of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we have all fallen short of His glory through sin, we are naturally separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who was truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or cultural identity (Ephesians 2:8-9). This is the "Good News" for all people, both Jew and Gentile.

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. Is there anything in particular that makes you curious about the origins of this symbol or your own relationship with God?
Why does the KJV Bible mention the unicorn?

The presence of the word "unicorn" in the King James Version of the Bible is a result of translation history and the way language has changed since 1611. When the translators of the King James Version encountered the Hebrew word "re'em," they looked to previous translations for guidance. The ancient Greek translation of the Old Testament, known as the Septuagint, had translated "re'em" as "monokeros," which means "one-horned." The Latin Vulgate later used the term "unicornis." Following this tradition, the King James translators used the English word "unicorn" (Numbers 23:22; Numbers 24:8).

In the early 17th century, the word "unicorn" did not necessarily refer to the mythical, horse-like creature with magical powers that we see in modern fantasy. Instead, it was often used to describe any powerful animal with a single prominent horn, such as a rhinoceros. Modern biblical scholarship and archaeology have since identified the "re'em" as the aurochs, a large, fierce, and now-extinct species of wild ox. The aurochs was known for its immense strength and its refusal to be tamed by man, which fits the descriptions found in Scripture (Job 39:9-10; Psalm 29:6).

The Bible uses this animal to illustrate the strength and majesty of God. For instance, the horns of the "re'em" are used as a metaphor for power and victory (Deuteronomy 33:17; Psalm 92:10). The point of these passages is not to describe a mythological creature, but to point the reader toward the awesome power of the Creator. God challenges Job by asking if he can tame such a beast, highlighting that if man cannot control a single part of creation, he certainly cannot stand in judgment over the Creator (Job 39:9-12).

This same powerful God who created the untamable wild ox is also the God of perfect holiness and justice. While His strength is intimidating, His love is even more profound. Scripture tells us that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), but God did not leave us in our separation. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

The strength of God, once a cause for fear because of our sin, becomes our refuge when we trust in Christ. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in the strength and mercy of Jesus today?
Is loving God an emotion, a feeling, or a decision?

Loving God is best understood as a holistic response of the entire person, involving the heart, soul, mind, and strength, as described in Mark 12:30. It is not limited to just a feeling or just a decision, but is a beautiful integration of both, rooted in a relationship with Him.

Loving God is certainly a decision and an act of the will. Jesus taught that love is demonstrated through our choices and obedience when He said, "If you love me, you will keep my commandments," in John 14:15. This kind of love is a steadfast commitment to God's glory and His ways, even during seasons when our emotions may feel dry or distant. It is a covenantal loyalty, much like the commitment found in a healthy marriage, where one chooses to act for the good of the other regardless of temporary moods.

At the same time, the Bible describes love as a deep affection and delight. Psalm 37:4 encourages us to "Delight yourself in the Lord." It is more than a cold duty; it is a response of the heart that finds joy and satisfaction in God's character. While feelings may fluctuate based on our circumstances or physical health, the Holy Spirit works in believers to produce a genuine, lasting affection for the Father. Our emotions follow our focus, and as we meditate on God's goodness, our feelings often align with our decision to love Him.

Ultimately, we are only able to love God because He first loved us, as stated in 1 John 4:19. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God and unable to love Him as we should. However, God showed His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us, according to Romans 5:8. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers reconciliation to all who believe.

When we trust in Jesus, God gives us a new heart and the Holy Spirit, who enables us to grow in both the desire and the decision to love Him. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider these truths, what has your own experience been in seeking to love God with both your heart and your choices?
How does God’s sovereignty work with free will?

The relationship between God’s absolute sovereignty and human responsibility is one of the most profound mysteries of the Christian faith. The Bible teaches both truths with equal clarity, and while they can seem like a contradiction to our limited human understanding, they work together in perfect harmony within the mind of God.

God’s sovereignty means that He is the supreme ruler of the universe, and nothing happens outside of His control or His eternal plan. The Scriptures tell us that God works all things according to the counsel of His will (Ephesians 1:11), and that no purpose of His can be thwarted (Job 42:2). From the rising of the sun to the details of our lives, God is in control (Proverbs 16:33; Matthew 10:29–30).

At the same time, the Bible clearly teaches that human beings are responsible moral agents. We make real choices, and we are held accountable for those choices. We are commanded to obey, to repent, and to believe. For example, the heart of man plans his way, but the Lord establishes his steps (Proverbs 16:9). This means that while we are making active decisions, God’s sovereign hand is ultimately directing the outcome to fulfill His purposes.

The clearest example of these two truths working together is found in the crucifixion of Jesus Christ. In the book of Acts, Peter explains that Jesus was delivered up according to the definite plan and foreknowledge of God, yet He was crucified and killed by the hands of lawless men (Acts 2:23). God sovereignly ordained the death of His Son for the salvation of sinners, yet the individuals who carried out the act were fully responsible for their sinful choices. Another example is seen in the life of Joseph, who told his brothers that even though they meant evil against him, God meant it for good to bring about the saving of many lives (Genesis 50:20).

In our own lives, this tension is expressed in the call to spiritual growth. We are told to work out our own salvation with fear and trembling, but the very next verse explains that it is God who works in us, both to will and to work for His good pleasure (Philippians 2:12–13). Our effort is real, but it is empowered by God’s sovereign grace.

This leads us to the heart of the Gospel. Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him (Isaiah 59:2). We are unable to save ourselves through our own will or good works (Ephesians 2:8–9). In His sovereign love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

God sovereignly offers this gift of life, and He calls everyone to respond. Salvation is found in Christ alone, and it is received by grace alone through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. 

What is it that makes it difficult for you to reconcile God’s control with your own choices?
What happened during Jesus’ last hours on earth?

The final hours of Jesus’ life on earth were the fulfillment of God’s redemptive plan, beginning in an upper room and ending at a garden tomb. These events demonstrate the depth of God’s love and the seriousness of His justice.

The evening began with the Last Supper, where Jesus shared a final meal with His disciples. During this time, He washed their feet to demonstrate humble service (John 13:1–17) and established the Lord’s Supper. He identified the bread as His body and the wine as the new covenant in His blood, which was to be poured out for the forgiveness of sins (Matthew 26:26–28; Luke 22:19–20). 

Following the meal, Jesus went to the Garden of Gethsemane to pray. In deep spiritual agony, He asked the Father if there were any other way for salvation to be accomplished, yet He submitted completely, saying, "not as I will, but as you will" (Matthew 26:39). It was there that Judas Iscariot betrayed Him with a kiss, leading to His arrest by an armed mob (John 18:1–11).

Throughout the night and into the early morning, Jesus faced a series of unjust trials. He was brought before the Jewish high priests and the Sanhedrin, where He was falsely accused and mocked. He was then sent to the Roman governor, Pontius Pilate, and to Herod Antipas. Though Pilate found no guilt in Him, he ultimately succumbed to the pressure of the crowd and sentenced Jesus to be crucified (Luke 23:13–25; John 18:28–40).

On the cross at Golgotha, Jesus suffered for six hours. This was not merely a physical execution but a spiritual transaction. Because God is perfectly holy and supreme, our sin creates a barrier that we cannot overcome on our own. Jesus, being truly God and truly man, lived a sinless life and took the divine wrath that we deserved upon Himself. In His final moments, He cried out, "It is finished" (John 19:30), signaling that the work of atonement was complete. He died, was buried in a borrowed tomb, and three days later, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 1:4).

This message is the heart of the Gospel: salvation is not earned by our works, but is a gift secured by Christ's historical death and resurrection. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What was the purpose of the biblical sign gifts?

The Bible presents sign gifts, such as miracles, healing, and speaking in tongues, as specific manifestations of the Holy Spirit intended to serve several vital purposes in the history of redemption.

The primary purpose of sign gifts was to authenticate the messenger and the message of God. In both the Old and New Testaments, when God revealed new truth or moved into a new era of His plan, He often accompanied His word with miraculous signs to prove that the speaker was truly from Him. For example, Moses was given signs so that the Israelites would believe God had appeared to him (Exodus 4:1-5). In the New Testament, Jesus was "attested to you by God with mighty works and wonders and signs" (Acts 2:22), which proved He was the promised Messiah and the Son of God. John explains that the signs Jesus performed were recorded so that readers might believe He is the Christ and have life in His name (John 20:30-31).

Following Christ’s ascension, these gifts were also used to confirm the authority of the Apostles as they laid the foundation of the early church. Paul refers to "the signs of a true apostle" being performed among the believers (2 Corinthians 12:12). These miracles served as a divine "seal of approval" on the Gospel message they preached before the New Testament was fully written and collected. The author of Hebrews notes that the message of salvation was "declared at first by the Lord, and it was attested to us by those who heard, while God also bore witness by signs and wonders and various miracles and by gifts of the Holy Spirit" (Hebrews 2:3-4).

Additionally, spiritual gifts were given for the edification and building up of the church. While some gifts were more miraculous in nature, all gifts were intended for the "common good" of the body of believers (1 Corinthians 12:7). Their purpose was to help the church mature and remain unified in the faith (1 Corinthians 14:12).

Ultimately, all these signs point to the greatest truth of all: the Gospel of Jesus Christ. While signs and wonders are remarkable, they are meant to lead us to the one who performed them. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we were in need of a Savior. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. This salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Shintoism?

Shintoism is the indigenous religion of Japan, rooted in the belief that spiritual beings or essences called *kami* inhabit all things in the natural world. Unlike many other major religions, Shintoism does not have a single founder or a central sacred text, nor does it strictly define right and wrong through a formal moral code. Instead, it focuses on living in harmony with nature and maintaining purity through rituals and traditions.

The core of Shinto practice involves the veneration of *kami*, which can be found in landscapes, forces of nature, animals, and even the spirits of ancestors. People often visit shrines to offer prayers, leave small offerings, and undergo purification rituals to wash away what they consider "impurity" or "pollution," known as *kegare*.

From a biblical perspective, we can appreciate the Shinto desire to recognize the beauty and wonder of the world around us. Scripture teaches that the heavens and the earth truly do declare the glory of God (Psalm 19:1). However, the Bible makes a clear distinction between the creation and the Creator. While Shintoism often finds divinity within nature itself, Christians believe that nature is a masterpiece created by a holy, sovereign, and personal God who exists independently of His creation (Genesis 1:1; Colossians 1:16–17).

The Apostle Paul spoke about this distinction, noting that God’s eternal power and divine nature are clearly seen through what has been made, yet he cautioned against worshiping the creation rather than the Creator (Romans 1:20; Romans 1:25).

In Shintoism, "impurity" is often viewed as a temporary state that can be addressed through physical or ritual washing. In contrast, the Bible teaches that our deepest problem is sin, a spiritual separation from a holy God that cannot be resolved through our own efforts or rituals (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). Because God is perfectly just and loving, He provided the only way for us to be truly purified and reconciled to Him.

This is the heart of the Gospel: God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, took our sins upon Himself, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; 1 Peter 3:18). Salvation is not earned by rituals or works, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Knowing that God is the Creator of all things allows us to enjoy nature with a deep sense of gratitude to the One who made it. Have you ever considered the difference between finding God *in* nature versus knowing the God who *created* nature?
Who is the man of lawlessness in 2 Thessalonians 2:1-12?

The identity of the man of lawlessness mentioned in 2 Thessalonians 2:1-12 is a subject that Christians have studied and discussed for centuries. In the context of the letter, the Apostle Paul was writing to clear up confusion among the believers in Thessalonica who feared that the Day of the Lord had already come. Paul explains that certain events must happen first, including a great rebellion and the revealing of this specific figure.

The Bible describes the man of lawlessness as a person of great destruction who opposes and exalts himself against everything that is called God or is worshiped. He is said to even seat himself in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God 2 Thessalonians 2:3-4. His coming is described as being in accord with the work of Satan, accompanied by counterfeit miracles, signs, and wonders intended to deceive those who are perishing 2 Thessalonians 2:9-10.

Because the Bible does not give a specific name for this individual, several different views have emerged within the church regarding his identity. These are often categorized as tertiary doctrines, meaning that while they are important for our understanding of the end times, faithful Christians can disagree on them while remaining united in the core essentials of the faith.

Many Christians hold a futurist view, believing that the man of lawlessness is a literal individual, often referred to as the Antichrist, who will appear on the world stage just before the second coming of Jesus Christ. Others hold a historicist view, which was common among many Reformation leaders, suggesting that this figure represents a corrupt system or office that opposes the true Gospel over a long period. A preterist view suggests that Paul was referring to someone in his own time, such as a Roman emperor like Nero. Finally, an idealist view sees the man of lawlessness as a symbol of the spirit of rebellion and pride that manifests in various ways throughout human history.

Regardless of which specific interpretation one holds, the passage provides several certainties for the believer. First, there is currently something or someone restraining this lawlessness until the appointed time 2 Thessalonians 2:6-7. Second, the man of lawlessness is not an equal power to God. The Lord Jesus will overthrow him with the breath of His mouth and destroy him by the splendor of His coming 2 Thessalonians 2:8. This serves as a powerful reminder that God is sovereign over history and that evil has a definite expiration date.

The focus of this passage is ultimately to encourage believers to stand firm and hold to the truths they have been taught 2 Thessalonians 2:15. While the description of lawlessness can be sobering, it highlights our profound need for the salvation that only Jesus Christ provides. We live in a world where sin and rebellion are real, but God in His holiness has provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

The Gospel tells us that although we are separated from God by our sin, He loved us enough to send His Son. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was physically raised from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all and will one day return to judge the world and make all things new.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's work alone, and it is received by grace through faith, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Knowing the certainty of Christ's final victory allows us to live with hope rather than fear. What thoughts or questions come to mind as you reflect on the ultimate victory of Jesus over lawlessness?
What are some commonalities in Eastern religions?

Eastern religions, including traditions such as Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism, share several foundational concepts that shape their view of the world and the human condition. While each tradition has unique expressions, several commonalities often emerge in their philosophical frameworks.

One major commonality is the belief in Samsara, which is the continuous cycle of birth, death, and rebirth. In this view, existence is seen as a repetitive journey where the soul or consciousness inhabits different forms over many lifetimes. Related to this is the concept of Karma, the universal law of cause and effect. Karma suggests that every action, word, and thought has a moral consequence that influences one’s current life and future incarnations.

Another shared element is the pursuit of liberation from this cycle. In Hinduism, this is often called Moksha, while in Buddhism, it is referred to as Nirvana. This liberation represents a release from the suffering and attachments of the material world, often achieved through spiritual discipline, ethical living, and meditation. Many Eastern traditions also emphasize an internal path to truth, focusing on self-realization or the discovery of an ultimate reality that dwells within or permeates all things.

From a biblical perspective, these views offer a different understanding of history and the human soul than what is revealed in Scripture. The Bible teaches that human history is linear, moving from creation to a final renewal, and that each person lives one life on earth followed by a judgment (Hebrews 9:27). Rather than a cycle of rebirths to atone for past actions, the Bible presents a personal God who is the holy, supreme, and sovereign Creator of all things (Genesis 1:1; Revelation 4:11).

While the law of Karma seeks to address the problem of human wrongdoing through personal effort over many lifetimes, the Gospel addresses the reality of our separation from God in a different way. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of this sin, we are spiritually separated from God and cannot bridge the gap through our own works or meditations.

In His great love and mercy, God provided the only way for reconciliation. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect, sinless life (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). Salvation is not something we earn through cycles of effort, but a gift secured by Christ's historical work and received by grace alone through faith alone in Him alone (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

Jesus Christ is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the bottomless pit (Revelation 9:1-12)?

The "bottomless pit," also known as the "abyss" in the original Greek language, refers to a place of confinement for fallen spiritual beings and demonic forces. In the context of Revelation 9:1–12, it is depicted as a subterranean realm that is opened during the sounding of the fifth trumpet.

The passage describes a "star" falling from heaven to earth, which is given the key to the shaft of the bottomless pit. Many scholars understand this star to be an angelic being, possibly a fallen one, who carries out God's sovereign decree. When the pit is opened, smoke rises like that of a great furnace, darkening the sun and the air. Out of this smoke come creatures described as locusts, though their appearance and behavior are far from ordinary insects. They have the power to torment people who do not have the seal of God on their foreheads for five months, using stings like those of scorpions.

The ruler over these demonic forces is the king of the abyss, named Abaddon in Hebrew and Apollyon in Greek, both of which mean "Destroyer." This imagery emphasizes the destructive nature of evil when it is permitted to act under God's judgment.

The concept of the abyss appears elsewhere in Scripture as well. In Luke 8:31, the demons that Jesus cast out of a man begged Him not to command them to go into the "abyss." Later in Revelation 20:1–3, Satan himself is bound and thrown into the bottomless pit for a thousand years so that he might not deceive the nations anymore.

These descriptions serve as a sobering reminder of the reality of spiritual darkness and the holiness of God. God is holy, supreme, and sovereign, and His wrath against sin is real. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, took on our sin, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile us to God. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin, death, and the very powers of the abyss.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's work alone and is received by grace through faith, not by our own works or merit. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is biblical theology?

Biblical theology is a way of reading the Bible that seeks to understand how the entire Scripture fits together as a single, unified story centered on Jesus Christ. While systematic theology organizes the Bible’s teachings into topics (such as the nature of God, the person of the Holy Spirit, or the doctrine of salvation), biblical theology tracks the progression of God’s revelation through history. It looks at how themes, promises, and symbols develop from Genesis to Revelation.

The foundation of biblical theology is the belief that the Bible has one primary author, God, and one primary subject, Christ. Jesus Himself taught this approach when He spoke to His disciples after His resurrection. In Luke 24:27, it says that beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, He interpreted to them in all the Scriptures the things concerning Himself. Later, in Luke 24:44, He told them that everything written about Him in the Law of Moses and the Prophets and the Psalms must be fulfilled.

This method of study often focuses on several key areas:

1. The Narrative Arc: Biblical theology views the Bible through the lens of a grand drama consisting of Creation (Genesis 1-2), the Fall (Genesis 3), Redemption (the rest of the Old and New Testaments), and New Creation or Consummation (Revelation 21-22).
2. The Covenants: It examines how God relates to His people through various covenants, such as those with Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, and David, all of which find their fulfillment in the New Covenant established by Jesus (Jeremiah 31:31-34; Hebrews 8:6-13).
3. Types and Shadows: It identifies "types," which are people, events, or institutions in the Old Testament that prefigure or point toward the person and work of Christ. For example, the sacrificial lamb in Exodus 12:1-13 points toward Jesus as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (John 1:29).

At the heart of this unified story is the Gospel. The Bible reveals that God is holy and just, yet humanity has rebelled against Him, resulting in spiritual death and separation (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because of His great mercy, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live, died an atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord and will one day return to judge the living and the dead.

Salvation is not earned by our own good works or moral efforts but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

When you look at the Bible this way, you see that every page is intended to lead us to a deeper knowledge of God's grace in Christ. Have you ever noticed how certain themes, like the presence of God or the idea of sacrifice, seem to grow and change as you move from the Old Testament to the New Testament?
What is the significance of Massah in the Bible?

The word Massah means testing or trial. In the Bible, it refers to a specific location in the wilderness of Rephidim where the Israelites camped after being delivered from slavery in Egypt. Its significance lies in the way it reveals both the persistent unbelief of the human heart and the patient, providing grace of God.

In the Book of Exodus, the Israelites found themselves without water and began to grumble against Moses and, ultimately, against God. They questioned God's presence and care, asking, "Is the LORD among us, or not?" as recorded in Exodus 17:7. Despite the miraculous plagues, the parting of the Red Sea, and the daily provision of manna, they doubted God’s character and His promises. To test God at Massah was to demand that He prove Himself on their terms, rather than trusting in His established faithfulness.

God responded to this rebellion with mercy rather than immediate judgment. He instructed Moses to strike a rock at Horeb with his staff. When Moses did so, water flowed out to satisfy the people’s thirst, as described in Exodus 17:5-6. This event served as a lasting warning for future generations. In Deuteronomy 6:16, the people are commanded, "Ye shall not tempt the LORD your God, as ye tempted him in Massah."

The significance of Massah continues throughout the rest of Scripture. Psalm 95:8 warns the people not to harden their hearts "as in the day of temptation (Massah) in the wilderness." This passage is quoted extensively in the Book of Hebrews to warn believers against having an evil heart of unbelief that departs from the living God, as seen in Hebrews 3:7-12.

Massah also points us toward the person and work of Jesus Christ. In the New Testament, the Apostle Paul identifies the spiritual rock that followed Israel in the wilderness as Christ Himself in 1 Corinthians 10:4. Just as the rock was struck to provide life-giving water to a rebellious people, Jesus was struck for our transgressions to provide the living water of eternal life.

This historical event highlights a profound spiritual reality: while we often test God because of our sin and lack of faith, God has already proven His holiness and love through His Son. We are all like the Israelites at Massah, often prone to grumbling and spiritual thirst because of our separation from God due to sin. However, God, in His mercy, sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, victorious over sin and death, to reconcile us to God. Salvation is found by grace alone, through faith alone, in Christ alone, and not by our own works or efforts.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you find yourself struggling with doubt or "thirsting" for peace, remember that God invites you to come to Him directly in prayer. He is faithful and near to those who call upon Him in truth, as promised in Psalm 145:18. I also encourage you to seek out a local church where the Word of God is faithfully preached, so that you may grow in the grace and knowledge of our Lord.
Can people in heaven look down and see those of us who are still on the earth?

The question of whether our loved ones in heaven can see us is one that many of us hold close to our hearts, especially when we miss those who have gone before us. While the Bible does not give us a definitive answer with exhaustive detail, it does provide some glimpses into the state of those who are with the Lord.

A common passage people look to is Hebrews 12:1, which speaks of a "great cloud of witnesses" surrounding us. In the context of Hebrews 11, these are the heroes of the faith who have already finished their earthly journey. Most biblical scholars believe this refers to the testimony of their lives encouraging us, rather than them literally watching us from a balcony in heaven. Their lives "witness" to us that God is faithful.

However, there are hints that there is some awareness of earthly events in heaven. Jesus said in Luke 15:7 and Luke 15:10 that there is joy in the presence of the angels of God over one sinner who repents. This suggests that the residents of heaven are made aware of at least some significant spiritual turning points on earth. Additionally, in Revelation 6:9-10, we see those who were martyred for their faith in heaven asking how long it will be before God judges the earth, showing they have some understanding of the timeline of God's justice.

While they may have some awareness, the Bible emphasizes that their primary focus is the overwhelming glory of God. In heaven, every tear is wiped away and death is no more, as promised in Revelation 21:4. If they do see us, it is in the light of God's perfect sovereignty and goodness. They are not distressed by our struggles because they see the "end of the story" and the victory of Christ.

The most important truth for us to hold onto is that those who die in Christ are fully alive and in the presence of the Lord. This hope is only possible because of what Jesus Christ has done. Because of our sin, we were separated from a holy God, but Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Salvation is a gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, not by our own works. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. This trust ensures that one day we will join that heavenly assembly, not just to see our loved ones, but to see Christ face to face.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who are the Free Will Baptists, and what do they believe?

Free Will Baptists are a group of evangelical Christians who trace their roots back to the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, emerging primarily from the General Baptist movement in England and early colonial America. They are distinguished from other Baptist groups primarily by their adherence to Arminian theology, which emphasizes that God's grace is available to all people and that individuals have the freedom to accept or reject His offer of salvation.

Historically, the movement in America grew through two main branches. The northern line was organized by Benjamin Randall in 1780 in New Hampshire, while the southern line was established earlier in 1727 by Paul Palmer in North Carolina. Today, these lines are united in the National Association of Free Will Baptists.

Their beliefs are grounded in the following key areas:

The Authority of Scripture
Like all faithful Christians, Free Will Baptists believe the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God. They look to it as the final authority for all matters of faith and practice, believing that it contains everything necessary for a person to know God and live a life that honors Him (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Timothy 3:17).

General Atonement and Free Will
The name Free Will Baptist reflects their conviction that Christ died for the sins of the entire world, not just for a specific group of people. This is often called General Atonement. They believe that while God is sovereign, He has given every person the ability to respond to the drawing of the Holy Spirit. No one is forced to be saved, and no one is excluded from the possibility of salvation (John 3:16; 1 Timothy 2:3; 1 Timothy 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9).

Salvation and Perseverance
Free Will Baptists believe that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ alone and not by human works. However, they hold a view on the security of the believer that differs from many other Baptist traditions. They believe that because faith is a free choice, a believer must continue in faith to remain in a state of grace. They caution that it is possible for a person to willfully turn away from God and abandon their faith, thereby losing their salvation (Hebrews 6:4; Hebrews 6:5; Hebrews 6:6; 2 Peter 2:20; 2 Peter 2:21; 2 Peter 2:22).

The Ordinances
In addition to the Lord's Supper and believer's baptism by immersion, many Free Will Baptist churches practice a third ordinance: the washing of the saints' feet. This is viewed as an act of humility and service patterned after the example of Jesus at the Last Supper (John 13:14; John 13:15).

Church Governance
They generally follow a congregational form of church government, where the local church is autonomous but cooperates with other churches in associations for the purpose of missions, education, and mutual support.

At the heart of their ministry is a desire to see all people come to a saving knowledge of Jesus Christ. They share the same core Gospel that all believers cherish: God is holy and just, yet because of our sin, we are separated from Him. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, conquering death and providing the only way for us to be reconciled to God.

This salvation is not something we can earn by our own effort or goodness, but it is found through grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him?
Why does Proverbs 23:10 tell us not to remove the ancient landmark?

Proverbs 23:10 says, "Do not move an ancient landmark or enter the fields of the fatherless." In the ancient world, landmarks were stone markers that established property lines and family inheritances. Moving these markers was a deceptive way to steal land, slowly expanding one’s own borders at the expense of a neighbor. 

This command is rooted in God's concern for justice and the protection of the vulnerable. In the Law given to Israel, God explicitly forbade this practice because the land was a gift from Him, and the boundaries represented the inheritance He provided for each family (Deuteronomy 19:14; Deuteronomy 27:17). 

The second half of the verse in Proverbs 23:10 highlights why this is so serious, it mentions the "fields of the fatherless." In that society, orphans and widows were often the most powerless members of the community. They lacked the social or physical standing to defend their property rights in court. By moving a landmark, a greedy person was taking advantage of someone who could not fight back. However, the very next verse provides a sobering reminder: "for their Redeemer is strong; he will plead their cause against you" (Proverbs 23:11). The word "Redeemer" (Goel) refers to a protector or kinsman who steps in to defend and provide for those who are helpless. God Himself takes on that role for the oppressed.

Ultimately, this passage teaches us that God values integrity and the protection of the weak. It reveals that our treatment of others, especially our honesty in business and our care for the vulnerable, is a direct reflection of our heart before God.

On a deeper level, we all struggle with the temptation to overstep the boundaries God has set for us. We often seek our own gain or try to secure our future through our own strength and sometimes at the expense of others. This is the nature of sin, which separates us from a holy and just God. But the good news of the Gospel is that while we were helpless and without a protector, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, never violating God's law or taking advantage of others. He then died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the judgment we deserved for our greed and selfishness. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. 

Through Christ, God offers us a different kind of inheritance, one that is "imperishable, undefiled, and unfading" (1 Peter 1:4). We do not receive this through our own works or by shifting boundaries in our favor, but by grace alone through faith in Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is your understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
What does it mean that you are the head and not the tail in Deuteronomy 28:13?

In Deuteronomy 28:13, the promise that the Lord will make His people "the head and not the tail" is part of a larger section describing the blessings for obedience to the Mosaic Covenant. In this context, the "head" represents a position of leadership, influence, and dignity, while the "tail" represents a state of subservience, dependency, or being overlooked. To be the head meant that the nation of Israel would be respected and prominent among the nations, leading others rather than being controlled by them. This blessing was directly tied to their faithfulness in following God's commands (Deuteronomy 28:1; Deuteronomy 28:13).

While this promise describes God's desire to bless and elevate His people, it is important to recognize the condition attached to it: "if you obey the commandments of the Lord your God." The history of Israel shows that because of human sin and the hardness of the heart, the people were unable to keep this law perfectly. They often found themselves being "the tail" because of their rebellion against God, eventually leading to exile (Deuteronomy 28:44; Lamentations 1:5).

This highlights our universal human condition. God is perfectly holy and righteous, and His standards are perfect (Matthew 5:48; Habakkuk 1:13). Because all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, none of us can claim these blessings based on our own merit or performance (Romans 3:23; Galatians 3:10). We are naturally separated from God and unable to save ourselves through our own efforts.

However, the good news of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, came to earth and lived the perfect life of obedience that we could not live. He is the true "Head" over all things (Colossians 1:18; Ephesians 1:22). On the cross, Jesus took the curses we deserved for our disobedience and died as a substitute for sinners (Galatians 3:13; 1 Peter 2:24). He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Through Jesus, the blessings of God are not earned by our works but are received as a gift by grace through faith. When you trust in Him, you are reconciled to God and given a new identity in Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What is stopping you from placing your trust in Him today?

As you grow in your faith, it is helpful to remember that God’s primary goal for you is not just worldly status, but to be conformed to the image of His Son (Romans 8:29). I encourage you to read the book of Galatians to see how Christ has fulfilled the law for us, and to find a local church where you can study the Word and grow in fellowship with other believers. If you ever feel overwhelmed by your own failings, remember that God's grace is sufficient for you, and His power is made perfect in weakness (2 Corinthians 12:9).
What is theistic agnosticism / agnostic theism?

Agnostic theism, also known as theistic agnosticism, is a philosophical position that combines the belief in a deity with the conviction that the existence of that deity is unknown or inherently unknowable. While a theist believes God exists and an agnostic believes the truth about God's existence cannot be certain, an agnostic theist holds both views simultaneously. They may say, "I believe God exists, but I do not claim to have certain knowledge or proof of it."

This perspective often draws a distinction between belief (a matter of faith or conviction) and knowledge (a matter of evidence or certainty). From a biblical perspective, however, there is a beautiful and vital connection between faith and knowledge. While it is true that God is infinite and beyond our full human comprehension, as noted in Isaiah 55:8 and Isaiah 55:9, He has not left us in the dark.

The Bible teaches that God has clearly revealed His existence and His character to all people through the things He has created, as described in Romans 1:19 and Romans 1:20. Furthermore, God has spoken most clearly through His Word and through His Son, Jesus Christ. Christianity is not a "blind leap" into the unknown, but a response to the God who has made Himself known. The apostle John wrote his gospel so that readers might believe and have life in Christ's name, as stated in John 20:31, and he later wrote that believers can "know" they have eternal life, as seen in 1 John 5:13.

If you find yourself in a place of believing but feeling uncertain about whether God can truly be known, it is helpful to look at the person of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that God is holy and supreme, but our sins have created a separation between us and Him. Because of His great love and mercy, God the Son became a man, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and then rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

Salvation is not something we earn by achieving perfect intellectual certainty or by doing good works. It is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. Through Him, we do not just believe in a distant idea of God, but we can enter into a personal and certain relationship with our Creator.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the biggest obstacle for you in moving from a general belief to a personal trust in Him?
The Fruit of the Holy Spirit – What is kindness?

Kindness, as described in the Fruit of the Spirit, is a sweet and gracious disposition that leads to helpful and compassionate action. The Greek word used in the New Testament is *chrestotes*, which refers to a goodness that is useful and serves others. It is not merely a polite manner or a pleasant personality, but a deep-seated desire to seek the well-being of others, modeled after the very heart of God.

The Bible teaches that this kindness is a direct result of the Holy Spirit’s work in the life of a believer. In Galatians 5:22-23, it is listed alongside love, joy, and peace as a characteristic that grows as we remain connected to Christ. This kindness is characterized by a readiness to forgive and a heart of compassion, as described in Ephesians 4:32: "Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving one another, as God in Christ forgave you."

To truly understand kindness, we must look at how God has treated us. Though we were separated from Him by our sin and our rebellion against His holy standards, God did not respond with immediate judgment. Instead, He responded with an overwhelming offer of peace. Titus 3:4-5 says, "But when the goodness and loving kindness of God our Savior appeared, he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewal of the Holy Spirit."

This is the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and our sin created a debt we could never pay. Out of His great kindness, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life on our behalf. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is not something we earn through our own moral efforts or by being "kind enough," but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ.

God’s kindness is meant to lead you to repentance (Romans 2:4). You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How should Christians view the idea of reparations for slavery?

The question of reparations for slavery is a deeply sensitive and complex issue that touches on history, justice, and the restoration of relationships. As Christians, we look to the Bible as our final authority to provide a framework for thinking through such matters, recognizing that while believers may disagree on specific policies, we are united by biblical principles of justice, restitution, and love for our neighbor.

The Bible teaches that God is a God of justice and righteousness (Psalm 89:14). Throughout the Old Testament, the concept of restitution is central to the law. If someone was wronged, the offender was often required to make amends that went beyond simple repayment (Exodus 22:1). This principle suggests that justice is not just about stopping a wrong, but about making things right for the victim. In the New Testament, we see this heart in Zacchaeus, who, after meeting Jesus, committed to restoring fourfold what he had taken by fraud (Luke 19:8). While this was an individual act, it demonstrates that a heart transformed by God desires to address past harms.

Another biblical theme to consider is the tension between individual and corporate responsibility. Scripture teaches that individuals are responsible for their own sins (Ezekiel 18:20). However, the Bible also shows godly leaders confessing the sins of their ancestors and their nation, recognizing that the consequences of past sins often persist into the present (Nehemiah 1:6; Daniel 9:5-6). For many, the argument for reparations is rooted in the idea that the systemic effects of slavery and subsequent injustices have created a lasting disadvantage that requires a communal and structural response.

Christians are also called to be ministers of reconciliation (2 Corinthians 5:18). This involves more than just words, it requires a commitment to the "weightier matters of the law" such as justice and mercy (Matthew 23:23). In the pursuit of such justice, we must be guided by the command to love our neighbor as ourselves (Matthew 22:39). This love should lead us to listen to those who suffer from the legacy of past wrongs and to seek the flourishing of all people in our communities (Jeremiah 29:7).

Because the Bible does not provide a specific command regarding modern civil reparations, this is often considered an area of Christian liberty where believers may hold different views on the best way to achieve justice. Some may focus on direct financial payments, while others may emphasize equalizing opportunities in education, housing, or economic development. In all these discussions, we should maintain "the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace" (Ephesians 4:3), treating one another with gentleness and respect.

Ultimately, while we strive for justice on earth, we recognize that no human system can fully repair the brokenness caused by sin. The deepest "debt" we all face is our sin against a holy God, a debt we could never pay on our own. God, in His infinite mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise bodily from the grave to reconcile us to Himself. Salvation is a gift of grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not earned by our own works or efforts at justice (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Through Christ’s sacrifice, God offers full forgiveness and the hope of a new heaven and a new earth where true justice will dwell forever. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What holds you back from trusting Him with your life and your pursuit of justice today?
Who are we to pray to, the Father, the Son, or the Holy Spirit?

Scripture shows us that because God is one in essence and eternally exists in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit, we may rightly address our prayers to the triune God. The most frequent pattern modeled in the Bible is praying to the Father, through the Son, and by the power of the Holy Spirit.

Jesus explicitly taught His followers to address their prayers to the Father (Matthew 6:9; Luke 11:2). This highlights our relationship with God as children who can come boldly to their heavenly Father because of His love and grace (Ephesians 3:14; Hebrews 4:16).

We also see the biblical precedent for praying to the Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus invited His disciples to ask Him for things in His name (John 14:14). In the book of Acts, Stephen prayed directly to Jesus during his final moments, saying, "Lord Jesus, receive my spirit" (Acts 7:59). Since Jesus is our Mediator and is truly God, it is biblical and appropriate to worship Him and speak to Him in prayer (1 Timothy 2:5; Romans 8:34).

The Holy Spirit is also fully God and is active in our prayer life. While the New Testament does not record a specific command to pray to the Spirit, it frequently speaks of praying in the Spirit (Ephesians 6:18; Jude 1:20). The Spirit is the one who empowers our prayers and even intercedes for us when we do not know what to say (Romans 8:26-27). Because He is a person of the Trinity and fully divine, it is not wrong to speak to Him, though the biblical emphasis remains on His role in leading us to the Father through the Son.

This beautiful access to God is only possible because of the Gospel. Though we were once separated from God by our sin, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What is your experience with prayer, and is there anything that makes it feel difficult for you to approach God?
Why does God hate sin?

God hates sin because it is fundamentally contrary to His nature and destructive to His creation. To understand why God hates sin, it is helpful to look at who He is and what sin does to the world He loves.

First, God is holy, which means He is perfectly pure, righteous, and set apart from all evil. Because His nature is light, there is no darkness in Him at all (1 John 1:5). Habakkuk 1:13 says that God’s eyes are too pure to look on evil and He cannot tolerate wrongdoing. Sin is a direct rebellion against His holy character and His rightful authority as the Creator.

Second, God hates sin because of the devastating impact it has on people. God is the source of life, and since sin is a turning away from Him, it naturally leads to death. Romans 6:23 tells us that the wages of sin is death. Sin separates us from the presence of God, as described in Isaiah 59:2, where it says that our iniquities have made a separation between us and our God. Because God is good and loves His creation, He hates the thing that brings suffering, brokenness, and eternal separation to those He made in His image.

Furthermore, God’s hatred of sin is an expression of His justice. A good judge must hate and punish crime, or he is not truly just. Because God is perfectly just, He cannot simply ignore sin or treat it as if it does not matter. His wrath against sin is a holy response to the ways sin violates His glory and harms others.

However, the Bible also reveals that while God hates the sin, He has demonstrated profound love for sinners. This is the heart of the Gospel. Although we have all sinned and fallen short of God’s glory (Romans 3:23), God did not leave us in our brokenness. Because of His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfectly sinless life, fulfilling the requirements of God's holiness. He then died on a cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Through Jesus, God provides a way for sin to be forgiven and for our relationship with Him to be restored. This salvation is not earned by our own good works, but is a gift received through grace alone by faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What is it that makes it difficult for you to think about God's view of sin?
What will we look like in Heaven?

The Bible offers us a beautiful and hopeful picture of what we will be like in the presence of God. While we do not know every specific detail, Scripture provides several clear truths about our future state.

First, we will have real, physical bodies, but they will be transformed and glorified. Our current bodies are subject to aging, sickness, and death because of the fall of humanity into sin, but the resurrection body will be different. The Apostle Paul explains this change by saying that what is sown in decay is raised in incorruption, and what is sown in weakness is raised in power in 1 Corinthians 15:42-44. This means that any disabilities, illnesses, or effects of age we experience now will be gone forever. In the new creation, God will wipe away every tear, and there will be no more death, mourning, crying, or pain, as promised in Revelation 21:4.

Second, our new bodies will be like the resurrected body of Jesus Christ. Philippians 3:20-21 tells us that Jesus will transform our lowly bodies so that they will be like His glorious body. After His resurrection, Jesus was not a ghost; He could be touched, and He ate food, yet He was no longer limited by the physical constraints of this fallen world, as seen in Luke 24:39-43 and John 20:19. We are told in 1 John 3:2 that while it has not yet been fully revealed what we will be, we know that when He appears, we shall be like Him.

Third, it appears we will remain recognizable individuals. In the account of the Transfiguration in Matthew 17:3, Moses and Elijah were recognizable even centuries after their earthly lives had ended. Just as Jesus was known by His disciples after His resurrection, you will still be "you," but the most perfected, glorious version of yourself, fully reflecting the image of God without the blemish of sin.

The only way to experience this glorious future is through a relationship with Jesus Christ. Because God is perfectly holy, our sin separates us from Him. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone, not by our own good works or efforts. I encourage you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive this eternal life and the promise of a new body.

What is it about the idea of a new, glorified body that most encourages you today?
What is sola scriptura?

*Sola scriptura* is a Latin phrase meaning "by Scripture alone." It is the conviction that the Bible is the final and supreme authority for the Christian life and the church. While Christians often value history, tradition, and the wisdom of teachers, *sola scriptura* means that the Bible is the only infallible rule of faith and practice. Any teaching, tradition, or personal experience must be tested against what God has revealed in His Word.

The Bible is described as "breathed out by God" and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, so that the person of God may be complete and equipped for every good work (2 Timothy 3:16–17). Because the Scriptures are God's own Word, they carry His divine authority and are sufficient for knowing His will. Jesus Himself used Scripture as the final word when resisting temptation, stating that man lives by every word that comes from the mouth of God (Matthew 4:4). In the early church, the Bereans were commended because they examined the Scriptures daily to see if the things they were being told were true (Acts 17:11).

This doctrine is vital because the Scriptures are what point us clearly to the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible reveals that God is holy and supreme, yet human beings have sinned and are separated from Him. Out of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. 

Salvation is secured by this historical work of Christ and is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9; Romans 10:9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What did Jesus mean when He said He would give us “whatever you ask in my name”?

When Jesus tells His disciples that He will do whatever they ask in His name, He is offering a profound promise about the power and purpose of prayer. This teaching, found in passages like John 14:13-14 and John 16:23-24, is not a "blank check" for our personal or selfish desires. Instead, it is an invitation to participate in His work through a life of dependence on Him.

In the biblical context, a person’s "name" represents their entire character, authority, and will. To ask for something "in the name of Jesus" means to ask as His authorized representative. Just as an ambassador speaks in the name of their country by expressing that country’s official will, a believer asks in Jesus’ name when their requests align with His heart and mission. As Jesus explains in John 14:13, the ultimate goal of this answered prayer is "so that the Father may be glorified in the Son."

True prayer in Jesus' name flows from a relationship of "abiding" or remaining in Him. Jesus says in John 15:7, "If you remain in me and my words remain in you, ask whatever you wish, and it will be done for you." When we spend time in His Word and our hearts are shaped by His Spirit, our desires begin to change. We start to want what He wants, and we pray for things that advance His kingdom, such as the strength to endure trials, the wisdom to love others, and the courage to share the Gospel. The apostle John later clarified this by saying, "This is the confidence we have in approaching God: that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us" (1 John 5:14-15).

Our ability to come before God at all is a gift of His grace. Because God is holy and we are fallen and sinful, we are naturally separated from Him (Isaiah 59:2). However, Jesus Christ, who is both truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Through His sacrifice, Jesus opened the way for us to have a relationship with the Father. We are not heard because of our own goodness or the "rightness" of our words, but because of the merit of Jesus Christ alone. Salvation is found in no one else (Acts 4:12), and it is received by grace alone through faith alone. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about witchcraft? Should a Christian fear witchcraft?

The Bible addresses witchcraft, sorcery, and divination with consistent clarity, identifying these practices as attempts to gain power, knowledge, or control through supernatural means apart from God. In the Old Testament, God strictly forbade His people from engaging in these activities, as seen in Deuteronomy 18:10-12, where He describes anyone who practices divination, tells fortunes, interprets omens, or uses sorcery as an abomination to Him. This prohibition exists because witchcraft represents a rejection of God's sovereignty and a turning toward spiritual forces that are in rebellion against Him. In 1 Samuel 15:23, the Bible even compares the sin of rebellion to the sin of divination, showing that both involve a heart that seeks its own way rather than submitting to the Lord.

In the New Testament, sorcery is listed among the works of the flesh in Galatians 5:19-21, and the book of Acts records instances where people who practiced magic were called to repent. In Acts 19:18-19, many who became believers publicly confessed their deeds and burned their expensive magic books, demonstrating that a life following Christ is incompatible with occult practices.

A Christian should not fear witchcraft or the powers of darkness. While the Bible acknowledges that spiritual forces of evil are real in Ephesians 6:12, it also declares the total supremacy of Jesus Christ. Through His death on the cross and His bodily resurrection, Jesus disarmed the spiritual rulers and authorities, putting them to open shame by triumphing over them in Him as stated in Colossians 2:15. If you belong to Christ, you are protected by His power. 1 John 4:4 provides a great comfort to believers, saying, "Little children, you are from God and have overcome them, for he who is in you is greater than he who is in the world." Romans 8:38-39 further assures us that no principalities or powers are able to separate us from the love of God in Christ Jesus our Lord.

The desire to control our lives through witchcraft often stems from a fear of the unknown or a desire for power, but the Gospel offers a better way. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin and our attempts to rule our own lives. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die as a substitute for sinners. He rose from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin, death, and the devil. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone, not through our own works or secret rituals.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why am I here?

The question of why you are here is perhaps the most significant question you can ever ask. According to the Bible, you are not the result of a random accident, but the intentional creation of a loving and holy God.

God created human beings in His own image to know Him, to enjoy Him, and to reflect His glory to the world (Genesis 1:27; Isaiah 43:7). This means your deepest purpose is found in a relationship with your Creator. However, we all face a deep sense of disconnect because sin has entered the world. Sin is our decision to live for ourselves rather than for God, and it creates a barrier between us and the holy God who made us (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23).

Because God is both just and merciful, He did not leave us in this state of separation. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus lived a perfectly holy life and then willingly died on a cross, taking the punishment for our sins as our substitute (1 Peter 2:24; 2 Corinthians 5:21). He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His power over death and confirming that His sacrifice was sufficient (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord of all.

Salvation and the discovery of your true purpose do not come through your own good works or effort, but by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8-9). When you are restored to God through Christ, you begin to walk in the good works that God prepared beforehand for you to do (Ephesians 2:10).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What do you feel has been the biggest obstacle in your search for purpose?
Who was John Bunyan?

John Bunyan (1628–1688) was an English minister and author who became one of the most influential figures in Christian history. He is best known for writing "The Pilgrim’s Progress," an allegory that describes the journey of a man named Christian from the "City of Destruction" to the "Celestial City." This book has been translated into hundreds of languages and has encouraged generations of believers to persevere in their faith.

Bunyan was born in Elstow, England, and followed his father’s trade as a tinker, or a repairer of metal pots. In his youth, he was not a religious man and later described himself as someone who took pleasure in many vices. However, through the influence of his wife, the witness of several poor women talking about the new birth, and the preaching of his pastor, John Gifford, Bunyan underwent a profound and often agonizing spiritual struggle. He eventually found peace by trusting in the sufficiency of Jesus Christ’s righteousness rather than his own efforts. His autobiography, "Grace Abounding to the Chief of Sinners," vividly recounts this journey from guilt to the assurance of God's grace.

His life illustrates the biblical truth that God is holy and just, yet merciful to sinners. Because of our sin, we are separated from God, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. Bunyan’s own conversion reminds us that salvation is a gift received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Bunyan became a popular "non-conformist" preacher, meaning he did not belong to the established Church of England. After the restoration of the monarchy, it became illegal to preach without a license from the state church. Because Bunyan refused to stop preaching the Gospel, he was arrested and spent about twelve years in the Bedford county jail. It was during this time of suffering and refinement that he wrote much of his famous work. His life is a testament to the promise in Romans 8:28, which says, "And we know that for those who love God all things work together for good, for those who are called according to his purpose."

Even in prison, Bunyan’s primary authority remained the Bible. He was often described as a man whose blood was "bibline," meaning that if you pricked him, he would bleed Scripture. He held firmly to the perseverance of the saints, believing that those truly in Christ are kept by God’s power and will not finally fall away, as seen in John 10:27–29 and Philippians 1:6. 

Bunyan's legacy continues to point people to the lordship of Christ and the necessity of a personal, saving relationship with Him. What is your own understanding of the journey Bunyan describes, and have you ever felt that weight of sin he spoke of?
Did Adam and Eve have belly buttons / navels?

The Bible does not explicitly state whether Adam and Eve had belly buttons. Because this is a detail Scripture does not address, Christians have historically viewed this as a matter of pious speculation rather than a core doctrine of the faith.

From a physiological perspective, many believe that Adam and Eve did not have navels. A belly button is essentially a scar left behind from the umbilical cord, which connects a developing baby to their mother in the womb to provide nourishment and oxygen. According to the account in Genesis, Adam was created directly by God from the dust of the ground (Genesis 2:7), and Eve was created from Adam's rib (Genesis 2:21-22). Since neither was born of a woman, they would have had no biological need for an umbilical cord or the resulting scar.

On the other hand, some suggest that God might have created Adam and Eve with the appearance of age or history, much like the trees in the Garden of Eden likely had rings or the starlight was already visible on earth. In this view, God might have given them navels so they would be "prototypical" humans, representing the complete human form that all their descendants would possess.

Ultimately, whether they had belly buttons does not change the essential truths revealed in the opening chapters of Genesis. What is most important is that God created humanity in His own image (Genesis 1:27) and that Adam and Eve were the historical parents of all people.

While we may wonder about their physical appearance, the Bible focuses more on their spiritual journey. Though they were created in a state of innocence, their later disobedience brought sin and death into the world, affecting every generation that followed (Romans 5:12). This separation from God is something we all experience, but the Good News is that God did not leave us without hope.

Jesus Christ, who is called the "last Adam" (1 Corinthians 15:45), came to earth as God in the flesh. He lived a perfect, sinless life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death forever. Through His sacrifice, we can be reconciled to God. 

You can turn to God today by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and eternal life. What do you think about the relationship between the first Adam and the work of Jesus?
What was the relationship between David and Jonathan?

The relationship between David and Jonathan is one of the most profound examples of faithful, sacrificial friendship recorded in the Bible. Their bond began shortly after David defeated Goliath, when Jonathan, the son of King Saul and heir to the throne, recognized God’s hand upon David. The Scriptures describe their connection by stating that the soul of Jonathan was knit to the soul of David, and Jonathan loved him as his own soul (1 Samuel 18:1).

This relationship was defined by a formal covenant, a serious and binding agreement made before God (1 Samuel 18:3; 1 Samuel 23:18). In an extraordinary act of humility and faith, Jonathan gave David his royal robe, his armor, and even his sword and bow (1 Samuel 18:4). This was not merely a gift of equipment but a symbolic recognition that David was God’s chosen next king of Israel. Jonathan chose to honor God’s will above his own political ambitions, demonstrating a selfless love that prioritized the kingdom of God over his own status.

Throughout their lives, Jonathan acted as a protector for David. When King Saul sought to kill David out of jealousy, Jonathan risked his own life and his relationship with his father to warn and defend his friend (1 Samuel 19:1-7; 1 Samuel 20:1-42). This loyalty was rooted in their shared faith in God and their mutual commitment to the covenant they had made. Even when they were forced to live apart, Jonathan sought David out in the wilderness to strengthen his hand in God (1 Samuel 23:16).

When Jonathan died in battle on Mount Gilboa, David’s grief was intense. He composed a lament, famously saying of Jonathan, your love to me was extraordinary, surpassing the love of women (2 Samuel 1:26). In the biblical and cultural context, this highlights a deep, platonic, brotherly devotion that exceeded the standard social and familial expectations of the time. David later honored their covenant by showing great kindness to Jonathan’s son, Mephibosheth, providing for him as if he were one of David’s own sons (2 Samuel 9:1-13).

The sacrificial love seen in David and Jonathan points us toward an even greater reality. While David and Jonathan were faithful friends, all human relationships are eventually impacted by sin and death. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, but God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the ultimate friend of sinners. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day to secure victory over death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers reconciliation to all who believe.

Through Christ, we receive a grace that no human friendship can fully provide. I invite you to turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What do you find most striking about the loyalty David and Jonathan showed to one another?
What is an appropriate level of physical intimacy before marriage?

The Bible teaches that physical intimacy is a beautiful gift from God, designed to be fully expressed and enjoyed exclusively within the covenant of marriage between one man and one woman. Because marriage represents the committed, sacrificial love between Christ and His church, the sexual union is reserved as the seal of that lifelong promise.
