This is not a command for a woman to never speak, but rather a description of the peace that fills her heart when she relies on God, as seen in Philippians 4:6-7.

Peter emphasizes that this inner character is more valuable than outward adornment because it is eternal. While fashion and physical appearance fade, a spirit rooted in God’s grace remains beautiful. This beauty is "precious in the sight of God" because it reflects the character of His Son, Jesus Christ.

This disposition is particularly powerful in the context of relationships. Peter explains that a woman’s godly conduct and respectful spirit can be a testimony that wins others to Christ without a word being spoken, as noted in 1 Peter 3:1-2. It is an expression of deep faith, believing that God is the ultimate judge and protector.

Ultimately, this gentle and quiet spirit is a fruit of the Holy Spirit’s work in a believer’s life. We are all prone to pride, anxiety, and self-assertion because of our sinful nature, which separates us from a holy God. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross for our sins, and rise again to give us new life. When we recognize our need for Him, we find that His grace transforms us from the inside out.

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As you walk with Him, He begins to cultivate this lasting, inner beauty in your heart through His Word and prayer. What makes it difficult for you to trust God with the things that cause your spirit to feel restless?
What does it mean to be free from sin?

To be free from sin is a beautiful and central theme of the Christian faith, but it is often misunderstood as reaching a state of perfection where we never struggle again. In the Bible, being free from sin has three vital dimensions, often described as freedom from the penalty, the power, and eventually, the presence of sin.

First, being free from sin means being free from its legal penalty. The Bible teaches that the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23). When we trust in Jesus Christ, His perfect life and sacrificial death are credited to us. We are justified, which means God declares us righteous in His sight. Because of what Christ has done, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). This is a legal freedom where our debt is fully paid, and we are no longer under God's judgment.

Second, being free from sin means being free from its mastery or power in our daily lives. Before knowing Christ, we were slaves to sin, unable to truly please God. However, through the Holy Spirit, the "old self" was crucified with Christ so that we might no longer be enslaved to sin (Romans 6:6). While we still feel the presence of temptation, sin no longer has the final word or dominion over us because we are under grace (Romans 6:14). We are now free to choose obedience and to grow in holiness, a process called sanctification (Romans 6:22).

Finally, Christians look forward to a future freedom from the very presence of sin. This will happen when Christ returns and we are given resurrected bodies. In that day, God will wipe away every tear, and there will be no more death, mourning, or pain, because the old order of things, including sin and its effects, will have passed away (Revelation 21:4).

This freedom is only possible through the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory. Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to take the punishment we deserved. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation and freedom from sin are not earned by our good works or moral effort, but are received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is God man-made? Did man create the idea of God?

According to the Bible, God is not man-made, nor is He a product of human imagination. Instead, the Scriptures teach that God is the eternal, self-existent Creator who was present before time began. Genesis 1:1 states, "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth." Rather than humans creating the idea of God, the Bible reveals that God created humanity in His own image. Genesis 1:27 says, "So God created man in his own image, in the image of God he created him; male and female he created them."

The reason people across different cultures and eras have a sense of the divine is that God has made His existence known to everyone through the world He created. Romans 1:19–20 explains that what can be known about God is plain to people because God has shown it to them, and His invisible attributes, namely His eternal power and divine nature, have been clearly perceived in the things that have been made.

This God who created the universe is perfectly holy and just, but because of sin, humanity has become separated from Him. However, in His great mercy, God did not leave us in that separation. He entered into His own creation in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on a cross as a substitute for sinners, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation and a restored relationship with God are found only through Him, not by our own works or efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What leads you to ask if the idea of God began with humans?
What are the Hallel Psalms?

The Hallel Psalms are specific groups of songs found in the book of Psalms that focus on giving praise and thanks to God. The word Hallel itself comes from the Hebrew word meaning to praise, which is also the root of the word Hallelujah, or Praise the Lord.

The most well known group is the Egyptian Hallel, which consists of Psalm 113 through Psalm 118. These were historically sung by the Jewish people during the major annual festivals, particularly during the Passover meal. Psalm 113 and Psalm 114 were typically sung before the meal, while Psalm 115 through Psalm 118 were sung after the meal was finished (Psalm 113:1-9; Psalm 118:1-29).

It is deeply moving to consider that when the New Testament records that Jesus and His disciples sang a hymn before heading to the Mount of Olives on the night He was betrayed, they were likely singing these very psalms (Matthew 26:30; Mark 14:26). In these verses, the Messiah would have been singing about God lifting the needy from the ash heap and the stone that the builders rejected becoming the cornerstone (Psalm 113:7; Psalm 118:22).

There are two other groups often referred to as Hallel. The Great Hallel usually refers to Psalm 136, which is famous for the repeating refrain, His steadfast love endures forever (Psalm 136:1-26). Finally, the Daily Hallel consists of the final five psalms in the Bible, Psalm 146 through Psalm 150, which all begin and end with the call to praise the Lord (Psalm 146:1; Psalm 150:6).

These psalms point us to the character of our holy and sovereign God, who is worthy of all our worship. While we are separated from God by our sin and deserve His just judgment, God showed His immense love by sending His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone and not by any works we can do.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is a bulwark in the Bible?

A bulwark in the Bible refers to a fortification, rampart, or defensive wall used to protect a city from enemy attacks. In a literal sense, ancient civilizations constructed these structures out of earth, stone, or wood to provide a vantage point for defenders and a barrier against invaders. For example, Deuteronomy 20:20 describes the building of bulwarks during a siege to overcome a city that is at war.

Beyond the physical structures of warfare, the Bible frequently uses the term "bulwark" as a metaphor for the spiritual protection and security found in God. The authors of Scripture often describe God’s salvation and His presence as an impenetrable wall that keeps His people safe from harm. Isaiah 26:1 declares that God appoints salvation as walls and bulwarks for His people. Similarly, Psalm 48:13 encourages believers to consider the bulwarks of Zion, the city of God, as a testimony to His enduring faithfulness and protection over generations.

While physical bulwarks can be breached and earthly kingdoms can fall, the Bible points us to a security that is eternal and unshakable. This security is necessary because every person faces a spiritual crisis more significant than any physical siege. God is perfectly holy, but human beings are separated from Him by sin, which leads to spiritual death and judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). No human effort or moral "bulwark" we build for ourselves can bridge this gap or protect us from the consequences of our rebellion against God.

However, in His great love and mercy, God provided the ultimate refuge through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth and lived a perfect, sinless life that we could never live. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all creation.

Salvation is not earned by our own good works or religious efforts, but is a gift of God received through grace alone, through faith alone, in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). When we trust in Him, He becomes our ultimate bulwark and our righteousness. You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you find yourself seeking safety or peace in things that are temporary, I encourage you to look to the Lord, who is a "very present help in trouble" (Psalm 46:1). Following Jesus involves a daily walk of prayer and reading His Word, the Bible, which is our final authority for faith and life. Connecting with a local, Bible-believing church is also a vital step for encouragement and growth in this new life.
What is the significance of the statement “God is within her; she will not fall” in Psalm 46:5?

The statement found in Psalm 46:5 is a beautiful declaration of God's protective presence and the security it provides to His people. To understand its full significance, it is helpful to look at the historical context, the identity of the "her" mentioned, and the theological truth it conveys for believers today.

In the context of Psalm 46, the "her" refers to the city of God, specifically Jerusalem or Zion. The psalmist describes a world in total chaos, where mountains shake and waters roar, yet the city where God dwells remains peaceful and secure. Psalm 46:4 mentions the holy habitation of the Most High, and verse 5 continues this thought by explaining why the city is unshakeable: God is in the midst of her. Because God is present within the city, it cannot be moved by the surrounding turmoil. The verse also promises that God will help her when morning dawns, signifying that His deliverance is certain and timely as expressed in Psalm 46:1; Psalm 46:4; Psalm 46:5.

The spiritual significance of this passage lies in the character of God. He is described throughout the Psalm as a refuge and a very present help in trouble. The stability of the city does not come from its walls or its own strength, but from the fact that the sovereign, holy, and faithful God has chosen to dwell there. This reflects the biblical truth that God is the source of all peace and security. In the Old Covenant, His presence was uniquely tied to the Temple in Jerusalem. Under the New Covenant, this promise takes on a deeper meaning as God dwells within His people through the Holy Spirit. Those who are in Christ are described as the temple of the living God in 1 Corinthians 6:19; 2 Corinthians 6:16.

For the believer, this verse serves as a reminder that when God is at the center of your life, you possess a stability that the world cannot provide or take away. While we may face trials, persecutions, or personal hardships, we are held secure by the power of God. This does not mean that Christians will never experience suffering, but it does mean that we will not be destroyed or permanently overcome, because God's grace and power sustain us as noted in 2 Corinthians 4:8; 2 Corinthians 4:9.

This ultimate security is only possible through a relationship with God through Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, and our sins naturally separate us from His presence. However, God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own good works, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from trusting Him today? If you find yourself in a season of uncertainty, remember that the same God who kept Zion secure is the one who invites you to find rest and refuge in Him. He is faithful to keep those who belong to Him until the end as promised in Philippians 1:6; Jude 1:24.
What is the history and significance of the church in Philippi?

The church in Philippi holds a special place in the history of the early church as the first congregation established by the Apostle Paul in Europe. Its story begins during Paul’s second missionary journey, around 50 AD, when he received a vision of a man from Macedonia pleading for help, as recorded in Acts 16:9.

Philippi was a Roman colony in eastern Macedonia, populated largely by retired Roman soldiers and their families. This gave the city a distinct "miniature Rome" atmosphere, where Roman law, the Latin language, and imperial pride were central to daily life. This context is vital for understanding why Paul emphasized that the believers' true citizenship is in heaven, as stated in Philippians 3:20.

The church’s foundation was marked by diverse and miraculous conversions. The first was Lydia, a wealthy merchant of purple cloth whose heart the Lord opened to pay attention to Paul’s message, mentioned in Acts 16:14–15. Soon after, following the miraculous opening of prison doors after an earthquake, the Philippian jailer and his entire household came to faith. When the jailer asked what he must do to be saved, Paul and Silas replied, "Believe in the Lord Jesus, and you will be saved, you and your household," in Acts 16:31.

The significance of the Philippian church lies in its deep and affectionate partnership with Paul. Unlike some other churches that struggled with significant division or false teaching, the Philippians were noted for their consistent financial and prayerful support of Paul’s ministry. He calls them his "joy and crown" in Philippians 4:1 and thanks them for their "partnership in the gospel from the first day until now" in Philippians 1:5.

Theologically, the letter Paul wrote to this church contains one of the most profound descriptions of Jesus Christ in all of Scripture. In Philippians 2:5–11, often called the "Christ Hymn," Paul describes how Jesus, being in the very nature God, did not cling to His divine privileges but humbled Himself by becoming a man and dying on a cross. Because of His obedience, God has exalted Him to the highest place, so that at the name of Jesus every knee should bow.

At its core, the history of the Philippian church is a testimony to the power of the Gospel to transcend social and cultural barriers. It reminds us that salvation is not earned through our own merit, status, or works, but is a gift from God. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we needed a Savior. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord who offers forgiveness to all who trust in Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who was Araunah the Jebusite? What was the threshing floor of Araunah?

Araunah the Jebusite was a man who lived in Jerusalem during the reign of King David. His name is also recorded as Ornan in some accounts. As a Jebusite, he belonged to the original inhabitants of the city before David captured it to make it the capital of Israel. Araunah is most famous for owning a specific piece of land, a threshing floor, which became the site of a profound encounter between God and King David as recorded in 2 Samuel 24:16 and 1 Chronicles 21:15.

A threshing floor was typically a flat, elevated area of hard ground or rock where farmers would beat stalks of grain to separate the kernels from the chaff. Because these floors were located on high ground to catch the wind, they were prominent landmarks. The threshing floor of Araunah was located on Mount Moriah, just outside the city walls of Jerusalem at that time.

The significance of this location began when King David sinned against God by conducting a census of Israel. This act showed a heart of pride and a reliance on military strength rather than on God's provision. As a result of this sin, a plague struck the nation. As the angel of the Lord was stretching out his hand to destroy Jerusalem, the Lord relented from the calamity and commanded the angel to stop at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite (2 Samuel 24:16).

The prophet Gad instructed David to go up and build an altar to the Lord on that very spot. When David arrived, Araunah generously offered to give the land, the oxen for sacrifice, and the wooden threshing sledges for fuel to the king for free. However, David famously responded that he would not offer burnt offerings to the Lord that cost him nothing. David purchased the floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver, built the altar, and offered sacrifices. God answered David's prayer, and the plague was stayed from Israel (2 Samuel 24:24-25).

The threshing floor of Araunah holds immense historical and spiritual importance for three primary reasons:

First, it became the permanent site of the Temple. David’s son, Solomon, built the first Temple on this exact location, as noted in 2 Chronicles 3:1. This transformed a place of agricultural labor and divine judgment into the dwelling place of God’s glory and the center of worship for Israel.

Second, it is traditionally associated with Mount Moriah, the same place where centuries earlier Abraham was tested by God and told to sacrifice his son, Isaac (Genesis 22:2). In both instances, God provided a sacrifice to take the place of the one facing death, demonstrating His mercy and His role as the ultimate Provider.

Third, this site points us toward the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Just as David stood at the threshing floor and saw the judgment of God stopped through a sacrifice, we recognize that our own sin deserves judgment. However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the perfect sacrifice. Jesus lived a sinless life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His death and resurrection, Christ satisfied the righteous wrath of God against sin, offering us forgiveness and reconciliation.

This history reminds us that God is both holy and merciful. While our sin separates us from Him, He has provided the way for us to be restored through faith in His Son. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about worship?

The Bible presents worship not merely as a musical style or a specific event, but as a comprehensive response of the heart, mind, and body to the glory and grace of God. True worship begins with a right understanding of who God is and what He has done.

Jesus taught that the essence of worship is internal rather than being tied to a specific physical location. In John 4:23-24, He says, "But the hour is coming, and is now here, when the true worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth, for the Father is seeking such people to worship him. God is spirit, and those who worship him must worship in spirit and truth." To worship in spirit means it comes from a heart transformed by the Holy Spirit, and to worship in truth means it is grounded in the reality of God as revealed in His Word.

Worship is also described as a total commitment of one's life. Romans 12:1 says, "I appeal to you therefore, brothers, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God, which is your spiritual worship." This indicates that every action, whether at work, at home, or in the community, can be an act of worship when done for the glory of God.

While worship is a lifestyle, the Bible also emphasizes the importance of believers gathering together. Corporate worship serves to encourage the church and honor God collectively. Hebrews 10:24-25 says, "And let us consider how to stir up one another to love and good works, not neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some, but encouraging one another, and all the more as you see the Day drawing near." During these gatherings, the early church engaged in prayer, the reading of Scripture, and singing. Colossians 3:16 says, "Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, teaching and admonishing one another in all wisdom, singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in your hearts to God."

God is deeply concerned with the sincerity of our worship. He values a humble and contrite heart over empty religious rituals. In Matthew 15:8, Jesus quotes the prophet Isaiah to rebuke those whose outward actions did not match their inner state, saying, "This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me." 

Ultimately, our ability to worship God is a result of the Gospel. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we could not approach Him on our own. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over sin and death, those who trust in Him are reconciled to God. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Once we are reconciled to God through faith, our lives become a continuous song of praise to the One who rescued us. What do you find most challenging about making worship a daily priority?
Who wrote the book of Exodus? Who was the author of Exodus?

The historic and traditional view held by the Church and the Jewish community is that Moses is the author of the book of Exodus. This understanding is rooted in the text itself and is supported by the testimony of the rest of Scripture.

Within the book, there are several instances where God specifically commands Moses to record events and laws in writing. For example, after a victory in battle, the Lord instructed Moses to write the account in a book (Exodus 17:14). Moses is also recorded as writing down all the words of the Lord (Exodus 24:4) and the specific instructions of the covenant (Exodus 34:27).

The New Testament provides further confirmation through the words of Jesus and the Apostles. Jesus frequently referred to the first five books of the Bible, known as the Pentateuch, as the writings of Moses (Mark 12:26; Luke 24:44). He explicitly stated that Moses wrote about Him (John 5:46-47). Because the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, Christians trust this internal and external testimony regarding its authorship.

While some modern academic theories suggest that the book was compiled from multiple sources over a long period, the essential Christian conviction remains that Moses was the primary author. It is possible that later inspired editors updated certain geographical names or added minor details to ensure clarity for future generations, but the core content remains the work of Moses under the inspiration of the Holy Spirit.

The central theme of Exodus is God's power to redeem His people from bondage, a theme that points directly to the Gospel. Just as God delivered Israel from slavery in Egypt, He has provided a greater deliverance from the power of sin through Jesus Christ. You can turn from sin today and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. What are your thoughts on how God interacts with His people in these ancient texts?
What is more important, the death of Christ or His resurrection?

In the Christian faith, the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ are not viewed as competing events, but as two inseparable parts of a single, saving work. To ask which is more important is like asking which wing of an airplane is more necessary for flight. Both are essential for our salvation.

The death of Christ was the necessary sacrifice to pay the penalty for our sins. Because God is holy and just, sin must be punished. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died in our place, taking the wrath of God upon Himself. Without His death, there would be no payment for our debt, and we would remain under God's judgment. Scripture tells us that God shows His love for us in that while we were still sinners, Christ died for us (Romans 5:8). We are healed by His wounds (1 Peter 2:24).

The resurrection of Christ is equally vital because it is the victory over death and the proof that God accepted Jesus' sacrifice. If Jesus had remained in the grave, He would be seen as just another man who died, and our hope would be buried with Him. The Apostle Paul writes that if Christ has not been raised, our preaching is in vain and your faith is futile; you are still in your sins (1 Corinthians 15:14; 1 Corinthians 15:17). The resurrection confirms Jesus' identity as the Son of God and guarantees our own future resurrection and new life. He was delivered up for our trespasses and raised for our justification (Romans 4:25).

Together, these events form the heart of the Gospel. The full message is that God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin. Out of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the life we could not live and die the death we deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, ascending to heaven where He reigns as Lord. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the emerging / emergent church movement?

The emerging church movement is a diverse and decentralized trend within Christianity that gained significant attention in the late 1990s and early 2000s. It primarily arose as a reaction to perceived shortcomings in the modern evangelical church, seeking to engage more effectively with a postmodern culture. While it is difficult to define as a single organization, it generally emphasizes authentic community, social justice, and a "missional" lifestyle.

The movement is often categorized into two overlapping groups. The broader "emerging" wing frequently focused on changing the style and methods of church, such as moving away from traditional programs toward smaller, more organic gatherings. The "emergent" wing, however, often moved into the realm of theological deconstruction. This branch frequently questioned historical Christian doctrines, including the nature of biblical authority, the exclusivity of Christ, and traditional views on sin and judgment.

From a biblical perspective, there are several key areas to consider when evaluating this movement.

The movement's emphasis on authentic community and living out one's faith in the world is a helpful reminder of biblical commands to love our neighbors. The Bible calls believers to be the "salt of the earth" and the "light of the world" in Matthew 5:13-16. Genuine fellowship and caring for the vulnerable are central to the Christian life, as seen in James 1:27 and Acts 2:42-47.

However, a significant concern regarding the emergent branch is its tendency toward theological relativism. Many voices within the movement favored "conversation" over the declaration of absolute truth. While humility is a Christian virtue, the Bible presents truth as something that can be known and must be defended. Scripture is the inspired and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority for faith and practice, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16-17. We are warned in 2 Timothy 4:3-4 that a time will come when people will not endure sound teaching but will wander into myths.

Furthermore, the Gospel itself can become obscured when the focus shifts primarily to social reform or personal experience. The core of the Christian faith is the historical reality of what God has done through Jesus Christ. God is perfectly holy and just, as described in Isaiah 6:3 and Psalm 89:14. Because of our sin, every human being is separated from God and deserves His righteous judgment, according to Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23.

In His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, as seen in John 1:1 and John 1:14. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, which is recorded in 1 Peter 2:24 and 2 Corinthians 5:21. He was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts, social activism, or religious traditions. It is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, as taught in Ephesians 2:8-9. Jesus Himself said in John 14:6, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me."

If you find yourself searching for meaning or feeling the weight of your own imperfections, I want to invite you to consider the hope found in Jesus. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that holds you back from placing your trust in Him today?

In all things, believers are encouraged to "test everything; hold fast what is good," as written in 1 Thessalonians 5:21. While we should always look for ways to share the Gospel clearly with our culture, we must ensure that the message we share remains grounded in the unchanging truth of God's Word. Hebrews 13:8 reminds us that "Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever."
What is the Mount of Transfiguration?

The Mount of Transfiguration refers to the location where Jesus Christ’s divine glory was briefly revealed to three of His disciples, Peter, James, and John. Although the Bible does not name the specific mountain, this event is recorded in the Gospels of Matthew 17:1–9, Mark 9:2–10, and Luke 9:28–36. Traditionally, many have identified this site as Mount Tabor or Mount Hermon.

During this miraculous event, Jesus took His disciples up a high mountain to pray. As He was praying, the appearance of His face changed, shining like the sun, and His clothing became dazzling white (Matthew 17:2; Luke 9:29). This was a physical manifestation of His divine nature, which is usually veiled by His humanity. It served as a powerful confirmation to the disciples that Jesus is truly the Son of God.

Two significant figures from the Old Testament, Moses and Elijah, appeared and talked with Jesus. Their presence represented the Law (Moses) and the Prophets (Elijah) testifying that Jesus is the fulfillment of all God's promises to His people (Matthew 17:3). Luke 9:31 notes that they spoke about Jesus' "departure," referring to His upcoming death, burial, and resurrection in Jerusalem.

A bright cloud then overshadowed them, and the voice of God the Father spoke from the cloud, saying, "This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased; listen to him" (Matthew 17:5). This divine endorsement commanded the disciples to look to Jesus as the ultimate authority.

The Transfiguration is deeply significant because it reminds us that Jesus is not merely a good teacher or a prophet, but the holy, sovereign Lord of all. Because He is the sinless Son of God who took on human flesh, His sacrificial death on the cross is the only sufficient way for us to be reconciled to God. We are all separated from God by our sin, but Christ lived a perfect life, died in our place to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

Through His work, salvation is offered by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What do you think about the claim that Jesus is truly God in human form?
What does the Bible say about underage drinking?

The Bible does not mention a specific "legal drinking age" because such laws are modern developments. However, Scripture provides clear principles regarding the consumption of alcohol, obedience to authority, and the preservation of a sober mind that directly apply to the question of underage drinking.

First, the Bible commands believers to obey the laws of the governing authorities. In Romans 13:1, it says, "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God." Additionally, 1 Peter 2:13–14 instructs Christians to be subject to every human institution for the Lord’s sake. If the law of the land sets a specific age for drinking, a Christian honors God by obeying that law. To drink under the legal age is to disobey the authorities God has placed over us, unless those authorities command us to do something that directly violates God's Word.

Second, Scripture is very clear in its prohibition of drunkenness. Ephesians 5:18 says, "And do not get drunk with wine, for that is debauchery, but be filled with the Spirit." Proverbs 20:1 warns that "Wine is a mocker, strong drink a brawler, and whoever is led astray by it is not wise." The Bible calls believers to lives of self-control and sobriety. 1 Peter 5:8 tells us to "Be sober-minded; be watchful," because our adversary the devil prowls around like a roaring lion. Alcohol, especially when used by those who are young or seeking to bypass the law, often leads to a loss of judgment and self-control.

Third, for those who are still under the care of their parents, the Bible emphasizes the importance of honor and obedience. Ephesians 6:1 says, "Children, obey your parents in the Lord, for this is right." Honoring your parents involves respecting their rules and the boundaries they set for your safety and spiritual growth.

While the Bible warns against the dangers of alcohol and the sin of drunkenness, it also points us to a deeper need. We all struggle with the desire to assert our own will over God’s commands or the laws of man. This inclination is what the Bible calls sin, which separates us from a holy and perfect God. Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of complete obedience to the Father. He died on a cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the wrath we deserved upon Himself. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not something we earn by following rules or being "good enough"; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. When we trust in Him, the Holy Spirit begins a work of transformation in our hearts, giving us new desires to honor God and live with the self-control He requires. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Will there be a great apostasy / falling away during the end times?

The New Testament explicitly teaches that a period of significant spiritual rebellion and abandonment of the faith will precede the return of Jesus Christ. This event is often referred to as the apostasy or the falling away.

In 2 Thessalonians 2:3, the Apostle Paul writes, "Let no one deceive you in any way. For that day will not come, unless the rebellion comes first, and the man of lawlessness is revealed, the son of destruction." The word for rebellion in the original Greek is *apostasia*, which refers to a defection or a departure from a previously held position. This suggests a large scale turning away from the truth of God by many who may have outwardly identified with the church.

Jesus also warned His disciples about this period of testing in Matthew 24:10–12, stating, "And then many will fall away and betray one another and hate one another. And many false prophets will arise and lead many astray. And because lawlessness will be increased, the love of many will grow cold." This passage indicates that the end times will be characterized by deception and a decline in genuine Christian love and devotion.

The Scriptures further explain that this falling away involves a rejection of sound doctrine in favor of teachings that appeal to human desires. Paul warned Timothy in 2 Timothy 4:3–4, "For the time is coming when people will not endure sound teaching, but having itching ears they will accumulate for themselves teachers to suit their own passions, and will turn away from listening to the truth and wander off into myths." Similarly, 1 Timothy 4:1 notes that "in later times some will depart from the faith by devoting themselves to deceitful spirits and teachings of demons."

While these passages are sobering, they serve as a call for believers to remain vigilant, grounded in the Word of God, and dependent on the Holy Spirit. We can find great comfort in the promise that those who are truly in Christ are kept by God's power. Jude 1:24–25 reminds us that God is able to keep us from stumbling and to present us blameless before the presence of His glory with great joy.

The reality of a falling away highlights the vital importance of having a genuine, saving relationship with Jesus Christ rather than a mere outward form of religion. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from a holy and just God, but God, in His mercy, provided a way for reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts, but it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Does the thought of a great falling away cause you to feel any uncertainty about your own spiritual walk or the state of the world today?
Should all pronouns referring to God be capitalized?

The practice of capitalizing pronouns referring to God (such as He, Him, and His) is a tradition often called reverential capitalization. While many believers find this to be a helpful way to show honor to the Lord, it is important to understand that the Bible does not explicitly command it.

In the original languages of Scripture, Hebrew and Greek, the manuscripts did not use capitalization to distinguish pronouns in the way modern English does. Because of this, many faithful English translations like the King James Version, the English Standard Version, and the New International Version do not capitalize these pronouns. On the other hand, translations like the New King James Version and the New American Standard Bible do use capitalization to help the reader clearly identify when God is the subject and to express deep respect for His personhood.

Whether you choose to capitalize these pronouns is a matter of Christian liberty and personal or denominational style. What matters most is the posture of your heart toward God. He is infinitely holy, supreme, and worthy of all honor (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). When we speak or write about Him, our primary concern should be to "hallow" or treat His name as holy, just as Jesus taught in the Lord’s Prayer (Matthew 6:9).

Because God is perfectly holy and we are fallen, our sin creates a separation between us and Him (Isaiah 59:2). However, the Gospel is the beautiful truth that God reached out to us. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose again bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or outward traditions (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As you think about how we honor God in our writing, what do you find most helpful in focusing your heart on His holiness?
What is Kabbalah?

Kabbalah is a school of thought within Jewish mysticism that aims to explain the relationship between the eternal, mysterious, and infinite God and the finite, physical universe. It primarily emerged in its classic form during the twelfth and thirteenth centuries in Spain and France, notably through a collection of writings known as the Zohar. Kabbalists believe that the Torah contains hidden meanings and secret codes that, when decoded, reveal deep truths about the nature of God and the cosmos.

Central to Kabbalistic teaching are the ten "Sefirot," which are described as emanations or attributes through which the infinite God reveals Himself and interacts with the world. Practitioners often seek a direct, mystical experience of the divine through the study of these emanations, meditation, and specific prayers.

From a biblical perspective, there are significant differences between Kabbalah and the historic Christian faith. While Kabbalah often searches for "secret" knowledge or mystical layers beyond the text, the Bible teaches that God has fully and clearly revealed Himself to us in the person of Jesus Christ. The Book of Hebrews tells us that while God spoke in various ways in the past, He has "in these last days spoken to us by his Son" (Hebrews 1:1–2). Furthermore, the Apostle Paul warned believers to beware of philosophy and traditions that are not according to Christ (Colossians 2:8), affirming that in Jesus "the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily" (Colossians 2:9).

Scripture teaches that we do not need esoteric systems or hidden codes to know God. Instead, we are called to rest in the clarity and sufficiency of God's Word, which is "breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness" (2 Timothy 3:16). While some mystical traditions focus on a human ascent toward the divine, the heart of the Christian faith is the Gospel, which is the message of God descending to us.

The Gospel reveals that God is holy and just, but our sin has separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). Out of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die on the cross as a sacrifice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, demonstrating His power over death and His identity as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 1:4). Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not through achieving secret knowledge or performing works (Ephesians 2:8–9). Jesus is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6).

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Should a Christian be a prepper or in any way involved with doomsday prepping?

The Bible does not provide a single command that either forbids or requires what is commonly known as prepping. Instead, it offers principles of wisdom, stewardship, and faith that help a believer navigate these choices. The decision to prepare for future hardships is largely a matter of Christian liberty and individual conscience, guided by one's motivations and trust in God.

Scripture often commends the person who looks ahead and acts with prudence. In the book of Proverbs, we are told that a prudent person sees danger and takes precautions, while the simple keep going and pay the penalty (Proverbs 22:3). We are also encouraged to observe the ant, which stores up food in the summer to prepare for the winter (Proverbs 6:6-8). A classic biblical example of large scale preparation is found in the life of Joseph, whom God used to store grain during seven years of plenty so that many nations would not perish during seven years of famine (Genesis 41:33-36). From this perspective, having a reasonable supply of food, water, or medicine can be a way to provide for your household and even have resources to share with neighbors during a crisis (1 Timothy 5:8).

However, the heart behind the preparation is what matters most to God. Prepping can become a spiritual snare if it is rooted in fear, or if it leads to a spirit of self-reliance that replaces trust in God's providence. Jesus explicitly teaches His followers not to be anxious about what they will eat or wear, reminding them that their Heavenly Father knows their needs and is faithful to provide (Matthew 6:25-34). If prepping is driven by a desire to "save oneself" apart from God, or if it leads to hoarding while others suffer, it contradicts the call to love our neighbors as ourselves (Matthew 22:39).

Furthermore, a Christian’s ultimate security must never rest in a stockpile of earthly goods. While physical preparation has a place, spiritual preparation is far more vital. We live in a world broken by sin, and even the best-prepared person cannot avoid the reality of mortality or the uncertainties of life. Our true hope is found in the Gospel of Jesus Christ.

The Gospel begins with the reality that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can do (Ephesians 2:8-9).

The greatest "prep" any person can make is to be reconciled to God through His Son. I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

In summary, a Christian may choose to prepare for emergencies as an act of wise stewardship and love for others, provided it is done with a heart of faith rather than fear. Seek the Lord in prayer and perhaps talk with your local church elders as you weigh how to balance practical wisdom with a radical reliance on God’s daily bread (Matthew 6:11). Always remember that our lives are hidden with Christ, and He is the one who holds our future secure (Colossians 3:3).
What is the church?

In the most fundamental sense, the church is not a building or a physical location, but rather the assembly of people who have been called out by God and reconciled to Him through Jesus Christ. The New Testament describes the church in two primary ways: the universal church and the local church.

The universal church consists of all true believers in Jesus Christ across all of history and throughout the entire world. It is described as the body of Christ, with Jesus Himself as the head (Ephesians 1:22–23; Colossians 1:18). This body is made up of people from every tribe, language, and nation who are united by one Spirit through faith in the Gospel (1 Corinthians 12:12–13; Revelation 5:9).

The local church is a specific, visible gathering of believers in a particular location who meet regularly for worship, prayer, the study of Scripture, and the administration of baptism and the Lord's Supper (Acts 2:42; Hebrews 10:24–25). These local assemblies are where believers exercise their spiritual gifts to serve one another and where they sit under the leadership of elders or pastors (1 Peter 5:1–3; Titus 1:5).

The Bible also uses beautiful metaphors to describe the identity of the church. It is called the household of God (1 Timothy 3:15), a holy temple where God dwells by His Spirit (Ephesians 2:19–22), and the bride of Christ, whom Jesus loved and gave Himself up for to make her holy (Ephesians 5:25–27).

At its heart, the church is defined by the Gospel. We are a people who recognize that God is holy and supreme, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him. Out of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

We become part of God's family and His church not through our own good works or religious efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What has been your experience with the church, or what has led you to ask about its meaning?
Does a cashless society have anything to do with the end times?

The relationship between a cashless society and the end times is a topic that many Christians consider because of certain descriptions found in the book of Revelation. While the Bible does not use the specific term "cashless society," it does describe a future global economic system characterized by total control.

The primary passage people point to is Revelation 13:16-17, which says that a coming world leader will cause all people to receive a mark on their right hand or their forehead, so that no one can buy or sell unless he has the mark, that is, the name of the beast or the number of its name. For such a system to be enforced globally, it would require a level of monitoring and control that is difficult to achieve with physical cash, which is anonymous and hard to track. A digital or cashless system provides the infrastructure where an authority could potentially "switch off" an individual's ability to participate in the economy.

Throughout history, many technological and social shifts have been viewed as potential precursors to these events. In the modern era, the move toward digital currencies, biometric scanning, and central bank digital currencies (CBDCs) is seen by many believers as the development of the "plumbing" or framework that could one day be used by the system described in Revelation.

However, Christians hold a variety of views on exactly how these prophecies will be fulfilled. Some believe these events are literal and future, while others see them as symbolic of the ways worldly powers have always tried to coerce the worship of believers. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the central biblical message is one of vigilance and trust in God’s sovereignty. We are encouraged in 2 Timothy 1:7 that God has not given us a spirit of fear, but of power and love and self control.

The ultimate focus of end-times prophecy is not merely on the rise of a global system, but on the return of Jesus Christ to judge the world and set all things right. This reminds us that our primary concern should be our relationship with God.

Scripture teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all turned away from Him through sin, which separates us from His presence (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried (John 3:16; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4). On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:20). He is now exalted as Lord and will one day return to judge the living and the dead (Acts 2:33; Revelation 22:12). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or efforts (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

As we see the world change, it is helpful to stay grounded in the Word of God and in fellowship with a local church. What are your thoughts on these changes in our world, and do they cause you more curiosity or concern?
I am a former Catholic. Should I continue to go to Catholic Church so I can reach people for Christ?

It is a noble and Christ-like desire to want your friends and family to know the fullness of the Gospel. When considering whether to remain in a Catholic environment for the sake of evangelism, there are several biblical principles to weigh with prayer and wisdom.

First, your own spiritual health and growth are a priority. Every believer needs to be part of a local church where the Word of God is the final authority and the Gospel is preached clearly. Scripture commands us not to neglect meeting together for the purpose of stirring one another up to love and good works (Hebrews 10:24-25). If the teaching you receive does not align with the biblical truth that we are justified by faith alone apart from works, you may find your own soul becoming weary or confused over time.

Second, consider the message your presence sends. If you disagree with certain doctrines but continue to participate in the Mass, you may inadvertently signal to others that those doctrinal differences do not matter. In his letter to the Galatians, the Apostle Paul was very firm that there is only one Gospel, and any message that adds human effort to the finished work of Christ is a different gospel entirely (Galatians 1:6-9). True love for others involves being clear about the truth, even when it is difficult.

Third, you might find that you can be a more effective witness from the "outside." By joining a Bible-teaching church, you can invite your Catholic friends to come and hear the Word preached in a way they may never have experienced. You can then discuss the differences over a meal or in your home, pointing them directly to the Scriptures (Acts 17:11).

The heart of the message we share is this: God is perfectly holy and supreme, but we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, leaving us separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Out of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God's justice. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, triumphed over sin and death, and is now exalted as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Philippians 2:9-11). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9; Titus 3:5).

I invite you to continue to turn from any reliance on yourself and trust fully in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for your forgiveness and peace with God.

As you navigate this transition, it may be helpful to ask yourself: does staying in that environment help my friends see the distinct beauty of Christ’s finished work, or does it make that work seem like just one part of a larger religious system? Seeking the counsel of a pastor at a faithful, Bible-centered church would be a wise next step to help you discern how to honor God and love your neighbor in this specific situation.
What does the Bible say about world missions?

The Bible reveals that world missions is not merely a program of the church, but the very heartbeat of God. From the beginning of Scripture to its end, God’s purpose has been to call a people for Himself from every tribe, tongue, and nation.

This mission began with God’s promise to Abraham, where He declared that through Abraham’s offspring, all the families of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 12:1–3). The Psalms echo this global vision, calling for God’s way to be known on earth and His saving power among all nations (Psalm 67:1–2). The ultimate fulfillment of this mission is found in Jesus Christ, who commanded His followers to go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:18–20). This work is empowered by the Holy Spirit, who enables believers to be witnesses to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8).

The necessity of missions is rooted in the character of God and the state of humanity. God is perfectly holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3), yet every person has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), resulting in spiritual separation from Him. In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man (John 1:14). Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God (Romans 5:8). He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all (Philippians 2:9–11).

Because of what Christ has done, salvation is available to everyone who believes. It is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9). The goal of missions is to bring this Good News to everyone so that a great multitude from every nation will one day stand before the throne of God in worship (Revelation 7:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
How should we live our lives in light of our identity in Christ?

Living in light of our identity in Christ begins with the understanding that our standing before God is not based on what we do for Him, but on what Jesus Christ has already done for us. When we trust in Christ, we are no longer defined by our past failures, our worldly achievements, or the labels others place on us. Instead, we are defined by our union with Jesus.

The primary way we live out this identity is through a life of grateful response. Because God has chosen us, redeemed us, and adopted us as His own children (Ephesians 1:3-7), our motive for obedience shifts from fear of punishment or a desire to earn favor to a heart of love and thankfulness. We are "created in Christ Jesus for good works, which God prepared beforehand, that we should walk in them" (Ephesians 2:10). Our good works are the fruit of our salvation, not the root of it.

This new identity leads to a transformation of our character, often described in Scripture as "putting off" the old self and "putting on" the new self. Since we have been raised with Christ, we are encouraged to set our minds on things above, where Christ is (Colossians 3:1-2). This involves intentionally turning away from sin and worldly desires while cultivating the fruit of the Spirit, such as love, joy, peace, patience, kindness, goodness, faithfulness, gentleness, and self control (Galatians 5:22-23). This process, known as sanctification, is not something we do in our own strength, but by the power of the Holy Spirit dwelling within us.

Furthermore, our identity in Christ changes how we relate to the world around us. We are called to be ambassadors for Christ, as though God were making His appeal through us (2 Corinthians 5:20). This means we live with a sense of mission, showing the love of Christ to our neighbors and sharing the hope we have found in Him. We also find our place within the local church, the body of Christ, where we use our God given gifts to serve and encourage one another (1 Corinthians 12:12-27).

If you find yourself struggling to live out this identity or feel weighed down by guilt, it is helpful to return to the core of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, and because of our sin, we were once separated from Him. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die the death we deserved. Jesus took the punishment for our sins on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all.

Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and the new life of being His child.

How does the truth that God has already fully accepted you in Christ change the way you view your daily struggles?
How do I know the Bible is not just mythology?

The question of whether the Bible is historical truth or merely mythology is one that many people have asked throughout history. While myths typically describe events in a distant, timeless "once upon a time" setting, the Bible is deeply rooted in specific historical, geographical, and political contexts.

One of the most significant differences between the Bible and mythology is the presence of eyewitness testimony. The authors of the New Testament frequently emphasize that they were recording events they actually saw and heard. For example, the apostle Peter wrote, "For we did not follow cleverly devised myths when we made known to you the power and coming of our Lord Jesus Christ, but we were eyewitnesses of his majesty" (2 Peter 1:16). Similarly, the Gospel writer Luke explains that he conducted a careful investigation of the facts to provide an "orderly account" based on those who were eyewitnesses from the beginning (Luke 1:1–4).

From an archaeological and historical standpoint, the Bible has demonstrated remarkable accuracy. It names specific rulers, such as Pontius Pilate, Herod the Great, and Caesar Augustus, and describes cities and customs that have been verified by modern discoveries. Furthermore, the manuscript evidence for the Bible is far greater than for any other ancient work. There are thousands of ancient copies of the New Testament that allow scholars to confirm that the text we have today is essentially the same as what was written originally.

The central claim that separates the Bible from myth is the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ. Mythology often deals with metaphorical cycles of life and death, but the Bible claims that Jesus truly died and was physically raised back to life in a specific place (Jerusalem) at a specific time (during the reign of Tiberius Caesar). The apostle Paul notes that the risen Jesus appeared to more than five hundred people at once, many of whom were still alive and could be questioned when he wrote his letter (1 Corinthians 15:3–8). If the resurrection did not happen, the Christian faith would have no foundation, but the historical transformation of the apostles from fearful deserters into bold martyrs suggests they had truly encountered the risen Lord.

Ultimately, the Bible is not just a collection of historical facts, but God’s revelation of His character and His plan to rescue humanity. The Bible teaches that God is holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8), yet all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love and mercy, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life on our behalf. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord and will one day return to judge the world and make all things new.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life.

What holds you back from trusting that the claims of the Bible are true?
What does it mean that the Bible is infallible? What is biblical infallibility?

Biblical infallibility is the belief that the Bible is completely reliable and incapable of failing in its purpose to reveal God's truth to humanity. When we say the Scriptures are infallible, we are affirming that they are a perfect and certain guide in all matters of faith, doctrine, and practice. Because the Bible is breathed out by God, it carries His authority and cannot mislead those who sincerely seek Him through its pages (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Psalm 19:7).

While the term is often used interchangeably with "inerrancy," there is a helpful distinction between the two. Inerrancy focuses on the fact that the original manuscripts of the Bible contain no errors of any kind, whether historical, scientific, or theological. Infallibility goes a step further by emphasizing the Bible’s enduring power and trustworthiness. It means that God’s Word is sure and will always accomplish what He intends for it to do (Isaiah 55:11). This quality is rooted in the character of God Himself, who is the source of all truth and cannot lie (Titus 1:2; Hebrews 6:18).

This doctrine is essential for the Christian life for several reasons:

First, it establishes a firm foundation for our knowledge of God. If the Bible could fail us in its teachings, we would have no certain way to know who God is or what He requires of us. Instead, we can trust that "Thy word is truth" (John 17:17) and that it serves as a reliable lamp to our feet and a light to our path (Psalm 119:105).

Second, the infallibility of Scripture ensures that the message of the Gospel is preserved. The Bible reveals that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because God is merciful, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This message is not a human invention but a divinely preserved truth that offers us the only way to be reconciled to God.

Finally, knowing the Bible is infallible gives us great comfort and confidence. We do not have to rely on shifting human opinions or our own fluctuating emotions. We can rest in the "prophetic word more fully confirmed" (2 Peter 1:19), knowing that God has spoken clearly and that His promises will never fail.

The ultimate purpose of the infallible Scriptures is to lead us to a saving relationship with Jesus Christ. Through the Bible, we hear the call to turn from our sin and trust in Jesus alone for forgiveness and eternal life. You can turn to God now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

Is there anything specific about the reliability of the Bible that you find difficult to embrace?
What does the Bible mean when it says something is unclean?

In the Bible, the concept of being "unclean" refers primarily to a state of ritual or ceremonial impurity rather than just physical dirtiness or moral sin. Under the Old Covenant, particularly in the books of the Law, being unclean meant that a person was temporarily unfit to participate in the corporate worship of God at the Tabernacle or Temple (Leviticus 15:31).

This ritual uncleanness was categorized in several ways:

1. Dietary Laws: God designated certain animals as "unclean" and prohibited the Israelites from eating them (Leviticus 11:1–47).
2. Physical Conditions: Contact with death, certain skin diseases, and various bodily discharges rendered a person ceremonially unclean (Leviticus 13:1–59; Numbers 19:11–22).
3. Moral Corruption: While ritual uncleanness was often unavoidable and not necessarily sinful (such as burying the dead), the Bible also uses the term "unclean" to describe the spiritual stain of sin and rebellion against God (Isaiah 64:6).

The primary purpose of these laws was to teach the people of Israel about the absolute holiness of God. By requiring specific washings or sacrifices to move from a state of "unclean" to "clean," God was showing that a person must be purified to enter His presence. These regulations served as a vivid, daily reminder that God is set apart from everything common, decaying, or sinful.

In the New Testament, Jesus Christ brought a profound shift to this understanding. He taught that true uncleanness is not a matter of what we eat or touch, but a matter of the heart. He explained that evil thoughts, murders, and thefts are what truly defile a person (Mark 7:18–23). When Jesus touched those considered "unclean," such as lepers or the dead, He did not become unclean Himself; instead, His holiness and power flowed outward to heal and cleanse them (Matthew 8:1–3).

This points us to the heart of the Gospel. Because of our sin, every human being is spiritually "unclean" and separated from a holy God. We cannot cleanse ourselves through our own works or rituals. However, God, who is rich in mercy and justice, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He took the "uncleanness" of our sin upon Himself, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through Christ, we are offered total purification. Scripture promises that if we confess our sins, He is faithful and just to forgive us and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness (1 John 1:9). This cleansing is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the meaning of life?

The meaning of life is fundamentally rooted in our relationship with the Creator who made us for Himself. According to the Bible, we were created by God and for His glory (Colossians 1:16; Isaiah 43:7). Our highest purpose is to know God, to love Him, and to reflect His character in the world.

However, humanity has turned away from God through sin, which is a rejection of His authority and a failure to live for His glory (Romans 3:23). This separation from God is why so many people feel a sense of emptiness or lack of direction. Because God is holy and just, our sin carries the penalty of death and eternal separation from Him (Romans 6:23).

The good news, or the Gospel, is that God did not leave us in our brokenness. Because of His great mercy, Jesus Christ, the Son of God, became a man to rescue us. He lived a life of perfect obedience that we could not live, died a substitutionary death on the cross to take the punishment for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the dead, proving His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

True meaning is found when we are reconciled to God, not through our own good works or efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8–9; John 17:3). When we trust in Him, we are given a new identity, the gift of the Holy Spirit, and a hope that transcends our earthly circumstances.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can there be subordination in the Trinity?

Understanding the relationship between the members of the Trinity requires a distinction between who God is in His essence and how the Father, Son, and Holy Spirit relate to one another in the work of redemption. Historically, Christians distinguish between ontological equality and functional subordination.

In His essence, or what God is, there is no subordination. The Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are one God, co-eternal and co-equal in power, glory, and majesty. Scripture affirms that Jesus is fully God, as seen in John 10:30 where He says, "I and the Father are one," and in Colossians 2:9, which states, "For in him the whole fullness of deity dwells bodily." Because they share the same divine nature, the Son is not "less than" the Father in His being or status.

However, in the "economy" or the outworking of God’s plan, there is a willing subordination of roles. This is often called functional or economic subordination. This means that while the Son is equal to the Father in nature, He voluntarily submits to the Father's will to accomplish the plan of salvation. Jesus highlights this relationship in John 5:19 when He says, "the Son can do nothing of his own accord, but only what he sees the Father doing," and in John 14:28 where He says, "the Father is greater than I," referring to His role and state of humiliation during His earthly ministry.

This order is not a sign of inferiority, just as a citizen submitting to a governing official or a child honoring a parent does not mean they are less of a human being. In the Trinity, this order is characterized by perfect love and harmony. The Father sends the Son, the Son accomplishes the work of redemption, and the Spirit applies that work to believers, as seen in John 16:13–15 and 1 Corinthians 15:28.

This divine order is central to the Gospel. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we could not save ourselves. In an act of incredible grace, the Father sent the Son into the world. Jesus Christ, the eternal Word, took on human nature, lived a perfectly sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God’s justice on our behalf. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord. 

We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why are there so many religions? Do all religions lead to God?

The existence of so many religions speaks to a deep, universal human longing to connect with something greater than ourselves. The Bible suggests that God has placed a sense of eternity in the human heart (Ecclesiastes 3:11) and has made His existence and attributes plain to everyone through what He has created (Romans 1:19-20).

However, while everyone has an innate awareness of God, the Bible explains that human sin has clouded our understanding and fractured our relationship with Him. Instead of worshiping the Creator as He has revealed Himself, humanity has often tried to reach the divine through various systems of rituals, moral codes, and different gods (Romans 1:21-23). These religions represent the human effort to bridge the gap between ourselves and God.

Regarding whether all religions lead to God, the Bible presents a unique and exclusive claim. While many religions share common moral principles, they differ fundamentally on the nature of God, the problem of humanity, and the solution for our separation from the Creator. Jesus Christ did not describe Himself as one of many paths, but stated clearly, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). The early apostles echoed this, teaching that there is salvation in no one else, for there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved (Acts 4:12).

The core of the Christian faith is the Gospel, which is distinct from every other religious system. While other religions often focus on what humans must do to reach God, the Gospel is about what God has done to reach us.

God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3), yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Our sin creates a separation that we cannot bridge through our own good works or religious devotion. Because God is loving and merciful, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus is truly God and truly man, born of a virgin, and He lived a perfect, sinless life.

Out of love, Jesus took our place, dying a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all. This salvation is a gift that cannot be earned; it is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the story of David and Bathsheba?

The story of David and Bathsheba, found in 2 Samuel 11 and 12, is a sobering account of how even a man after God's own heart can fall into devastating sin. It begins at a time when kings typically went out to battle, but David chose to remain behind in Jerusalem (2 Samuel 11:1). While walking on his roof, he saw a woman named Bathsheba bathing. Despite learning she was the wife of Uriah the Hittite, one of his loyal soldiers, David sent for her and committed adultery with her (2 Samuel 11:2–4).

When Bathsheba sent word that she was pregnant, David attempted to hide his sin. He summoned Uriah from the war, hoping he would go home to his wife so the child would be presumed to be Uriah's.
