However, when we look at the character of God through the lens of Scripture, we find that while His love is indeed beyond human comprehension, it is never reckless in the sense of being careless, ill-advised, or accidental.

God is perfectly wise and sovereign. He never acts without a holy purpose, nor does He take risks, because He knows all things from the beginning to the end, as stated in Isaiah 46:10. His plan to redeem a fallen people through the sacrifice of His Son was not a last-minute or impulsive decision; it was a deliberate choice made before the foundation of the world, according to Ephesians 1:4.

What we might perceive as recklessness is actually the sheer magnitude of His grace. In the eyes of the world, leaving ninety-nine sheep to find one lost one might seem unwise, as described in Luke 15:4. Likewise, the cross appears as foolishness to those who are perishing, but to those who are being saved, it is the power and wisdom of God, as seen in 1 Corinthians 1:18; 1 Corinthians 1:24. God's love is better described as relentless, sacrificial, and intentional. He knew exactly what it would cost to save us, which was the life of His own Son, and He chose to pay that price anyway, driven by His own goodness and mercy, as noted in Romans 5:8.

The Gospel shows us that while God is perfectly holy and must judge sin, He is also rich in mercy. We have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, according to Romans 3:23, creating a separation that we cannot bridge on our own. Yet, out of His great love, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Through the work of Christ, salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Him alone, not by our own works, as stated in Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible teach that there is a gift of celibacy?

Yes, the Bible teaches that there is a gift of celibacy, or singleness, which is given by God to some for the sake of His kingdom. This is most clearly addressed by Jesus and the Apostle Paul.

In the Gospel of Matthew, after teaching on the sanctity of marriage, Jesus responds to His disciples' concerns about the difficulty of marriage by saying, "Not everyone can receive this saying, but only those to whom it is given" Matthew 19:11. He goes on to speak of those who have made themselves "eunuchs for the sake of the kingdom of heaven," concluding with the invitation, "Let the one who is able to receive this receive it" Matthew 19:12. Here, Jesus presents the ability to remain single and devoted to God as a specific calling or enablement given by God.

The Apostle Paul expands on this in his first letter to the Corinthians. Paul, who was himself single, writes, "I wish that all were as I myself am. But each has his own gift from God, one of one kind and one of another" 1 Corinthians 7:7. The Greek word Paul uses for gift is "charisma," the same word used for other spiritual gifts. Paul explains that the benefit of this gift is that it allows a person to have "undivided devotion to the Lord," free from the worldly anxieties and responsibilities that naturally come with caring for a spouse and family 1 Corinthians 7:32-35.

It is important to understand that this gift is not a mark of superior holiness. Both marriage and singleness are described as gifts from God. Paul clarifies that if a person does not have this specific gift and finds themselves struggling with sexual desire, it is better for them to marry than to "burn with passion" 1 Corinthians 7:9.

Whether one is called to marriage or to celibacy, our ultimate identity and peace are not found in our relationship status, but in our relationship with God through Jesus Christ. We are all separated from God by our sin, but God, in His holiness and love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a sacrifice for our sins, and rise bodily from the dead on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, to find forgiveness and eternal life.

If you are wondering whether you have this gift, it can be helpful to reflect on your desires and your ability to serve God in your current season. Are you finding that your singleness allows you a unique freedom to serve the church and others? What do you feel might be holding you back from fully trusting God with your future, whether in singleness or marriage? Regardless of your calling, God promises to provide the grace you need for each day.
What did it mean that Satan wanted to be like the Most High in Isaiah 14:14?

In Isaiah 14:14, the statement "I will make myself like the Most High" captures the essence of the pride that led to the fall of the one often identified as the spiritual power behind the King of Babylon. While the immediate context of Isaiah 14 is a taunt against a human ruler, many believers and scholars have long understood these verses to describe the rebellion of Satan. This desire to be "like the Most High" was not a desire to be holy or good like God, but rather a craving for God’s unique status, authority, and glory.

To be like the Most High meant seeking a position that belongs to God alone. It was a rejection of the relationship between Creator and creature. In his pride, he desired to be self-sufficient and independent of God's rule. This ambition is further described in the preceding verses, where he says, "I will ascend to heaven; above the stars of God I will set my throne on high" in Isaiah 14:13. This was an attempt to usurp the sovereignty that only the Almighty possesses.

We see a parallel to this fall in Ezekiel 28:12–17, which describes a being of great beauty and wisdom who became proud because of his splendor, leading to his being cast out. The New Testament also warns against this specific sin, noting that a leader should not be a new convert "or he may become puffed up with conceit and fall into the condemnation of the devil" in 1 Timothy 3:6.

This same temptation was used in the Garden of Eden. The serpent told Eve that if she ate the forbidden fruit, her eyes would be opened and she would "be like God, knowing good and evil" in Genesis 3:5. The root of all sin is this desire to step off the path of trustful obedience and try to sit on the throne of our own lives, deciding for ourselves what is right and wrong independent of God.

While Satan sought to exalt himself and fell, the Bible shows us a beautiful contrast in Jesus Christ. Though Jesus is truly God, he did not grasp for status but humbled himself, taking on human nature to save us. Philippians 2:5–8 explains that Christ "emptied himself, by taking the form of a servant," even to the point of dying on a cross for our sins.

This brings us to the core of the Gospel. Because of our own pride and sin, we are all naturally separated from God’s holiness and under His just judgment. However, God, who is rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die in our place for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over death, He offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who turn to Him. Salvation is not something we earn by being "god-like" through our own efforts, but a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the biggest challenge for you in trusting Him with your life today?
What does the Bible say about the hornet?

The Bible mentions the hornet in three specific passages within the Old Testament, each time describing it as an instrument used by God to drive out the enemies of Israel from the Promised Land.

In Exodus 23:27-28, God promises His people, "I will send My fear before you, I will cause confusion among all the people to whom you come, and I will make all your enemies turn their backs to you. And I will send hornets before you, which shall drive out the Hivite, the Canaanite, and the Hittite from before you." Similarly, in Deuteronomy 7:20, Moses encourages the Israelites by saying, "Moreover the LORD your God will send the hornet among them, until those who are left, who hide themselves from you, are destroyed." Later, in Joshua 24:12, as Joshua reviews God's faithfulness, he notes, "I sent the hornet before you which drove them out from before you, even the two kings of the Amorites, but not with your sword or with your bow."

Biblical scholars and commentators often discuss whether these hornets were literal swarms of stinging insects sent by God as a plague, or if the word serves as a metaphor for a "divine panic" or "stinging terror" that God struck into the hearts of the inhabitants of Canaan. Regardless of the physical nature of the hornet, the primary spiritual lesson is the same: God is sovereign over all creation and is the one who fights for His people. The victory did not come through Israel's military might, sword, or bow, but through the power of God.

This reminds us that God is holy and just, and He is the ultimate deliverer of His people. Just as the Israelites could not conquer the land by their own strength, we cannot overcome the power of sin or the sting of death by our own efforts. We all fall short of God’s perfect standard, but God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross, and rise bodily on the third day. Through His victory, He has defeated sin and death once and for all. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What thoughts or questions do you have about trusting Him today?
What were the key events in the life of Jesus Christ?

The life of Jesus Christ is the central turning point of human history, revealing God's character and His plan to redeem a fallen world. His journey on earth can be understood through several defining moments recorded in the Holy Scriptures.

His life began with the Incarnation, where the eternal Son of God took on human nature, being conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the Virgin Mary (Matthew 1:18–25; Luke 2:1–21). This event shows that Jesus is truly God and truly man, coming to dwell among us (John 1:1; John 1:14).

As an adult, Jesus was baptized by John the Baptist, where the Father and the Holy Spirit publicly affirmed His identity and the beginning of His earthly mission (Matthew 3:13–17). Immediately following this, He was led into the wilderness to be tempted by the devil. Unlike the first man, Adam, Jesus remained perfectly sinless and obedient to God's Word, proving His fitness to be our Savior (Matthew 4:1–11).

During His public ministry, Jesus traveled throughout Israel preaching the Gospel of the Kingdom of God. He performed many miracles, such as healing the sick and raising the dead, which served as signs of His divine authority and His compassion for a hurting world (Mark 1:14–15; John 20:30–31). He taught with authority, calling people to repentance and faith.

The culmination of His mission began with the Last Supper, where He identified Himself as the sacrifice of the New Covenant (Luke 22:14–20). Shortly after, He was betrayed, arrested, and sentenced to death by crucifixion. On the cross, Jesus died as a penal substitutionary atonement, meaning He took the punishment that our sins deserved to reconcile us to a holy God (Romans 5:8; 2 Corinthians 5:21).

Three days after His burial, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Luke 24:1–6). This resurrection confirms that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father and that all who trust in Him will also be raised to eternal life. After appearing to many witnesses, He ascended into heaven, where He now sits at the right hand of God the Father, interceding for those who follow Him (Acts 1:9–11; Hebrews 7:25).

The message of Jesus' life is that while God is perfectly holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received not by our own good works, but through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Do Christians need a day of rest?

The concept of a day of rest is woven into the very fabric of creation. In the beginning, after completing the work of creation, God rested on the seventh day, not because He was tired, but to set a pattern for humanity (Genesis 2:2; Genesis 2:3). This principle was later formalized in the Ten Commandments, where God commanded His people to remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy (Exodus 20:8; Exodus 20:9; Exodus 20:10; Exodus 20:11).

Jesus taught that the Sabbath was made for man, not man for the Sabbath (Mark 2:27). This means that a day of rest is a gift intended for our benefit, allowing us to cease from our labors, reflect on God's goodness, and gather with other believers for worship. While Christians often observe this on the Lord’s Day, which is Sunday, to celebrate the resurrection of Jesus (Acts 20:7; 1 Corinthians 16:2; Revelation 1:10), the underlying need for rest remains. It serves as a vital reminder that we are not the ones sustaining the world, God is (Psalm 127:1; Psalm 127:2).

Practicing a day of rest is an act of trust. When we stop our work, we acknowledge that our lives and our provisions are ultimately in God's hands. It is a spiritual discipline that helps prevent burnout and keeps our hearts focused on our Creator rather than our productivity.

More importantly, physical rest points us toward a deeper spiritual rest. Our souls are often restless because of the weight of sin and the attempt to justify ourselves through our own efforts. True, eternal rest is found only in Jesus Christ, who says, "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matthew 11:28).

The Gospel reveals that God is perfectly holy, and we have all separated ourselves from Him through our sin (Romans 3:23). Because of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). This salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious observance (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Are you finding it difficult to find true rest in your life right now?
How can I be a good Christian?

The desire to be a "good Christian" often reflects a heart that wants to please God, which is a wonderful starting point. However, the most important truth to understand is that being a Christian is not primarily about our own performance or being good in our own strength. It is about a relationship with God made possible by what Jesus Christ has already done.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3). Because of sin, every person is separated from God and cannot reach His standard through their own efforts (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

We are not made right with God by trying harder to be good, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). To be a Christian is to realize you cannot save yourself and to put your total trust in Jesus as your Lord and Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

Once you have placed your faith in Christ, living as a Christian becomes a matter of growing in your love for Him and following His lead. This process is called sanctification, and it is the work of the Holy Spirit within you (Philippians 1:6; Galatians 5:16). Here are a few ways to grow in your faith:

1. Spend time in God's Word. The Bible is the inspired and infallible Word of God, serving as our ultimate authority (2 Timothy 3:16-17). Reading it daily helps you know His character and His will.
2. Walk in prayer. God invites you to talk to Him directly about your needs, your thanks, and your struggles (Philippians 4:6-7). You may also seek prayer from trusted, mature believers, for the prayer of a righteous person has great power (James 5:16).
3. Commit to a local church. We are not meant to follow Jesus alone. Finding a community of believers where the Bible is faithfully preached and where you can experience fellowship and the Lord's Supper is vital for your spiritual health (Hebrews 10:24-25).
4. Love and serve others. Jesus taught that the greatest commandments are to love God with all your heart and to love your neighbor as yourself (Matthew 22:37-39).

True Christian living is resting in the grace God has given you and allowing that grace to transform how you live, work, and relate to others each day. If you are experiencing a spiritual crisis or need specific guidance, I encourage you to reach out to a local pastor or a certified biblical counselor who can walk alongside you.
What does the Bible say about lawsuits / suing?

The Bible provides wisdom regarding lawsuits and legal disputes, emphasizing reconciliation, the testimony of the church, and the heart's motivations.

**Disputes Between Believers**
The most direct teaching on lawsuits between Christians is found in 1 Corinthians 6:1-8. The Apostle Paul expresses concern that believers were taking one another to secular courts. He argues that the church should be able to provide wise members to mediate such matters (1 Corinthians 6:5). Paul suggests that it is better for a Christian to be wronged or cheated than to bring a lawsuit against a fellow believer before non-believers, as doing so can damage the public witness of the Gospel (1 Corinthians 6:7). The priority for Christians is to demonstrate the transformative power of Christ's love through internal conflict resolution and forgiveness.

**Settling Matters Quickly**
Jesus also addressed legal conflicts in the Sermon on the Mount. He encouraged His followers to settle matters quickly with an adversary while they were still on the way to court (Matthew 5:25-26). This teaching emphasizes humility and the avoidance of escalating conflict. Rather than demanding every personal right, Jesus calls His followers to a standard of grace that seeks peace over winning a legal battle (Matthew 5:38-42).

**The Role of Civil Authorities**
While the Bible encourages private or church-based resolution, it also recognizes that God has established civil authorities to maintain order and execute justice (Romans 13:1-7). In situations involving criminal activity, protection of the vulnerable, or matters where the law requires state involvement, the legal system serves a necessary role. However, for the believer, the motivation should never be greed, vengeance, or a lack of love. Scripture instructs us, "If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone" (Romans 12:18).

**The Greatest Reconciliation**
The Bible’s focus on legal standing and reconciliation points to a vital spiritual reality. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Psalm 18:30), while we have all sinned and are separated from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we were like debtors who could never pay what we owed. In His mercy, God became man in Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His Lordship over all. Salvation is not something we earn through works or legal adherence, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What prevents you from placing your trust in the One who has already settled your greatest debt?
What is kingdom now teaching?

Kingdom Now teaching is a theological perspective which emphasizes the responsibility of the Church to establish God's physical and spiritual kingdom on earth before Jesus Christ returns. This movement suggests that when humanity fell into sin, Satan gained legal control over the earth, and it is now the task of believers to reclaim that authority through spiritual warfare, social influence, and political dominion.

Followers of this view often point to the mandate given to Adam and Eve to rule over the earth in Genesis 1:28, arguing that Christ's work on the cross restored this mandate to His followers. They believe the Church must take control of the major areas of society, often referred to as the seven mountains of culture, which include government, family, education, media, arts, business, and religion. In some forms of this teaching, Jesus is seen as being held in heaven until the Church has successfully prepared the world for His arrival.

While the desire to see God's will done on earth as it is in heaven is a biblical prayer found in Matthew 6:10, many historic Christian traditions express caution regarding Kingdom Now theology for several reasons.

First, the New Testament often emphasizes the spiritual nature of the Kingdom in this present age. Jesus told Pilate that His kingdom was not of this world in John 18:36. While Christians are called to be salt and light in Matthew 5:13-16, the Bible also teaches that we are sojourners and exiles in 1 Peter 2:11. Our primary mission is to proclaim the Gospel and make disciples of all nations as instructed in Matthew 28:19-20.

Second, this teaching can sometimes shift the focus from what Christ has already accomplished to what humans must achieve. The core of the Christian faith is that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and was bodily resurrected on the third day as described in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. This victory over sin and death is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not through our own efforts to perfect the world in Ephesians 2:8-9.

Third, Scripture teaches that the fullness of the Kingdom will be established by Christ Himself at His second coming. While the Kingdom is already present in the hearts of believers through the Holy Spirit in Luke 17:21, we still groan for the final redemption of all things in Romans 8:22-23. We look forward to the day when Christ personally returns to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new in Revelation 21:1-5.

True transformation does not come through social or political control, but through the power of the Gospel. We are all naturally separated from a holy God by our sin, but God in His great mercy sent His Son to reconcile us to Himself. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As we live in the tension of the kingdom that is already here but not yet fully realized, we find our hope in the finished work of Jesus. What are your thoughts on how the Church should engage with the world today?
What is The Infinite Way?

The Infinite Way is a spiritual message and movement founded in 1947 by Joel S. Goldsmith. It is often categorized within the New Thought tradition, although Goldsmith considered it a mystical teaching that transcended organized religion. The movement emphasizes the "inner realization" of God and the idea that God is the only reality, power, and presence in existence.

In the teachings of The Infinite Way, the physical world and its problems (such as sin, disease, and death) are viewed as "nothingness" or illusions created by the human mind. According to this view, when a person achieves a certain state of consciousness or "illumination," they realize their oneness with God, which supposedly results in the healing of the body and the harmonization of their life circumstances. 

While The Infinite Way uses Christian terminology and frequently quotes the Bible, its core beliefs differ significantly from historic, biblical Christianity in several essential areas:

First, regarding the nature of God, The Infinite Way teaches a form of monism, suggesting that God is an impersonal, infinite consciousness that constitutes the essence of all things. In contrast, the Bible reveals God as a personal, holy Creator who is distinct from His creation (Genesis 1:1; Isaiah 6:3). The Bible also teaches that God eternally exists as the Trinity: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14).

Second, regarding human nature and sin, The Infinite Way views sin and evil as "erroneous beliefs" or illusions. However, Scripture teaches that sin is a real and tragic rebellion against a holy God, resulting in actual spiritual death and separation from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23; Ephesians 2:1).

Third, regarding Jesus Christ, The Infinite Way often distinguishes between "Jesus" the man and "the Christ" as a universal state of consciousness that anyone can achieve. The Bible teaches that Jesus is the Christ, the unique and eternal Son of God who is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9). He did not merely reach a higher state of awareness; He is the sovereign Lord who came to save us.

Finally, regarding salvation, The Infinite Way focuses on self-realization and changing one's consciousness. The Gospel of Jesus Christ, however, is not about realizing our own divinity, but about being reconciled to God through the work of another. Because God is perfectly just and holy, our sin requires a sacrifice. Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was physically raised from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; 2 Corinthians 5:21).

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's historical work, and it is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or mental shifts (Ephesians 2:8–9). 

I want to gently encourage you to consider the Jesus of the Bible, who is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5). He offers a peace that is not based on ignoring the reality of the world's pain, but on His victory over it. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life.

What are your thoughts on how the Bible describes our relationship with God compared to these other spiritual teachings?
Who was Balak in the Bible?

Balak was the king of the Moabites who lived during the time of the Israelites' journey through the wilderness toward the Promised Land. He is primarily known for his interactions with the prophet Balaam in the Book of Numbers.

As the Israelites camped in the plains of Moab, Balak became filled with intense fear and dread because of their large numbers and their recent military victories over the Amorites (Numbers 22:2-4). Because he did not believe he could defeat Israel in a physical battle, he sought a spiritual solution by hiring a seer named Balaam to pronounce a supernatural curse upon the people of Israel (Numbers 22:5-6). Balak promised Balaam great wealth and honor if he would successfully curse them, hoping this would allow the Moabites to drive the Israelites out of the land.

However, God intervened and would not allow the Israelites to be cursed. Every time Balak took Balaam to a high place to look over the camp of Israel, God compelled Balaam to speak words of blessing instead of a curse (Numbers 23:7-10; Numbers 23:18-24; Numbers 24:3-9). This deeply frustrated Balak, who eventually sent Balaam away in anger (Numbers 24:10-11).

While Balak could not secure a verbal curse, he later succeeded in harming Israel through a different strategy. Based on Balaam’s counsel, the women of Moab enticed the Israelite men into sexual immorality and the worship of their god, Baal of Peor (Numbers 25:1-3). This led to a plague among the Israelites because of their unfaithfulness to God.

The New Testament references Balak in the book of Revelation, where Jesus speaks to the church in Pergamum. He reminds them that Balak was taught by Balaam to put a stumbling block before the Israelites, leading them into idolatry and immorality (Revelation 2:14).

The account of Balak serves as a reminder of God's sovereign protection over His people. Even when enemies sought to bring a curse, God turned it into a blessing (Deuteronomy 23:5). This points us to the greatest blessing God has provided through Jesus Christ. While we were separated from God by our own sin and deserving of judgment, Christ took the curse of our sin upon Himself through His death on the cross and His resurrection (Galatians 3:13-14).

Because of what Jesus has done, anyone can be reconciled to God and receive the blessing of eternal life. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does God have favorites?

Scripture teaches that God does not show partiality or favoritism in the way that humans often do. In the book of Romans 2:11, it is written, "For God shows no partiality." This truth is repeated in the book of Acts 10:34-35, where the apostle Peter realizes that "God shows no partiality, but in every nation anyone who fears him and does what is right is acceptable to him."

When people think of favorites, they often think of individuals receiving special treatment because of their status, wealth, or personal appeal. God, however, is perfectly just and holy. He does not look at the outward appearance but looks at the heart, as noted in 1 Samuel 16:7.

While God does not have favorites based on human merit, He does show sovereign favor, or grace, to people. This favor is not something anyone earns. For example, God chose the nation of Israel to be His people, but He told them in the book of Deuteronomy 7:7-8 that He did not choose them because they were more numerous or better than other nations, but simply because He loved them and was faithful to His promises. In the New Testament, we see Jesus had a close circle of friends, such as Peter, James, and John, but this reflected the depth of their fellowship and the specific roles He gave them rather than a ranking of their worth.

This same grace is at the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and all humans have sinned and fallen short of His glory, which is mentioned in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are separated from Him. Yet, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death.

Salvation is not reserved for a special class of people. It is offered to everyone who believes. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What makes you wonder if God might have favorites?
What is a Samaritan?

In the biblical and historical context, a Samaritan was a member of a group of people who lived in the region of Samaria, located between Galilee to the north and Judea to the south. Their identity and the deep-seated tension between them and the Jewish people of Jesus' time are rooted in the history of the divided kingdom of Israel.

The origins of the Samaritans trace back to the eighth century B.C. when the Northern Kingdom of Israel was conquered by the Assyrian Empire. According to 2 Kings 17:24-41, the Assyrians deported many Israelites and replaced them with foreigners from various parts of their empire. These settlers intermarried with the remaining Israelites, leading to a population that was ethnically mixed. Over time, they developed a religious system that combined the worship of the Lord with other practices, though they eventually established their own version of the Torah and built a temple on Mount Gerizim rather than in Jerusalem.

By the time of the New Testament, the animosity between Jews and Samaritans was intense. Jews generally viewed Samaritans as "half-breeds" who had corrupted the true worship of God. This social and religious hostility was so strong that many Jewish travelers would go out of their way to bypass Samaria entirely. This context makes the interactions of Jesus with Samaritans particularly significant.

In the Gospel of John, Jesus intentionally traveled through Samaria and spoke with a Samaritan woman at a well. He addressed her spiritual thirst and revealed Himself as the Messiah, leading many in her town to believe in Him as recorded in John 4:1-42. Similarly, in the Parable of the Good Samaritan found in Luke 10:25-37, Jesus used a Samaritan as the hero of the story to define "neighbor" in a way that challenged the prejudices of His listeners. 

These accounts demonstrate that the Gospel is not restricted by ethnic or social boundaries. While the Samaritans were outcasts in the eyes of many, Jesus sought them out with the truth of who He is. This reminds us of the core of the Gospel message: God is holy and we are all separated from Him by our sin, yet God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day to secure victory over death for all who believe. 

Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith in Christ alone, not through our heritage or our own works. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find most striking about the way Jesus interacted with those who were considered outcasts in His day?
How can I live a holy life?

Living a holy life begins with understanding that holiness is not something we achieve by our own power to impress God, but rather a gift and a calling that flows from a relationship with Him. In the Bible, holiness means being set apart for God's purposes and reflecting His character in our daily lives.

The foundation of holiness is the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, righteous, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him and cannot make ourselves holy through our own effort or moral works (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 64:6). However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect, holy life we could not live. Jesus, being fully God and fully man, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He did this to pay the penalty for sin and to reconcile us to God. We are justified, or declared righteous in God's sight, by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9; Romans 5:1).

Once you have placed your trust in Jesus, the Holy Spirit comes to live within you, beginning the process of progressive sanctification. This is the lifelong journey of becoming more like Christ in your thoughts, words, and actions. You can live a holy life by focusing on these biblical principles:

Depend on the Holy Spirit: You cannot be holy by your own strength. It is God who works in you both to will and to work for His good pleasure (Philippians 2:13). As you walk by the Spirit, you will find the strength to turn away from sinful desires (Galatians 5:16).

Renew Your Mind with Scripture: God's Word is the primary tool He uses to transform us. By reading, studying, and meditating on the Bible, you learn God's heart and how He desires for you to live (Psalm 119:9; Psalm 119:11). Jesus prayed that His followers would be sanctified by the truth, noting that God's Word is truth (John 17:17).

Practice the Mortification of Sin: This involves actively turning away from and putting to death sinful habits through the power of the Spirit (Romans 8:13; Colossians 3:5). This is not just about stopping bad behavior, but about putting on the new self and bearing the fruit of the Spirit, such as love, joy, peace, and self control (Galatians 5:22; Galatians 5:23; Ephesians 4:22; Ephesians 4:23; Ephesians 4:24).

Engage in Prayer and Fellowship: Constant communication with God through prayer keeps you dependent on His grace and alert to temptation (1 Thessalonians 5:17; Matthew 26:41). Additionally, being an active part of a local church provides the accountability, encouragement, and teaching necessary for spiritual growth (Hebrews 10:24; Hebrews 10:25; Acts 2:42).

Living a holy life is a joyful response to the overwhelming love God has shown you in Christ. If you have not yet done so, you can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the biggest challenge you face in seeking to live for Him?
What is the sinner's prayer?

The sinner's prayer is a term often used to describe a prayer where an individual confesses their sin, asks for God's forgiveness, and expresses faith in Jesus Christ as their Lord and Savior. It is a vocal expression of a person's desire to turn from their old life and begin a relationship with God.

It is important to understand that there is no specific, "magic" formula of words in the Bible that a person must say to be saved. Rather, the prayer is an outward expression of an inward heart change. Salvation is a work of God's grace, and it occurs when a person, convicted by the Holy Spirit, turns from their sin and puts their total trust in the person and work of Jesus Christ.

The foundation of this prayer is the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because every human has sinned, we are separated from Him and deserve His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, because of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect, sinless life. He died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; 1 Peter 2:24). He is now exalted as Lord over all (Acts 2:36).

We are justified, or made right with God, by His grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works or efforts (Galatians 2:16; Ephesians 2:8–9). This faith involves repentance, which is a change of mind that leads to turning away from sin and self-reliance and turning toward God. As the Bible promises, if you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised Him from the dead, you will be saved (Romans 10:9; Romans 10:10).

A sinner's prayer typically includes several key elements:
1. Acknowledging that one is a sinner in need of God's mercy (Luke 18:13).
2. Asking for forgiveness based solely on Christ's finished work on the cross.
3. Expressing belief in the resurrection of Jesus.
4. Surrendering to Jesus Christ as the Lord and Master of one's life.

While the words of a prayer do not save in and of themselves, they reflect a heart that is calling upon the name of the Lord (Romans 10:13). 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can a Christian avoid being a doormat for other people?

It is a common misconception that being a Christian means allowing others to mistreat or manipulate you. Biblical meekness is not the same as weakness, rather, it is power under the control of the Holy Spirit. To avoid being a doormat, a believer must ground their identity in Christ and practice the wisdom found in the Word of God.

A primary step is recognizing the difference between biblical service and the fear of man. Scripture warns that the fear of man lays a snare, but whoever trusts in the Lord is safe (Proverbs 29:25). When we allow others to walk over us because we are afraid of their disapproval or conflict, we are often seeking the approval of people more than the approval of God. The Apostle Paul wrote that if he were still trying to please man, he would not be a servant of Christ (Galatians 1:10). Our primary loyalty is to the Lord, and this gives us the freedom to say no when someone's demands conflict with our health, our stewardship, or God's commands.

Love also requires speaking the truth. We are called to speak the truth in love so that we may grow up in every way into Christ (Ephesians 4:15). If you allow someone to behave selfishly or sinfully toward you without ever setting a boundary, you are not truly loving them. True love does not enable sin; it seeks the spiritual well-being of the other person, which sometimes requires a firm and honest confrontation. Jesus Himself often confronted those who were acting unjustly or hypocritically, even as He maintained a heart of perfect love (Matthew 23:13-36).

Furthermore, Jesus modeled the use of boundaries. Although He came to serve, He frequently withdrew from the crowds to pray and rest, even when there were many people still waiting to be healed (Luke 5:16). He understood His specific mission and did not let the demands of others dictate His every move. We are called to be wise as serpents and innocent as doves (Matthew 10:16). This wisdom involves discerning when to give sacrificially and when to protect the resources and time God has entrusted to us for His glory.

At the heart of this struggle is often a question of identity. If we feel we must say yes to everyone to be valuable, we have forgotten the Gospel. The Bible teaches that God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, yet because of His great love, Jesus Christ lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross, and rose bodily on the third day to pay for our sins. When we trust in Him, we are justified by grace through faith alone, not by our works or by how well we please others. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose again to give you new life and a firm identity in Him.

When you know you are fully loved and accepted by God because of what Jesus has done, you no longer need to find your worth in the opinions of others. This security allows you to serve others out of a place of strength and choice, rather than a place of fear or obligation. What makes it most difficult for you to set boundaries in your current relationships?
What does it mean that the workers are few (Matthew 9:37)?

When Jesus says that the workers are few in Matthew 9:37, He is speaking from a heart of deep compassion for the people around Him. The context of this statement is found in the preceding verse, where Jesus looks at the crowds and sees them as "harassed and helpless, like sheep without a shepherd" (Matthew 9:36).

The "harvest" refers to the vast number of people who are spiritually needy, searching, or ready to hear the message of God's kingdom. Jesus describes this harvest as "plentiful," meaning there is no shortage of souls who need the grace and truth of God. The "workers" are those who are called to go out and share the Gospel, teach the Word, and care for the spiritual needs of others.

By saying the workers are few, Jesus highlights a significant gap between the great spiritual need of the world and the number of people actively engaged in the mission of reaching them. He points out that while the opportunity for people to be brought into God's kingdom is immense, there are not enough people dedicated to the task of gathering them in.

Jesus provides the solution in the very next sentence, where He instructs His disciples to "ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into his harvest field" (Matthew 9:38). This reminds us that the work of salvation and ministry belongs to God, and it is He who calls, equips, and sends people to do His work.

The reason this harvest is so urgent is that every person is created by a holy God but separated from Him by sin. Because God is just, sin must be judged, but because He is merciful, He provided a way for us to be reconciled. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of grace that cannot be earned by works; it is received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What do you think is the biggest challenge for believers today in answering the call to be workers in the harvest?
What is living cosmology?

Living cosmology is a philosophical and spiritual worldview that describes the universe as a single, vast, living, and unfolding story rather than a collection of inanimate objects. It suggests that the cosmos is an interconnected, organic whole that is constantly evolving and characterized by a sense of sacredness or interiority. Proponents of this view often blend scientific insights about the history of the universe with spiritual reflections, aiming to foster a deeper sense of belonging and responsibility toward the Earth.

From the perspective of biblical Christianity, we share the belief that the universe is magnificent and interconnected, but our understanding of its origin and nature is rooted in the Creator who stands outside of it. The Bible teaches that "The heavens declare the glory of God, and the sky above proclaims his handiwork" (Psalm 19:1). However, Scripture makes a vital distinction between the Creator and the creation. While living cosmology often implies that the universe itself is divine or has its own inherent consciousness, the Bible teaches that God created the world out of nothing (Genesis 1:1; Hebrews 11:3).

God is not the universe, nor is the universe an extension of His being. Rather, God is the sovereign Lord who sustains all things by His powerful word (Hebrews 1:3). We see the evidence of His "eternal power and divine nature" in the things He has made (Romans 1:20), yet we are cautioned not to worship the creation instead of the Creator (Romans 1:25).

The biblical narrative also explains why the world often seems broken or in pain. Because of human sin, the "whole creation has been groaning together in the pains of childbirth until now" (Romans 8:22). The universe is not simply evolving toward a higher state of being on its own; it is awaiting the restoration that comes through Jesus Christ.

This restoration is at the heart of the Gospel. God the Son became part of His own creation in the person of Jesus Christ to reconcile us to Himself. He lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). It is through Him, and not through our connection to the physical cosmos, that we find true life and peace.

The beauty of the stars and the complexity of life are meant to point us to the person of Jesus, who promises to one day make all things new (Revelation 21:5). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. 

What are your thoughts on how the origin of the universe affects the way we find meaning in life?
How old was Abraham when he died?

Abraham lived to be 175 years old. Scripture records that he breathed his last and died at a good old age, an old man and full of years, and was gathered to his people (Genesis 25:7; Genesis 25:8).

Abraham’s life serves as a profound example of faith in God’s promises. The Bible explains that Abraham believed God, and it was counted to him as righteousness (Genesis 15:6; Romans 4:3). This points us to the heart of the Gospel: that we are justified by grace through faith in Jesus Christ. Jesus, the descendant of Abraham, lived a perfect life, died for our sins, and rose again to give us victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Galatians 3:16). You can turn to God today by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life.
Who was Clarence Larkin?

Clarence Larkin (1850–1924) was an influential American Baptist pastor, author, and Bible teacher who is best remembered for his detailed, hand-drawn charts and maps of biblical prophecy and dispensational theology. Before entering the ministry, Larkin was trained as a mechanical engineer and worked as a professional draftsman. This background significantly influenced his approach to teaching the Bible, as he sought to visualize the Word of God through intricate illustrations that explained complex theological concepts.

His most famous work, Dispensational Truth (also known as Charting the Divine Plan), remains a classic in the study of eschatology, which is the study of the end times. Larkin was a proponent of dispensationalism, a framework that views God’s dealings with humanity through different periods or "dispensations" of history. He emphasized the importance of 2 Timothy 2:15, which encourages believers to "Do your best to present yourself to God as one approved, a worker who has no need to be ashamed, rightly handling the word of truth."

Larkin’s charts covered a wide range of topics, including the Second Coming of Christ, the Book of Revelation, and the prophecies in the Book of Daniel. His goal was to show the harmony of the entire Bible, from Genesis to Revelation, demonstrating God’s sovereign control over history and His faithfulness to His promises. This perspective is rooted in the belief that God has spoken to humanity in various ways and at different times, as noted in Hebrews 1:1–2.

While Larkin’s specific views on the timeline of the end times represent one particular tradition within Christianity, his work reflects a deep love for the Scriptures and a desire to see God's plan clearly. At the heart of his teaching, and the heart of the Bible itself, is the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Scriptures testify that God is holy and that our sin separates us from Him. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, Christ secured victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works we can do. You can turn to God now. Repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most interesting or perhaps confusing when you think about God's plan for the future?
What was the purpose of the Levitical Law?

The Levitical Law served several vital purposes in God's redemptive plan, primarily centering on His holiness and our need for a Savior.

First, the law revealed the character of God. By giving specific instructions on worship, morality, and daily living, God demonstrated that He is perfectly holy and set apart from sin (Leviticus 19:2; Leviticus 20:26). It established a standard that showed what was required for a people to live in the presence of a holy God.

Second, the law was intended to expose human sinfulness. It functioned like a mirror, showing people that they could not meet God's perfect standard on their own strength. The Apostle Paul explains that through the law comes the knowledge of sin (Romans 3:20; Romans 7:7). It acted as a guardian or tutor to lead us to Christ, so that we might be justified by faith (Galatians 3:24-25).

Third, the Levitical system of sacrifices and the priesthood served as a shadow or a physical illustration of spiritual realities. The repeated animal sacrifices pointed to the ultimate, once-for-all sacrifice that Jesus Christ would make. These rituals were never meant to take away sins permanently, but to point forward to the coming of the Messiah (Hebrews 10:1-4; Hebrews 10:11-14).

Ultimately, the purpose of the law was to prepare the way for the Gospel. While the law shows us our debt, Jesus Christ came to pay it. Jesus lived the perfect life that we could not live, fulfilling every requirement of the law (Matthew 5:17). Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son. Jesus took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His lordship and His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not found in keeping the law, which no one can do perfectly, but is a gift secured by Christ's work alone. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 3:28).

You can turn to God even now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is your understanding of how a person is made right with God?
What does the Bible say about confession of sin to a priest?

The Bible provides a beautiful picture of how believers interact with God and one another regarding sin. While the practice of confessing to a priest is a significant tradition in some branches of Christianity, the Scriptures emphasize our direct access to God through Jesus Christ and the importance of mutual confession within the community of believers.

The primary teaching of the New Testament is that Jesus Christ is our Great High Priest. Because of His perfect life, sacrificial death, and bodily resurrection, the barrier between God and man has been removed. Hebrews 4:14–16 explains that because we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus, the Son of God, we can "with confidence draw near to the throne of grace, that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need." This means that every believer has the privilege of going directly to God to seek forgiveness.

The Scriptures are clear that there is only one person who functions as a mediator between God and humanity. 1 Timothy 2:5 states, "For there is one God, and there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus." Because Jesus is the eternal mediator, we do not require an earthly priest to bridge the gap between us and the Father. 1 John 1:9 offers a direct promise to all who approach God: "If we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness."

While our judicial forgiveness is settled by God through Christ, the Bible also highlights the value of confessing sins to other believers for the sake of spiritual health and community support. James 5:16 says, "Therefore, confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed. The prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working." This type of confession is not for the purpose of obtaining legal absolution from a priest, but for finding healing, accountability, and the support of the church body.

Furthermore, the New Testament describes all believers as part of a "royal priesthood" (1 Peter 2:9). This concept, often called the priesthood of all believers, means that you have the right to read the Scriptures, pray directly to God, and minister to others without needing a special class of earthly priests to stand between you and the Lord.

At the heart of this discussion is the Gospel itself. God is holy and just, and our sins have separated us from Him. However, in His mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take upon Himself the punishment we deserved. Jesus died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or religious rituals we perform.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What holds you back from trusting in the finished work of Jesus today?
Should Christians go to nightclubs? Is clubbing a sin?

When considering whether a Christian should attend nightclubs, it is helpful to move beyond asking if it is a specific sin and instead ask if it honors God, protects your testimony, and guards your heart. While the Bible does not mention nightclubs specifically, it provides clear principles regarding the environments we choose and the motivations of our hearts.

The Scriptures teach that while we have freedom in Christ, not everything we are free to do is beneficial for our spiritual life. In 1 Corinthians 10:23, it says, "All things are lawful, but not all things are helpful. All things are lawful, but not all things build up." We must ask ourselves if being in a nightclub environment, which is often characterized by excessive drinking, immodest behavior, and music that may not honor God, helps us grow in our relationship with Him or if it creates a stumbling block for ourselves and others.

The atmosphere of most nightclubs often aligns with what the Bible calls "revelry" or "orgies" in some translations, which are listed among the works of the flesh. Galatians 5:19-21 notes that "envy, drunkenness, orgies, and things like these" are contrary to the Spirit of God. Similarly, Romans 13:13-14 encourages believers to "walk properly as in the daytime, not in orgies and drunkenness, not in sexual immorality and sensuality, not in quarreling and jealousy. But put on the Lord Jesus Christ, and make no provision for the flesh, to gratify its desires." Because these environments are specifically designed to provoke the desires of the flesh, many Christians find that "clubbing" is an unwise provision for sin.

Furthermore, our bodies are not our own; they are temples of the living God. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20 reminds us, "Or do you not know that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God? You are not your own, for you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body." This means our choices regarding where we go and what we do should reflect the high price Jesus paid for our redemption.

It is also important to consider our witness to the world. We are called to be "the light of the world" (Matthew 5:14). If a believer is seen in an environment associated with worldliness and sin, it may confuse those who are watching our lives to see what it means to follow Jesus. 1 Peter 4:3 says, "For the time that is past suffices for doing what the Gentiles want to do, living in sensuality, passions, drunkenness, orgies, drinking parties, and lawless idolatry." Our new life in Christ should look distinctly different from our old life.

The core of this issue is often what we are seeking. If we are searching for joy, acceptance, or excitement in the world's shadows, we will always come away empty. True life and lasting joy are found only in Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, and our sins separate us from Him. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory, He offers us forgiveness and a new heart with new desires.

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you navigate these questions, consider what you are truly looking for in that environment. Are you seeking fellowship, or perhaps an escape? What holds you back from seeking that fulfillment in Christ and a healthy church community instead? Finding a local church where you can be discipled and find godly friends will provide far more lasting joy than any temporary worldly excitement.
Does the statue outside the United Nations have any connection to the beast of the end times?

Many people have observed similarities between the "Guardian for International Peace and Security" statue, which was temporarily displayed at the United Nations, and certain descriptions found in biblical prophecy. In the book of Daniel, the prophet describes a vision of four great beasts representing earthly kingdoms, the first of which was like a lion and had eagle's wings (Daniel 7:4). Similarly, the book of Revelation describes a composite beast rising out of the sea that resembles a leopard, with feet like a bear and a mouth like a lion (Revelation 13:2).

While the visual resemblance is striking to many students of the Bible, it is helpful to remember that the primary purpose of these prophetic images is to illustrate the nature of earthly powers and their relationship to God's sovereign plan. Whether or not a specific modern monument is a direct fulfillment of prophecy, these passages serve as a reminder that all human institutions are temporary and that God remains the ultimate authority over the nations.

The most important aspect of studying the end times is not merely identifying symbols, but recognizing our own standing before the Lord. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of this sin, we are separated from Him. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, confirming His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3, 1 Corinthians 15:4).

Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith in Christ alone, and not by any good works or moral effort of our own (Ephesians 2:8, Ephesians 2:9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What thoughts or concerns do you have when you think about the end times?
What is a trespass offering / guilt offering?

The trespass offering, often called the guilt offering or "asham" in Hebrew, was a specific sacrifice required under the Mosaic Law for offenses that involved a "breach of faith" against God or a neighbor. While the sin offering focused on the purification from the defilement of sin, the guilt offering focused on making reparation or restitution for a debt incurred through wrongdoing.

According to the instructions in Leviticus 5:14–19 and Leviticus 6:1–7, this offering was required in cases such as unintentionally violating holy things belonging to the Lord or committing a sin against a neighbor through deception, robbery, or keeping lost property. The Law required the individual to bring a ram without blemish as a sacrifice. Crucially, the offender was also required to make full restitution for the value of the wrong and add an additional one-fifth (20 percent) of the value as a penalty. This practice demonstrated that sin is not only a moral failure but also a debt that requires satisfaction and restoration.

In the New Testament, we see the ultimate fulfillment of these laws in the person and work of Jesus Christ. The prophet Isaiah describes the suffering Servant, the Messiah, by saying that the Lord would make His soul an "offering for sin," or a guilt offering (Isaiah 53:10). This means that Jesus did not only die to purify us, but He died to pay the debt we owed to God because of our transgressions. While the Old Testament ram could provide a temporary covering, Jesus Christ offered a perfect and final satisfaction for our guilt.

The core of the Gospel message is that God is perfectly holy and just, and our sins have created a separation from Him (Isaiah 59:2). Because we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we are unable to pay the debt we owe or make ourselves right with Him through our own works (Romans 3:23; Ephesians 2:8–9). However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Through His sacrifice, the debt of our guilt is fully paid. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you reflect on the mercy of God, remember that the guilt offering teaches us that God values restoration. If you are struggling with the weight of past wrongs, you can find peace in the finished work of Christ, who invites us to draw near to the throne of grace with confidence (Hebrews 4:14–16). Following Him often involves seeking to make things right with those we have harmed, not to earn salvation, but as a fruit of the transformation God is working in us. I encourage you to read the Book of Hebrews, which explains in detail how Christ is the mediator of a better covenant and the perfect sacrifice for our souls (Hebrews 9:11–14).
What is the sign of Jonah?

The sign of Jonah refers to the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. In the Gospel of Matthew, when certain scribes and Pharisees asked Jesus for a miraculous sign to prove His authority, He replied that no sign would be given to that evil and adulterous generation except the sign of the prophet Jonah (Matthew 12:38–39). 

Jesus explained the meaning of this sign by drawing a parallel between Jonah’s experience and His own mission. Just as Jonah spent three days and three nights inside the belly of the great fish, Jesus declared that the Son of Man would spend three days and three nights in the heart of the earth (Matthew 12:40). This was a clear prophecy of His coming death and His bodily resurrection on the third day.

This sign also carries a message of repentance. Jesus noted that the people of Nineveh repented at the preaching of Jonah, but the generation standing before Jesus was rejecting someone much greater than Jonah (Luke 11:32). While Jonah was a reluctant messenger to a foreign city, Jesus is the divine Son of God who came to save the world.

The significance of the sign of Jonah is that the resurrection is the ultimate proof of Jesus’ identity and the sufficiency of His work. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and are separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because of His great mercy, God the Son became man, lived a perfectly sinless life, and died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins. His resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all creation (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 1:4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What does it mean to walk worthy?

To walk worthy is a beautiful and solemn call for every believer to live a life that corresponds to the weight and value of the Gospel. The term comes from the Greek word "axios," which refers to a balance scale. It describes a life that "weights" the same as the calling we have received from God. When the Apostle Paul urges us to "walk worthy of the calling with which you were called" in Ephesians 4:1, he is not telling us to earn our salvation, but rather to live in a way that reflects the incredible gift we have already been given.

This worthy walk is characterized by several key biblical principles:

First, it is a response to God’s grace. In the book of Ephesians, Paul spends the first three chapters explaining the glorious truths of what God has done for us in Christ, how He chose us, redeemed us through His blood, and sealed us with the Holy Spirit (Ephesians 1:3-14). Only after establishing that we are saved by grace alone through faith alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9), does he call us to walk in a way that honors that grace. We do not walk worthy to become God’s children; we walk worthy because we already are His children.

Second, walking worthy involves specific character traits. In Ephesians 4:2, Paul describes this walk as being "with all lowliness and gentleness, with longsuffering, bearing with one another in love." It is a life of humility that seeks the unity of the Spirit and the well-being of the church.

Third, it is a life of fruitful obedience. In Colossians 1:10, we are encouraged to "walk worthy of the Lord, fully pleasing Him, being fruitful in every good work and increasing in the knowledge of God." This means our daily actions, our words, and our thoughts should be aligned with God’s revealed will in Scripture. It involves a "holy conduct and godliness" as we wait for the return of Christ (2 Peter 3:11).

Fourth, it is a life lived for God's glory. We are called to "walk worthy of God who calls you into His own kingdom and glory" (1 Thessalonians 2:12). This means our ultimate ambition is no longer our own fame or comfort, but the exaltation of Jesus Christ in everything we do.

It is important to remember that none of us can walk worthy by our own strength. We are all sinners who fall short of God's perfect holiness (Romans 3:23). The only reason we can even begin to walk with God is because Jesus Christ lived a perfectly worthy life on our behalf, died for our sins, and rose again to give us new life. Through His sacrifice, He reconciles us to a holy God.

If you find yourself struggling to live up to this calling, remember that God provides the power of the Holy Spirit to transform us from the inside out. He is faithful to complete the work He started in you (Philippians 1:6).

You can turn to God even now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and the power to live a new life. What do you feel is the greatest challenge in your own journey to walk worthy of the Lord?
Who was the Queen of Sheba?

The Queen of Sheba was a powerful monarch from a distant land who visited King Solomon in Jerusalem during the tenth century B.C. While the Bible does not name her personally, she is described as the ruler of Sheba, a kingdom generally believed by historians and scholars to be located in the region of modern-day Yemen or Ethiopia.

Her visit is recorded in 1 Kings 10:1–13 and 2 Chronicles 9:1–12. She traveled a great distance with a large caravan carrying spices, gold, and precious stones because she had heard reports of Solomon’s immense wisdom and his relationship with the Lord. Her primary goal was to test Solomon with difficult questions to see if the reports she heard were true.

When she arrived and spoke with him, she discovered that his wisdom and the prosperity of his kingdom far exceeded what she had been told. The Scriptures note that she was overwhelmed by what she saw and heard. In 1 Kings 10:9, she praised God, saying, "Praise be to the Lord your God, who has delighted in you and placed you on the throne of Israel. Because of the Lord’s eternal love for Israel, he has made you king to maintain justice and righteousness."

In the New Testament, Jesus refers to her as the "Queen of the South" in Matthew 12:42 and Luke 11:31. Jesus used her example to challenge the religious leaders of His day. He pointed out that she traveled from the ends of the earth to hear the wisdom of Solomon, yet someone far greater than Solomon was standing right in front of them.

This "greater one" is Jesus Christ Himself. Just as the Queen of Sheba sought out the wisdom of a king, we are invited to seek the King of Kings. While Solomon was a man of great wisdom, he was still a fallen human being. Jesus, however, is the holy and perfect Son of God who came into the world to reconcile us to God. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God’s holiness, but Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all.

The Queen of Sheba’s journey reminds us that God's truth is worth seeking above all earthly treasures. You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What do you think about the wisdom Jesus offers compared to the wisdom of the world?
What is a divine appointment?

A divine appointment is a moment or circumstance that is intentionally orchestrated by God to fulfill His specific purposes. While these encounters may seem like "coincidences" or "random chance" from a human perspective, believers understand them as expressions of God’s sovereignty and His active providence in the world. Scripture teaches that God is the one who establishes our steps (Proverbs 16:9; Proverbs 19:21) and that He has prepared good works for us to walk in beforehand (Ephesians 2:10).

These appointments often serve several purposes:

1. **Evangelism**: God may bring two people together so that the Gospel can be shared. A clear example is when the Holy Spirit led Philip to a specific desert road to meet an Ethiopian official who was reading Scripture but needed help understanding it (Acts 8:26-35).
2. **Encouragement or Provision**: God may prompt a believer to cross paths with someone who is hurting or in need, providing a timely word of hope or practical help (Galatians 6:2; Galatians 6:10).
3. **Spiritual Growth**: Sometimes a divine appointment is meant to challenge or refine a believer, placing them in a situation where they must rely more fully on God's grace.

The most significant "appointment" in history is God's pursuit of humanity through Jesus Christ. Although God is holy and our sin has created a separation from Him, He lovingly intervened. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, triumping over sin and death. Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts, but is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Does the thought that God is actively involved in the details of your daily life bring you comfort, or does it raise more questions for you?
What is the Qur’an?

The Qur’an is the central religious text of Islam. Muslims believe it is the literal word of God, which they call Allah, revealed to the prophet Muhammad over a period of about twenty-three years in the seventh century. It is composed of 114 chapters, known as surahs, and serves as the primary source of authority for Islamic law, theology, and daily life.

From a Christian perspective, while we respect the deep devotion our Muslim neighbors have for the Qur’an, we hold that the Bible is the unique, inspired, and final Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21). There are significant differences between the two texts concerning the nature of God, the identity of Jesus Christ, and the way of salvation.

The Qur’an presents Jesus, called Isa, as a great prophet and a messenger of God who was born of a virgin and performed miracles. However, it explicitly denies that Jesus is the Son of God or that He is divine. Furthermore, the Qur’an teaches that Jesus was not actually crucified or killed, but that it only appeared so to those present.

In contrast, the Bible teaches that Jesus Christ is truly God and truly man, the second person of the Trinity (John 1:1; John 1:14; Colossians 2:9). The core of the Christian faith is the historical fact that Jesus died on a cross as a perfect sacrifice for our sins and was bodily raised from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

The Bible reveals that God is holy and just, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because we cannot save ourselves through our own works or religious devotion, God, in His great love, sent His Son to live the perfect life we could not live and to take the punishment we deserved (John 3:16; Romans 5:8). Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). This is the wonderful news of the Gospel: that through Christ, we can be fully forgiven and reconciled to God.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What do you find most significant about the differences between the message of the Qur'an and the message of the Bible?
What would have happened “if the Lord had not been on our side” (Psalm 124:2)?

Psalm 124 is a song of gratitude that acknowledges God as the sole reason for the survival and deliverance of His people. When the psalmist asks what would have happened if the Lord had not been on their side, he uses vivid and terrifying imagery to describe the alternative.

If the Lord had not intervened, the psalmist explains that their enemies would have swallowed them up alive in the heat of their anger (Psalm 124:3). He uses the metaphor of a flood to show the overwhelming power of the opposition, stating that the waters would have engulfed them and the torrent would have swept over their very souls (Psalm 124:4). Without God’s protective hand, the "proud waters" would have completely overwhelmed them (Psalm 124:5). The passage further describes being like prey in the teeth of a predator, suggesting that without divine help, they would have been torn apart and destroyed (Psalm 124:6).

Ultimately, the psalm concludes that their help is found in the name of the Lord, who is the Maker of heaven and earth (Psalm 124:8). This passage teaches us that human effort and strength are insufficient against the forces of evil and the chaos of a fallen world. It is only by God’s sovereign grace and power that we are not consumed.
