Jesus went even further, instructing His followers to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them (Matthew 5:44). If we are called to love those who might oppose us, how much more should we show kindness and protection to those who are suffering?

Standing against persecution does not require a person to abandon their biblical convictions regarding marriage or sexuality. Instead, it is a direct application of the Gospel. Christians can hold to the historical, biblical view of human sexuality while simultaneously being the loudest voices against cruelty and dehumanization. We serve a God who is both perfectly holy and perfectly loving, and He expresses His character by defending the vulnerable and the oppressed (Psalm 146:7-9).

Our interactions should always be marked by the fruit of the Spirit, which includes love, kindness, gentleness, and self control (Galatians 5:22-23). We are called to live peaceably with all people as much as it depends on us (Romans 12:18). When we respond to others with harshness or indifference to their suffering, we fail to represent the heart of Jesus, who was full of grace and truth (John 1:14).

Ultimately, our concern for our neighbors stems from the reality that we are all in need of God's mercy. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, and that all of us have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His sacrifice, Jesus offers reconciliation and new life to anyone who turns to Him.

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or moral improvements (Ephesians 2:8-9). This message of hope is for every person. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life.

What questions do you have about how the love of Christ transforms the way we see and treat others?
Why do some cultures practice female genital mutilation (FGM)?

The practice of female genital mutilation, often referred to as FGM, is a deeply complex and painful issue that affects millions of women and girls worldwide. It is generally rooted in cultural and social traditions rather than specific religious mandates.

Societies that practice FGM often do so because it is viewed as a necessary rite of passage into womanhood. In these contexts, it is frequently tied to notions of cultural identity and social acceptance. Families may feel intense pressure to conform to the practice to ensure their daughters are considered marriageable or to avoid social exclusion. In some cultures, there is also a mistaken belief that the procedure promotes hygiene or preserves a woman’s virginity and modesty by controlling female sexuality.

From a biblical perspective, every person is created in the image of God and possesses inherent dignity and worth. Genesis 1:26-27. Because we are made by Him, our bodies are not our own to mar or harm, but are to be treated with honor. 1 Corinthians 6:19-20. Scripture emphasizes that God’s design for humanity involves flourishing and protection, especially for the vulnerable. While some may attempt to justify such practices through a lens of purity or tradition, the Bible does not command or support FGM. Instead, it teaches that true purity and transformation come from the heart through the work of the Holy Spirit, not through physical alterations or legalistic rituals.

The brokenness seen in such practices highlights the universal need for the restoration found only in Jesus Christ. We live in a world marred by sin and the desire to control others, but God, in His holiness and love, sent His Son to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is the Lord who offers healing to the brokenhearted and value to those whom society may have harmed. 

Through Christ, we are reminded that our identity is not found in cultural rituals or physical marks, but in being children of God by grace. Galatians 3:28. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and it brings a new life defined by peace and restoration. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What thoughts or questions do you have about how God views our physical bodies?

If you or someone you know is in immediate danger of harm or is dealing with the physical and emotional trauma of FGM, please reach out to local emergency services or a qualified medical professional. It is also helpful to seek support from a trusted pastor or a certified biblical counselor who can provide compassionate care and guidance through the healing process.
Who was Theophilus at the beginning of Luke and Acts?

Theophilus is the person to whom the physician Luke addressed both the Gospel of Luke and the Book of Acts. His name comes from two Greek words, "theos" and "philos," which together mean "friend of God" or "loved by God."

In the opening of the Gospel of Luke, he is addressed as "most excellent Theophilus" (Luke 1:3). This specific title, "most excellent," was commonly used in the first century to address high-ranking Roman officials or people of significant social status. For example, the same title is used later in the New Testament to address Roman governors like Felix and Festus (Acts 23:26; Acts 26:25). Because of this, many scholars believe Theophilus was a real person, likely a wealthy or influential Roman official who may have acted as a patron to fund the writing and distribution of Luke's two-part historical account.

Luke explains that his purpose in writing to Theophilus was so that he might "have certainty concerning the things you have been taught" (Luke 1:4). This suggests that Theophilus was likely a new convert to Christianity or someone very interested in the faith who was already receiving instruction about Jesus.

Some people have suggested that "Theophilus" could be a symbolic name referring to any "friend of God" who reads the books, but the use of the formal title "most excellent" strongly points to a specific historical individual. By the time Luke writes the Book of Acts, he addresses him simply as "Theophilus" (Acts 1:1), which might suggest a growing friendship or a deepening spiritual brotherly bond between the author and the recipient.

Through these writings, Theophilus was presented with the reality of God's holiness and the history of how Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for human sin. Luke records that Jesus was buried and then rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His lordship over all (Luke 24:6-7; Acts 2:32-36). This message remains the same for us today: salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone rather than by our own works. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Have you ever considered what it means to be a "friend of God" through faith in His Son?
Was Jesus a Jew?

Yes, Jesus was a Jew. His Jewish identity is fundamental to who He is and how He fulfilled God’s promises throughout the Scriptures.

Jesus was born into a Jewish family in the lineage of King David and the patriarch Abraham (Matthew 1:1; Luke 3:23-38). He was raised in a Jewish home, was circumcised on the eighth day according to the Law of Moses (Luke 2:21), and was presented at the Temple in Jerusalem as a child (Luke 2:22-24). Throughout His earthly ministry, Jesus observed Jewish customs and festivals, such as the Passover (Luke 22:15) and the Feast of Booths (John 7:2-10). He regularly taught in the synagogues and was recognized by His contemporaries as a Jewish teacher, or Rabbi (Matthew 4:23; John 4:9).

This identity was not accidental. Jesus came to fulfill the Law and the Prophets rather than to abolish them (Matthew 5:17). He is the promised Messiah, the "Lion of the tribe of Judah" (Revelation 5:5), whom God pledged would come to save His people. In His conversation with a Samaritan woman, Jesus explicitly stated that "salvation is from the Jews" (John 4:22), pointing to the fact that God chose the nation of Israel to bring the Savior into the world for all people.

The significance of Jesus being the Jewish Messiah extends to every person today. God is perfectly holy, and because all human beings have sinned, we are separated from Him (Romans 3:23). In His great love, God sent Jesus, who lived a perfect life of obedience to the Law, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Because of His sacrifice, salvation is available to everyone, regardless of their background. It is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or works (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your understanding of who Jesus is, and how does His identity as the Messiah shape your perspective on God?
How did Satan and other angels sin if heaven is sinless?

The Bible teaches that heaven is a place of holiness and the immediate presence of God, but it also reveals that sin originated through the pride and rebellion of certain angelic beings. While Scripture does not provide every detail regarding the exact moment sin first appeared, it points to a heart change within a high ranking angel, often identified as Satan.

In Ezekiel 28:12–17, we see a description of a being who was the seal of perfection, yet iniquity was found in him because his heart became proud due to his beauty. Similarly, Isaiah 14:12–14 describes a fall driven by the desire to be like the Most High. This suggests that even in a perfect environment, these created beings possessed a will and the capacity to turn away from God's authority. Their sin was not caused by an outside influence, but was a self generated choice to reject God's lordship in favor of their own glory.

The New Testament confirms this rebellion, noting that God did not spare the angels who sinned but cast them out (2 Peter 2:4; Jude 1:6). This indicates that heaven is sinless not because it is impossible for a creature to rebel, but because God is holy and does not allow sin to dwell in His presence. Once rebellion occurred, Satan and the angels who followed him were excluded from their original dwelling.

This reality reminds us of the gravity of sin. Just as the angels fell through pride, humanity also fell into sin through the desire to be like God on our own terms (Genesis 3:1–7). Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin separates us from Him. However, unlike the fallen angels, God has provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

The Gospel is the good news that although we are sinners deserving of judgment, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to take our place. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the greatest sin?

To understand what the greatest sin is, we must look at what Jesus Christ called the greatest commandment. When asked which was the most important law, Jesus replied that the greatest commandment is to love the Lord your God with all your heart, with all your soul, and with all your mind (Matthew 22:37-38; Mark 12:29-30). Therefore, the greatest sin is the violation of this commandment, which is the failure to love, honor, and believe in God. This manifests as unbelief and idolatry, where something else is placed in the position of authority and affection that belongs only to God.

Scripture also speaks of a specific sin that is uniquely grave, often called the unpardonable sin. Jesus described this as blasphemy against the Holy Spirit (Matthew 12:31-32). In its context, this refers to a hardened, final rejection of the Holy Spirit's testimony to the person and work of Jesus Christ. While all other sins can be forgiven, a person who continually and finally rejects the only means of forgiveness, which is the grace of God offered through the Spirit, cannot be forgiven because they refuse the remedy.

From the perspective of God's perfect holiness, every sin is a profound rebellion against His authority. James 2:10 explains that whoever keeps the whole law but fails in one point has become guilty of all of it. This is because sin is not just a list of mistakes, but a condition of the human heart that is separated from a holy God.

God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just, and His nature requires that sin be punished. Because of our nature and our choices, every human being has sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God is also merciful and loving. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus took our sins upon Himself and died a substitutionary death on the cross, satisfying the justice of God. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all things.

Salvation is not earned by our own efforts or by trying to outweigh our "great" sins with "great" works. It is a gift secured by Christ's historical work and is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). No matter how great your sin may feel, the grace of God in Christ is greater still.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about sedition?

The Bible addresses the concept of sedition, often translated as dissensions, factions, or stirring up rebellion, by emphasizing the importance of order, submission to God-ordained authority, and the peaceable character of the believer. In the New Testament, sedition is identified as a work of the flesh that stands in opposition to the fruit of the Spirit.

Scripture teaches that all authority is ultimately established by God. For this reason, believers are generally called to live as law-abiding citizens. Romans 13:1-2 states, "Let every person be subject to the governing authorities. For there is no authority except from God, and those that exist have been instituted by God. Therefore whoever resists the authorities resists what God has appointed, and those who resist will incur judgment." This call to submission is echoed in 1 Peter 2:13-14, which encourages believers to be subject to every human institution for the Lord’s sake.

The Bible categorizes sedition and the sowing of discord as behaviors that are contrary to a life transformed by God. In Galatians 5:19-21, the Apostle Paul lists "dissensions" and "factions" (which some older translations call "seditions") among the works of the flesh, warning that those who make a practice of such things will not inherit the kingdom of God. Furthermore, Proverbs 6:16-19 notes that the Lord hates "one who sows discord among brothers." Instead of inciting unrest, Christians are urged to pray for those in authority so that they may lead a "peaceful and quiet life, godly and dignified in every way" as written in 1 Timothy 2:1-2.

However, the Bible also recognizes a limit to human authority. When a government or authority commands a believer to sin or forbids them from obeying God, the believer's primary allegiance is to Christ. This is seen in Acts 5:29, when the apostles stated, "We must obey God rather than men." Even in these moments of "civil disobedience," the biblical model is one of peaceful witness and willingness to suffer for the truth, rather than violent sedition or the seeking of personal power.

At its heart, the impulse toward sedition or rebellion is often a reflection of our deeper human condition. We have all participated in a spiritual sedition against our Creator. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all turned away from His righteous rule, choosing our own path and separating ourselves from Him through sin. This rebellion deserves God's judgment.

Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life of submission to the Father. He was falsely accused of sedition Himself and was crucified on a cross. His death was a substitutionary sacrifice, paying the debt for our sins, and His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift secured by His work alone, received by grace alone through faith alone, not by our own works or moral efforts.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and rebellion, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the International Christian Church (ICC), and what do they believe?

The International Christian Church (ICC), also known as the Sold-Out Discipling Movement, is a religious organization founded in 2006 by Kip McKean. It began in Portland, Oregon, after McKean’s departure from the International Churches of Christ (ICOC), a group he had also previously led. The ICC is characterized by its intense focus on world evangelism, a highly centralized leadership structure, and a specific view of what it means to be a true follower of Jesus.

The beliefs of the ICC share some common ground with historic Christianity, such as the belief in the Trinity, the deity of Jesus Christ, and the inspiration of the Bible. However, there are several distinct doctrines and practices that set them apart:

The ICC teaches that only those who are "sold-out" disciples are true Christians. They define a disciple as someone who has given up everything to follow Jesus. In their view, one must become a disciple before they can be baptized, and only those baptized as disciples in their movement or a recognized sister church are considered saved. This is often based on their interpretation of Matthew 28:18-20 and Acts 2:38.

A central pillar of the ICC is the requirement for every member to have a "discipling partner," which is a more experienced member who provides direction, correction, and guidance in almost every area of life. This hierarchical structure is intended to ensure spiritual growth and accountability, though it has been criticized by some as being overly controlling.

The ICC holds to the view that baptism by immersion is the exact moment a person receives the forgiveness of sins and the gift of the Holy Spirit. Without baptism specifically performed with the understanding that it is for salvation, they believe a person remains lost. This is drawn from passages such as Acts 2:38 and 1 Peter 3:21.

While the ICC emphasizes total commitment and baptism, the historic Christian faith teaches that salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). The Gospel is the good news that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is not something we can earn through the intensity of our commitment or the precision of our religious rituals. It is secured entirely by the finished work of Jesus. When we stop trying to save ourselves and instead trust in the Lordship and sacrifice of Jesus Christ, we are reconciled to God (Romans 10:9-10).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

If you are looking for a spiritual home, I encourage you to seek out a local church that honors the Bible as the final authority and emphasizes the sufficiency of God's grace. It is also helpful to speak with a trusted pastor or elder who can walk through these scriptures with you. What are your thoughts on the relationship between God's grace and our response to Him?
Who was Eutychus in the Bible?

Eutychus was a young man from Troas whose story is recorded in the book of Acts. His name, which means fortunate, is associated with a remarkable miracle that occurred during the Apostle Paul's third missionary journey (Acts 20:7–12).

The account takes place on the first day of the week, when the local believers gathered to break bread. Because Paul intended to leave the next day, he continued speaking to the assembly until midnight. The meeting was held in an upstairs room where many lamps were burning, which likely contributed to the warmth and the late hour (Acts 20:7–8).

Eutychus was sitting in a window and, as Paul spoke for a long time, he was overcome by sleep. He fell from the third story to the ground and was picked up dead (Acts 20:9). When Paul went down, he threw himself on the young man and embraced him, telling the worried crowd not to be alarmed because life was in him. Paul then went back upstairs, broke bread, and continued talking until daybreak. The believers were greatly comforted when they took the young man home alive (Acts 20:10–12).

This event serves as a powerful testimony to the life giving power of God working through the apostles. It confirmed that the message Paul preached was backed by the authority of the Holy Spirit.

While the restoration of Eutychus was a physical miracle, it points us to the even greater reality of spiritual life found in the Gospel. The Bible teaches that we are all naturally dead in our sins and separated from a holy God (Ephesians 2:1; Romans 3:23). However, God is rich in mercy and sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over death and His authority as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

True life is found only through Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Do some Christmas traditions have pagan origins?

It is a matter of historical record that many traditions we associate with Christmas today have roots in the ancient world, often originating in cultures that did not worship the God of the Bible. For example, the date of December 25th was chosen by the early church partly because it coincided with Roman festivals like Saturnalia and the birth of the sun god, Sol Invictus. By celebrating the birth of Jesus at that time, early Christians sought to proclaim that Christ is the true "Light of the World" (John 8:12).

Similarly, the use of evergreen trees and greenery was common among Germanic and Nordic peoples during the winter solstice to represent the endurance of life. Over time, Christians adopted these symbols to represent the eternal life found in Jesus Christ. While these customs have ancient, non-Christian origins, the church throughout history has often sought to "redeem" cultural symbols, repurposing them to point toward the glory of God and the reality of the Incarnation.

In the Bible, we find principles that help us navigate these questions. In Romans 14:5-6, the Apostle Paul writes that one person considers one day more sacred than another, while another considers every day alike, and each should be fully convinced in their own mind. He notes that whoever regards one day as special does so to the Lord. The focus for a believer is not on the ancient history of a decorative plant or a specific date, but on whether the heart is directed toward honoring God.

Ultimately, Christmas is a time to reflect on the most profound event in history: that the holy and sovereign God, because of His great love and mercy, sent His Son into the world to save sinners. We are all separated from God by our sin, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, was born of a virgin and lived a perfect life of obedience. He then died a substitutionary, atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Through His sacrifice, salvation is offered as a free gift of grace to all who believe. You can turn to God now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life.

What thoughts or questions come to mind as you consider the meaning behind these celebrations?
What is the International House of Prayer (IHOP)?

The International House of Prayer (commonly known as IHOPKC) is an evangelical missions organization based in Kansas City, Missouri, that is best known for its commitment to perpetual prayer and worship. Founded in 1999 by Mike Bickle, the organization established a Global Prayer Room that has maintained 24/7 intercession and musical worship without interruption for over two decades.

This practice is rooted in what they call the "Davidic Tabernacle" model, referencing how King David appointed singers and musicians to worship God continually in 1 Chronicles 16:1–6 and 1 Chronicles 23:5. The movement emphasizes the "Harp and Bowl" style of worship, a term drawn from Revelation 5:8, where the "harps" represent music and the "bowls" represent the prayers of the saints.

Theologically, IHOPKC focuses heavily on several key themes:
1. The Great Commandment: A primary emphasis on loving God wholeheartedly and growing in "bridal intimacy" with Jesus Christ.
2. The Forerunner Message: A focus on preparing the Church for the second coming of Christ and interpreting "End Times" biblical prophecy.
3. Intercession: A belief that night and day prayer is a catalyst for spiritual revival, social justice, and the fulfillment of the Great Commission.

While many have been encouraged by their focus on prayer, the organization has also been a subject of discussion and concern within the broader Body of Christ. Some theologians have raised questions regarding their specific interpretations of prophecy and their association with the New Apostolic Reformation. Furthermore, in late 2023 and 2024, the organization entered a period of significant crisis and leadership transition following serious allegations of misconduct involving its founder.

This serves as a sobering reminder that while spiritual disciplines like prayer and worship are vital, our ultimate security and hope rest not in any human leader or specific ministry model, but in the person and work of Jesus Christ alone. Scripture reminds us that Jesus is our great High Priest who "always lives to make intercession" for us in Hebrews 7:25.

If you are exploring these themes of prayer and God's presence, it is important to remember the foundation of our relationship with Him. God is perfectly holy, yet because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. Out of His great love, God the Son became man in the person of Jesus Christ, lived a perfect and sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. We are not saved by the intensity or length of our prayers, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

In your journey, I encourage you to remain grounded in the Word of God, which is our ultimate authority for faith and practice as noted in 2 Timothy 3:16–17. Seek fellowship in a healthy local church where the Gospel is preached and where there is clear biblical accountability for leadership. Always remember that "the prayer of a righteous person has great power as it is working," as written in James 5:16, yet that righteousness is a gift given to us through faith in Christ.
Where does the Old Testament predict the coming of Christ?

The Old Testament is a beautiful tapestry that points entirely toward the person and work of Jesus Christ. From the very first pages of Genesis to the final words of the prophets, God reveals His plan to rescue humanity through a promised Savior. Jesus Himself told His followers that the Law of Moses, the Prophets, and the Psalms were all written about Him in Luke 24:44.

One of the earliest promises is found in Genesis 3:15, often called the first Gospel. After the first man and woman sinned, God promised that the seed of the woman would eventually crush the head of the serpent, even though the serpent would bruise His heel. This points to Christ's ultimate victory over Satan through His death and resurrection.

As the Old Testament progresses, the details about this coming Savior become more specific. The prophet Isaiah spoke of His miraculous arrival, stating that a virgin would conceive and bear a son named Immanuel, which means "God with us" in Isaiah 7:14. Micah 5:2 predicted that this Ruler of Israel would be born in the small town of Bethlehem.

Beyond His birth, the prophets described the nature of His ministry and His sacrificial death. Isaiah 53 is perhaps the most famous passage regarding the "Suffering Servant." It describes one who was pierced for our transgressions and crushed for our iniquities in Isaiah 53:5. This passage clearly predicts the penal substitutionary atonement, where Jesus took the punishment we deserved so that we could be healed and reconciled to God. 

The details of His crucifixion were even foretold in the Psalms. Psalm 22:16-18 describes His hands and feet being pierced and people casting lots for His clothing, centuries before the Roman method of crucifixion was even invented. Furthermore, the resurrection is hinted at in Psalm 16:10, where the psalmist declares that God would not allow His Holy One to see decay, a passage that the apostles later used to prove Christ's victory over death in Acts 2:25-32.

These prophecies demonstrate that God is sovereign and faithful to His word. Because God is holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory as noted in Romans 3:23, we are naturally separated from Him. However, out of His great love, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a sacrifice for sin, was buried, and rose again on the third day to prove His power over death.

Through Christ alone, we can find forgiveness and eternal life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. Is there anything about the life or promises of Jesus that you find difficult to understand or believe?
What is spiritual adultery?

Spiritual adultery is a biblical metaphor used to describe the unfaithfulness of God's people when they turn away from Him to worship idols or prioritize the things of this world over their relationship with the Lord. In the Bible, God often describes His relationship with His people as a marriage covenant. He is the faithful Husband, and His people are His bride. When we give our deepest affections, trust, or worship to anything other than God, we are committing a form of spiritual betrayal.

In the Old Testament, the prophets frequently used this language to call Israel back to God. The book of Hosea is a powerful example, where the prophet is told to marry an unfaithful woman to illustrate how Israel had "played the harlot" by departing from the Lord (Hosea 1:2). God expresses His grief over this betrayal, saying that while His people went after other lovers, He remained ready to show mercy and restore the relationship (Hosea 2:19; Hosea 2:20). Jeremiah also spoke of this, noting that the people had forgotten God, their primary love, to seek after gods that could not save them (Jeremiah 2:32; Jeremiah 3:1).

In the New Testament, James 4:4 uses very direct language: "You adulterous people, do you not know that friendship with the world is enmity with God? Therefore whoever wishes to be a friend of the world makes himself an enemy of God." This shows that spiritual adultery is not just about bowing down to statues, but about a heart that is divided. When we seek satisfaction, identity, or security in money, power, relationships, or self-pleasure rather than in God, we are acting unfaithfully toward our Creator.

This tendency toward unfaithfulness is a result of our fallen nature. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just (Exodus 34:14), but all human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment (Romans 6:23). However, God in His great love did not leave us in our unfaithfulness. 

Jesus Christ, the Son of God, came to earth as truly God and truly man to be the perfect Bridegroom for His people. He lived a sinless life, remaining perfectly faithful to the Father. He then suffered and died on a cross as a substitutionary sacrifice to pay the debt for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). Through His death and resurrection, Jesus conquered sin and death, making a way for us to be forgiven and reconciled to God.

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or efforts (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). Though we have been unfaithful, Christ offers us a new life and a restored relationship with Him.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can we rely on the power of God?

Relying on the power of God begins with a humble recognition of our own insufficiency. We often struggle when we try to navigate life's challenges through our own wisdom or strength, but Scripture reminds us that God's power is most clearly seen when we admit we are weak (2 Corinthians 12:9; 2 Corinthians 12:10). To rely on His power is to shift our weight from ourselves onto His unchanging character and promises.

One of the primary ways we lean on His power is through prayer. God invites us to draw near to His throne of grace with confidence, so that we may receive mercy and find grace to help in time of need (Hebrews 4:16). When we pray, we are not merely reciting words, we are actively depending on the Creator of the universe to sustain us. This reliance is also nurtured through the Word of God, which is living and active, providing the spiritual nourishment and guidance necessary for every season of life (Hebrews 4:12; Psalm 119:105).

Furthermore, believers rely on the power of the Holy Spirit, whom Jesus promised would dwell within His people to provide strength, comfort, and the ability to bear fruit (Acts 1:8; Ephesians 3:16). This power is not a tool we use for our own ends, but a gift that enables us to live according to His will and to endure trials with hope.

The greatest demonstration of God's power is found in the Gospel. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we were powerless to save ourselves. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation and the power to live a new life are gifts of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works (Romans 1:16; Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider relying on Him, what holds you back from trusting Him with your whole heart? Daily rhythms of reading the Bible, such as the Gospel of John or the Psalms, and connecting with a local church family can be wonderful next steps in learning to walk by His strength.
What does it mean that Jesus fulfilled the law, but did not abolish it?

In Matthew 5:17, Jesus says, "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them." To understand this, it is helpful to see how Jesus acts as the completion and the goal of everything God established in the Old Testament.

When Jesus says He did not come to abolish the Law, He is affirming that the Old Testament is the inspired and enduring Word of God. He did not come to cast it aside as if it were a mistake or a failed project. Instead, to fulfill the Law means that He brought it to its intended climax and perfect expression.

First, Jesus fulfilled the Law through His perfect obedience. While every other human being has failed to keep God's commands, Jesus lived a sinless life, meeting every moral requirement of the Law in thought, word, and deed as seen in Hebrews 4:15. Because He is truly God and truly man, His perfect life qualifies Him to be our representative.

Second, Jesus fulfilled the ceremonial aspects of the Law. The Old Testament system of sacrifices, priesthoods, and festivals were shadows that pointed to a greater reality. Jesus is the final and perfect sacrifice, the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world as noted in John 1:29. When He died on the cross, the veil in the temple was torn, signifying that the shadows were finished because the Substance had arrived as described in Matthew 27:51 and Hebrews 10:1-14.

Third, Jesus fulfilled the Law by taking upon Himself the penalty that the Law required for our disobedience. The Law reveals God's holiness and our sin, and it pronounces a curse on those who do not keep it. Jesus redeemed us from that curse by becoming a curse for us on the cross as stated in Galatians 3:13. He satisfied the righteous demands of God’s justice.

Finally, Jesus is the goal of the Law. Romans 10:4 tells us that "Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to everyone who believes." This means the Law was always intended to lead us to Him by showing us our need for a Savior.

This brings us to the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. We cannot save ourselves by trying to keep the Law, for even our best efforts fall short. However, in His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus took on human nature, lived the perfect life we could not live, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Because Jesus fulfilled the Law on your behalf, salvation is not earned by your works but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is hedonism? What is a hedonist?

Hedonism is the philosophical belief that pleasure is the highest good and the primary goal of human life. A hedonist is an individual who lives according to this principle, seeking to maximize their own enjoyment and avoid any form of pain or discomfort as their highest priority.

While the Bible acknowledges that God gives us good things to enjoy in their proper context (1 Timothy 6:17), it warns against the pursuit of pleasure as an end in itself. In 2 Timothy 3:4, the Apostle Paul describes a time when people would be lovers of pleasure rather than lovers of God. King Solomon explored the extremes of hedonism in the book of Ecclesiastes, concluding that seeking satisfaction in possessions, laughter, and indulgence apart from God is ultimately empty and a striving after wind (Ecclesiastes 2:1–11).

The core issue with hedonism from a biblical perspective is not the desire for happiness, but the direction of that desire. We were created to find our deepest delight in God Himself. In Psalm 16:11, the psalmist writes, "You make known to me the path of life; in your presence there is fullness of joy; at your right hand are pleasures forevermore." When we place earthly pleasures above God, they become idols that fail to satisfy the soul.

Our tendency to seek fulfillment in things other than God is the result of our fallen nature. We have all sinned and separated ourselves from our holy Creator. Yet, God in His mercy did not leave us in our brokenness. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and sin. Salvation is a free gift given by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or our pursuit of pleasure.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What are the Major Prophets and Minor Prophets?

In the Bible, the terms Major Prophets and Minor Prophets refer to two groups of books in the Old Testament. This distinction is based primarily on the length of the books and the size of the scrolls they were originally written on, rather than the importance of the prophet or the authority of their message. All of these books are the inspired Word of God, profitable for teaching and training in righteousness. 2 Timothy 3:16.

**The Major Prophets**
There are five books usually classified as the Major Prophets: Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, and Daniel. These books are typically longer and offer a broad, detailed look at God's dealings with Israel and the surrounding nations. For example, the book of Isaiah contains sixty six chapters and provides some of the most profound prophecies regarding the birth, suffering, and future reign of Jesus Christ. Isaiah 7:14; Isaiah 53:3-5. Jeremiah is often called the weeping prophet because of his deep sorrow over the sins of his people and the coming judgment, yet he also spoke of a New Covenant that God would make with His people. Jeremiah 31:31-34.

**The Minor Prophets**
The Minor Prophets consist of twelve books: Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi. In the Hebrew Bible, these were often collected together on a single scroll known as The Twelve. While these books are shorter, their messages are vital. They addressed specific sins, called the people to radical repentance, and promised that God would one day restore His people. Micah, for instance, prophesied exactly where the Messiah would be born. Micah 5:2.

**The Message of the Prophets**
The primary role of a prophet was to be a spokesperson for God. They stood as watchmen, warning the people that their sin and rebellion against God’s holy standards would lead to judgment. However, they also provided a message of hope. They looked forward to a day when God would deal with sin once and for all through a Savior. 

This hope is fulfilled in Jesus Christ. While the prophets spoke of judgment for sin, Jesus took that judgment upon Himself on the cross. He lived a perfect life, died as a substitute for sinners, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to prove His victory over death. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4. Through His sacrifice, we can be reconciled to God and receive eternal life.

You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most interesting or perhaps most challenging about the messages the prophets delivered to God's people?
Why believe in Jesus?

Believing in Jesus Christ is the most important decision a person can make because it concerns our relationship with God, our purpose in this life, and our eternal destiny. The Bible teaches that God is holy, just, and the creator of all things (Exodus 34:6-7). He created us to know Him and enjoy Him, but every human being has turned away from God, choosing to live by their own rules rather than His. This is what the Bible calls sin, and it separates us from a holy God (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2).

The reason to believe in Jesus is that He is the only one who can bridge this gap. Jesus is truly God and truly man, the eternal Son who took on human nature to rescue us (John 1:1; John 1:14). He lived a life of perfect obedience to God, a life that none of us could live. Out of immense love, He chose to die on a cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved (Romans 5:8; 1 Peter 2:24).

His story did not end at the grave. Three days after His burial, Jesus rose bodily from the dead, proving that He is the Son of God and that He has successfully conquered sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4). He is now the exalted Lord of all, and He offers forgiveness and new life to anyone who turns to Him (Philippians 2:9-11; Acts 2:36).

We do not earn God’s favor by being "good enough" or through religious rituals. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6; Acts 4:12). To believe in Him is to trust that His sacrifice was enough to pay for your sins and to submit to Him as your Lord and Savior.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life.

What is it about the person or claims of Jesus that interests you or perhaps feels difficult to accept?
Who was Eli in the Bible?

Eli was a high priest and a judge over Israel during the period before the nation had a king. He lived and served at the Tabernacle in Shiloh, where the Ark of the Covenant was kept. His story is primarily found in the early chapters of the book of 1 Samuel.

One of the most well known accounts of Eli's life involves his interaction with Hannah. When Hannah was praying fervently for a son, Eli initially thought she was drunk. After realizing her sincerity, he blessed her, and God eventually answered her prayer by giving her a son named Samuel (1 Samuel 1:12-17). Once Samuel was weaned, Hannah dedicated him to the Lord, and he grew up under Eli's guidance and mentorship (1 Samuel 1:24-28).

While Eli was a servant of God, his life also serves as a warning regarding family and leadership. Eli had two sons, Hophni and Phinehas, who served as priests but were deeply corrupt. They showed contempt for the Lord's sacrifices and acted immorally. Scripture describes them as "worthless men" who did not know the Lord (1 Samuel 2:12-17). Although Eli rebuked them, he failed to restrain them or remove them from their positions. Because he honored his sons more than he honored God, a prophecy was delivered that his family would be removed from the priesthood (1 Samuel 2:27-36; 1 Samuel 3:11-14).

Eli's life ended in tragedy. During a battle with the Philistines, his sons were killed and the Ark of God was captured. Upon hearing this news, the ninety eight year old Eli fell backward from his chair, broke his neck, and died (1 Samuel 4:12-18).

Eli’s failures as a high priest point us toward our need for a better, more faithful mediator. While Eli was unable to fully restrain sin or save his house, Jesus Christ came as the perfect High Priest (Hebrews 4:14-16). Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. He now reigns as Lord and intercedes for those who come to Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What aspects of Eli’s story stand out most to you?
What are the ophanim?

The term Ophanim comes from the Hebrew word for wheels, and it refers to the mysterious, celestial entities described in the visions of the prophet Ezekiel. These beings are part of the divine chariot that carries the glory of the Lord.

In the first chapter of the book of Ezekiel, the prophet describes seeing four wheels on the earth beside the living creatures, which are later identified as cherubim. These wheels are described as having the appearance of sparkling beryl and were constructed as a wheel within a wheel (Ezekiel 1:15, Ezekiel 1:16). Their rims were high and awesome, and they were filled with eyes all around (Ezekiel 1:18). These wheels moved in perfect harmony with the living creatures because the spirit of the living creatures was in the wheels (Ezekiel 1:19, Ezekiel 1:21).

In Ezekiel 10, the prophet has a similar vision and specifically notes that the wheels were called the whirling wheels (Ezekiel 10:13). While the Bible does not explicitly label them as a class of angels in the same way it does with the Seraphim or Cherubim, later Christian and Jewish traditions have often categorized the Ophanim as one of the highest orders of celestial beings who surround the throne of God.

The purpose of these Ophanim in Scripture is to demonstrate the absolute sovereignty, omnipresence, and holiness of God. The eyes on the wheels suggest that God sees all things, and the ability of the wheels to move in any direction without turning shows that God’s Spirit and authority are not limited by space or time (Ezekiel 1:17, Ezekiel 1:20).

This vivid description of God's heavenly court reminds us of the immense holiness and majesty of the Creator. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our own sin creates a separation between us and Him. However, the same God who sits on this glorious throne is also a God of infinite mercy. He did not leave us in our separation. Instead, God the Son, Jesus Christ, took on human nature through a miraculous virgin birth to live the perfect life we could not live. He then suffered a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. Now exalted as Lord, He offers salvation by grace alone through faith alone to all who believe in Him.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who was Heinrich Bullinger?

Heinrich Bullinger (1504–1575) was a leading figure of the Swiss Reformation and the successor to Huldrych Zwingli as the head of the church in Zurich. While Zwingli is often remembered for the initial break with Rome in Switzerland, it was Bullinger who stabilized, organized, and expanded the Reformed movement over his forty four years of leadership.

Bullinger was a man deeply committed to the authority of the Bible. He believed that the Holy Scriptures are the inspired and sufficient Word of God, serving as the final rule for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Timothy 3:17). His most famous work, the Decades, was a collection of fifty sermons that covered the major points of Christian doctrine. This work was so influential that it was used as a primary textbook for clergy in England during the reign of Elizabeth I.

One of his greatest contributions to the church was his development of covenant theology. Bullinger emphasized that God has always related to His people through a covenant of grace, highlighting the continuity between the Old and New Testaments. He was also the primary author of the Second Helvetic Confession in 1566, which remains one of the most important and widely accepted statements of faith in the Reformed tradition. 

Bullinger was known for his pastoral heart and his pursuit of unity within the body of Christ. He maintained a massive correspondence with thousands of people, including fellow reformers like John Calvin and political leaders across Europe, offering them spiritual guidance and encouragement. He worked diligently to find common ground on difficult theological issues, such as the nature of the Lord’s Supper, reflecting the biblical call to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace (Ephesians 4:3).

At the core of Bullinger’s life and ministry was the Gospel of Jesus Christ. He taught that because of human sin, we are separated from a holy and just God. However, through the incarnation, perfect life, and substitutionary death of Jesus Christ, and His victorious bodily resurrection, God provides the only way for sinners to be reconciled to Him. Bullinger championed the truth that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Romans 3:24; Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

His life serves as a testament to the power of God’s grace in sustaining the church through seasons of trial and transition. The same Christ that Bullinger preached is the one who offers forgiveness and new life today. You can turn to God even now, repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive the gift of eternal life. What do you find most interesting about the history of how God has preserved His truth through people like Bullinger?
What is a Bible Baptist Church, and what do Bible Baptists believe?

A Bible Baptist Church is typically a congregation that identifies with the historic Baptist tradition, emphasizing the Bible as the sole, final authority for all matters of faith and practice. While many of these churches are independent and not part of a formal denomination, they often share a common set of convictions regarding the scriptures, the nature of salvation, and the structure of the local church.

The core beliefs of Bible Baptists are centered on several foundational truths:

The Authority of Scripture
Bible Baptists believe the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God. It is the complete revelation of God's will for humanity and serves as the ultimate guide for how a believer should live and how a church should function (2 Timothy 3:16-17; 2 Peter 1:20-21).

The Nature of God and Jesus Christ
They affirm the Trinity, believing in one God who eternally exists in three persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). They hold firmly to the deity of Jesus Christ, His virgin birth, His sinless life, and His bodily resurrection (John 1:1; Virgin Birth: Matthew 1:23; Resurrection: 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation by Grace through Faith
The central message of a Bible Baptist Church is the Gospel. They teach that God is holy and supreme, yet all human beings have sinned and are separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 6:3). Because of this separation, humans cannot save themselves through good works or religious rituals. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, secured only through the substitutionary atoning death and bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 5:8).

Baptist Distinctives
These churches practice believer's baptism, which is the immersion of a person in water after they have personally trusted in Christ. They view baptism not as a means of salvation, but as an outward sign of an inward change and an act of obedience to Christ (Acts 2:41; Romans 6:4). They also believe in the autonomy of the local church, meaning each congregation is self-governing under the lordship of Christ, and the "priesthood of all believers," which means every Christian has direct access to God through Christ (1 Peter 2:9; Colossians 1:18).

The Gospel Invitation
At the heart of these beliefs is the reality that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died the death we deserved to pay for our sins. He rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Because of what Jesus has done, you can be reconciled to God and receive the forgiveness of your sins. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Have you ever come to a place where you trusted in Christ’s work alone for your salvation?

In addition to these core truths, Bible Baptists often emphasize progressive sanctification, which is the process of becoming more like Christ through the power of the Holy Spirit, prayer, and the study of the Word. They also look forward to the personal and bodily return of Jesus Christ to judge the living and the dead and to renew all things (Titus 2:13; Revelation 21:1-5).
What is sexual ethics?

Sexual ethics is the framework of moral principles that governs human sexual behavior, desires, and relationships. For the Christian, sexual ethics is not defined by cultural trends or personal feelings, but by the character of God and His revealed Word in the Bible. It is the study of how we are to honor God with our bodies and how we should treat others in the context of intimacy and attraction.

At the heart of a biblical sexual ethic is the belief that God is the creator of sex and that He designed it for a specific, beautiful purpose. Scripture teaches that God created humanity as male and female in His own image, as seen in Genesis 1:27. He instituted marriage as a lifelong, covenantal union between one man and one woman, intending for sexual intimacy to be expressed exclusively within that boundary, according to Genesis 2:24 and Matthew 19:4–6. Within this context, sex serves the purposes of procreation, the strengthening of the marital bond, and mutual joy.

A biblical sexual ethic also addresses the reality of sin. Because God is holy and His designs are perfect, any sexual activity outside of the marriage covenant (such as adultery or sexual immorality) is considered a departure from His good will and a misuse of His gift, as noted in 1 Thessalonians 4:3–5 and Hebrews 13:4. This includes not only outward actions but also the intentions of the heart and the wandering of the eye, which Jesus spoke about in Matthew 5:27–28.

However, sexual ethics is not merely a list of prohibitions. It is an invitation to see the human body as sacred. The Bible teaches that for those who follow Christ, their bodies are temples of the Holy Spirit and have been bought with a price, according to 1 Corinthians 6:19–20. Therefore, the goal of sexual ethics is to live in a way that reflects God’s holiness and love.

The weight of these standards often reveals our own failures and our need for a Savior. We have all fallen short of God’s perfect standard for holiness, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, the heart of the Christian faith is the Gospel: God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over sin and death, Jesus offers complete forgiveness and a restored relationship with God.

Salvation is not earned by our ability to keep a moral code or by our own sexual purity, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

As you reflect on these truths, what makes it difficult or encouraging for you to consider God's design for human relationships?
Who was Simon the Pharisee?

Simon the Pharisee is a man mentioned in the Gospel of Luke who invited Jesus to have a meal at his home (Luke 7:36). While he showed an interest in Jesus by hosting Him, the interaction that followed revealed a significant contrast between Simon’s self-righteousness and the humble repentance of a woman who entered the house during the dinner.

The account in Luke 7:36-50 describes how a woman who was known in the city as a sinner came to Simon’s house with an alabaster flask of ointment. Standing behind Jesus at His feet, she wept, began to wet His feet with her tears, wiped them with her hair, kissed them, and anointed them with the ointment (Luke 7:37-38).

Simon, watching this, doubted Jesus' identity as a prophet. He thought to himself that if Jesus were truly a prophet, He would know what kind of woman was touching Him (Luke 7:39). In his view, a holy man would not allow such a person to come near him. Jesus, knowing Simon’s thoughts, responded by telling a parable about two people who owed money to a certain moneylender. One owed five hundred denarii and the other fifty. When they could not pay, the moneylender cancelled the debts of both. Jesus asked Simon which of them would love the moneylender more (Luke 7:40-42).

Simon correctly answered that it would be the one for whom the larger debt was cancelled. Jesus then pointed out that Simon had failed to provide the common courtesies of hospitality, such as water for His feet, a kiss of greeting, or oil for His head. In contrast, the woman had shown great love and humility through her actions. Jesus explained that her many sins were forgiven, and therefore she loved much, whereas he who is forgiven little, loves little (Luke 7:44-47).

This story highlights that God is holy and supreme, and that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). We are all like the debtors in the parable, unable to pay the debt we owe to God because of our sin. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and His lordship over all creation. Salvation is not earned by our own works or religious status, like that of the Pharisee, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What does yoke mean in the Bible?

In the Bible, a yoke refers literally to a wooden cross-piece that is fastened over the necks of two animals, such as oxen, and attached to a plow or cart. This tool allows the animals to work together to pull a heavy load. While the term describes a physical object, the biblical writers frequently use it as a metaphor for spiritual and relational reality.

Often, a yoke symbolizes a burden of bondage, slavery, or legalism. In the Old Testament, it was used to describe the oppression of one nation by another or the weight of one's own sin (Lamentations 1:14; Lamentations 3:27). In the New Testament, the Apostle Paul warned believers not to be "subject again to a yoke of slavery," referring to the heavy burden of trying to achieve righteousness through human effort and the strict keeping of the Law (Galatians 5:1). This legalistic yoke is impossible for any human to bear perfectly (Acts 15:10).

The most beautiful use of this metaphor comes from Jesus Himself. In Matthew 11:28-30, He says, "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest. Take my yoke upon you, and learn from me, for I am gentle and lowly in heart, and you will find rest for your souls. For my yoke is easy, and my burden is light."

In this context, taking Christ’s yoke means entering into a relationship of discipleship with Him. It is an invitation to submit to His leadership and walk in step with Him. Unlike the heavy, crushing yoke of sin or legalism, Jesus offers a yoke of grace. He carries the weight, and we walk in His strength.

This invitation is grounded in the Gospel. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, we naturally carry a burden of guilt and separation from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, Jesus Christ, the Son of God, took on human nature, lived a sinless life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you have questions about following Jesus, a wonderful next step is to begin reading the Gospel of John in the Bible and to speak with a pastor at a local, Bible-teaching church. They can provide guidance as you learn what it means to walk in the "easy yoke" of Christ.
What is spiritual sickness? What does it mean to be spiritually sick?

Spiritual sickness is a term used to describe the condition of a human soul that is alienated from God, burdened by sin, or experiencing a deep lack of spiritual health and vitality. In the Bible, physical illness is often used as a metaphor to help us understand our spiritual state. Just as a physical body can be weakened or even destroyed by disease, our spirits can be paralyzed by sin and separation from our Creator.

The most fundamental form of spiritual sickness is the universal condition of sin. The Bible teaches that every person has turned away from God's perfect standards. This condition is described as a state of being "dead in the trespasses and sins" in Ephesians 2:1. It manifests as a lack of desire for God, a hardened heart, and a tendency to seek satisfaction in things other than Him. Isaiah the prophet described the spiritual state of a rebellious nation by saying that "the whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint" in Isaiah 1:5.

Jesus Christ often spoke of Himself as a physician for the soul. When He was criticized for spending time with those seen as outcasts and sinners, He responded by saying, "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick. I came not to call the righteous, but sinners" in Mark 2:17. This indicates that the first step toward spiritual health is recognizing our own "sickness" or our need for God’s intervention and forgiveness.

For those who are already believers, spiritual sickness can refer to a period of declining faith, unconfessed sin, or a coldness toward the things of God. This might involve a loss of joy, a lack of prayer, or a feeling of distance from the Holy Spirit. In Psalm 32:3-4, King David describes the physical and spiritual exhaustion he felt when he kept his sin secret, noting that his "strength was dried up as by the heat of summer."

The only cure for this spiritual condition is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet we are all sinners who fall short of His glory as stated in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His judgment. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life on our behalf. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death as described in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Salvation and spiritual healing are not earned by our own good works or efforts to "get better" on our own. Instead, they are a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. As Ephesians 2:8-9 says, "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast."

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you find yourself feeling spiritually weary or sick, the Scriptures encourage you to draw near to God through prayer, the reading of His Word, and fellowship with other believers. James 5:16 encourages us to "confess your sins to one another and pray for one another, that you may be healed." Rest in the promise that God is faithful to restore those who come to Him with a contrite heart.
What is fellowship with God?

Fellowship with God is a deep, personal, and interactive relationship where a person shares in the life and purposes of God. The Bible uses the word koinonia to describe this, which carries the meaning of partnership, communion, or sharing things in common. It is not merely knowing facts about God, but knowing Him personally and being known by Him.

This fellowship is made possible only through the person and work of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and we are sinful, our wrongdoing creates a barrier that separates us from Him (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to restore this broken relationship. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

Through Christ, we are reconciled to the Father. Fellowship begins when a person turns from their sin and trusts in Jesus Christ alone for forgiveness and new life. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. As we are justified by faith, we enter into a permanent state of peace and communion with God (Romans 5:1).

In our daily lives, fellowship with God involves several key aspects:

1. Walking in the Light: Fellowship requires honesty and confession. When we agree with God about our sin and seek His forgiveness, our daily walk with Him remains clear and vibrant (1 John 1:7; 1 John 1:9).
2. Communication: We speak to God through prayer, expressing our needs, thanks, and adoration (Philippians 4:6). God speaks to us through His inspired and authoritative Word, the Bible, which guides our paths and reveals His character (Psalm 119:105; John 17:17).
3. The Presence of the Holy Spirit: Every believer is indwelt by the Holy Spirit, who empowers us to experience God's love and provides the "fellowship of the Spirit" (2 Corinthians 13:14; Romans 8:15-16).
4. Obedience: Jesus taught that those who love Him will keep His commandments, and in that obedience, they experience a special intimacy with the Father and the Son (John 14:23).

Fellowship is also expressed through the local church. As we have fellowship with God, we naturally have fellowship with other believers, sharing in the same Spirit and the same mission (1 John 1:3).

What holds you back from trusting Him or seeking this closeness with Him today?
What role does Iran play in the end times?

In biblical prophecy, the nation we know today as Iran is primarily identified by its ancient name, Persia. The most prominent mention of Persia in a prophetic context is found in the book of Ezekiel, specifically in the account of a future conflict often called the "War of Gog and Magog" in Ezekiel 38:1-23 and Ezekiel 39:1-29.

In Ezekiel 38:5, Persia is listed as one of the key allies in a massive coalition of nations that descends upon Israel. Many biblical scholars and students of prophecy believe this describes a literal military campaign in the latter days. They point to the geographical and political shifts in the Middle East as potential precursors to these events. However, because eschatology (the study of the end times) is a tertiary doctrine, faithful Christians hold several different views on how to interpret these passages.

Some interpret these prophecies as literal events that will take place before or during the return of Jesus Christ. Others view the descriptions of these battles as symbolic of the ongoing spiritual and physical opposition that the people of God face throughout history, culminating in a final victory for God’s kingdom. Regardless of the specific interpretation, the underlying biblical truth is that God is sovereign over the nations. He is the one who "changes times and seasons" and "removes kings and sets up kings" (Daniel 2:21). He has determined the periods and boundaries of all peoples (Acts 17:26).

While world events and international tensions can cause anxiety, the Bible encourages believers to look to the character of God and the promise of His return. The ultimate "end" described in Scripture is not just a series of geopolitical conflicts, but the restoration of all things under the lordship of Jesus Christ.

This reality of future judgment and restoration reminds us of our own standing before God. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, and that our sin has separated us from Him. Because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a sacrificial and substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5; John 14:6; Acts 4:12). Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone, and not by any works we can do.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As we watch world events unfold, we can find peace knowing that the same God who governs the movement of nations is the same God who offers mercy to every individual who calls upon Him. What holds you back from trusting Him with your future today?
What does Jesus mean when He says, “Verily, verily” or “Truly, truly,” in the gospels?

When Jesus uses the phrase "Truly, truly," or "Verily, verily," He is using a translation of the Hebrew word "Amen." While most people in the ancient world used this word at the end of a prayer or a statement to express agreement or a wish that something would come to pass, Jesus used it in a way that was entirely unique. By placing "Amen" at the beginning of His statements, He was signaling that what He was about to say was absolutely certain, authoritative, and true in itself.

This repetitive phrasing is found specifically in the Gospel of John. It serves several important purposes:

First, it emphasizes His divine authority. When the prophets of the Old Testament spoke, they often began with "Thus says the Lord," appealing to God’s authority. However, Jesus spoke on His own authority as the Son of God. When He says "Truly, truly," He is essentially saying, "I am telling you the absolute truth on My own authority." We see this in John 5:24 where He says, "Truly, truly, I say to you, he who hears My word, and believes Him who sent Me, has eternal life, and does not come into judgment, but has passed out of death into life."

Second, the repetition acts as a spiritual "wake-up call." It highlights a truth that is profound, often mysterious, or essential for our salvation. For example, in John 3:3, Jesus tells Nicodemus, "Truly, truly, I say to you, unless one is born again he cannot see the kingdom of God." By repeating the word, He is calling for our undivided attention to a reality that we might otherwise miss or misunderstand.

Finally, the word "Amen" is actually a title for Jesus Himself. In Revelation 3:14, He is called "the Amen, the faithful and true Witness." This tells us that Jesus is not just a teacher of truth, but He is the Truth itself.

Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him, but Jesus spoke these "truly, truly" statements to reveal the way back to the Father. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or efforts.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about His teachings that you find difficult to trust or understand?
What should we learn from the tribe of Asher?

The tribe of Asher, named after the eighth son of Jacob, provides several significant lessons for believers today regarding God's provision, the risks of spiritual complacency, and the persistence of God's grace through the generations.

**God’s Abundant Provision**
The name Asher means "happy" or "blessed," and the history of this tribe is marked by material abundance. When Jacob blessed his sons, he prophesied that Asher’s food would be "rich" and he would yield "royal delicacies" (Genesis 49:20). Later, Moses blessed the tribe by saying Asher should "dip his foot in oil" and that his strength would match his days (Deuteronomy 33:24-25). 

The territory assigned to Asher was along the Mediterranean coast, a fertile region famous for its olive groves and crops. This teaches us that God is a generous provider who cares for the physical needs of His people. We are reminded to receive God's gifts with gratitude, acknowledging that every good thing comes from His hand (James 1:17).

**The Danger of Complacency**
While Asher was blessed with wealth and fertile land, their history also serves as a somber warning. In the book of Judges, we read that Asher "did not drive out the inhabitants" of the land but instead "lived among the Canaanites" (Judges 1:31-32). During the time of Deborah, the tribe remained by the coast and "stayed at his landings" rather than joining their brothers in the battle for the Lord (Judges 5:17).

The lesson here is that material prosperity can sometimes lead to spiritual apathy. When life is comfortable, it is easy to settle for a peaceful co-existence with the world rather than pursuing the holiness and mission God has called us to. We must be careful that the "royal delicacies" of this life do not dull our hunger for God’s kingdom.

**The Beauty of a Remnant and Persistent Hope**
Despite the tribe's failures and the eventual exile of the northern kingdom, God did not forget the people of Asher. When King Hezekiah called for a return to the Lord, some from the tribe of Asher "humbled themselves and came to Jerusalem" to keep the Passover (2 Chronicles 30:11). 

Centuries later, we meet Anna the prophetess, who was from the tribe of Asher (Luke 2:36). She spent her life in the temple, fasting and praying, and was among the first to recognize and speak of the infant Jesus to all who were looking for redemption. Anna's life teaches us that God preserves a faithful remnant in every age. No matter our background or the failings of those who came before us, God values individual faithfulness and persistence in prayer.

**The Ultimate Satisfaction**
The "rich food" and "royal delicacies" promised to Asher point us toward a greater spiritual reality. While physical blessings are gifts from God, they cannot satisfy the deepest hunger of the human soul. We are all separated from a holy God by our sin, and no amount of earthly abundance can bridge that gap.
