Jesus Christ, the Son of God, came as the true "Bread of Life" to provide what physical food never could. He lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts or comfort, but a gift received by grace alone through faith in Christ alone. 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why does the Bible speak against braided hair?

The Bible addresses the topic of braided hair in two primary passages, 1 Timothy 2:9 and 1 Peter 3:3. To understand why these instructions were given, it is helpful to look at the heart of the message and the cultural context of the early church.

In the Greco-Roman world of the first century, elaborate hairstyles involving complex braids interwoven with gold, pearls, and expensive jewelry were often used as a public display of great wealth, social status, and vanity. When the apostles Paul and Peter wrote these instructions, they were concerned that such displays would create distractions and divisions within the church. If some women came to worship flaunting high-fashion symbols of luxury, it could make the poor feel insignificant or shift the focus of the gathering away from the Lord.

The primary concern in these verses is not a legalistic prohibition against a specific hairstyle, but an exhortation to prioritize "the hidden person of the heart" (1 Peter 3:4). The Bible teaches that a believer’s beauty should not come from trying to impress others through outward luxury, but from a life transformed by the Holy Spirit. Paul emphasizes that women should adorn themselves with "respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control," and especially with "good works" (1 Timothy 2:9-10).

This teaching reminds us that God does not look at the outward appearance in the same way humans do; He looks at the heart (1 Samuel 16:7). The goal for every Christian is to reflect the character of Jesus Christ rather than drawing undue attention to themselves.

Ultimately, our worth and "adornment" are not found in what we wear or how we style our hair, but in the righteousness given to us by God. We are all sinners separated from a holy God, but through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, we can be reconciled to Him. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or appearance. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life.

Does this help clarify the heart behind those biblical instructions for you?

1 Timothy 2:9-10; 1 Peter 3:3-4; 1 Samuel 16:7; Romans 10:9-10
What is the difference between a covenant and a contract?

In common usage, the difference between a covenant and a contract is profound, particularly when we look at how God relates to His people in the Bible. While both involve agreements, their nature, duration, and foundation are quite distinct.

A contract is primarily a legal agreement between two or more parties. It is usually transactional and conditional, centered on the exchange of goods, services, or property. In a contract, the focus is on protected interests, "I will do this if you do that." If one party fails to meet their obligations, the contract is often considered broken or void. It is a relationship of convenience and law, governed by the terms written on a page.

A covenant, however, is a relational bond and a solemn commitment, often made in the presence of God. While a contract protects interests, a covenant protects a relationship. It is an exchange of persons rather than just an exchange of things. In the Bible, a covenant involves promises, oaths, and a deep level of loyalty and love that persists even when one party falters. For example, marriage is described in Scripture as a covenantal union where two become one flesh, rather than just a legal contract (Malachi 2:14; Matthew 19:4-6).

The most significant difference is found in how God interacts with humanity. Biblical covenants demonstrate God’s sovereign initiative and His steadfast faithfulness. In the Old Testament, when God made a covenant with Abraham, He alone passed through the pieces of the sacrifice, signifyng that He would take the penalty upon Himself if the covenant were broken (Genesis 15:17-18). This shows that a biblical covenant is grounded in the character of God, who remains faithful even when we are faithless (2 Timothy 2:13).

Ultimately, all biblical covenants point toward the New Covenant established by Jesus Christ. This is the most beautiful expression of God’s commitment to us. In a contract, if you fail, the deal is over. In the New Covenant, God provides the solution for our failure through the life, death, and resurrection of His Son.

The Gospel tells us that God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. We cannot bridge this gap through our own works or by trying to fulfill a moral contract. However, God, in His great mercy, sent Jesus Christ to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Through this New Covenant, salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone. It is not a transaction where we earn God’s favor, but a covenantal relationship where we are adopted into His family. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is it that might be holding you back from trusting in Him today?
What is the history and significance of the church in Corinth?

The church in Corinth holds a unique place in the New Testament as a powerful example of how the Gospel of Jesus Christ takes root and flourishes in a complex, pluralistic, and often immoral society.

The city of Corinth was a major cosmopolitan hub in the Roman Empire, serving as the capital of the province of Achaia. Located on an isthmus between two major seas, it was a wealthy center of trade, philosophy, and diverse religious practices. It was famous for the Isthmian Games and notorious for its moral laxity, so much so that to "act like a Corinthian" was a common ancient euphemism for living a life of debauchery. The city was home to many pagan temples, most notably the temple of Aphrodite, which influenced the social and moral fabric of the community.

The church was founded by the Apostle Paul during his second missionary journey, around AD 50 or 51. According to Acts 18:1-4, Paul arrived in Corinth from Athens and met Aquila and Priscilla, fellow tentmakers who had recently been expelled from Rome. Paul began by preaching in the synagogue, but when he faced opposition from some of the Jewish leaders, he turned his focus to the Gentiles. God encouraged Paul in a vision, telling him not to be afraid because there were many people in that city who belonged to the Lord (Acts 18:9-10). Paul remained in Corinth for eighteen months, teaching the Word of God and establishing a vibrant, though troubled, congregation.

The significance of the Corinthian church lies in the profound theological and practical lessons we learn from Paul's letters to them. Because the believers in Corinth brought many of their former worldly habits and cultural values into the church, they struggled with deep divisions, legal disputes among members, sexual immorality, and confusion regarding spiritual gifts and the Lord's Supper. Paul’s response to these issues provides the Church today with essential teaching on Christian unity, the nature of love (1 Corinthians 13:1-13), the proper use of spiritual gifts, and the absolute necessity of the bodily resurrection of Jesus Christ (1 Corinthians 15:12-20).

Perhaps most significantly, the history of this church demonstrates the transformative power of God's grace. In 1 Corinthians 6:9-10, Paul lists various sins that characterized the lives of many in the city, but he follows this with a beautiful declaration of the Gospel in 1 Corinthians 6:11: "And such were some of you. But you were washed, you were sanctified, you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and by the Spirit of our God."

This transformation is only possible because of the central message Paul preached: that Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This message of the cross is the power of God for those who are being saved (1 Corinthians 1:18). We are reconciled to a holy God not by our own works or moral improvements, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

The history of the church in Corinth serves as a reminder that no matter how broken a person’s past or how dark a city’s culture may be, the light of Christ can bring forgiveness and new life. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What is your current understanding of who Jesus is and what He has done for you?
If Jesus was God, why did He say "No one is good but God alone"?

This is a thoughtful question that touches on the heart of who Jesus is and how He taught those around Him. When Jesus asked, "Why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone" (Mark 10:18; Luke 18:19), He was not denying His own divinity or His own goodness. Instead, He was challenging the rich young man to consider the weight of the words he was using.

In that culture, "Good Teacher" was not a common way to address a rabbi. By using this title, the young man was showing a certain level of respect, but Jesus wanted to know if the man truly understood who he was talking to. Jesus was essentially posing a divine dilemma: If you acknowledge that only God is truly, perfectly good, and you call Me good, are you prepared to acknowledge that I am God?

Throughout the Gospels, Jesus makes it clear that He shares the same nature and essence as the Father. He said, "I and the Father are one" (John 10:30) and "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father" (John 14:9). Furthermore, the Bible describes Jesus as being without sin (Hebrews 4:15), and Jesus Himself refers to Himself as the "Good Shepherd" (John 10:11). Since He is truly good and only God is truly good, the logical conclusion is that Jesus is indeed God in the flesh.

This interaction also highlights the profound gap between God's holiness and our human condition. The young man believed he had kept all the commandments, but Jesus pointed him back to the standard of God's perfect goodness to show him his need for a Savior. Scripture teaches that God is holy and supreme (Isaiah 6:3), while all human beings have fallen short of His glory through sin (Romans 3:23). Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is not earned by our own "goodness" or works, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the most difficult part of accepting that Jesus is God?
What does it mean that God will reveal the hidden things (Daniel 2:22 and 28)?

In the context of Daniel 2, the statement that God reveals hidden things highlights His absolute sovereignty and perfect knowledge. At this moment in history, King Nebuchadnezzar had a dream that troubled him, but he refused to tell his advisors what the dream was, demanding that they tell him both the dream and its interpretation. When all human wisdom failed, Daniel turned to God, recognizing that what is hidden from man is fully known by the Creator.

Daniel 2:22 says, "He reveals deep and hidden things; he knows what is in the darkness, and the light dwells with him." This teaches us about God’s omniscience, meaning He knows all things perfectly, including the secrets of the human heart and the events of the future. Nothing is obscured from His sight because He is the source of all truth and light.

In Daniel 2:28, Daniel tells the king, "But there is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, and he has made known to King Nebuchadnezzar what will be in the latter days." Here, the "hidden things" specifically refer to God's sovereign plan for human history. He is not a distant observer but the one who directs the rise and fall of kingdoms. By revealing the dream, God demonstrated that He alone holds the keys to the future and that human power is temporary compared to His eternal kingdom.

On a personal level, this truth reminds us that we cannot hide our lives from God. Hebrews 4:13 says, "And no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account." While this can be a sobering thought because of our sin, it is also a source of great comfort for those who trust Him, for He knows our needs and our struggles even when we cannot find the words to express them.

The ultimate "hidden thing" or mystery that God has revealed is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. For generations, the full picture of how God would redeem the world was partially veiled, but it has now been made clear through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we earn through our own efforts or wisdom, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What items should be included in a church constitution?

A church constitution serves as a vital tool for maintaining order and clarity within a local body of believers. Scripture reminds us that all things should be done decently and in order (1 Corinthians 14:40), and a well-crafted constitution helps the church remain faithful to its calling. While specific needs may vary by tradition, several core elements are typically included to ensure the health and protection of the congregation.

The Statement of Faith is arguably the most critical component. It outlines the core doctrines the church holds to be true, such as the nature of the Trinity, the authority of the Bible as the inspired Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16-17), the deity of Christ, and the Gospel of salvation by grace alone through faith alone. This document ensures that the teaching from the pulpit and in the classrooms remains grounded in biblical truth.

A section on Membership is also essential. This defines what it means to be a part of the local body, including the requirements for joining, such as a credible profession of faith and baptism. It should also outline the responsibilities of members, such as regular attendance, financial support, and the exercise of spiritual gifts, as well as the process for removing a member from the rolls through resignation or, when necessary, restorative church discipline (Matthew 18:15-17).

Leadership and Governance structures must be clearly defined. This typically includes the qualifications and duties of elders and deacons (1 Timothy 3:1-13; Titus 1:5-9). The constitution should explain how these leaders are selected, their terms of service, and how the congregation participates in major decisions. Defining these roles helps prevent confusion and promotes accountability among those who shepherd the flock (1 Peter 5:1-4).

The Practice of Ordinances is another key item. The constitution should state how the church observes Baptism and the Lord’s Supper, reflecting the commands of Jesus (Matthew 28:19; Luke 22:19-20). This ensures these sacred acts are performed with reverence and in accordance with the church’s theological convictions.

Other practical sections often include the Church Purpose or Mission Statement, guidelines for Meetings and Voting, and the process for Amendments. It is also wise to include a section on Conflict Resolution and the distribution of assets should the church ever dissolve, ensuring that all things are handled in a way that honors God.

Ultimately, these documents exist to protect the proclamation of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received not through our own works, but through trust in Christ's finished work.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today? If you are involved in drafting a constitution, it is also helpful to seek the guidance of your local elders or a trusted network of churches to ensure your documents reflect your specific convictions and provide the necessary legal protections for your ministry.
What is topical preaching? Should a pastor preach topically?

Topical preaching is a method of teaching where a pastor selects a specific subject, theme, or issue and then gathers various scriptures from across the Bible to explain what God has revealed about that topic. This is distinct from expository preaching, which usually involves working through a specific book or passage of the Bible verse by verse to uncover the author's original intent in its immediate context. Common topics for this style of preaching might include marriage, suffering, the nature of prayer, or specific doctrines like the Trinity.

Whether a pastor should preach topically is a matter of wisdom and pastoral strategy. There are several factors to consider when evaluating this method.

One benefit of topical preaching is that it allows a pastor to address the immediate and specific needs of the congregation. If a community is experiencing a season of intense grief or facing a particular ethical challenge, a topical series can provide direct biblical guidance. It is also helpful for teaching systematic theology, as it allows the preacher to synthesize what the entire Bible says about a complex truth, much like how Ezra and the Levites helped the people understand the Law by giving the sense of it (Nehemiah 8:8).

However, there are significant cautions to observe. The primary danger of topical preaching is that the pastor may inadvertently take verses out of their original context to support a preconceived point, a practice known as proof-texting. There is also the risk that a pastor might only preach on favorite subjects, avoiding difficult or convicting passages that would naturally be addressed in a verse-by-verse study. A steady diet of only topical sermons can sometimes leave a congregation with a fragmented understanding of the Bible rather than a grasp of the whole counsel of God (Acts 20:27).

Many pastors find that the healthiest approach is to make expository preaching the main foundation of the church's diet, while using topical sermons or series for specific purposes. Regardless of the method used, the ultimate goal of all preaching is to proclaim the Word of God faithfully (2 Timothy 4:2).

The most vital aspect of any sermon, whether topical or expository, is that it remains centered on the Gospel of Jesus Christ. The Bible reveals that God is perfectly holy, yet we are all separated from Him by our sin and rebellion. Because we could never save ourselves through our own works, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

True preaching aims to build up the body of Christ and bring glory to God by pointing every listener to the sufficiency of the Savior (1 Corinthians 2:2; Colossians 1:28). Faith comes by hearing, and hearing through the word of Christ (Romans 10:17). Whether a pastor is walking through a book of the Bible or exploring a specific theme, the focus must always be on the authority of Scripture and the glory of the Lord.
Who wrote the book of Matthew? Who was the author of Matthew?

Traditionally, the author of the first Gospel is Matthew, also known as Levi, who was one of the twelve apostles of Jesus. While the Gospel of Matthew is technically anonymous because the author does not sign his name within the text, the early church was unanimous in attributing it to him.

Matthew was a tax collector before Jesus called him to follow Him, as recorded in Matthew 9:9 and Matthew 10:3. His background in tax collection would have likely made him skilled in shorthand and record keeping, which served him well in documenting the detailed sermons and parables of Jesus. As an eyewitness to the ministry, death, and resurrection of Christ, Matthew wrote his account primarily for a Jewish audience to prove that Jesus is the long awaited Messiah and King who fulfills the Old Testament prophecies.

The central message Matthew conveys is that God is holy and sovereign, yet humanity is separated from Him by sin. In His great love, God sent Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to Himself. Matthew 28:1–10 records the glorious news of Jesus' bodily resurrection, confirming His victory over sin and death.

Because of what Jesus has done, salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What are tribulation saints?

The term "tribulation saints" refers to individuals who put their faith in Jesus Christ during a period of intense global trial known as the Great Tribulation. This period is described in the book of Revelation as a time of divine judgment and significant hardship for the earth preceding the second coming of Christ.

Revelation 7:9–14 describes a "great multitude that no one could number, from every nation, from all tribes and peoples and languages," standing before the throne of God. One of the elders in the vision explains that "these are the ones coming out of the great tribulation. They have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb" (Revelation 7:14). These individuals are often characterized by their endurance and their refusal to worship the "beast" or receive its mark, even at the cost of their lives (Revelation 13:7; Revelation 20:4).

Interpretations of these saints vary among Christians depending on their understanding of the end times. Some believers, particularly those who hold to a pre-tribulation rapture, see these individuals as a distinct group who come to Christ after the Church has been removed from the earth. Other believers, such as those holding to post-tribulation or historic premillennial views, believe these saints are the members of the Church who remain on earth to endure these final trials. While these perspectives differ on the timing and identity of the group, all agree that God remains faithful to preserve a people for Himself even in the darkest times.

Ultimately, the existence of believers during the tribulation highlights the mercy of God, showing that His offer of salvation remains open even during a time of judgment. The Bible teaches that God is holy and must judge sin, but because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for sin, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 5:8).

Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works or human effort (Ephesians 2:8–9). This message is the only hope for any person, whether today or in the days to come. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Who are the Black Hebrews / Black Israelites?

The term Black Hebrew Israelites (also known as Hebrew Israelites or Black Hebrews) refers to a diverse movement of people, predominantly of African descent, who believe they are the physical and spiritual descendants of the ancient biblical Israelites. The movement emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries, primarily in the United States, and has since grown into many different branches with varying beliefs and practices.

While there is a wide spectrum of groups within this movement, they generally share the conviction that the "lost tribes" of Israel are found among the people of the African Diaspora, as well as some Hispanic and Native American populations. Many within the movement point to the curses described in Deuteronomy 28:15–68, particularly the references to being taken into slavery by ships, as prophetic evidence that their ancestors were the true Israelites of the Bible.

The movement is not a single denomination and can be categorized into three broad groups:

1. Messianic or Christian-aligned groups: These individuals often hold to traditional Christian doctrines, including the deity of Jesus (whom they often call by His Hebrew name, Yeshua), while also observing Old Testament laws, such as the Sabbath and dietary restrictions. They see their Israelite identity as a fulfillment of their Christian faith.

2. Hebrew-centric groups: These groups may move away from traditional Christianity to focus more on Torah observance. Some may view Jesus as a prophet or a great teacher but not necessarily as God incarnate.

3. Radical or extremist fringe groups: These are the groups most often seen preaching in public spaces. They often hold views that are exclusionary or confrontational, sometimes teaching that other races are inherently inferior or that salvation is reserved only for physical descendants of Israel.

From a historical Christian perspective, we recognize that every person is created in the image of God and possesses inherent dignity, as stated in Genesis 1:26–27. However, the Bible teaches that our primary identity and our standing before God are not determined by physical lineage, skin color, or earthly ancestry. The Apostle Paul wrote that "there is neither Jew nor Greek, there is neither slave nor free, there is no male and female, for you are all one in Christ Jesus," in Galatians 3:28. He also clarified that being a true child of Abraham is a matter of faith in God’s promise rather than physical descent, as noted in Romans 9:6–8 and Galatians 3:7.

The central message of the Scriptures is the Gospel of Jesus Christ. God is holy and just, yet because of human sin, we are all separated from Him (Romans 3:23). In His great love, God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life. Jesus died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice to pay the penalty for our sins and was bodily resurrected on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works or heritage (Ephesians 2:8–9). Jesus Himself declared, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me," in John 14:6. This invitation to reconciliation with God is extended to people of every nation, tribe, and tongue (Revelation 7:9).

I invite you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, to find your true identity and the forgiveness of sins. What thoughts or questions do you have about finding identity in Christ?
What does the Bible say about church-hopping?

The Bible does not use the specific term "church-hopping," but it speaks extensively about the nature of the local church and the commitment believers should have to one another. At its heart, the Christian life is designed to be lived in a community where we are known, held accountable, and able to serve others with our spiritual gifts.

One of the primary metaphors for the church is the body of Christ. In 1 Corinthians 12:12-27, the Apostle Paul explains that every believer is a member of the body and that "the eye cannot say to the hand, ‘I have no need of you’" (1 Corinthians 12:21). When someone moves from church to church without committing, they are like a limb trying to function apart from the body. They miss out on the spiritual growth that comes from stable relationships, and the local congregation misses out on the unique ways God intended to use them to bless others.

Commitment is also necessary for biblical leadership to function as God intended. Hebrews 13:17 instructs believers to "Obey your leaders and submit to them, for they are keeping watch over your souls, as those who will have to give an account." If a person is constantly hopping between churches, they are not truly under the care of specific elders who know them and can shepherd them through trials or hold them accountable.

Furthermore, the writer of Hebrews warns against "neglecting to meet together, as is the habit of some" (Hebrews 10:25). The purpose of meeting is to "stir up one another to love and good works" (Hebrews 10:24). This stirring up requires a depth of relationship and trust that is difficult to achieve if one is always a visitor rather than a committed member.

While there are biblical reasons to leave a church, such as the teaching of a false gospel (Galatians 1:6-9), unaddressed systemic sin (1 Corinthians 5:1-2), or relocating to a new area, the trend of church-hopping often stems from a consumer mindset rather than a servant mindset. We are called to imitate Christ, who did not come to be served but to serve and to give His life as a ransom for many (Mark 10:45).

This sacrificial love is the very foundation of the Gospel. Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), we were separated from Him. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we earn through our own works or by finding the perfect church, but it is a gift received through grace alone by faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What is it that you find most challenging about committing to one local church?
What is sectarianism?

Sectarianism is a spirit of narrow-mindedness or excessive devotion to a particular group, denomination, or party, often leading to prejudice and division within the larger body of believers. In a Christian context, it occurs when people elevate secondary or tertiary doctrines to the level of essential truths, creating walls between themselves and other genuine followers of Christ.

The Bible warns against this kind of factionalism. In the early church at Corinth, believers began to divide themselves based on which teacher they followed. The Apostle Paul corrected them, asking if Christ was divided and pointing out that such behavior stems from spiritual immaturity (1 Corinthians 1:10-13; 1 Corinthians 3:3-4). While it is important to be committed to a local church and to hold convictions about what the Bible teaches, sectarianism becomes a sin when it replaces the love and unity Christ commanded with pride and exclusion (John 13:34-35).

True unity is not found in agreeing on every minor point of tradition or interpretation, but in our shared identity in Jesus Christ. At the heart of our faith is the reality that God is holy and we are all sinners separated from Him by our transgressions. Because of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. We are saved by God's grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our works or our affiliation with a specific human group (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 10:9-10).

Sectarianism often loses sight of this central Gospel message by focusing on the boundaries of a "sect" rather than the beauty of the Savior. As believers, we are called to maintain "the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace" (Ephesians 4:3). We should strive for unity in the essential doctrines of the faith, offer liberty in non-essential matters, and practice charity in all things (Romans 14:1-4).

If you find yourself feeling separated from God or caught in the confusion of human divisions, know that peace is found in Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What has been your experience with different denominations or groups within the church?
Dance in worship - what does the Bible say?

The Bible presents dance as a powerful expression of joy, celebration, and praise directed toward God. Throughout Scripture, dance is often the physical response of a heart overwhelmed by God’s deliverance and goodness.

In the Old Testament, we see several prominent examples of this. After the Israelites were miraculously led through the Red Sea, Miriam the prophetess took a tambourine in her hand and all the women went out after her with tambourines and dancing in Exodus 15:20. Later, when the ark of the covenant was brought into Jerusalem, King David danced before the Lord with all his might in 2 Samuel 6:14. His dance was a humble, exuberant act of worship, even though it was misunderstood by some.

The Psalms specifically command and invite the use of dance in worship. Psalm 149:3 says, "Let them praise his name with dancing, making melody to him with tambourine and lyre." In the final call to praise in the Psalter, Psalm 150:4 exhorts believers to "praise him with tambourine and dance; praise him with strings and pipe." These passages suggest that God finds pleasure in the physical expression of His people's joy.

While the Bible affirms dance, it also provides principles for its context. Ecclesiastes 3:4 notes that there is "a time to mourn, and a time to dance," suggesting that our outward expressions should reflect the spiritual reality of the moment. In the New Testament, the focus of corporate worship shifts toward the edification of the body and the inner posture of the heart. While dance is not explicitly forbidden or commanded in the New Testament church gatherings, the apostle Paul provides a guiding principle for all worship in 1 Corinthians 14:40, stating that "all things should be done decently and in order."

The heart of worship, whether expressed through singing, prayer, or dance, is a response to the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Scripture teaches that we are all sinners separated from a holy God in Romans 3:23. However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. We are reconciled to God not by our own works or religious performances, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone in Ephesians 2:8-9.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

When considering dance in worship today, it is helpful to ask whether the motive is to glorify God or to seek attention for oneself. In the context of a local church, believers should move forward with charity and respect for the conscience of others, ensuring that the focus remains on the worthiness of Christ. What is your primary hope when you think about expressing your worship to God?
What was the reason behind Peter’s denial of Jesus?

Peter’s denial of Jesus, recorded in all four Gospels, was the result of a complex mixture of human frailty, fear, and spiritual overconfidence. While Peter loved Jesus deeply, several factors led to his three denials on the night of the crucifixion.

First, Peter struggled with pride and self-reliance. Earlier that evening, when Jesus warned the disciples that they would all fall away, Peter boldly claimed that even if everyone else deserted Jesus, he never would (Matthew 26:33; Mark 14:29). By trusting in his own strength rather than acknowledging his need for God’s sustaining grace, Peter left himself vulnerable to temptation.

Second, there was a lack of spiritual preparation. In the Garden of Gethsemane, Jesus specifically told Peter to watch and pray so that he would not fall into temptation, noting that the spirit is willing but the flesh is weak (Matthew 26:41). Instead of praying, Peter fell asleep, missing the opportunity to be spiritually strengthened for the trials ahead.

Third, Peter was overwhelmed by fear and the instinct for self-preservation. After Jesus was arrested, the atmosphere was one of intense hostility and danger. Peter followed Jesus into the courtyard of the high priest, putting himself in a position where he was surrounded by those who had just seen Jesus seized (John 18:15–18). When he was identified as a follower of Jesus, the immediate threat of arrest or physical harm likely prompted him to protect himself by lying.

Ultimately, Peter’s denial serves as a powerful reminder of the human condition. Even the most devoted believers are capable of failing when they rely on their own power. However, the story does not end with Peter’s failure. After His resurrection, Jesus personally restored Peter, demonstrating that His grace is greater than our greatest sins (John 21:15–19).

This restoration is at the heart of the message of the Bible. While God is perfectly holy and just, we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, often denying Him through our actions or our silence (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. Yet, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to earth. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming His authority as Lord of all.

Salvation is not earned by our own strength or good works, but is a gift received through grace alone, by faith alone, in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is secular humanism?

Secular humanism is a worldview that centers on human reason, ethics, and social justice while specifically rejecting religious dogma, supernaturalism, and the existence of God. It is a philosophy that suggests human beings are capable of being ethical and moral without the need for a divine being or sacred texts. Instead of looking to revelation from God, secular humanists rely on the scientific method, rationalism, and human experience to understand the world and solve problems.

From a biblical perspective, secular humanism represents a shift in authority. While secular humanists view man as the measure of all things, the Bible teaches that God is the Creator and the ultimate source of truth and morality (Genesis 1:1; Proverbs 1:7). There are three primary areas where secular humanism and Christianity differ significantly:

First, secular humanism relies on human autonomy, the idea that we are our own highest authority. The Bible, however, teaches that we are created by God and are accountable to Him. Romans 11:36 states, "For from him and through him and to him are all things. To him be glory forever. Amen."

Second, secular humanism typically views human nature as inherently good or as a blank slate that can be perfected through education and social progress. In contrast, the Scriptures teach that while every person is made in the image of God and has immense value (Genesis 1:27), we are also fallen and corrupted by sin. Romans 3:23 says, "for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." This means that human effort alone cannot fix the fundamental brokenness of the human heart.

Third, secular humanism seeks to find meaning and "salvation" through human achievement and the improvement of the earthly life. The Christian faith teaches that our greatest need is reconciliation with God, which we cannot achieve on our own. 

This leads to the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet He is also rich in mercy. Because of our sin, we are separated from Him, but God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfectly sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is not something we earn through human reasoning or good works, but it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting in a source of truth that is higher than human reason?
Does the Bible predict the destruction of Damascus?

The Bible does contain specific prophecies concerning the destruction of Damascus, which is one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world. The most prominent of these is found in the book of Isaiah.

In Isaiah 17:1, the prophet records a "burden" or oracle against the city: "Behold, Damascus will cease from being a city, and it will be a ruinous heap." This prophecy describes a total devastation where the city would no longer be inhabitable. Other prophets also spoke of judgment coming to Damascus. In Jeremiah 49:23–27, the prophet describes the city losing heart and being consumed by fire. Similarly, in Amos 1:3–5, God proclaims judgment on Damascus because of its cruelty to the people of Gilead, stating that He would break the gate bar of Damascus and send the people into captivity.

In a historical sense, Damascus suffered a major defeat by the Assyrians in 732 BC, which many scholars believe fulfilled significant portions of these prophecies. However, because Damascus has remained an inhabited city throughout most of history, some students of the Bible believe there may be a future fulfillment of the prophecy in Isaiah 17:1 where the city is completely destroyed.

These prophecies remind us that God is the sovereign Lord over all nations and that He is holy and just in His dealings with humanity. The judgment of nations points to a larger reality: all of us stand before a holy God. Scripture teaches that our own sins separate us from God and deserve His righteous judgment. Yet, because God is also merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him.

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life of obedience that we could never live. He died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, taking the wrath we deserved upon Himself. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. Now, salvation is not something we earn by our own works, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What is your current understanding of how a person can be right with God?
What does it mean to watch and pray?

The instruction to "watch and pray" is a vital call to spiritual vigilance and humble dependence on God. It is most famously spoken by Jesus to His disciples in the Garden of Gethsemane, just before His arrest, as recorded in Matthew 26:41 and Mark 14:38. To understand this command, it is helpful to look at both components.

Watching refers to spiritual alertness and sobriety. It means being awake to the realities of the spiritual world, the presence of temptation, and the schemes of the enemy. In 1 Peter 5:8, the Bible warns believers to be sober minded and watchful because the devil prowls around like a roaring lion, seeking someone to devour. To watch is to examine your own heart, to recognize your specific weaknesses, and to be aware of the worldly influences that might pull you away from God. It also carries the sense of looking forward with hope to the return of Jesus Christ, as seen in Luke 21:36.

Praying is the necessary companion to watching. While watching identifies the danger, praying secures the help. Jesus noted that "the spirit indeed is willing, but the flesh is weak" in Matthew 26:41. We may have the desire to do what is right, but in our own strength, we lack the power to resist sin. Prayer is the act of turning away from self-reliance and leaning entirely on God's grace and strength. It is an admission that we cannot walk the Christian life alone. Paul encourages this constant state of readiness in Colossians 4:2, telling believers to continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving.

Together, watching and praying help us to avoid "entering into temptation." It is a proactive stance. Rather than waiting for a crisis to occur, a believer who watches and prays is already fortified by the Holy Spirit.

This call to vigilance also reminds us of our deep need for a Savior. Because God is perfectly holy and just, and because we often fail to watch and pray as we should, we find ourselves separated from Him by our sin. However, Jesus Christ lived the perfectly watchful and prayerful life that we cannot. He never gave in to temptation. Out of His great love, He took the punishment for our sins by dying on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by how well we watch or how long we pray, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there anything specific about your own spiritual journey or your struggle with temptation that prompted this question?
Why did Judas betray Jesus?

The betrayal of Jesus by Judas Iscariot is one of the most sobering accounts in the Bible. While only God truly knows the depths of a person's heart, the Scriptures provide several interconnected reasons why this tragic event took place.

First, the Bible points to Judas's struggle with greed and materialism. As the treasurer for the disciples, Judas was known to steal from the money bag, as noted in John 12:6. His decision to go to the chief priests was motivated by a desire for financial gain, as he asked, "What are you willing to give me if I deliver Him to you?" and they settled on thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:14–15).

Second, there was a spiritual dimension to the betrayal. The Gospel of Luke tells us that "Satan entered Judas, surnamed Iscariot" (Luke 22:3), and John 13:27 records a similar moment during the Last Supper. This does not absolve Judas of his personal responsibility, but it shows the spiritual warfare surrounding the life and mission of Jesus.

Third, many scholars suggest that Judas may have been disillusioned with the nature of Jesus's kingdom. Like many of his contemporaries, Judas likely expected a political Messiah who would overthrow Roman rule and establish an earthly kingdom. When Jesus began to speak more clearly about His upcoming suffering and death, Judas may have felt that Jesus was not the kind of leader he wanted to follow.

Ultimately, however, the betrayal served the sovereign purpose of God. While Judas acted out of his own sinful desires, his actions were part of God’s plan for the salvation of the world. In the book of Acts, the apostle Peter explains that Jesus was "delivered by the determined purpose and foreknowledge of God" (Acts 2:23). This betrayal led directly to the cross, where the greatest evil ever committed was used by God to accomplish the greatest good.

The story of Judas reminds us of the profound holiness of God and the reality of human sin, which separates us from Him. We have all, in various ways, turned our backs on God's ways. Yet, the heart of the Christian message is that Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Because of His finished work, salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How is eternity in Hell a fair punishment for sin?

The question of how a finite life can result in an eternal consequence is a profound one that touches on the very nature of God and the gravity of sin. From a biblical perspective, the fairness of eternal punishment is understood through several key truths regarding who God is and what sin actually represents.

First, the weight of an offense is often measured by the dignity of the person who is offended. Because God is an infinitely holy, perfect, and eternal Being, a rebellion against Him carries an infinite weight. Scripture describes God as being so pure that He cannot look upon evil (Habakkuk 1:13), and His holiness is the standard by which all things are judged (Isaiah 6:3). When we sin, we are not just breaking a rule; we are rejecting the eternal Creator and Sustainer of our lives.

Second, the Bible presents hell as the ultimate result of a person’s settled choice to live apart from God. God is the source of all light, love, joy, and life. To spend eternity apart from Him is to be in a place where those things do not exist. In His respect for human will, God eventually grants the person what they have sought throughout their life, which is independence from His authority. As the apostle Paul writes, the wages of sin is death (Romans 6:23), which refers to both a physical and a spiritual separation from God.

Third, God’s justice and His mercy are both perfect. Because God is just, He cannot simply overlook sin, for that would be a compromise of His goodness (Psalm 89:14; Nahum 1:3). Yet, because He is merciful, He has provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him so that no one has to face this punishment.

This brings us to the heart of the Christian faith. While we have all sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23), God’s love moved Him to act on our behalf. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect, sinless life that we could not live. Jesus then willingly died on a cross, taking upon Himself the punishment that our sins deserved. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or good works; it is a gift secured by Christ's historical death and resurrection. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. God invites everyone to be reconciled to Him through the work of His Son.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the law of the excluded middle?

The law of the excluded middle is a fundamental principle of classical logic. It states that for any proposition or statement, that statement is either true or its negation is true. In other words, there is no third or middle possibility between being true and being false.

From a Christian perspective, we understand that logic is not an arbitrary human invention but a reflection of the character of God. God is the source of all truth, and He is perfectly consistent and non-contradictory. Scripture tells us that God cannot lie (Titus 1:2) and that He is not a God of confusion but of peace (1 Corinthians 14:33). Because God’s nature is the foundation of reality, the laws of logic, including the law of the excluded middle, help us to think clearly and rightly about the world He created.

Jesus Christ emphasized the importance of clear, truthful speech and the binary nature of truth when He taught that we should let our "Yes" be "Yes" and our "No" be "No" (Matthew 5:37). This logical principle reminds us that truth is objective rather than relative. Something cannot be both true and not true at the same time and in the same sense.

This principle also has significant implications for our spiritual lives. In the Bible, we often see clear distinctions that leave no middle ground: light and darkness, life and death, and being in Christ or being apart from Him. Jesus made a definitive claim about Himself when He said, "I am the way, the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). According to the law of the excluded middle, this statement is either true or it is false.

The core of the Christian message rests on these clear truths. The Bible teaches that all people have sinned and are separated from a holy God (Romans 3:23), and the consequence of that sin is death (Romans 6:23). However, God in His great love sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This is the Gospel: that salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or effort.

Because of what Jesus has done, there is a clear choice set before every person. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life (Acts 17:30). What is it that might be holding you back from trusting Him today?
How is Satan god of this world (2 Corinthians 4:4)?

In 2 Corinthians 4:4, the apostle Paul refers to Satan as the god of this world to describe the temporary and limited influence he exerts over the current fallen world system. This title does not suggest that Satan is equal to the one true God or that he possesses ultimate sovereignty. Instead, it highlights his role as the leader of the spiritual forces that oppose God's kingdom and deceive those who do not believe.

While God is the Creator and rightful owner of the entire universe (Psalm 24:1), He has, for a season, permitted Satan to exercise a level of dominion over the systems of this world, which includes its sinful philosophies, values, and lusts (1 John 5:19). Satan is also called the prince of the power of the air (Ephesians 2:2) and the ruler of this world (John 12:31). His primary goal, as Paul notes in 2 Corinthians 4:4, is to blind the minds of unbelievers so they cannot see the light of the Gospel of the glory of Christ.

This spiritual blindness is the result of human sin and the active deception of the enemy. Because God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3), our sin creates a separation between us and Him (Isaiah 59:2). We are born with a nature that is prone to follow the ways of this world rather than the ways of God (Romans 3:23). Satan uses this inclination to keep people trapped in darkness, away from the truth that could set them free.

However, the authority Satan holds is delegated and temporary. Through the incarnation, perfect life, and sacrificial death of Jesus Christ, the power of sin and death has been broken. Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, took the punishment we deserved by dying on a cross. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His total victory over the ruler of this world (Colossians 2:15; 1 Corinthians 15:3–4).

The Gospel is the good news that although we were once blinded and lost in our sins, we can be reconciled to God. This salvation is not something we earn by our own works, but it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9). When a person trusts in Christ, the veil of spiritual blindness is removed, and they are transferred from the kingdom of darkness into the kingdom of God's beloved Son (Colossians 1:13).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is faith intellectual assent?

In the Christian tradition, faith certainly includes intellectual assent, but it is much more than that. Intellectual assent is the act of agreeing that certain facts are true, such as acknowledging that God exists or that Jesus lived. However, Scripture makes it clear that simply agreeing with facts is not the same as saving faith. For example, James 2:19 explains that even the demons believe that God is one, and they shudder, yet they are not saved.

Saving faith is often described as having three essential parts. First is knowledge, which is understanding the message of the Gospel. Second is assent, which is the conviction that this message is true. Third, and most importantly, is trust or reliance. This third part, often called "fiducia" in Latin, is the act of the heart resting upon Jesus Christ alone for salvation. It is moving from believing *that* something is true to believing *in* a Person. Faith is a gift from God that involves the whole person, including the mind, the emotions, and the will, as seen in Ephesians 2:8, Ephesians 2:9, and Romans 10:9, Romans 10:10.

This distinction is vital because the heart of the Christian message is that we are reconciled to a holy God through the work of Jesus Christ. Because of our sin, we are separated from God, but God in His love sent His Son to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. Salvation is not earned by our good works or our perfect understanding, but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean to be saved by grace?

To be saved by grace means that your reconciliation with God is a free gift that you did not earn and do not deserve. Grace is often described as unmerited favor, it is God giving us the opposite of what our actions have earned.

The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because of sin, every human being is separated from God and is under His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). On our own, we are spiritually dead and unable to bridge the gap between ourselves and God through good works, moral living, or religious effort (Ephesians 2:1-3; Titus 3:5).

Because God is rich in mercy and love, He provided the only way for us to be saved. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we failed to live. Jesus then took the punishment for our sins by dying on the cross as our substitute (1 Peter 2:24; 2 Corinthians 5:21). His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death, proving that His sacrifice was sufficient to reconcile us to the Father (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

This salvation is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). It is not a reward for being "good enough," rather, it is a gift that God gives to those who stop trying to save themselves and instead place their total trust in Jesus. When a person believes in Him, they are justified, meaning God declares them righteous because of what Christ has done (Romans 5:1).

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is epilepsy mentioned in the Bible?

While the modern word "epilepsy" is not found in older English translations like the King James Version, the Bible describes conditions that align with what we now recognize as seizures.

In the Gospels, there are accounts of individuals experiencing symptoms such as sudden falls, foaming at the mouth, and muscle rigidity. A specific example is found in Matthew 17:14–20, Mark 9:14–29, and Luke 9:37–43, where a father brings his son to Jesus for healing. The father describes the boy as suffering greatly from seizures. The Greek word used in Matthew 17:15 is "seleniazomai," which historically meant "moonstruck," but it referred to the symptoms of epilepsy.

The Bible makes a distinction between different types of suffering. For instance, Matthew 4:24 lists those who were ill, those in severe pain, the demon-possessed, those having seizures, and the paralyzed. This suggests that the biblical writers understood that physical ailments, while sometimes having spiritual components, were also a distinct part of human suffering.

Seeing these accounts of Jesus healing the sick reminds us that God is not indifferent to our pain. He is a holy and sovereign Creator who is also a merciful Father. Though we all face the reality of sin and its effects on our bodies, God has provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him and to have hope beyond physical suffering. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from a holy God, but Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's work alone, received by grace through faith in Him, not by our own works or merit. You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you or someone you know is experiencing seizures or medical concerns, please seek immediate help from a qualified medical professional. While we believe in the power of prayer and God's ability to heal, it is important to honor the wisdom and care provided through healthcare services. You may also find it helpful to speak with a local pastor or a certified biblical counselor for spiritual support and guidance.

For further reading on how Jesus interacted with those who were suffering, you can look at Matthew 8:14–17 and Luke 4:40.
What is the kalam cosmological argument for the existence of God?

The Kalam Cosmological Argument is a philosophical argument for the existence of God that traces its roots back to early Christian and later medieval Islamic thinkers. It is a simple yet powerful logical structure intended to show that the universe must have a beginning and that this beginning requires a Cause.

The argument is typically presented in three steps:

1. Whatever begins to exist has a cause.
2. The universe began to exist.
3. Therefore, the universe has a cause.

The first premise rests on the principle of causality, suggesting that things do not simply pop into existence from nothing without a reason or a source. The second premise is supported by both philosophical reasoning, such as the impossibility of an actual infinite number of past events, and scientific evidence, such as the expansion of the universe and the laws of thermodynamics.

Scripture affirms this truth in the very first verse of the Bible, stating that "In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth" (Genesis 1:1). It also reminds us that "By faith we understand that the universe was created by the word of God, so that what is seen was not made out of things that are visible" (Hebrews 11:3).

When we analyze the nature of this "Cause," logic suggests it must be uncaused, timeless, spaceless, immaterial, and enormously powerful. For Christians, this cause is not an abstract force but a personal God. The Bible reveals that this Creator is Jesus Christ, for "by him all things were created, in heaven and on earth, visible and invisible, whether thrones or dominions or rulers or authorities; all things were created through him and for him" (Colossians 1:16).

While the Kalam argument points us to a Creator, the Bible tells us the full story of who this Creator is and why He made us. God is holy and perfect, yet we have all sinned and separated ourselves from Him through our disobedience. Because God is just, our sin deserves judgment, but because He is merciful, He stepped into His own creation in the person of Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord.

Salvation is not something we can earn by being good or through philosophical study. It is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who was Dan in the Bible?

Dan was the fifth son of Jacob and the first son of Bilhah, who was the servant of Jacob’s wife, Rachel. His name is significant because it means "judge" or "vindication." When he was born, Rachel celebrated God’s intervention in her life, saying, "God has judged me, and has also heard my voice and given me a son," as recorded in Genesis 30:6.

As the founder of one of the twelve tribes of Israel, Dan’s descendants have a complex history in the Bible. When Jacob blessed his sons before his death, he said of Dan, "Dan shall judge his people as one of the tribes of Israel. Dan shall be a serpent by the way, a viper by the path, that bites the horse's heels so that its rider shall fall backward," in Genesis 49:16–17. This prophecy suggested that the tribe would provide leadership but also perhaps be a source of trouble or craftiness.

The most famous individual from the tribe of Dan was Samson. He was a judge who possessed supernatural strength and was called by God to begin the deliverance of Israel from the Philistines, as described in Judges 13:2–5. Samson’s life, however, also reflected the spiritual struggles of his tribe, as he often grappled with personal sin and temptation.

The tribe of Dan eventually faced significant challenges. They were originally assigned territory along the Mediterranean coast, but they struggled to secure it from the Philistines. This led a portion of the tribe to migrate north, where they captured the city of Laish and renamed it Dan, according to Judges 18:27–29. Unfortunately, this migration was marked by the establishment of a private priesthood and the worship of carved images in Judges 18:30–31. Later, after the kingdom of Israel split, King Jeroboam set up one of his two golden calves in the city of Dan to prevent the people from traveling to Jerusalem to worship, as seen in 1 Kings 12:28–30.

The history of Dan serves as a sober reminder of how easily we can drift from the worship of the true God into the worship of things we create. Scripture teaches that God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him. Yet, God in His mercy provided a way for us to be reconciled. Jesus Christ, who is God in the flesh, lived the perfect life we could not live and died on the cross as a substitute for our sins. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as the true Judge and Savior.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What do you find most interesting about the way God works through imperfect people like those in the tribe of Dan?
What is Jacob’s Ladder?

Jacob's ladder refers to a significant vision given by God to the patriarch Jacob, which is recorded in the book of Genesis. This event occurred while Jacob was traveling from Beersheba toward Haran, fleeing from the anger of his brother, Esau.

According to the account in Genesis 28:10–12, Jacob stopped for the night and used a stone as a pillow. While he slept, he had a dream in which he saw a stairway, often translated as a ladder, resting on the earth with its top reaching to heaven. He saw the angels of God ascending and descending on it. In the vision, the Lord stood above the ladder and reaffirmed the covenant promises He had made to Jacob's grandfather Abraham and his father Isaac. God promised to give Jacob and his descendants the land on which he was lying and declared that through Jacob's offspring, all the families of the earth would be blessed (Genesis 28:13–15).

When Jacob awoke, he was filled with awe, realizing that the Lord was in that place. He named the location Bethel, which means "House of God," and he set up the stone he had used as a pillow as a pillar to mark the site (Genesis 28:16–19).

Theologically, the ladder represents the bridge between heaven and earth, showing that God is not distant or uninvolved but is actively present in the lives of His people. In the New Testament, Jesus Christ reveals that He is the ultimate fulfillment of this imagery. In the Gospel of John, Jesus tells His disciple Nathanael that he would see heaven open and the angels of God ascending and descending on the Son of Man (John 1:51).

This points to a vital truth: because of our sin, we are naturally separated from a holy and perfect God. No amount of human effort or "climbing" can bridge that gap. Jesus, being fully God and fully man, is the only mediator who provides the way for us to be reconciled to the Father (1 Timothy 2:5). Through His perfect life, His death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, He opened the way for us to have eternal life and a relationship with God.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why is the virgin birth so important?

The virgin birth is a foundational truth of the Christian faith because it explains how Jesus Christ is both fully God and fully man. This miraculous event, often called the Incarnation, is the moment God the Son took on human nature to rescue us from sin.

One primary reason the virgin birth is essential is that it affirms the deity of Jesus. If Jesus had been conceived by two human parents, He would be a human being like any other. However, the Bible teaches that He was conceived by the Holy Spirit (Matthew 1:18; Luke 1:35). This means that while He is truly man, having been born of Mary, He is also truly God. This unique nature, often described as Vera Deus, vera homo (truly God, truly man), is necessary because only someone who is fully God could bear the infinite weight of God's justice against sin, and only someone who is fully man could represent humanity on the cross.

Another vital reason involves the sinlessness of Christ. Because of the fall of Adam, every human being is born with a sinful nature and is separated from God (Romans 5:12). For Jesus to be our Savior, He had to be the "lamb without blemish" (1 Peter 1:19). The virgin birth allowed Jesus to enter the world as a second Adam, perfectly human yet without the inherited corruption of sin that affects all other descendants of Adam (Hebrews 4:15; 2 Corinthians 5:21). Without the virgin birth, Jesus would have been a sinner in need of a savior Himself, rather than the sinless Savior the world requires.

Furthermore, the virgin birth demonstrates God's faithfulness to His Word. Centuries before Jesus was born, the prophet Isaiah declared, "Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Behold, the virgin shall conceive and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel" (Isaiah 7:14). Matthew's Gospel explicitly points to this prophecy to show that Jesus is the promised Messiah who has come to save His people from their sins (Matthew 1:21-23).

Ultimately, the virgin birth teaches us that salvation is entirely a work of God. It was not accomplished by human effort or natural means, but by God’s sovereign grace reaching down to us. 

This beautiful truth leads us to the heart of the Gospel. God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His judgment. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who was conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary. He lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord of all.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can I overcome disappointment with life?

I am so sorry to hear that you are walking through a season of deep disappointment. It is a heavy burden to carry when life does not look the way you hoped it would, or when your heart feels the weight of unmet expectations and broken dreams. Please know that your feelings are seen by God, and you do not have to navigate this journey alone.

Disappointment often stems from the reality that we live in a fallen world where things are not yet as they should be. The Bible acknowledges this groaning of creation in Romans 8:20 through Romans 8:22. Even the most faithful servants of God experienced profound disappointment. Job lost everything, David was betrayed by his own son, and the disciples felt the crushing weight of despair when Jesus was crucified.

To begin overcoming this disappointment, it is helpful to start with honest lament. God invites you to pour out your heart to Him, even the messy and painful parts. In Psalm 62:8, we are encouraged to trust in Him at all times and pour out our hearts before Him, for God is a refuge for us. You do not need to hide your frustration or sadness from Him; He already knows, and He cares for you deeply.

As you bring your pain to Him, try to shift your focus from your circumstances to the character of God. Our circumstances change, but He remains the same. Hebrews 13:8 reminds us that Jesus Christ is the same yesterday and today and forever. When life feels unstable, we can anchor ourselves in His sovereignty, His goodness, and His wisdom. Isaiah 55:8 through Isaiah 55:9 tells us that His thoughts and ways are higher than ours. While we may not understand why certain things have happened, we can trust the heart of the One who holds our future.

The ultimate answer to the disappointments of this life is found in the person and work of Jesus Christ. We are all separated from God by our sin, which is the root of all the brokenness and disappointment we see in the world. But God, in His holiness and great love, sent His Son to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He has defeated sin and death, offering us a hope that is not tied to our earthly success or comfort.

Salvation is a gift of grace alone, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own. When we place our trust in Jesus, we are given a new identity and a future hope that cannot be taken away. This does not mean our earthly disappointments disappear, but it means they no longer have the final word. Romans 8:28 promises that for those who love God, all things work together for good, for those who are called according to His purpose. God is able to take the very things that cause us pain and use them to refine our character and draw us closer to Him.

I encourage you to lean into a local church community and talk with a pastor or a mature believer. We were never meant to carry our sorrows in isolation. Galatians 6:2 tells us to bear one another's burdens. Surrounding yourself with the Word of God and the fellowship of other believers can provide the strength you need when your own feels depleted.

You can turn to God right now. Repent of any sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to find the forgiveness and peace that only He can provide. What do you feel is the biggest hurdle in trusting God with your disappointment today?
Who was Mary of Bethany in the Bible?

Mary of Bethany was a devoted follower of Jesus and the sister of Martha and Lazarus. They lived in the village of Bethany, located about two miles from Jerusalem. Mary is featured in three significant accounts in the Gospels, each of which reveals her deep love for the Lord and her posture of worship.

In the first account, found in Luke 10:38–42, Mary is shown sitting at Jesus' feet, listening to His teaching. While her sister Martha was distracted by many preparations and complained that Mary was not helping, Jesus defended Mary. He stated that she had chosen the "good portion," which would not be taken away from her. This highlights her priority of spiritual devotion over the busyness of life.

The second account appears in John 11:1–44, surrounding the death of her brother, Lazarus. When Jesus arrived in Bethany four days after the burial, Mary fell at His feet in grief.
