Unlike the rest of creation, humans were created in the image of God (Genesis 1:26; Genesis 1:27). This means we are moral agents with the capacity to understand right from wrong and the responsibility to act according to God’s character. While a computer or a thunderstorm is amoral, the choices people make usually carry moral weight because they reflect our relationship with God and our neighbors (Matthew 22:37-40).

Even in areas of "Christian liberty," where the Bible does not give a specific command, our hearts and motives are still under God's scrutiny. The Apostle Paul noted that while many things are "lawful" or permissible, not everything is helpful or builds others up (1 Corinthians 10:23-24). This teaches us that even when dealing with things that might seem amoral or neutral, the way we use them reveals our spiritual condition.

Because we are moral beings, we find ourselves accountable to a holy and just God. The Bible teaches that while God is perfectly good, every human being has failed to live up to His perfect moral standard (Romans 3:23). This separation from God because of sin is a burden we cannot resolve on our own through good works or moral effort. 

The heart of the Gospel is that God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge this gap. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Him alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What thoughts or questions does this raise for you as you consider how God views our choices?
What does it mean to put off the old man (Ephesians 4:22)?

In Ephesians 4:22, the Apostle Paul uses the imagery of changing clothes to describe a profound spiritual transformation in the life of a believer. To put off the old man, or the old self, means to renounce and turn away from the sinful nature and the way of life that characterized us before we knew Jesus Christ.

The Bible teaches that the old man is our former self, which was dominated by sinful desires and was spiritually dead because of our rebellion against God (Ephesians 2:1; Ephesians 4:22). This old nature is described as being corrupt and led astray by deceitful lusts. To put it off is an intentional act of the will, empowered by the Holy Spirit, where a person decides to no longer live according to those old patterns, habits, and attitudes. 

This process is not about self improvement or simply trying harder to be a good person. It is rooted in what Jesus Christ has already accomplished. Scripture tells us that our old self was crucified with Christ so that the body ruled by sin might be done away with, that we should no longer be slaves to sin (Romans 6:6). Because Jesus died for our sins and rose again, believers have the spiritual power to reject the old way of life.

Putting off the old man is closely connected to two other actions mentioned in the following verses. First, there is the renewal of the mind (Ephesians 4:23), where the Holy Spirit changes how we think through the Word of God. Second, there is the putting on of the new man (Ephesians 4:24), which is created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness. Like taking off a set of filthy, tattered rags and putting on clean, royal garments, the Christian life involves a daily rhythm of turning away from sin and turning toward Christ.

This transformation begins with understanding the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, but every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from God and deserves His judgment. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3, 4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8, 9).

If you have not yet experienced this new life, you can turn to God now. Repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and a restored relationship with God. Have you ever considered what it means to fully trust Jesus with your life?

For those who are in Christ, putting off the old man is part of progressive sanctification. It involves identifying specific sins, such as anger, falsehood, or greed, and choosing to replace them with the fruit of the Spirit, such as love, truth, and generosity (Ephesians 4:25, 32). This is a lifelong journey of becoming more like Jesus as we depend on His grace.

If you are struggling to put off a particular habit or sin, remember that you are not alone. God provides His Word, His Spirit, and the fellowship of a local church to help you grow. It may be helpful to speak with a pastor or a mature believer who can walk alongside you in this process (Hebrews 10:24, 25).
What is a freethinker?

A freethinker is generally defined as a person who forms their opinions and beliefs based on reason, logic, and empirical evidence rather than on tradition, authority, or religious dogma. The term became prominent during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries, often describing individuals who rejected the idea of divine revelation and sought to understand the world through a purely secular or scientific lens. In many contexts today, the label is associated with atheism, agnosticism, or secular humanism.

From a biblical perspective, we recognize that God is the author of reason and that He invites us to use our minds to seek Him. However, the Christian view of the mind differs from the secular concept of freethought in a few significant ways.

First, we believe that human reason, while a gift from God, is finite and has been affected by our fallen nature. Scripture suggests that without the guidance of God's Spirit, our understanding can become darkened or self-centered. In Colossians 2:8, the Bible warns, "See to it that no one takes you captive by philosophy and empty deceit, according to human tradition, according to the elemental spirits of the world, and not according to Christ." This suggests that "free" thought is not always as objective as it claims, because it is often shaped by the cultural and personal biases of a world separated from its Creator.

Second, the Bible teaches that true wisdom begins with a relationship with God. Proverbs 1:7 says, "The fear of the Lord is the beginning of knowledge; fools despise wisdom and instruction." For the believer, thinking is not truly free when it is disconnected from the Truth. We believe that since God created all things, all truth is ultimately His truth. Therefore, using our reason to explore the world is an act of worship when it is done in submission to Him.

Ultimately, many people identify as freethinkers because they are searching for truth and autonomy. However, the Bible reveals that our greatest problem is not a lack of information, but a separation from God caused by sin. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our rebellion cuts us off from the source of life and truth. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His resurrection, He proved His authority over death and sin.

True freedom is found not in absolute intellectual independence, but in being reconciled to the One who made us. Jesus said in John 8:31-32, "If you abide in my word, you are truly my disciples, and you will know the truth, and the truth will set you free." This freedom is a gift of grace that we receive through faith in Christ alone, not by our own intellectual achievements or good works.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and a new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is an overseer in the church?

In the New Testament, the term "overseer" (from the Greek word *episkopos*) refers to a specific office of leadership within the local church. While various traditions may use different titles today, the Bible uses the terms "overseer," "elder," and "pastor" interchangeably to describe the same role. For instance, in Acts 20:17 and Acts 20:28, the apostle Paul calls for the "elders" of the church in Ephesus and tells them that the Holy Spirit has made them "overseers" to "shepherd the church of God."

The primary responsibility of an overseer is to provide spiritual leadership, protection, and care for the congregation. This involves teaching the Word of God, presiding over the affairs of the church, and guarding the flock against false doctrines (Titus 1:9; 1 Timothy 5:17). Because this role carries great spiritual weight, the Bible sets forth high character qualifications. An overseer must be "above reproach," "the husband of one wife," "sober-minded," "able to teach," and one who "manages his own household well" (1 Timothy 3:1–7; Titus 1:5–9).

Scripture generally describes a plurality of overseers or elders leading a local church together, rather than a single individual holding all authority (Philippians 1:1; Acts 14:23). Their authority is not for personal gain or to lord it over others, but for the service and building up of the believers, following the humble example of Jesus Christ (1 Peter 5:1–3).

Ultimately, every earthly overseer serves under Jesus Christ, who is the "Shepherd and Overseer of your souls" (1 Peter 2:25). It is only through Christ's perfect life, His substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection on the third day that we can be reconciled to God. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own works. He is the Chief Shepherd who will return personally to judge the world and reward those who have trusted in Him (1 Peter 5:4).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life under His care. What has been your experience with leadership in the church, and does this biblical description clarify things for you?
What is the Messianic secret?

The Messianic secret is a theme found primarily in the Gospel of Mark where Jesus frequently commands his followers, the people he heals, and even demons to remain silent about his identity as the Messiah or the Son of God. While Jesus performed many miracles and taught with divine authority, he often insisted that these acts not be publicized.

There are several biblical and theological reasons why Jesus maintained this veil of secrecy during his earthly ministry. First, Jesus sought to avoid being misunderstood as a political or military liberator. Many in Israel at that time expected a Messiah who would overthrow the Roman occupation by force. By quieting reports of his power, Jesus ensured that his mission was not hijacked by those seeking an earthly kingdom rather than a spiritual one (John 6:15).

Second, the timing of his ministry was governed by a divine schedule. Jesus often spoke of his "hour" not yet being come (John 2:4; John 7:6). A premature public fervor could have led to an early confrontation with authorities before he had finished training his disciples and fulfilling his teaching ministry.

Most importantly, the full identity of Jesus as the Messiah cannot be rightly understood apart from the cross. Jesus knew that people would be tempted to follow him for the miracles alone without grasping his primary mission: to suffer, die, and rise again for the forgiveness of sins. In Mark 9:9, Jesus explicitly commanded his disciples not to tell anyone what they had seen on the Mount of Transfiguration until the Son of Man had risen from the dead. It was only after the resurrection that the "secret" was to be shouted from the rooftops, as the work of redemption was complete.

The identity of Jesus is no longer a secret today. The Bible reveals that God is holy and just, yet humanity is separated from him by sin and deserving of judgment. In his great love, God the Father sent his Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, he rose bodily from the grave, proving his victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Because of what Jesus has done, salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by any works we can perform. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Someone gave me a prophetic word/prophesied over me. What is the interpretation?

It is understandable to seek clarity when you receive a message that is presented as a word from God. In the Christian life, our greatest desire is to hear from our Creator and follow His will. When someone speaks a word over you, it is important to approach it with a heart of discernment, grounded in the truth that God has already spoken clearly and sufficiently through His Word, the Bible.

The first and most vital step is to compare what was said with the Holy Scriptures. The Bible is our final authority for faith and practice, and God will never contradict what He has already revealed in His Word (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Timothy 3:17). If the word you received encourages something that the Bible forbids, or if it distracts you from the central person and work of Jesus Christ, you should set it aside. Like the Bereans in the book of Acts, we are called to examine the Scriptures daily to see if these things are so (Acts 17:11).

In the New Testament, believers are told not to despise prophecies, but to test everything and hold fast to what is good (1 Thessalonians 5:19; 1 Thessalonians 5:20; 1 Thessalonians 5:21; 1 Thessalonians 5:22). This testing is not done in isolation. God has placed us within a community of believers for our protection and growth. I encourage you to take this word to the elders or pastors of your local church. They are called to watch over your soul and can provide biblical wisdom and perspective (Hebrews 13:17).

If you are feeling uncertain or anxious about your future or God's will, remember that the most important "word" God has spoken to humanity is His Son, Jesus Christ (Hebrews 1:1; Hebrews 1:2). We often look for specific directions for our lives, but God's primary will for every person is that they would know Him through the Gospel.

God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from Him. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on a cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). Through His sacrifice, He paid the debt for our sins and defeated death. Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone, not by our own works or efforts (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you seek interpretation for the word you received, pray for wisdom, for God promises to give it generously to those who ask (James 1:5). Rest in the fact that the Holy Spirit dwells within those who trust in Christ, guiding them into all truth and reminding them of what Jesus has taught (John 14:26).

What specifically was shared with you, and does it seem to align with the character of God as described in the Bible? If you are comfortable sharing more, I can help you look at biblical principles that might apply. Please remember that I do not provide professional medical, legal, or psychiatric advice, and it is always wise to consult with your local church leadership for ongoing spiritual guidance.
What is the harmony of the Gospels?

The harmony of the Gospels refers to the study and arrangement of the four biblical accounts of Jesus' life, Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John, to demonstrate their internal consistency and to construct a unified narrative of His earthly ministry. Because each Gospel writer wrote to a different audience and with a specific theological purpose, they often emphasize different details, chronological sequences, or themes. A "harmony" seeks to show that these variations are not contradictions but are instead complementary perspectives that provide a fuller picture of who Jesus is and what He accomplished.

The concept of harmony is rooted in the belief that the Bible is the inspired, inerrant, and infallible Word of God, as stated in 2 Timothy 3:16. While Matthew, Mark, and Luke are known as the Synoptic Gospels because they share a similar "common view" of Christ's life, the Gospel of John often provides a more profound theological reflection on Christ's deity and unique discourses not found in the others. When these four accounts are read together, they provide a reliable and multifaceted testimony to the historical reality of Jesus Christ.

The primary focus of all four Gospels is the person and work of Jesus. They collectively reveal that God is holy and supreme, yet humanity is separated from Him by sin. In His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, as seen in John 1:1 and John 1:14. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, fulfilling the law that we could not keep. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

This message is the heart of the "Good News" or Gospel. Salvation is not something we earn by our own works or moral efforts, but is a gift secured by Christ alone. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Him. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and eternal life.

What has been your experience with reading the different accounts of Jesus' life, and is there anything in His story that you find difficult to reconcile?
What is/isn’t a Christian married couple allowed to do, sexually?

God’s design for the sexual relationship between a husband and a wife is one of freedom, intimacy, and joy within the boundaries of a lifelong covenant. The Bible teaches that marriage is a holy union, and the physical expression of that union is a gift to be celebrated. In Hebrews 13:4, we are told, "Let marriage be held in honor among all, and let the marriage bed be undefiled, for God will judge the sexually immoral and adulterous." This indicates that within the safety of the marriage covenant, there is a great deal of liberty.

The primary biblical principles governing what a couple may do sexually are mutual consent, the law of love, and the exclusion of outside parties.

1. Mutual Consent and Authority
In 1 Corinthians 7:3–5, the apostle Paul explains that a husband and wife have a mutual responsibility to fulfill one another’s needs. Each spouse has authority over the other’s body, but this authority is never to be used for selfish or forceful demands. Instead, it is an invitation to serve one another in love. If a particular act makes one spouse feel uncomfortable, degraded, or violated, it should be avoided. Love "does not insist on its own way," according to 1 Corinthians 13:5.

2. The Conscience
Scripture emphasizes that we should not violate our own consciences or the consciences of others. Romans 14:23 notes that "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin." If a couple is considering a specific practice and one or both spouses feel it is wrong or cannot do it with a clear conscience before God, they should refrain.

3. Purity and Exclusivity
The marriage bed is "undefiled" when it remains exclusive to the two people in the covenant. Anything that introduces a third party, whether physically or through media like pornography, is a violation of the "one flesh" union. Jesus warned that "everyone who looks at a woman with lustful intent has already committed adultery with her in his heart" in Matthew 5:28. The sexual relationship is meant to be a private and sacred communion between husband and wife alone.

4. Honor and Holiness
Couples are encouraged to treat one another with dignity. 1 Thessalonians 4:3–5 says, "For this is the will of God, your sanctification: that you abstain from sexual immorality; that each one of you know how to control his own body in holiness and honor, not in the passion of lust like the Gentiles who do not know God." This suggests that sexual expressions should be life-giving and honor the image of God in one's spouse, rather than being dehumanizing or purely self-gratifying.

Ultimately, marriage is intended to be a beautiful picture of the relationship between Jesus Christ and His Church, as described in Ephesians 5:31–32. Just as Christ gave Himself up for the Church out of sacrificial love, a husband and wife are called to love one another in a way that reflects God’s holiness and grace.

This design for marriage and intimacy reminds us of God's greater plan for our lives. We have all fallen short of His perfect standard, but God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die for our sins, and rise again to reconcile us to Himself. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why is God going to give us a white stone with a new name (Revelation 2:17)?

The promise of the white stone in Revelation 2:17 is a beautiful image of the intimacy, acceptance, and victory that believers have in Jesus Christ. In the ancient world, white stones carried several meanings that help us understand what God is communicating to His people.

One common use of a white stone was in the legal system. When a jury voted, a white stone represented acquittal or "not guilty," while a black stone represented condemnation. For the believer, this stone signifies that through the sacrifice of Jesus, we are fully pardoned. Because Christ took our punishment on the cross, God declares us righteous in His sight. As Romans 8:1 says, there is therefore now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus.

Another meaning relates to hospitality and celebration. A white stone often served as an admission ticket to a special banquet or a public festival. By giving us this stone, God is inviting us into the eternal wedding supper of the Lamb, which is described in Revelation 19:9. It shows that we are welcome in His presence and have a place at His table forever.

The "new name" written on the stone represents a deeply personal and transformed identity. In Scripture, when God changed someone's name, such as Abram to Abraham or Simon to Peter, it signified a new purpose and a new relationship with Him. This name is known only to the one who receives it, which points to the unique, intimate bond between the Savior and the redeemed soul. It reminds us that we are not just faces in a crowd, but individuals whom God knows and loves by name.

This promise is given to "the one who conquers," which refers to those who remain faithful to Christ. We do not conquer by our own strength, but by trusting in the one who conquered sin and death for us. Jesus is the true bread of life, the "hidden manna" mentioned in the same verse, who sustains us through every trial (John 6:35; Revelation 2:17).

The Gospel is the foundation of this hope. We are all sinners who have fallen short of God's holiness and deserve judgment. However, God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die in our place for our sins, and rise again on the third day. Through His resurrection, He has secured victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone, not by any works we can do.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the meaning of the strange fire in Leviticus 10:1?

The account of the strange fire in Leviticus 10:1 is a sobering reminder of the holiness of God and the importance of approaching Him with the reverence He deserves. Nadab and Abihu, the sons of Aaron, were priests who had been given the high privilege of serving in the presence of the Lord. However, the Bible records that they offered unauthorized fire before the Lord, which He had not commanded them (Leviticus 10:1).

The meaning of this strange fire centers on the concept of holiness and obedience. Just before this event, God had miraculously ignited the fire on the altar of burnt offering, showing His acceptance of the priesthood (Leviticus 9:24). The priests were commanded to keep this specific fire burning and to use only coals from this divinely lit source when offering incense. By bringing strange fire, Nadab and Abihu likely used common fire from a different source, or perhaps they attempted to offer incense at a time or in a manner that God had not authorized. 

At its core, their sin was one of presumption and disobedience. They treated the worship of the Holy God as something they could modify according to their own will. Moses explained the tragedy by quoting the Lord, who said that among those who approach Him, He must be proved holy, and in the sight of all the people, He must be honored (Leviticus 10:3). This event teaches us that God is not a peer to be trifled with, but a sovereign Creator who defines the terms of how we relate to Him.

This passage points us toward the profound reality of God’s absolute holiness and our own human fallenness. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin separates us from Him, and we cannot bridge that gap through our own inventions, rituals, or "strange fires" of self-righteousness. This is why the Gospel of Jesus Christ is so vital. 

God is holy, supreme, and just, and His wrath against sin is real. Yet, because He is also gracious and loving, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be the perfect mediator we could never be. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. We are not saved by our own religious efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

As you reflect on the holiness of God, I encourage you to turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

In our daily walk, this account encourages us to worship God with thankfulness and awe, for our God is a consuming fire (Hebrews 12:28-29). It reminds us to rest in the finished work of Christ, the only "authorized" way to the Father (John 14:6; 1 Timothy 2:5). For those who are in Christ, we can approach the throne of grace with confidence, not because of our own merits, but because of His (Hebrews 4:16).
Is it possible to sell your soul to the devil?

The idea of selling one's soul to the devil is a common theme in literature and folklore, but it is not a concept found in the Bible in the way it is often portrayed in movies or stories. From a biblical perspective, you do not truly own your soul in a way that allows you to sell it to a third party.

The Scriptures teach that God is the Creator and the ultimate owner of every human soul. In Ezekiel 18:4, God says, "Behold, all souls are mine; the soul of the father as well as the soul of the son is mine." Because God is the sovereign Creator, Satan does not have the authority or the legal right to "purchase" what already belongs to God. Psalm 24:1 reinforces this by stating, "The earth is the LORD'S and the fullness thereof, the world and those who dwell therein."

While a person cannot make a literal legal contract to sell their soul, the Bible does describe people who give themselves over to evil. For example, 1 Kings 21:20 mentions that King Ahab "sold himself to do what was evil in the sight of the LORD." This does not mean a transaction took place with the devil, but rather that Ahab chose to devote his life and actions entirely to sin and rebellion against God.

The reality for every person is that, apart from Jesus Christ, we are already spiritually separated from God because of sin. Ephesians 2:1-3 describes all people before they know Christ as being "dead in the trespasses and sins" and following "the prince of the power of the air," which refers to the devil. We do not need to sign a contract to be lost; we are born into a state of sin that separates us from our Creator.

The good news of the Christian faith is that Jesus Christ came to redeem, or "buy back," those who are lost in sin. 1 Corinthians 6:20 tells believers, "For you were bought with a price. So glorify God in your body." That price was the precious blood of Jesus.

God is holy and just, and our sin creates a debt we cannot pay. Because of His great love, God the Son became a man, lived a perfect life, and died on the cross as a substitute for sinners. He took the punishment we deserved, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. This is the Gospel: that salvation is a free gift of grace, received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works or bargains we make.

No matter what a person has done or how far they feel they have wandered toward darkness, they are never beyond the reach of God's mercy. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is it that makes you ask about the possibility of selling your soul? Please know that in Christ, there is total freedom and a hope that no power of darkness can overcome. John 8:36 says, "So if the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed."
What does the Bible say about obedience?

In the Bible, obedience is presented as the natural and necessary response of a person who has experienced the love and grace of God. Rather than being a heavy burden or a way to earn God’s favor, true obedience is a fruit of faith and a reflection of our love for Him.

The foundation of biblical obedience is love. Jesus made this clear when He told His disciples, "If you love me, you will keep my commandments" (John 14:15). This means that we do not obey God to get Him to love us, but we obey Him because He first loved us (1 John 4:19). God is our holy and sovereign Creator, and His commands are given for our good and His glory (Deuteronomy 10:12-13).

The Scriptures also emphasize that God values the heart behind our actions more than outward religious rituals. When King Saul disobeyed God, the prophet Samuel told him, "Has the Lord as great delight in burnt offerings and sacrifices, as in obeying the voice of the Lord? Behold, to obey is better than sacrifice, and to listen than the fat of rams" (1 Samuel 15:22). God desires a heart that is surrendered and attentive to His Word.

Jesus Christ serves as our ultimate example of obedience. Though He is God, He took on human nature and lived a life of perfect submission to the Father. He was "obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross" (Philippians 2:8). Because of His perfect obedience, we are shown the path of trusting God even in the most difficult circumstances.

Beyond our relationship with God, the Bible calls us to obey earthly authorities, such as parents (Ephesians 6:1), employers (Colossians 3:22), and government leaders (Romans 13:1), seeing this as a way to honor God. However, if an earthly authority commands us to do something that contradicts God’s Word, the Bible teaches that "we must obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29).

While we strive to obey, the Bible is also honest about our inability to do so perfectly. God is perfectly holy, and His standard for obedience is perfection (Matthew 5:48). Because of sin, every human being falls short of this standard and is separated from God (Romans 3:23). This is why the Gospel is such good news. Jesus Christ lived the life of perfect obedience that we could not live and died as a substitute for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. Salvation and a right relationship with God are not earned by our own works of obedience, but are received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is your understanding of the relationship between faith and doing what God asks?
What does it mean that God is our refuge and strength and an ever-present help in trouble (Psalm 46:1)?

To say that God is our refuge and strength means that He is our ultimate source of safety and power when life feels overwhelming. This verse from the Psalms provides a beautiful picture of God's character and His posture toward His people in times of distress.

When the Bible calls God a refuge, it uses language that suggests a place of shelter or protection from a storm or an enemy. In the ancient world, a refuge was often a high tower or a fortified city where people could flee for safety. Finding refuge in God means that we can bring our fears and anxieties to Him, trusting in His sovereign care and the security of His promises (Proverbs 18:10; Psalm 91:1-2).

Calling God our strength acknowledges that we are often weak and unable to face our challenges on our own. God does not simply give us a little bit of help; He becomes the source of our endurance and capacity. His power is made perfect in our weakness, enabling us to persevere through trials that would otherwise be crushing (Isaiah 40:29; 2 Corinthians 12:9).

The description of God as an ever-present help means He is intimately near and readily accessible. He is not a distant deity who must be summoned from afar, but a Father who is already with us in the midst of our pain. The original Hebrew suggests He is "abundantly found" or "well-proved" in times of narrowness and distress. We can approach Him with confidence, knowing He is present to provide grace and mercy exactly when we need it (Psalm 145:18; Hebrews 4:14-16).

While God provides refuge in earthly troubles, the greatest refuge He offers is from the consequence of our own sin. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to be our ultimate refuge. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. This salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or merit of our own.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life.

What specific trouble are you facing today that makes you feel the need for His refuge?
Should a Christian woman wear a bikini?

The question of what a Christian woman should wear, including a bikini, falls into an area where believers often seek to balance Christian liberty with the biblical principles of modesty and love for others. Because the Bible does not give specific rules for modern swimwear, this is a matter of prayerful discernment, heart motivation, and the application of scriptural principles.

The starting point for any discussion on clothing is the heart. In 1 Timothy 2:9-10, the apostle Paul encourages women to adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self control, not with braided hair and gold or pearls or costly attire, but with what is proper for women who profess godliness, with good works. This suggests that modesty is not simply about the amount of skin covered, but about a heart that seeks to reflect the glory of God rather than drawing undue attention to oneself or one's wealth and status.

Another important principle is the stewardship of the body. Scripture teaches that your body is a temple of the Holy Spirit within you, whom you have from God, and that you are not your own, for you were bought with a price. Therefore, you are called to glorify God in your body, as stated in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20. When choosing what to wear, a helpful question to ask is whether a specific garment honors the body as God’s creation and reflects the dignity of being made in His image.

Christian liberty is also a factor. Romans 14 and 1 Corinthians 10 discuss how believers have freedom in many areas, but that freedom should always be governed by love for others. 1 Corinthians 10:23-24 says, "All things are lawful," but not all things are helpful. "All things are lawful," but not all things build up. Let no one seek his own good, but the good of his neighbor. In a community of believers, we want to be mindful of how our choices might affect the consciences of others or potentially serve as a distraction or a stumbling block. 1 Corinthians 10:31 summarizes this well by stating that whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God.

In practical terms, many Christian women reach different conclusions based on their conscience, their specific culture, and the setting they are in. Some may feel that a bikini is appropriate in certain contexts, while others may choose more modest options to ensure they are honoring God and their neighbors according to their personal convictions.

If you are struggling with this decision, you might consider asking yourself a few gentle questions:
1. What is my primary motivation for choosing this specific outfit?
2. Does this choice reflect the inward beauty of a heart devoted to Christ, as described in 1 Peter 3:3-4?
3. Am I seeking to honor God and love my brothers and sisters in Christ through this decision?

Ultimately, our identity and worth are not found in our outward appearance or the opinions of others, but in the finished work of Jesus Christ. We are all sinners saved by grace, reconciled to God through the death and resurrection of Jesus. It is through faith in Him alone that we are forgiven and empowered by the Holy Spirit to live lives that please Him. If you have not yet placed your trust in Him, you can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

For further guidance, it can be very helpful to speak with a trusted, mature woman in your local church or a pastor who can walk alongside you as you apply these biblical principles to your life. Seek the Lord in prayer, for James 1:5 promises that if any of you lacks wisdom, let him ask God, who gives generously to all without reproach, and it will be given him.
Is Christian rap music appropriate?

The question of whether Christian rap music is appropriate is an important one that touches on how believers use their liberty in Christ to honor God through the arts. In the Bible, music is presented as a powerful gift used for the praise of God and the encouragement of His people. The appropriateness of any musical genre, including rap, is best evaluated by looking at the heart behind the music, the truth within the lyrics, and the fruit it produces in the listener.

First, the primary goal for every Christian is to live for the glory of God. 1 Corinthians 10:31 says, "So, whether you eat or drink, or whatever you do, do all to the glory of God." Music is a form of creative expression, and since God is the Creator of sound, rhythm, and poetry, these elements can be used to reflect His character and beauty. If a rap artist creates music with a heart of worship and a desire to exalt the name of Jesus, the genre itself can be a legitimate vessel for honoring Him.

Second, the content of the lyrics is of utmost importance. Scripture instructs us to let the word of Christ dwell in us richly (Colossians 3:16). For music to be truly "Christian," it must align with the truth of the Bible. Rap, by its nature, allows for a high volume of words, which provides a unique opportunity to explain deep theological truths, share personal testimony, and address complex life issues from a biblical worldview. When the lyrics are true, honorable, just, pure, and commendable (Philippians 4:8), the music serves as a tool for edification.

Third, we must consider the impact of the music on our spiritual walk. While musical style is often a matter of Christian liberty (Romans 14:1-12), we are called to discern what helps us grow in holiness. If a particular song or artist leads you closer to Christ and encourages you to obey His Word, it can be a helpful resource. However, if the music mimics the worldly values of pride, greed, or immorality, it would not be beneficial for a believer.

Ultimately, our behavior and our preferences in music do not save us. We are all sinners who have turned away from God's perfect standard, and we cannot bridge the gap between ourselves and a holy God through our own efforts or religious choices. But God, being rich in mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to the earth. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

As you consider this topic, it may be helpful to ask: Does this music help me love God more and love my neighbor better? What is it about Christian rap that leads you to ask if it is appropriate?
What was the Levitical priesthood?

The Levitical priesthood was a group of men from the tribe of Levi whom God set apart to serve Him and the nation of Israel. This special calling began during the time of the Exodus from Egypt when God chose the descendants of Aaron, who was from the tribe of Levi, to serve as priests (Exodus 28:1). While all priests were Levites, not all Levites were priests. Those who were not of Aaron's immediate family assisted in the maintenance and transportation of the Tabernacle and later the Temple (Numbers 3:5-10).

The primary role of the Levitical priests was to act as mediators between a holy God and a sinful people. They were responsible for offering the animal sacrifices commanded in the Law to provide a temporary covering for the sins of the people (Leviticus 1:1-9). They also taught the Word of God to the Israelites, cared for the sanctuary, and sought God's will for the nation (Deuteronomy 33:10; Malachi 2:7).

The High Priest held the most significant position within this framework. Once a year, on the Day of Atonement, he alone would enter the Most Holy Place to offer blood for his own sins and the sins of the people (Leviticus 16:1-34). This system emphasized that sin creates a barrier between humanity and God that requires a blood sacrifice for reconciliation.

Ultimately, the Levitical priesthood was intended to be a shadow or a picture of a greater reality to come. Because these priests were themselves sinners and eventually died, their work had to be repeated continually and was never truly finished (Hebrews 7:23; Hebrews 10:1-4). This points us to the absolute holiness of God and our own separation from Him due to our sin.

The heart of the Gospel is that Jesus Christ came as the perfect and final High Priest. Unlike the Levitical priests, Jesus is holy, blameless, and lives forever (Hebrews 7:24-26). He did not offer the blood of animals, but offered His own perfect life on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Because of His sacrifice, the veil of the temple was torn, and we can now draw near to God directly through Him (Matthew 27:51; Hebrews 4:14-16).

Salvation is found in no one else, as Jesus is the only mediator between God and man (John 14:6; 1 Timothy 2:5). You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in Him as your High Priest today?
What is a "dark night of the soul"?

The term dark night of the soul originally comes from a poem and treatise by a sixteenth century Spanish monk named Saint John of the Cross. While it is not a direct biblical phrase, it describes a common experience among believers where they feel a profound sense of spiritual desolation, dryness, or the perceived absence of God. It is often a season where the emotional comforts of faith are stripped away, leaving a person feeling as though they are walking through a deep, spiritual darkness.

In the Bible, we see many faithful servants of God undergo similar seasons. Job experienced a period of intense loss and silence from heaven (Job 23:8-9). King David often cried out in the Psalms, asking how long God would hide His face (Psalm 13:1-2). Perhaps the most significant example is found in the life of Heman the Ezrahite, who described his soul as full of troubles and felt as though he was forgotten by God (Psalm 88:1-18).

From a pastoral perspective, a dark night of the soul is usually not a sign of God’s anger or a result of a specific hidden sin. Instead, it is often viewed as a period of purification or refinement. God may allow these seasons to transition a believer from a faith based on feelings and spiritual "sweetness" to a more mature faith rooted purely in His character and promises. It is a time when our love for God is tested to see if we love Him for who He is, or simply for the blessings and good feelings He provides (1 Peter 1:6-7).

Ultimately, our hope in the darkness is found in the person of Jesus Christ. He is the Holy God who took on human flesh to live a perfect life on our behalf. Because of our sin, we were separated from God and deserving of His just judgment. However, out of His great mercy, Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who intercedes for His people. On the cross, Jesus experienced the ultimate dark night, crying out, "My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?" (Matthew 27:46). He endured that absolute separation so that those who trust in Him will never truly be forsaken (Hebrews 13:5).

If you are feeling a sense of distance from God or a heavy burden of guilt, you can turn to God now. Repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

If you are currently walking through such a season, it is important to remember that your feelings are not the ultimate measure of God's presence. Continue to feed on the Word of God, participate in the fellowship of a local church, and pray honestly to Him, even when the words feel empty. God is often doing His deepest work in us when we feel Him the least.

What has led you to think about this concept lately?
What does the Bible say about self-defense?

The Bible teaches that human life is sacred because every person is created in the image of God (Genesis 1:27). Because life is a gift from God, there is a general biblical principle that protecting life, including one's own and especially the lives of the vulnerable, is a responsible and godly act.

In the Old Testament, the law made a distinction regarding a person defending their home. If a thief was caught breaking in at night and was struck and killed, the defender was not considered guilty of bloodshed (Exodus 22:2). This indicates that God recognizes the right to protect one's household from immediate, life-threatening danger. Additionally, when the people of Israel were rebuilding the walls of Jerusalem under threat of attack, Nehemiah encouraged them to arm themselves to defend their families and their homes (Nehemiah 4:14).

In the New Testament, Jesus provided teachings that call for a heart of non-retaliation and love for enemies. He told His followers, "Do not resist an evil person," and "If anyone slaps you on the right cheek, turn to them the other cheek also" (Matthew 5:39). Many biblical scholars understand this to be a command regarding personal insults and legal disputes rather than a prohibition against stopping a lethal physical attack. Jesus also told His disciples at the Last Supper to carry a sword as they prepared for the trials ahead (Luke 22:36). However, when Peter later used a sword to try and stop Jesus' arrest, Jesus rebuked him, saying, "Put your sword back in its place, for all who draw the sword will die by the sword" (Matthew 26:52). This suggests that while preparation for protection is not forbidden, we must not use violence to interfere with God's sovereign purposes or as a primary way of life.

The Christian's priority is always to live at peace with everyone as much as it depends on them (Romans 12:18). While the Bible allows for the protection of life, it warns against a heart of vengeance. We are called to trust in God as our ultimate shield and protector (Psalm 18:2; Psalm 28:7).

Ultimately, our greatest need for defense is not against physical harm, but against the consequence of our own sin before a holy God. We have all sinned and fall short of His glory (Romans 3:23), but God showed His love for us by sending Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for our sins, and rose again on the third day to prove His victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is the only mediator who can reconcile us to God (1 Timothy 2:5).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What is it that makes you most concerned about your safety or the need for defense?
What was the significance of Jesus being dead for three days?

The significance of Jesus being dead for three days is rooted in the fulfillment of prophecy, the validation of His identity, and the certainty of His victory over death. In the Scriptures, this timeframe is not an accidental detail but a central part of God’s redemptive plan.

First, it fulfilled the prophetic sign of Jonah which Jesus Himself spoke about during His ministry. He told the religious leaders that just as Jonah was in the belly of the great fish for three days and three nights, so the Son of Man would be in the heart of the earth for three days and three nights (Matthew 12:40). His rising on the third day was also a fulfillment of the Old Testament Scriptures and His own repeated predictions to His disciples (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Hosea 6:2; Luke 24:44–46).

Second, the three day period provided undeniable proof that Jesus was truly dead. In the cultural context of that time, three days served as a definitive marker that life had fully departed the body. By remaining in the tomb for this duration, Jesus demonstrated that His resurrection was a genuine miracle of God’s power and not a mere recovery from a physical swoon or temporary unconsciousness. This event declared Him to be the Son of God with power according to the Spirit of holiness (Romans 1:4).

Ultimately, the three days highlight the gravity of the human condition and the greatness of God’s grace. Because God is perfectly holy and supreme, our sin separates us from Him and carries the penalty of death. Yet, in His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to reconcile sinners to Himself. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and showing that the Father had accepted His sacrifice as sufficient.

This is the core of the Gospel, that we are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works. His resurrection is the guarantee that He is Lord over all and that everyone who trusts in Him will be raised to new and eternal life.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is the idea of chi compatible with the Christian faith?

When exploring whether the concept of chi is compatible with the Christian faith, it is helpful to look at the foundations of both worldviews. Chi, often described in Eastern philosophies as a vital life force or energy that flows through all living things, typically rests on a pantheistic or monistic foundation. This means it suggests that the divine and the universe are one, or that a single impersonal energy binds everything together.

In contrast, the Bible teaches that God is the personal, sovereign Creator who is distinct from His creation. Genesis 1:1 states that "In the beginning, God created the heavens and the earth." He is not an impersonal force that we manipulate or balance, but a holy and loving Being who created us for a relationship with Himself. While chi is often viewed as a neutral energy to be harnessed, the Bible teaches that all life is sustained by the deliberate, ongoing will of God (Colossians 1:16-17; Acts 17:24-25).

There are three primary areas where the concept of chi and Christian doctrine diverge:

First, the Bible emphasizes the distinction between the Creator and the creature. In Christian theology, God gives life through His "breath" (Genesis 2:7), but this life is a gift from a personal God, not a divine substance that flows through us or makes up our essence. To view ourselves as part of a universal energy can lead to a misunderstanding of our dependence on God as our Lord and Savior.

Second, the role of the Holy Spirit is unique. In the Christian faith, the Holy Spirit is not a force or an energy, but the third Person of the Trinity (Matthew 28:19). He indwells believers to comfort, convict, and empower them for godliness (John 14:16-17). While chi is often sought for health or physical power, the Holy Spirit is given to transform our hearts and reconcile us to God.

Third, the Bible warns us to be cautious of philosophies that are not centered on Christ. Colossians 2:8 says, "See to it that no one takes you captive through hollow and deceptive philosophy, which depends on human tradition and the elemental spiritual forces of this world rather than on Christ."

Ultimately, our deepest human need is not for the balancing of energy, but for the forgiveness of sins and reconciliation with our Creator. We are separated from God's holiness by our sin, and no amount of energy manipulation can bridge that gap. God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day to defeat death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is a free gift of grace received through faith in Jesus Christ alone, not by any works or techniques of our own (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Through Christ’s death and resurrection, He has been exalted as Lord of all (Philippians 2:9-11). He offers us not just a "life force," but eternal life and a personal relationship with the living God. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the peace that passes all understanding?

The peace that passes all understanding is a supernatural tranquility of heart and mind given by God to those who are in a relationship with Him through Jesus Christ. This phrase comes from the book of Philippians 4:7, where the Apostle Paul writes, "and the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, will guard your hearts and your minds in Christ Jesus."

This peace is described as surpassing understanding because it does not depend on favorable circumstances. While the world offers a version of peace that is merely the absence of conflict or trouble, God's peace remains steady even in the midst of suffering, anxiety, or trial. It is a gift that guards the believer's inner life like a soldier guarding a city, preventing fear and worry from taking control. 

According to the surrounding verses in Philippians 4:6, this peace is experienced as we bring our anxieties to God through prayer, supplication, and thanksgiving. It is rooted in the character of God, who is sovereign, faithful, and good. When we recognize that God is in control and that He loves us, we can rest in His care regardless of what is happening around us (Philippians 4:4-9).

However, it is important to understand that the peace *of* God is only possible when we first have peace *with* God. Scripture teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, while we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). Because of our sin, we are naturally at enmity with God. 

The good news, or the Gospel, is that God in His mercy did not leave us in this state. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. Jesus was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Because of His sacrifice, salvation is available by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

When we trust in Jesus, we are justified and reconciled to God, which is the foundation for lasting peace (Romans 5:1). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Is an eclipse a sign of the end times?

The Bible teaches that God created the sun, moon, and stars to serve as signs and to mark seasons, days, and years (Genesis 1:14). While Scripture does mention celestial events in relation to the end of the age, it is important to distinguish between regular, predictable astronomical events like solar eclipses and the unique signs described in prophecy.

Many passages describe the sun being darkened and the moon not giving its light in connection with the return of Jesus Christ (Matthew 24:29; Joel 2:31; Revelation 6:12). However, solar eclipses are natural phenomena that have occurred regularly throughout history according to the laws of nature that God established. The Bible warns believers against being dismayed by signs in the heavens that cause the nations to fear (Jeremiah 10:2). Instead, these events can be seen as reminders of God’s majesty and His sovereign control over the universe (Psalm 19:1).

Jesus specifically taught that no one knows the exact day or hour of His return, not even the angels in heaven (Matthew 24:36). Rather than focusing on predicting the timing of the end through natural events, Scripture encourages us to live in a state of constant readiness and faithfulness (Luke 12:40).

The most important preparation for the future is ensuring you are reconciled to God. God is perfectly holy and just, but because of our sin, we are naturally separated from Him. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, triumphed over death, and is now exalted as Lord of all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Christ alone and not by any works of our own. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Are there specific things about the end times that cause you concern or make you wonder about God's plan?
Who are the multitude of all nations, tribes, peoples, and tongues in Revelation 7:9?

The great multitude described in Revelation 7:9 represents the vast assembly of all who have been redeemed by the blood of Jesus Christ throughout history. This passage provides a beautiful vision of the ultimate success of the Gospel, showing that God's plan of salvation is not limited to one ethnic group or geographic location, but extends to every corner of the earth.

These individuals are identified later in the same chapter as those who have come out of the great tribulation and have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb according to Revelation 7:14. Their presence before the throne of God is the fulfillment of the promise God made to Abraham, that through his offspring all the nations of the earth would be blessed as seen in Genesis 22:18. It also represents the fruit of the Great Commission, where Jesus commanded His followers to make disciples of all nations in Matthew 28:19.

The diversity of this group, including every tribe, tongue, and people, highlights that the Gospel is the power of God for salvation to everyone who believes as stated in Romans 1:16. Despite their different backgrounds, they are unified in their purpose: worshipping the Lamb, Jesus Christ, who was slain to ransom people for God from every tribe and language and people and nation according to Revelation 5:9. Their white robes symbolize the righteousness of Christ that has been credited to them through faith, and the palm branches signify their victory and joy in God’s presence.

This vision reminds us that while we are all separated from God by our sin, God in His holiness and mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, defeating sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all, offering forgiveness and eternal life to anyone who believes. Salvation is a gift of grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not earned by our own works or merit.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. What is it that might be keeping you from placing your full trust in Him today?
Who were the Cushites?

The Cushites were the descendants of Cush, who was the son of Ham and the grandson of Noah (Genesis 10:6). In the biblical narrative, the land of Cush generally refers to the region south of Egypt, encompassing modern-day Sudan, South Sudan, and Ethiopia.

Throughout Scripture, the Cushites appear as a distinct and often powerful people group. One of the most famous descendants of Cush was Nimrod, described as a mighty hunter and the builder of great cities (Genesis 10:8-12). The Bible also records that Moses married a Cushite woman, an event that led to a confrontation where God defended Moses against the criticisms of his siblings, Miriam and Aaron (Numbers 12:1-10).

The Cushites were often known for their physical stature and military strength (Isaiah 18:2; 2 Chronicles 14:9). However, the Bible also highlights individuals of great character from this nation. For instance, Ebed-Melech, a Cushite official in the palace of King Zedekiah, showed remarkable courage and compassion by rescuing the prophet Jeremiah from a muddy cistern where he had been left to die (Jeremiah 38:7-13). Because of his trust in God, Ebed-Melech was promised protection during the fall of Jerusalem (Jeremiah 39:15-18).

In the New Testament, we see a beautiful fulfillment of the promise that God would draw all nations to Himself. In the book of Acts, an angel of the Lord directed Philip to a desert road to meet an Ethiopian (Cushite) eunuch who was a high official under the Queen of the Ethiopians (Acts 8:26-27). This man was reading the prophet Isaiah but did not understand it until Philip explained that the passage referred to Jesus. The official believed the Gospel, was baptized, and carried the message of Christ back to his homeland (Acts 8:35-39).

This history reminds us that God's plan of redemption is not limited to any single ethnic group. Because of our sin, we are all naturally separated from a holy God. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily on the third day. He is now the exalted Lord who offers salvation to everyone who believes. This salvation is a gift of grace through faith in Christ alone, and it is not earned by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you find most interesting about how God interacts with people from different nations in the Bible?
What is the counsel of the ungodly, and how do we not walk in it (Psalm 1:1)?

Psalm 1:1 introduces us to the portrait of a blessed person by describing what they avoid. The "counsel of the ungodly" refers to the advice, philosophies, worldviews, and values that are rooted in human wisdom apart from God. It is a way of thinking that ignores God’s authority, prioritizes the self, and seeks fulfillment in things other than the Creator. This counsel is often subtle, appearing wise or popular by the world's standards, but it ultimately leads away from the life and peace found in Christ.

The verse describes a dangerous progression: walking, standing, and sitting. Walking in the counsel of the ungodly begins when we listen to and consider perspectives that contradict Scripture. If we continue in that path, we may find ourselves "standing" or lingering in sinful ways, eventually "sitting" or settling into a lifestyle characterized by a hardened heart or mockery of the things of God.

To avoid walking in this counsel, we must intentionally replace it with the counsel of God. Psalm 1:2 tells us that the blessed person’s "delight is in the law of the Lord, and on his law he meditates day and night." We guard our hearts by making the Bible our primary authority and the filter through which we process all other information. This involves consistent prayer and the study of God’s Word so that we can discern truth from error. Romans 12:2 encourages us not to be conformed to this world but to be transformed by the renewal of our minds.

We also need the fellowship of the local church. Walking with wise, godly people helps us stay on the right path, as Proverbs 13:20 reminds us that "whoever walks with the wise becomes wise, but the companion of fools will suffer harm." Seeking the guidance of the Holy Spirit is essential, for He leads us into all truth and empowers us to live according to God’s will.

Ultimately, we must recognize that we cannot live a blessed or righteous life by our own effort. God is perfectly holy and just, but we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Our sin separates us from God and makes us prone to follow the counsel of the world. However, God, in His mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect life of obedience that we could not live, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord of all.

Through Jesus, we are not only forgiven but also reconciled to God and given a new nature that desires His ways. You can turn to God now, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from fully trusting God’s wisdom over the wisdom of the world?
What is an alabaster box?

In the Bible, an "alabaster box" or jar refers to a vessel made from a specific type of soft, translucent stone typically found in Egypt. These containers were highly valued because the stone was non-porous and cool, making it ideal for preserving expensive ointments and perfumes. In the ancient world, these jars often had long, thin necks that were broken or unsealed when the contents were ready to be used.

The most famous mention of an alabaster box occurs in the Gospels when a woman approached Jesus and poured very expensive ointment on Him (Matthew 26:7; Mark 14:3; Luke 7:37). The perfume inside, often identified as pure nard, was worth approximately three hundred denarii, which represented an entire year’s wages for a common laborer (Mark 14:5). By breaking the jar and pouring out the oil, the woman was offering her most valuable possession in an act of total, sacrificial devotion to Christ.

Jesus explained that this act was a preparation for His burial (Matthew 26:12). This moment serves as a beautiful picture of recognizing the supreme worth of Jesus. While others in the room saw the act as a waste of money, Jesus saw it as a beautiful service to Him. This points us to the reality of who Jesus is and what He came to do.

The Bible teaches that God is holy and just, but we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, which separates us from Him (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; 1 Corinthians 15:20). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Just as the woman in the Gospel recognized the worth of Jesus, we are invited to see Him as our Lord and Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who were the Apostolic Fathers?

The Apostolic Fathers were a group of early Christian leaders and writers who lived and ministered in the generations immediately following the original Apostles, primarily during the late 1st century and the first half of the 2nd century. They are called "Apostolic" because many of them are believed to have been direct students of the Apostles or to have been significantly influenced by their personal teaching. Their writings provide a vital window into the life, struggles, and beliefs of the early Church as it moved beyond the New Testament era.

Prominent figures among the Apostolic Fathers include:

Clement of Rome: Traditionally identified as an early bishop of Rome, he wrote a letter known as 1 Clement to the church in Corinth, emphasizing order, humility, and the importance of those who serve in leadership.

Ignatius of Antioch: A bishop who was martyred in Rome, he wrote seven famous letters to various churches while he was under arrest. He is well known for his strong defense of the deity of Jesus Christ and his focus on church unity.

Polycarp of Smyrna: A disciple of the Apostle John, Polycarp served as the bishop of Smyrna. His letter to the Philippians and the account of his courageous martyrdom reflect a deep devotion to Christ even in the face of death.

Papias of Hierapolis: A contemporary of Polycarp who also heard the Apostle John, he is known for collecting oral traditions concerning the sayings of Jesus and the origins of the Gospels.

Other writings associated with this group include the Didache (an early manual on church practice), the Epistle of Barnabas, and The Shepherd of Hermas. These men were deeply committed to the instruction found in Hebrews 13:7, which says, "Remember your leaders, those who spoke to you the word of God. Consider the outcome of their way of life, and imitate their faith."

While their writings are not considered Scripture and do not carry the same authority as the Bible, they are respected for how they sought to preserve the teachings of the Apostles. The New Testament teaches that the church is "built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone" (Ephesians 2:20). The Apostolic Fathers saw themselves as guardians of this foundation.

At the heart of their message was the same Gospel that the Apostles preached. They taught that God is holy and supreme, yet humanity is separated from Him by sin. To reconcile us to Himself, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death. 

This salvation is not earned by our own efforts or religious works, but is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone. As the Bible says, "If you confess with your mouth that Jesus is Lord and believe in your heart that God raised him from the dead, you will be saved" (Romans 10:9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does heaven exist?

According to the Bible, heaven is a real and glorious reality. It is described not merely as a state of mind, but as the dwelling place of God and the ultimate home for those who are reconciled to Him. Jesus Himself spoke of heaven as a tangible place, telling His disciples, "In my Father’s house are many rooms. If it were not so, would I have told you that I go to prepare a place for you?" (John 14:2).

To understand heaven, we must understand the character of the God who dwells there. God is perfectly holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because He is holy, He cannot be in the presence of sin. This creates a significant problem for us, as the Bible teaches that all people have sinned and fallen short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Our sin separates us from God and deserves His righteous judgment.

However, because God is also rich in mercy and love, He provided a way for us to be forgiven and to spend eternity with Him. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to earth (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life that we could never live. He then willingly died on a cross, taking the punishment for our sins upon Himself as a substitute (1 Peter 2:24; Romans 5:8). Three days later, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and confirming that His sacrifice was sufficient (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation and the promise of heaven are not earned by our good works or religious efforts. Instead, they are a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). To be reconciled to God and have the assurance of heaven, one must turn away from sin and trust in the finished work of Jesus Christ.

The Bible describes the final state of heaven as a new heaven and a new earth where God will dwell with His people. In this place, there will be no more death, sorrow, crying, or pain, for the old order of things will have passed away (Revelation 21:1-4). It is a place of eternal joy, worship, and perfect fellowship with our Creator.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does the Bible teach geocentrism?

The Bible is the inspired and authoritative Word of God, though it is important to distinguish between what the Bible teaches as doctrine and the descriptive language it uses to communicate truth. While some historically interpreted certain passages as supporting geocentrism (the idea that the earth is the center of the universe), most Christian scholars today understand that the Bible uses "phenomenological language." This means the authors described things as they appeared to a human observer on the ground, much like we still say "the sun rises" or "the sun sets" today without intending to make a scientific statement about planetary motion.

For example, in Psalm 19:4-6, the psalmist describes the sun rising at one end of the heavens and making its circuit to the other. Similarly, in Joshua 10:12-13, the text describes the sun and moon standing still in the sky. These passages use the language of appearance to describe real, historical, or poetic events from a human perspective. They are not intended to be a scientific manual on celestial mechanics.

Other verses, such as Psalm 93:1 or Psalm 104:5, speak of the earth being established so that it cannot be moved. In the context of Hebrew poetry, this language typically refers to the stability, order, and security of the world under God's sovereign rule, rather than its physical lack of motion through space. The Bible's primary purpose is not to provide a scientific description of the solar system, but to reveal the character of God and His relationship with humanity.

Ultimately, the Scriptures point us toward the most important truth of all, which is the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Bible reveals that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3) and that we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23), which separates us from Him. Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. He lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute to pay the penalty for our sins, and was bodily raised from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.
