Why did Jesus say, “I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance” (Matthew 9:13)?

Jesus spoke these words in response to the Pharisees, the religious leaders of the day, who were offended that He was eating with tax collectors and people known for their sinful lifestyles (Matthew 9:10–11). To explain His actions, Jesus used the analogy of a doctor, saying that those who are healthy do not need a physician, but those who are sick do (Matthew 9:12).

When Jesus said He did not come to call the "righteous," He was pointing out a profound spiritual truth regarding the human heart. According to Scripture, no one is truly righteous on their own merits, for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). There is no one righteous, not even one (Romans 3:10). However, the Pharisees believed they were already righteous because of their strict adherence to religious laws and traditions. By using the term "righteous," Jesus was addressing those who were self-righteous and felt they had no need for God's mercy.

Jesus’ mission was to reach those who recognized their spiritual poverty and their desperate need for a Savior. A person cannot be healed if they do not believe they are sick, and a person cannot be saved if they do not believe they are lost. The call to repentance is an invitation to turn away from sin and self-reliance and to turn toward God for forgiveness and new life.

This is the heart of the Gospel. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Because we cannot make ourselves righteous through our own efforts, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth. He lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by any works we perform (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is God?

God is the infinite, eternal, and unchangeable Spirit who created and sustains everything that exists (Genesis 1:1; Colossians 1:16-17). He is not a force or a vague energy, but a personal and relational Being who has revealed Himself through His Word, the Bible.

The Bible teaches that God is one, yet He eternally exists in three distinct persons: the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). This is known as the Trinity. While these three persons are distinct, they are one in essence, power, and glory.

The character of God is marked by perfect attributes. He is holy, meaning He is set apart and completely pure (Isaiah 6:3). He is sovereign, holding supreme authority over all of history and creation (Psalm 115:3). He is also characterized by His goodness, patience, and justice. Most profoundly, the Bible tells us that God is love (1 John 4:8) and that He is rich in mercy and grace toward those who are far from Him (Ephesians 2:4-5).

Because God is perfectly holy, He is also a just judge who cannot overlook sin. Sin is our rebellion against His good and right authority, and it separates us from Him (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). However, because of His great love, God provided a way for us to be reconciled to Himself. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to the earth (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then willingly died on a cross to take the punishment that our sins deserved. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and showing that He is the Lord of all (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 1:4).

This salvation is not something we can earn by being good or doing religious works. It is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8-9). Through His sacrifice, we can be forgiven and enter into a personal relationship with the God who made us.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. What thoughts or questions do you have about who God is or what it means to trust Him?
How does the preservation of the Qur’an compare to the preservation of the Bible?

When comparing the preservation of the Qur'an and the Bible, it is helpful to look at the historical methods used by each tradition and how they provide confidence in the texts we have today. Both traditions value their scriptures deeply, but they followed different paths of transmission.

The preservation of the Qur'an is often described through the lens of a centralized standardization. After the death of Muhammad, the third Caliph, Uthman, gathered various existing copies and oral recitations to create a single, official codex. To ensure unity, he ordered other divergent versions to be burned. This process led to a high degree of textual uniformity in the written Arabic script used across the Islamic world. While Islamic tradition emphasizes a single, perfect preservation, historical study and early manuscripts show that different vocalizations and variant readings, known as Qira'at, have existed throughout history.

The preservation of the Bible, particularly the New Testament, followed a different path characterized by a vast, decentralized multiplication of manuscripts. Because the early church was often under persecution and spread rapidly across different regions, there was no central human authority to standardize the text by destroying variants. Instead, thousands of copies were made and distributed across the Roman Empire and beyond. Today, there are over 5,000 Greek manuscripts of the New Testament, ranging from small fragments to complete volumes, along with thousands more in languages like Latin, Syriac, and Coptic.

While these many manuscripts contain minor variations, such as spelling differences or word order, scholars of textual criticism use this "cloud of witnesses" to reconstruct the original text with a high degree of certainty. The sheer volume of manuscripts allows us to cross-reference and identify where a scribe might have made a slip of the pen. This transparency provides a robust historical foundation, demonstrating that the essential doctrines of the faith have remained unchanged. The discovery of the Dead Sea Scrolls further confirmed the meticulous care with which the Old Testament was preserved over centuries (Isaiah 40:8; Psalm 119:89).

As Christians, we believe that God has sovereignly protected His Word so that we may know the truth about who He is and how we can be reconciled to Him. The Bible reveals that God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all turned away from Him through sin, which separates us from His presence (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (John 1:1; John 3:16; 1 Peter 2:24).

Jesus did not remain in the grave but rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This is the heart of the Gospel: salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or religious efforts (Ephesians 2:8–9). The Bible is preserved so that every generation might hear this good news and find eternal life.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Does the idea of God preserving His Word through thousands of diverse witnesses give you more or less confidence in its message?
What is the meaning of Azazel / the scapegoat?

The term Azazel appears in the context of the Day of Atonement, an annual day of repentance and cleansing described in Leviticus 16:7-10. On this day, the high priest took two goats from the assembly of Israel. One goat was chosen by lot for the Lord to be a sin offering, and the other was designated for Azazel to be the scapegoat.

While scholars have debated the exact meaning of the word Azazel, often interpreting it as a remote place in the wilderness, a symbolic name for "entire removal," or even a reference to a desert place of judgment, its theological function is clearly defined in the biblical text. In Leviticus 16:21-22, the high priest was commanded to lay both hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the wickedness and rebellion of the Israelites. The goat was then sent away into the wilderness, symbolically carrying the people's sins away from the presence of God and the community.

This ritual illustrates two essential aspects of how God deals with sin, which theologians often call propitiation and expiation. The first goat, which was sacrificed, represents propitiation, the satisfying of God's holy justice and the payment of the penalty for sin. The second goat, the scapegoat, represents expiation, the actual removal of the guilt and stain of sin from the people. This reflects the promise found in Psalm 103:12, which states that as far as the east is from the west, so far has He removed our transgressions from us.

For believers, these two goats serve as a shadow of the work of Jesus Christ. He is the ultimate fulfillment of both roles. John the Baptist identified Him as the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world in John 1:29. Jesus not only died to pay the debt we owed to a holy God, but He also bore our sins in His own body to remove them from us completely. As Isaiah 53:6 explains, the Lord has laid on Him the iniquity of us all. Furthermore, Hebrews 13:12 notes that Jesus suffered outside the city gate to make the people holy through His own blood, mirroring the scapegoat being sent outside the camp.

The meaning of the scapegoat is a message of profound hope. It tells us that God does not merely overlook sin, but He deals with it justly and removes it so thoroughly that it no longer defines our standing before Him. Because of what Jesus has done, those who trust in Him are seen as righteous and are no longer burdened by the weight of their past rebellions.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the full forgiveness and removal of your guilt. 

What thoughts or questions do you have about how Jesus handles the weight of our sin?
Who are sons of disobedience (Ephesians 2:2)?

In Ephesians 2:2, the term "sons of disobedience" refers to individuals who live in a state of rebellion against God, governed by the values of this world and the influence of Satan, whom the Apostle Paul calls "the prince of the power of the air" (Ephesians 2:2). This description is not meant to isolate a specific group of especially "bad" people, but rather to describe the natural spiritual condition of every person apart from the saving work of Jesus Christ.

Paul clarifies in the following verse that "we all once lived" in this same condition, carrying out the desires of the body and the mind (Ephesians 2:3). To be a son of disobedience means to be characterized by a nature that is resistant to God's authority and disconnected from His life. Because God is perfectly holy and just, our sin and disobedience naturally separate us from Him and place us under His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23).

The beauty of the biblical message is that God did not leave us in this state. Out of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life of obedience that we could not live. He then died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin, death, and the power of the enemy (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4). He is now the exalted Lord who offers reconciliation to all.

Salvation is a gift secured by Christ's historical work and is received by grace alone through faith alone in Him alone, not by our own efforts or moral improvements (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). When someone trusts in Christ, they are no longer "sons of disobedience" but are made alive together with Him and adopted into God's family.

You can turn from sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Who was Othniel?

Othniel is a significant figure in the Old Testament, distinguished as the first judge of Israel. He was the son of Kenaz and the younger brother (or nephew) of Caleb, one of the two faithful spies who trusted God during the Israelites' journey through the wilderness.

Othniel first appears in the account of the conquest of the Promised Land. Caleb offered his daughter, Achsah, in marriage to whoever could capture the city of Kiriath-sepher. Othniel took up the challenge, conquered the city, and married Achsah (Joshua 15:16–17; Judges 1:12–13). This early account established his reputation as a courageous leader and a man of action.

His primary historical role, however, is recorded in the book of Judges. After the generation of Joshua passed away, the people of Israel forgot the Lord and began to worship the idols of the surrounding nations. As a consequence of their rebellion, God allowed them to be oppressed by Cushan-rishathaim, the king of Mesopotamia, for eight years (Judges 3:7–8).

When the Israelites cried out to the Lord in their distress, God raised up Othniel to deliver them. The Bible records that the Spirit of the Lord came upon him, and he went out to war, prevailing over the king of Mesopotamia. Under Othniel's leadership, the land of Israel enjoyed rest and peace for forty years (Judges 3:9–11).

Othniel’s life illustrates God’s faithfulness to provide a deliverer when His people repent. This pattern of bondage, crying out, and deliverance points us toward a much greater reality. Just as Israel was enslaved by physical enemies because of their sin, all of humanity is naturally enslaved by the power of sin and death. Othniel was a temporary deliverer, but God has provided an eternal Deliverer in Jesus Christ.

The Gospel is the good news that while we were separated from a holy God by our sin, God sent His Son into the world. Jesus Christ lived the perfect life we could not live, died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death and His authority as Lord (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or merits.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about pettiness?

While the Bible does not always use the specific word "pettiness," it speaks extensively about the heart attitudes that drive it, such as pride, irritability, and a lack of forgiveness. Pettiness often manifests as a focus on trivial offenses or a desire to "get even" in small ways, which stands in contrast to the character of Christ.

Scripture encourages believers to have a broad and gracious heart toward others. In Colossians 3:13, we are told to "bear with each other and forgive one another if any of you has a grievance against someone. Forgive as the Lord forgave you." This "bearing with" implies a willingness to overlook minor irritations and flaws for the sake of peace and love. Furthermore, 1 Corinthians 13:5 notes that love "is not easily angered" and "keeps no record of wrongs." A petty spirit often does the opposite, keeping a careful tally of small slights.

Pettiness is frequently fueled by pride and a preoccupation with one's own rights. Philippians 2:3 instructs us to "do nothing out of selfish ambition or vain conceit. Rather, in humility value others above yourselves." When we are more concerned with our own status than with the well-being of others, we become prone to quarreling over insignificant matters. Proverbs 17:14 warns that "starting a quarrel is like breaching a dam; so drop the matter before a dispute breaks out." Choosing to let go of a minor offense is often the most God-honoring path.

At its root, a tendency toward pettiness can reveal that we have lost sight of the immense grace God has shown us. In Matthew 18:21–35, Jesus tells the parable of a servant who was forgiven an astronomical debt by a king but then refused to forgive a very small debt owed to him by a fellow servant. This parable illustrates that when we understand how much God has forgiven us, it becomes impossible to justify holding onto small grievances against others.

The core of the Christian message is that we are all sinners who have fallen short of God’s holiness (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, He offers full forgiveness and reconciliation to all who turn to Him. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not earned by our own efforts or moral standing.

I invite you to turn from the weight of self-interest and trust in Jesus Christ, who died for your sins and rose again to give you a heart of peace and grace. What is it that makes it difficult to let go of the small offenses you encounter?
Who was Hazael in the Bible?

Hazael was a powerful king of Aram (Syria) who reigned during the ninth century B.C. His life and rise to power are documented primarily in the books of 1 Kings and 2 Kings. He is a significant biblical figure because his actions demonstrate that God is sovereign over all nations and uses even secular rulers to fulfill His divine purposes.

God first identified Hazael to the prophet Elijah at Mount Horeb. The Lord commanded Elijah to anoint Hazael as king over Aram, which signaled that God was directing the political shifts of the region (1 Kings 19:15). Years later, the prophet Elisha met Hazael in Damascus. King Ben-hadad II of Aram was ill and sent Hazael to ask the prophet if he would recover. Elisha told Hazael that the king would die and then began to weep. When Hazael asked why he was crying, Elisha revealed that he saw the terrible cruelty and violence Hazael would eventually inflict upon the people of Israel (2 Kings 8:7–13).

Following this encounter, Hazael returned to the king and murdered him by smothering him with a thick, wet cloth. Hazael then took the throne for himself (2 Kings 8:14–15). During his long reign, he became an instrument of discipline used by God against Israel because of their persistent turning away from the Lord to worship idols. Hazael conquered large portions of Israelite territory, particularly east of the Jordan River (2 Kings 10:32–33). The Bible notes that the Lord gave Israel into the hand of Hazael for many years because of their sins (2 Kings 13:3; 2 Kings 13:22).

The account of Hazael reminds us that God is holy and that He takes sin seriously. However, the Bible also reveals that God’s ultimate desire is not to remain in judgment, but to offer mercy. While Hazael was an instrument of justice, Jesus Christ came as the ultimate instrument of God's grace. Though we have all turned away from God and deserve judgment for our sins, Jesus lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose again on the third day to provide eternal life (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 5:8). Salvation is a gift from God, received through faith in Christ alone and not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8–9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and a new life. What do you find most striking about the way God interacts with people and nations in these historical accounts?
Why did Moses send twelve spies into the Promised Land?

Moses sent twelve spies, one from each ancestral tribe, to scout the land of Canaan before the Israelites entered it. According to Numbers 13:1–2, the Lord commanded Moses to send these men to explore the land He was giving to the Israelites. Deuteronomy 1:22–23 adds further context, noting that the people also approached Moses and requested this mission, wanting to know which route to take and what kind of cities they would encounter.

Moses gave the spies specific instructions to investigate the strength of the inhabitants, the quality of the soil, and the security of the fortifications. He also asked them to bring back some of the fruit of the land to show its productivity (Numbers 13:17–20). The primary purpose was to prepare the nation for the conquest by gathering intelligence, but it ultimately became a test of their faith in God's promises.

While the spies found a land flowing with milk and honey, ten of them focused on the strength of the giants and the fortified cities rather than the power of God. Because they spread a report of fear, the people rebelled in unbelief (Numbers 13:31–33; Numbers 14:1–4). Only Joshua and Caleb trusted that God would be faithful to His word. As a result of this lack of faith, that generation was not permitted to enter the land and instead wandered in the wilderness for forty years (Numbers 14:33–34).

This account serves as a sobering reminder of the consequences of unbelief. Just as the Israelites stood on the edge of the Promised Land, we all face the reality of our own sin and the barriers it creates between us and a holy God. Scripture teaches that no one can enter God's presence by their own strength or works (Romans 3:23; Ephesians 2:8–9). 

However, God provided a way through His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus is the truly faithful leader who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, defeating death and securing eternal life for all who believe (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is the only mediator between God and man (1 Timothy 2:5).

What is the “great and dreadful day of the Lord” (Malachi 4:5)?

The "great and dreadful day of the Lord" mentioned in Malachi 4:5 refers to a significant time in history when God intervenes directly and decisively to judge wickedness and bring salvation to His people. Throughout the Bible, the "Day of the Lord" is a theme that points to God’s sovereign rule over the nations and His holy requirement for justice.

The day is called "great" because it reveals the supreme power, glory, and holiness of God. It is "dreadful" or "terrible" because it involves God’s righteous wrath against sin and rebellion (Joel 2:11; Zephaniah 1:14-15). Malachi 4:1-3 describes this day as a fire that burns like an oven, where the arrogant and evildoers are consumed, but for those who fear God's name, "the sun of righteousness shall rise with healing in its wings" (Malachi 4:2).

Malachi 4:5 specifically promises that God would send "Elijah the prophet" before this day arrives. In the New Testament, Jesus identifies John the Baptist as the fulfillment of this promise, as John came in the spirit and power of Elijah to prepare the way for the Messiah (Matthew 11:13-14; Luke 1:17). John’s message was one of repentance, warning that the kingdom of God was at hand and that judgment was coming (Matthew 3:1-12).

Biblical prophecy often has a "near" and "far" fulfillment. While the coming of Christ began the fulfillment of these promises, the ultimate "Day of the Lord" points toward the second coming of Jesus Christ. At that time, He will return personally and bodily to judge the living and the dead and to make all things new (2 Peter 3:10-13; Revelation 19:11-16).

The reality of this coming day reminds us of our need for a Savior. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him, leaving us subject to His just judgment. However, God, in His great mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Salvation is secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or merit.

For those who trust in Jesus, the Day of the Lord is not a day of dread, but a day of joyful expectation when they will be fully reconciled to God and dwell with Him forever. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about self-righteousness?

Self-righteousness is the belief that one can achieve a right standing with God through their own moral efforts, religious works, or personal goodness. The Bible consistently warns against this mindset because it relies on human merit rather than God’s grace.

Jesus addressed self-righteousness most clearly in the parable of the Pharisee and the tax collector. The Pharisee stood and prayed about his own virtues, thanking God that he was better than others. Meanwhile, the tax collector would not even look up to heaven but beat his breast, saying, "God, be merciful to me, a sinner!" Jesus explained that the tax collector, who humbled himself, went home justified before God, while the self-righteous Pharisee did not (Luke 18:9–14).

The Scriptures teach that human righteousness, when compared to the absolute holiness of God, is deeply flawed. The prophet Isaiah wrote that "all our righteous acts are like filthy rags" (Isaiah 64:6). This does not mean that doing good is wrong, but rather that our good deeds can never bridge the gap created by our sin. The Apostle Paul emphasized that no one will be declared righteous in God’s sight by observing the law; rather, the purpose of the law is to make us conscious of our sin (Romans 3:20).

The heart of the Christian faith is the understanding that we cannot save ourselves. God is perfectly holy, and our sin separates us from Him. Because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live. Jesus died on the cross as a substitute, taking the punishment for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day to secure victory over death. Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith in what Christ has done, not through anything we earn (Ephesians 2:8–9; Galatians 2:16).

Self-righteousness is a barrier to the Gospel because it prevents a person from seeing their need for a Savior. To be made right with God, we must stop trusting in our own performance and instead trust in the finished work of Jesus. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting in His grace rather than your own efforts?
What is the meaning and purpose of saying grace before a meal?

The practice of saying grace, or offering a prayer of thanksgiving before a meal, is a tradition deeply rooted in the Bible and the life of Jesus Christ. At its heart, the meaning of this act is an expression of gratitude (the word grace itself comes from the Latin "gratias," meaning thanks) and a recognition that God is the ultimate provider of all we have.

One primary purpose of saying grace is to follow the example set by Jesus. During His ministry, He frequently gave thanks before eating, such as when He fed the five thousand in Matthew 14:19 and when He instituted the Lord's Supper in Luke 22:19. By pausing to pray, believers acknowledge that even the most basic necessities of life, like food and water, are gifts from a loving Heavenly Father. James 1:17 reminds us that every good gift and every perfect gift is from above, coming down from the Father of lights.

Another purpose is the sanctification of the meal. The apostle Paul wrote in 1 Timothy 4:4-5 that everything created by God is good, and nothing is to be rejected if it is received with thanksgiving, for it is made holy by the word of God and prayer. Saying grace serves as a moment to set the meal apart, inviting God's presence into the fellowship of the table and acknowledging His sovereignty over our physical health and sustenance. It helps us shift our focus from our own efforts to God’s faithfulness, as expressed in Psalm 145:15-16, which says the eyes of all look to God, and He gives them their food in due season.

While physical food sustains our bodies for a time, this practice also reminds us of our deeper need for spiritual life. Jesus described Himself as the bread of life in John 6:35, pointing to a hunger that physical food can never satisfy. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we cannot bridge the gap to Him through our own works or merit. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. Salvation is a gift of God's grace alone, received through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Saying grace is a small but significant way to practice the presence of God in the middle of a busy day. It turns a routine necessity into an act of worship and a testimony of dependence on the Creator. What holds you back from trusting Him with the various areas of your life today?
What is a chapel?

A chapel is traditionally a small building or a room used for Christian worship, prayer, and reflection. While a local church usually refers to a specific congregation of believers with a formal membership and leadership structure, a chapel is often a place designated for smaller gatherings or spiritual needs within a larger institution. You will frequently find chapels in places like hospitals, airports, military bases, schools, or even inside larger cathedral buildings.

In a biblical sense, it is important to remember that the true church is not a physical structure but the people of God. The Bible describes believers as living stones being built into a spiritual house (1 Peter 2:5). While God does not dwell in buildings made by human hands (Acts 17:24), He uses dedicated spaces for His people to meet and seek Him. Jesus promised that where two or three are gathered in His name, He is there among them (Matthew 18:20). A chapel provides a quiet, focused environment to pray, hear the Word of God, and find spiritual refreshment.

The existence of a chapel reminds us of our need for a sanctuary and a Savior. We are all naturally separated from God's holiness by our sin, but God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge that gap. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all. We are justified before God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any religious works or rituals we perform.

You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What has led you to think about the purpose of a chapel today?
What does the Bible say about fun and entertainment?

The Bible teaches that God is the source of all joy and the giver of every good gift. While the word "entertainment" is not used in ancient Scripture in the same way we use it today, the Bible provides clear principles regarding joy, celebration, rest, and discernment.

God created us with the capacity to enjoy His creation. In the book of Ecclesiastes, we are reminded that it is good and fitting for a person to eat, drink, and find satisfaction in their labor, for this is a gift from God (Ecclesiastes 3:12–13; Ecclesiastes 5:18–19). Celebration was also a regular part of the life of God’s people in the Old Testament through various feasts and festivals, which were times of communal rejoicing and gratitude (Deuteronomy 16:14–15).

However, Scripture also calls us to exercise wisdom and discernment in how we spend our time and what we choose to enjoy. Our primary goal in every area of life, including recreation, is to honor the Lord. 1 Corinthians 10:31 says that whether we eat or drink or whatever we do, we should do it all for the glory of God. This means we should ask ourselves if our choices in entertainment reflect His character and values.

A helpful standard for evaluating entertainment is found in Philippians 4:8, which encourages believers to dwell on things that are true, honorable, just, pure, lovely, commendable, excellent, and worthy of praise. Conversely, we are warned not to set anything worthless or vile before our eyes (Psalm 101:3) and to avoid things that stir up sinful desires or distract us from our devotion to Christ (1 John 2:15–17).

It is also important to remember that while entertainment can be a healthy form of rest, it should never become an idol or a way to escape from the responsibilities God has given us. God designed us for a rhythm of work and rest, as seen in the principle of the Sabbath (Exodus 20:8–11). Jesus Himself often withdrew to quiet places to pray and refresh His spirit (Mark 1:35; Luke 5:16).

Ultimately, the deepest and most lasting joy is not found in temporary amusements, but in a right relationship with God. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God’s holiness, but God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to bridge that gap. Jesus lived a perfect life, died on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over death. Through Him, we are offered forgiveness and a joy that the world cannot give (John 15:11).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive new life and eternal peace. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Does Romans 2:7 teach works salvation?

The short answer is no, Romans 2:7 does not teach that a person earns salvation through their own good works. To understand this verse, it is helpful to look at how the Apostle Paul builds his argument throughout the beginning of the book of Romans.

In Romans 2, Paul is describing the righteous and impartial nature of God's judgment. He explains that God will render to every person according to their deeds, as seen in Romans 2:6. When Paul says in Romans 2:7 that God will give eternal life to those who "by patience in well-doing seek for glory and honor and immortality," he is describing the standard of God’s perfect justice. If a person were to perfectly and consistently seek God through good works without ever sinning, they would indeed receive eternal life on that basis.

However, the rest of Paul’s letter makes it clear that no human being actually meets this standard. Later in the same argument, Paul writes that "none is righteous, no, not one" in Romans 3:10, and that "no human being will be justified in his sight by works of the law" in Romans 3:20. He concludes in Romans 3:23 that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God."

Therefore, Romans 2:7 serves as a mirror to show us our need for a Savior. Since no one can achieve the "patience in well-doing" required by God's perfect holiness, we are all in need of a righteousness that comes from outside of ourselves. Paul points us to this solution in Romans 3:24, explaining that we are "justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus."

The Bible consistently teaches that salvation is a gift received through faith, not a reward earned by effort. In Romans 4:5, Paul says, "And to the one who does not work but believes in him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted as righteousness." This is echoed in Ephesians 2:8-9, which states, "For by grace you have been saved through faith. And this is not your own doing; it is the gift of God, not a result of works, so that no one may boast." In Galatians 2:16, we are reminded that "a person is not justified by works of the law but through faith in Jesus Christ."

While good works are the necessary fruit and evidence of a heart changed by God, they are never the cause of our salvation. We are saved by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

This is the heart of the Gospel: God is perfectly holy, and our sin has separated us from Him. Because we could never reach Him through our own merits, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived the perfect life of "well-doing" that we could not live. He then took our sin upon Himself, dying a substitutionary death on the cross and rising bodily on the third day to defeat sin and death forever.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life. What is it that makes you wonder if your works are enough to please God?
Why are Psalms 14 and 53 nearly identical?

It is a fascinating feature of the Bible that Psalm 14 and Psalm 53 are almost identical. These are often called "doublets," which are passages or stories that appear more than once in the Scriptures. There are several historical and theological reasons why the Holy Spirit preserved this repetition for us.

One primary reason is the organizational structure of the Book of Psalms. The Psalter is divided into five smaller "books," likely modeled after the five books of the Torah. Psalm 14 is located in Book I (Psalm 1 to Psalm 41), which predominantly uses the covenant name of God, Yahweh, translated as "the LORD." Psalm 53 is located in Book II (Psalm 42 to Psalm 72), a section often called the Elohistic Psalter because it favors the more general name for God, *Elohim*. If you compare the two, you will notice that where Psalm 14:2, 4, and 7 use "the LORD," Psalm 53:2, 4, and 6 use "God."

Another reason for the repetition involves the liturgical use of these songs in ancient Israel. Just as modern believers might use different versions of a hymn for different settings, these Psalms likely served the community in various contexts. While they are nearly the same, there is a distinct difference in the middle. Psalm 14:5–6 focuses on God being the refuge of the poor, while Psalm 53:5 contains a more vivid description of God scattering the bones of those who encamped against His people. This suggests that Psalm 53 may have been adapted to celebrate a specific historical moment when God delivered Israel from a military threat.

Theologically, the repetition serves to emphasize a vital truth: the universal depravity of the human race. Both Psalms begin with the sobering statement, "The fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'" (Psalm 14:1; Psalm 53:1). David writes that God looks down from heaven to see if any seek Him, only to find that "there is none who does good, not even one" (Psalm 14:3; Psalm 53:3). This message is so central to understanding our condition that the Apostle Paul quotes these very verses in Romans 3:10–12 to show that every person is a sinner under the judgment of God.

This universal diagnosis of the human heart is intended to lead us to the only cure. Because we are all described as those who have "turned aside" and become "corrupt" (Psalm 14:3), we are incapable of saving ourselves through our own works or moral efforts. Our sin separates us from a holy and just God, but God in His great mercy did not leave us in this state. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfectly righteous life that we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day confirmed His victory over sin and death.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why do Christians believe in proselytization?

Christians believe in sharing their faith, often called evangelism or the Great Commission, because of the direct command of Jesus Christ and the nature of the message itself. Before His ascension into heaven, Jesus told His followers to go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them and teaching them to obey everything He commanded (Matthew 28:18–20). For a believer, this is not merely a suggestion but a joyful response of obedience to their Lord and Savior.

The primary motivation for this is a deep love for God and a sincere love for other people (Matthew 22:37–39). Christians believe that every person is created by God and for God, but that humanity has been separated from Him because of sin (Romans 3:23). This separation leads to spiritual death and a broken relationship with the Creator (Romans 6:23). Because God is holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin, yet because He is merciful and loving, He provided a way for people to be reconciled to Him.

The heart of this message, the Gospel, is that God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died on a cross as a substitute for sinners, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). This victory over sin and death means that forgiveness and eternal life are available as a free gift. Christians believe that salvation is not earned by human effort or good works, but is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8–9).

Because Christians believe that Jesus is the only way to the Father (John 14:6; Acts 4:12), they view sharing this news as an act of compassion. If someone has found the cure for a terminal disease or the way to life and peace, it would be unloving to keep that information to themselves. They see themselves as ambassadors for Christ, as though God were making His appeal through them (2 Corinthians 5:20).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you think about the claim that Jesus is the only way to God?
Why does Saul ask who David is, when he had met him earlier (1 Samuel 17:55)?

The question of why King Saul asks about David’s identity in 1 Samuel 17:55, despite David having previously served as his musician and armor bearer in 1 Samuel 16:14-23, is one of the most discussed points in the historical books of the Bible. While the text does not explicitly state the reason, scholars and students of Scripture have offered several faithful explanations that honor the integrity of the Word of God.

One of the most common perspectives is that Saul was not asking who David was personally, but rather who his father was. In 1 Samuel 17:25, Saul had promised that the man who killed Goliath would receive great wealth, the king’s daughter in marriage, and that his "father’s house" would be made free in Israel. Because this victory involved legal and social changes for David's entire family, such as tax exemptions or noble status, Saul needed to confirm David’s lineage. This is supported by Saul’s specific question in 1 Samuel 17:58, where he asks, "Whose son are you, young man?"

Another explanation considers the nature of Saul’s court and David’s role. In 1 Samuel 16:21, David is mentioned as one of many armor bearers. It is possible that in a royal court with many attendants, and given Saul’s recurring mental and spiritual distress mentioned in 1 Samuel 16:14-15, he did not closely track the family details of every servant. Furthermore, David had been traveling back and forth between his home in Bethlehem and the king’s camp, as noted in 1 Samuel 17:15. During this time, David may have matured from a boy into a young man, or his appearance as a shepherd warrior was significantly different from his appearance as a court musician.

Some also point to the literary structure of 1 Samuel. The author may be emphasizing David’s transition from a private servant to a public hero. Regardless of the specific reason for Saul's question, the narrative highlights how God was sovereignly elevating a humble shepherd to become the leader of His people.

This account reminds us that while man looks at the outward appearance, God looks at the heart, as stated in 1 Samuel 16:7. Just as God chose David, an unlikely candidate, to be the deliverer of Israel, He sent Jesus Christ, a descendant of David, to be the ultimate Deliverer for all humanity. Jesus lived a perfect life, died for our sins, and rose again to reconcile us to God. 

If you find yourself wondering about your own standing before God or how He sees you, I encourage you to turn to Him. You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What thoughts do you have about how God chooses to work through people who seem ordinary?
What does Exodus 23:2 teach us about not following the crowd?

Exodus 23:2 provides a vital principle for the life of a believer, stating, "You shall not fall in with the many to do evil, nor shall you bear witness in a lawsuit, siding with the many, so as to pervert justice" (Exodus 23:2). This command teaches us that truth and morality are not determined by a majority vote, but by God's holy standards.

The verse primarily warns against the danger of peer pressure and the "mob mentality." In a legal or social context, it is often tempting to agree with the crowd to avoid conflict, gain approval, or protect oneself. However, God calls His people to a higher standard of integrity. This passage highlights several key lessons:

First, the majority can be wrong. Because humanity is fallen and prone to sin, the popular path is often the one that leads away from God's will. Scripture reminds us in Romans 3:23 that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God." Therefore, following the "many" often means following a path of rebellion against God.

Second, God prizes justice and truth above social harmony. To pervert justice by siding with a crowd is to offend the character of God, who is perfectly just (Deuteronomy 32:4). Believers are called to be "transformed by the renewal of your mind" rather than being "conformed to this world" (Romans 12:2). This requires the courage to stand alone, as seen in the lives of prophets and apostles who chose to "obey God rather than men" (Acts 5:29).

Third, this verse serves as a reminder of our need for a Savior. We have all, at some point, followed the crowd into sin or remained silent when we should have spoken for truth. This tendency to drift with the world shows our desperate need for the grace found only in Jesus Christ. Jesus was the only person who never followed the crowd into evil. Instead, He perfectly obeyed the Father, even when the crowd turned against Him and demanded His crucifixion.

Jesus died for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day to reconcile us to God. Through His sacrifice, we are not only forgiven for our conformity to the world but are also given the Holy Spirit to empower us to stand firm in the truth. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts to be moral or independent.

If you find yourself struggling with the pressure to follow the crowd, you can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to give you new life and strength. What makes it difficult for you to stand apart from the crowd in your daily life?
Who was King Joash in the Bible?

In the Bible, there are two kings named Joash, though the most prominent is the King of Judah who began his reign as a child.

**King Joash of Judah**
Joash, also known as Jehoash, was the son of Ahaziah and the grandson of the wicked Queen Athaliah. His life began in great danger when Athaliah attempted to destroy the entire royal family to secure the throne for herself. However, his aunt Jehosheba took Joash while he was an infant and hid him in the Temple of the Lord for six years (2 Kings 11:1-3; 2 Chronicles 22:10-12).

At the age of seven, Joash was brought out of hiding by the high priest Jehoiada, who led a revolt against Athaliah and restored the rightful heir to the throne of David (2 Kings 11:12-21). Under the godly mentorship of Jehoiada, Joash did what was right in the eyes of the Lord for many years. His greatest contribution was the significant restoration of the Temple, which had been neglected and damaged during the reigns of previous kings (2 Kings 12:4-15; 2 Chronicles 24:4-14).

Sadly, after the death of Jehoiada, Joash was influenced by ungodly officials and turned to idolatry. When Zechariah, the son of Jehoiada, confronted the king regarding his sin, Joash ordered him to be stoned to death (2 Chronicles 24:17-22). Because of this unfaithfulness, God allowed the Syrian army to defeat Judah, and Joash was eventually assassinated by his own servants while he was recovering from his wounds (2 Kings 12:20-21; 2 Chronicles 24:23-26).

**King Joash of Israel**
The second Joash (or Jehoash) was the grandson of Jehu and ruled the northern kingdom of Israel. Unlike his contemporary in Judah, he did evil in the sight of the Lord by continuing the idolatrous practices of Jeroboam (2 Kings 13:10-11). Despite his disobedience, he showed a level of respect for the prophet Elisha, visiting him and weeping over him when the prophet was near death. He was known for his military success, recovering cities from Syria and even defeating King Amaziah of Judah in battle (2 Kings 13:14-25; 2 Kings 14:8-14).

**A Reflection on the Heart**
The life of Joash of Judah serves as a sobering reminder that a person can perform great religious works without having a heart that is permanently transformed by God. Joash followed the Lord while he had a godly mentor, but his faith did not endure when that support was removed. This points to our deep need for the Gospel. 

A holy God created us for His glory, but we are all separated from Him by our sin. We cannot bridge this gap through our own effort or outward religious reform. However, God, in His mercy, sent Jesus Christ to earth. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and His lordship over all creation. Salvation is not something we earn by being "good" or repairing temples, it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 5:8; 1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the Judgment of Solomon?

The Judgment of Solomon is a famous account found in the Bible in 1 Kings 3:16–28, which illustrates the extraordinary wisdom God granted to King Solomon. 

In this narrative, two women who lived in the same house both gave birth to infants within three days of each other. During the night, one of the babies died because his mother accidentally rolled over him. According to the account, the mother of the deceased child took the living child from the other woman's side while she slept and replaced him with the dead infant. When the second woman woke to nurse her son, she found a dead child, but upon closer inspection in the morning light, she realized it was not her own.

The two women brought their dispute before King Solomon, both claiming to be the mother of the living boy. With no witnesses or DNA evidence, Solomon faced a difficult task in discerning the truth. He called for a sword and gave a startling command: "Divide the living child in two, and give half to the one and half to the other" (1 Kings 3:25).

The reaction of the two women immediately revealed the truth. The real mother, filled with compassion and love for her son, begged the king to give the baby to the other woman rather than see him killed. The other woman, however, said, "He shall be neither mine nor yours; divide him" (1 Kings 3:26). Solomon then declared that the woman who showed mercy was the true mother and ordered that the child be given to her. This event caused all of Israel to stand in awe of the king, for they saw that the wisdom of God was in him to administer justice (1 Kings 3:28).

This story serves as a powerful reminder that God is the source of all true wisdom and that He cares deeply for justice and the protection of the innocent. It also points us toward a greater reality. While Solomon was a wise king, he was a fallen man who could only provide a shadow of the perfect justice and wisdom found in Jesus Christ. 

Scripture tells us that in Christ are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3). Because God is perfectly holy and just, He must judge sin, and we have all fallen short of His standard through our own disobedience. However, in His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die on a cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His sacrifice, He satisfied the justice of God so that we might be forgiven.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is Tisha B'Av?

Tisha B'Av, or the Ninth of Av, is an annual day of fasting and mourning in Judaism. It is often referred to as the saddest day on the Jewish calendar, primarily commemorating the destruction of the First and Second Temples in Jerusalem. According to Jewish tradition, both temples were destroyed on this same date, centuries apart.

The First Temple, built by King Solomon, was destroyed by the Babylonians in 586 BC. This event is chronicled in the Bible in 2 Kings 25:8–10 and 2 Chronicles 36:17–19. The Second Temple was destroyed by the Romans in 70 AD, an event that Jesus Christ specifically prophesied during His earthly ministry in Matthew 24:1–2 and Luke 19:41–44.

On Tisha B'Av, the book of Lamentations is typically read, which expresses the deep grief of the prophet Jeremiah over the fall of Jerusalem. For many, the day also commemorates other tragedies in Jewish history, such as the Bar Kokhba revolt, the Crusades, and the Holocaust. It is a time marked by mourning, repentance, and a longing for God’s presence.

From a Christian perspective, the history of the Temple is deeply significant. While the physical Temple was the center of sacrificial worship and the place where God’s presence dwelt among His people, Jesus revealed that He is the true and final Temple in John 2:19–21. The destruction of the Second Temple points to the transition from the Old Covenant to the New Covenant, where believers no longer need an earthly building to reach God.

The loss of the Temple highlights a profound truth: our sin creates a separation between us and a holy God. However, because of God's great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. His bodily resurrection on the third day proved His victory over death and His authority as Lord. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.

The brokenness we see in history and in our own lives reminds us of our need for a Savior. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should Christians care about physical appearance?

The question of how a Christian should view physical appearance involves a beautiful balance between honoring God’s creation and prioritizing the condition of the heart. Scripture teaches that our bodies are not our own but were created by God and redeemed by Christ. Because the Holy Spirit indwells believers, our bodies are described as temples of the Holy Spirit (1 Corinthians 6:19-20). This reality calls us to a secondary level of stewardship, caring for our physical health and presentation as a way of honoring the God who made us.

At the same time, the Bible consistently emphasizes that internal character is far more significant than external beauty. When the prophet Samuel was looking for a king, God gave him a vital principle: "For the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart" (1 Samuel 16:7). While the world often evaluates a person's worth based on their looks, the Christian is called to find their value in being an image-bearer of God (Genesis 1:27).

Regarding how we present ourselves, the New Testament encourages modesty and self-control. In 1 Timothy 2:9-10, the apostle Paul urges women to adorn themselves in respectable apparel, with modesty and self-control, emphasizing that good works are the proper "adornment" for those professing godliness. Similarly, 1 Peter 3:3-4 teaches that a person's beauty should not be merely external, such as the braiding of hair and the putting on of gold jewelry, but should be the hidden person of the heart with the imperishable beauty of a gentle and quiet spirit, which in God's sight is very precious. 

Caring about appearance becomes a spiritual trap when it turns into vanity, pride, or a source of identity. When we obsess over how we look, we often do so because we are seeking the approval of others rather than the approval of God. This points to a deeper need for the Gospel. The truth is that no amount of physical perfection can bridge the gap caused by our sin between us and a holy God. We are all fallen and in need of a Savior. Jesus Christ, the Son of God, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave to give us new life. 

When we trust in Jesus, we are justified by grace through faith alone, not by our works or our appearance. Our true "attire" becomes the righteousness of Christ (Galatians 3:27). You can turn to God right now by repenting of your sins and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to find a worth that never fades. 

What makes it difficult for you to feel secure in how God has made you?
What is the gospel of inclusion?

The gospel of inclusion is a theological viewpoint, often associated with universalism, which suggests that through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, all people have already been reconciled to God regardless of their personal faith or religious affiliation. Proponents of this view often argue that God's love is so expansive that no one will be eternally separated from Him, and that Christ’s work on the cross automatically covers every human being without the requirement of individual repentance or conscious belief in Jesus.

While the Bible certainly teaches that God desires all people to be saved (1 Timothy 2:4; 2 Peter 3:9), the historic Christian faith, grounded in Scripture, differs from the gospel of inclusion on several essential points.

The central message of the Bible, the true Gospel, reveals that God is perfectly holy and just (Isaiah 6:3; Habakkuk 1:13), and that all people have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin creates a separation between us and God that we cannot bridge on our own. In His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins (John 3:16; 1 Peter 2:24). Jesus was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3, 4).

Scripture teaches that while the offer of salvation is inclusive (it is for everyone who calls on the name of the Lord), the means of salvation is exclusive. Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me" (John 14:6). Furthermore, the Bible consistently emphasizes that this salvation must be received through personal faith and repentance. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or by a universal decree that ignores our response to God (Ephesians 2:8, 9; Romans 10:9, 10).

The gospel of inclusion tends to minimize the necessity of the individual's "turning" to God. However, the Apostles preached that people must "repent and believe in the gospel" (Mark 1:15; Acts 17:30). Without faith in Christ, the Bible warns that a person remains under God’s judgment (John 3:36).

I want to invite you to consider the hope found in Jesus Christ. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of eternal life.

If you are exploring these ideas, it is very helpful to study the Scriptures directly and to speak with a local pastor or elders at a Bible-believing church who can walk through these passages with you. What are your thoughts on the necessity of faith in Jesus for salvation?
What did Paul mean by the “Holy Scriptures” in 2 Timothy 3:15?

When Paul refers to the "Holy Scriptures" in his second letter to Timothy, he is primarily speaking about the Old Testament. At the time this letter was written, the collection of books we now call the New Testament was still being completed and circulated. Timothy, having been raised by a faithful mother and grandmother, would have been tutored in the Hebrew Scriptures (the Law, the Prophets, and the Writings) from his earliest days (2 Timothy 1:5; 2 Timothy 3:15).

Paul’s point is that these sacred writings are not merely historical records or moral guides, but are "able to make you wise for salvation through faith in Christ Jesus" (2 Timothy 3:15). This highlights a vital truth: the Old Testament serves as a witness to the coming Savior. Jesus Himself taught that the Scriptures point directly to Him (Luke 24:27; John 5:39). While we now have the full revelation of God in both the Old and New Testaments, Paul affirms that all of God's Word is breathed out by Him and is profitable for teaching and training in righteousness (2 Timothy 3:16).

The "salvation" Paul mentions is the central message of all Scripture. It begins with the reality that God is holy, supreme, and just (Isaiah 6:3; Revelation 4:8). Because of human sin and rebellion, every person is naturally separated from God and deserves His righteous judgment (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). However, God in His mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). He is now exalted as Lord over all.

This gift of forgiveness and eternal life is received not by our own good works or religious efforts, but by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ (Ephesians 2:8–9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Is the priesthood of all believers biblical?

The doctrine of the priesthood of all believers is a deeply biblical truth that lies at the heart of how Christians relate to God and one another. In the Old Testament, the priesthood was limited to a specific group of people from the tribe of Levi who acted as mediators between God and the nation of Israel. However, through the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, the way we relate to God has been radically transformed.

The New Testament teaches that every person who trusts in Jesus Christ is now part of a "royal priesthood." This is most clearly stated in 1 Peter 2:5, which says, "you also, as living stones, are being built up a spiritual house, a holy priesthood, to offer up spiritual sacrifices acceptable to God through Jesus Christ." A few verses later, 1 Peter 2:9 reinforces this by saying, "But you are a chosen generation, a royal priesthood, a holy nation, His own special people, that you may proclaim the praises of Him who called you out of darkness into His marvelous light."

Being part of this priesthood means two primary things for the believer. First, it means we have direct access to God. We no longer need a human mediator or an earthly priest to speak to God on our behalf because Jesus Christ is our Great High Priest (Hebrews 4:14-16). Through His sacrifice, the curtain of the temple was torn, signifying that the way into the presence of God is open to all who come through faith in Christ. As Hebrews 10:19-22 encourages us, we can now "have boldness to enter the Holiest by the blood of Jesus."

Second, the priesthood of all believers means that every Christian is called to ministry. We are called to "offer up spiritual sacrifices," which include our praise, our resources, and our very lives (Romans 12:1; Hebrews 13:15-16). This does not mean that the local church lacks leaders like pastors or elders, but it does mean that every member of the body of Christ has a vital role to play in serving others and sharing the Gospel. Revelation 1:6 and Revelation 5:10 confirm that Christ has made us "kings and priests to His God and Father."

This beautiful reality is only possible because of the finished work of Jesus. God is perfectly holy, and because of our sin, we were once separated from Him. However, God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is found by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works or merit. By trusting in His sacrifice and resurrection, we are forgiven, justified, and brought into this holy priesthood.

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

What has been your experience with the idea of having direct access to God through prayer and His Word?
Why is there a curse associated with hanging on a tree?

The association between a curse and hanging on a tree finds its roots in the Old Testament Law given to Israel. In the book of Deuteronomy, the law stated that if a person committed a sin worthy of death and was executed, their body was sometimes hung on a tree as a public sign of their rejection by both the community and God. This was not typically the method of execution itself, but a post-mortem display of the person's disgraced state.

Scripture explains this in Deuteronomy 21:22-23, which says, "And if a man has committed a crime punishable by death and he is put to death, and you hang him on a tree, his body shall not remain all night on the tree, but you shall bury him the same day, for a hanged man is cursed by God. You shall not defile your land that the Lord your God is giving you for an inheritance." To the Jewish people, being hung on a tree was the ultimate symbol of being under the divine wrath and curse of God.

This Old Testament concept is essential for understanding the significance of the death of Jesus Christ. The New Testament explains that Jesus, who was perfectly holy and without sin, chose to take this curse upon Himself on our behalf. Because every human being has sinned and fallen short of God’s glory, we are all naturally under the curse of the Law, which is death and eternal separation from a holy God.

However, the Gospel is the good news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and willingly died on a wooden cross (often referred to as a "tree") to pay the penalty for our sins. In Galatians 3:13, it says, "Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, for it is written, 'Cursed is everyone who is hanged on a tree.'" 

By His death and bodily resurrection on the third day, Jesus defeated sin and death, demonstrating His victory and His Lordship. This means that salvation is not something we can earn through our own good works or efforts, but is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. Through His sacrifice, the curse that belonged to us was placed on Him, and His righteousness is given to those who believe.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can we learn to trust God like the prophet Habakkuk (Habakkuk 3:17-19)?

Habakkuk's journey from a place of deep questioning to a place of unshakable joy offers a roadmap for trusting God during our own seasons of trial. In Habakkuk 3:17–19, the prophet describes a total collapse of his economy and food supply, yet he concludes with a song of praise. We can learn to trust God in this same way by focusing on three primary realities found in his experience.

First, we must be honest with God about our struggles. Habakkuk did not begin with praise; he began by crying out to God with his questions and his pain in Habakkuk 1:2–4. Trust is often forged in the fires of honest lament. God is holy and supreme, yet He invites us to bring our burdens to Him, for He is patient and merciful.

Second, we must ground our trust in God’s character rather than our current circumstances. Habakkuk 3:18 says, "yet I will rejoice in the Lord; I will take joy in the God of my salvation." Notice that his joy was not in the harvest, but in the Lord Himself. Even when the fig tree does not blossom, God’s holiness, sovereignty, and goodness remain unchanged. He is the strength that makes our feet like the deer's, enabling us to walk on high places even when the valley is dark (Habakkuk 3:19).

Third, we must remember that the righteous shall live by his faith (Habakkuk 2:4). This faith is not a vague feeling, but a firm reliance on God’s Word and His promises. We have an even greater reason to trust than Habakkuk did, because we see the fulfillment of God's promises in Jesus Christ. If God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, we can be certain He will graciously give us all that we truly need (Romans 8:32).

Ultimately, our ability to trust God in the dark is rooted in the light of the Gospel. Because God is holy and we are separated from Him by our sin, we were once without hope. However, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to reconcile sinners to God. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Why are we supposed to give thanks in everything (1 Thessalonians 5:18)?

Giving thanks in everything is one of the most challenging commands in Scripture, yet it is deeply rooted in the character of God and His purposes for His people. In 1 Thessalonians 5:18, the apostle Paul writes, "give thanks in all circumstances; for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you." This instruction does not mean we must be thankful *for* every specific event, such as suffering or sin, but rather that we are to remain thankful *in* the midst of every situation because of who God is and what He has done.

One primary reason we are called to give thanks is that it honors God's sovereignty. As believers, we trust that God is supreme and good, working all things together for the good of those who love Him and are called according to His purpose, as stated in Romans 8:28. When we give thanks during trials, we are declaring our confidence that God is still on His throne and that He is using our circumstances to refine our faith and conform us to the image of His Son.

Gratitude also protects our hearts from bitterness and pride. It reminds us that every good and perfect gift comes from above, from the Father of lights, as noted in James 1:17. By practicing thankfulness, we acknowledge our dependence on God and shift our focus away from our problems and toward His faithfulness. This perspective allows us to experience the peace of God, which surpasses all understanding, guarding our hearts and minds in Christ Jesus, as described in Philippians 4:6-7.

Ultimately, the deepest foundation for our gratitude is the Gospel. Even when our earthly circumstances are painful, the believer possesses spiritual blessings that can never be taken away. This begins with acknowledging that God is holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as seen in Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we were separated from God, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, according to 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Through Christ, we are offered salvation by grace alone through faith alone. This means that we are justified and reconciled to God not by our own works, but through trusting in the finished work of Jesus. When we realize that we have been rescued from eternal judgment and given the promise of eternal life, we find a reason for gratitude that transcends any temporary trial.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

Who was J. Gresham Machen?

John Gresham Machen was an influential American New Testament scholar and theologian who lived from 1881 to 1937. He is best remembered for his courageous defense of historic Christian orthodoxy during the theological conflicts of the early twentieth century.

Machen taught at Princeton Theological Seminary for over twenty years and became a leading voice against the rise of theological liberalism. In his landmark book, Christianity and Liberalism, published in 1923, he argued that liberalism was not merely a different version of Christianity but an entirely different religion. He believed that while Christianity is founded on the historical facts of what God has done in Jesus Christ, liberalism tended to reduce the faith to a moral program or a subjective religious experience.

His convictions were rooted in the authority of the Bible as the inspired and infallible Word of God (2 Timothy 3:16). When Princeton Seminary shifted toward a more pluralistic direction, Machen helped found Westminster Theological Seminary in 1929 to maintain a commitment to the Reformed tradition. He was also instrumental in forming the Orthodox Presbyterian Church after he was removed from his original denomination for his insistence on doctrinal purity in missions.

Machen’s life and work were motivated by the conviction that all people are sinners separated from a holy God. He taught that the only hope for humanity is the Gospel, which is the good news that Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature, lived a perfect life, and died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy the justice of God (Romans 3:23; Romans 3:24; Romans 3:25; Romans 3:26). Machen emphasized that Jesus Christ rose bodily from the dead on the third day, confirming His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3; 1 Corinthians 15:4).

He stood firmly on the truth that salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any human works or merit (Ephesians 2:8; Ephesians 2:9). Even on his deathbed, Machen sent a telegram to a friend expressing his gratitude for the active obedience of Christ, saying he was "so thankful for the active obedience of Christ; no hope without it."

You can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why did Jesus refer to the temple as “my Father’s house” (John 2:16)?

When Jesus called the temple "my Father's house" in John 2:16, He was making a profound statement about His identity, His authority, and the nature of true worship. This occurs during the cleansing of the temple, where Jesus drove out those who were selling cattle, sheep, and pigeons, and overturned the tables of the money-changers.

By using the specific phrase "my Father," Jesus was asserting a unique and intimate relationship with God that set Him apart from everyone else. While the Jewish people understood God as the Father of the nation of Israel, Jesus claimed a personal, filial bond that pointed to His deity. This claim of divine Sonship gave Him the absolute authority to judge the activities taking place within the temple walls. He was not merely a reformer, but the Son of the house overseeing His Father's affairs.

The temple was designed to be the place where God’s presence dwelt among His people and where they could approach Him through prayer and sacrifice. In 1 Kings 8:10-11, we see that when the first temple was dedicated, the glory of the Lord filled the house. By the time of Jesus' earthly ministry, the outer courts, which were intended for all nations to come and pray, had been turned into a marketplace. Jesus was moved by a holy zeal for God’s honor, as expressed in Psalm 69:9, which says, "For zeal for your house has consumed me." He was restoring the temple to its rightful purpose as a place of holiness and worship rather than a "house of trade" (John 2:16).

Furthermore, this event points toward a significant shift in how God relates to humanity. Later in the same passage, when asked for a sign of His authority, Jesus said, "Destroy this temple, and in three days I will raise it up" (John 2:19). The Gospel writer clarifies in John 2:21 that Jesus was speaking about the temple of His body. This indicates that Jesus Himself is the ultimate dwelling place of God. Through His life, death, and resurrection, He became the true "house" where we meet God.

The holiness of the Father’s house reminds us that God is holy and supreme, yet He desires to dwell with His people. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from this holy God. However, the Gospel tells us that God the Father sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord of all.

Through Christ, the barrier of sin is removed for all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin, and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. When we trust in Him, the Holy Spirit comes to dwell within us, making the believer's own body a temple of the living God as described in 1 Corinthians 6:19-20.
