What does the Bible say about child abuse?

If you or a child you know is in immediate danger, please contact emergency services (such as 911 in the United States) or a local child protective agency immediately. Child abuse is a serious crime as well as a grievous sin. As a Christian guide, I am here to provide biblical perspective and pastoral encouragement, but I do not provide legal, medical, or psychiatric advice. If you are a victim of abuse, I also strongly encourage you to seek support from a trusted pastor, elder, or a certified biblical counselor who can walk with you in safety and healing.

The Bible views child abuse as a profound evil that contradicts God’s character and His design for the family. Scripture teaches that children are a heritage from the Lord and a reward from Him (Psalm 127:3). God expresses deep love for the vulnerable and the small, and He expects those in positions of authority to protect and nurture them.

**God’s Heart for Children**
Jesus Christ displayed a unique tenderness toward children. When His disciples tried to turn them away, Jesus rebuked them, saying, "Let the little children come to me and do not hinder them, for to such belongs the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 19:14). By welcoming them, He showed that children are precious in God’s sight and deserving of care and respect.

**Warnings Against Harm**
The Bible contains some of its most sobering warnings for those who harm children or lead them into sin. Jesus said, "Whoever causes one of these little ones who believe in me to sin, it would be better for him to have a great millstone fastened around his neck and to be drowned in the depth of the sea" (Matthew 18:6). This passage highlights the severity with which God views the mistreatment or spiritual stumbling of a child.

**The Limits of Parental Authority**
While the Bible speaks of the importance of parental guidance and discipline, it strictly forbids harshness, cruelty, or provoking children. Scripture instructs, "Fathers, do not provoke your children to anger, but bring them up in the discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). Similarly, Colossians 3:21 warns, "Fathers, do not provoke your children, lest they become discouraged." Biblical discipline is meant to be an act of sacrificial love and patient instruction, modeled after God’s own fatherly care, never an outlet for selfish anger or violence.

**God as the Defender of the Weak**
God identifies Himself as the protector of those who cannot protect themselves. He is "Father of the fatherless and protector of widows" (Psalm 68:5). He sees every act of hidden cruelty and promises to bring justice. The Psalmist writes, "But you do see, for you note mischief and vexation, that you may take it into your hands; to you the helpless commits himself; you have been the helper of the fatherless" (Psalm 10:14).

**The Hope of the Gospel**
We live in a world deeply broken by sin, and abuse is one of the most painful evidences of that fallenness. Sin separates us from a holy God and leads to the exploitation of others. However, the Gospel provides the only path to true justice and restoration. God, in His holiness and justice, cannot overlook sin, but in His mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord who offers forgiveness and new life to all who come to Him.

Through Christ, God offers healing to the brokenhearted and a promise that He will one day make all things new and judge all unrighteousness. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What is Christianophobia?

Christianophobia refers to an irrational fear, hatred, or prejudice directed toward Christians and Christianity as a whole. This can manifest in various ways, including social exclusion, cultural mockery, legal discrimination, or, in many parts of the world, violent persecution. While the term itself is modern, the experience of facing opposition for one's faith is a central theme in the New Testament.

Jesus explicitly warned His followers that they would encounter hostility from the world. In John 15:18, He told His disciples, "If the world hates you, know that it has hated me before it hated you." He explained that this opposition occurs because the message of the Gospel is not of this world and often challenges the prevailing spirit of the age. Similarly, 2 Timothy 3:12 states that "all who desire to live a godly life in Christ Jesus will be persecuted."

The root of this tension often lies in the exclusive claims of Jesus Christ and the call to repentance. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet every human being has sinned and is separated from Him, as explained in Romans 3:23 and Romans 6:23. Out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to satisfy God’s justice, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord over all creation. Salvation is not earned by being a good person or following religious rules, but is received as a gift by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?

Even when faced with Christianophobia, the Bible instructs believers to respond with the character of Christ. In Matthew 5:44, Jesus says, "But I say to you, Love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you." We are called to share the reason for our hope with gentleness and respect, as encouraged in 1 Peter 3:15, trusting that God is sovereign and remains faithful to His people even in times of trial.
Who was Zadok in the Bible?

Zadok was a prominent high priest in the Old Testament who served during the reigns of King David and King Solomon. He was a descendant of Aaron through the line of Eleazar, 1 Chronicles 6:4-8, and is best known for his unwavering faithfulness to the Lord and the Davidic throne.

Zadok first appears in the biblical narrative as a leader who joined David at Hebron, 1 Chronicles 12:28. He later shared the responsibilities of the priesthood with Abiathar. When David’s son Absalom led a rebellion, Zadok and the Levites sought to carry the Ark of the Covenant with David as he fled Jerusalem. However, David instructed Zadok to return the Ark to the city, trusting in God's sovereignty, 2 Samuel 15:24-29.

One of the most significant moments in Zadok’s life occurred during the transition of power from David to Solomon. While Abiathar supported Adonijah’s attempt to seize the throne, Zadok remained loyal to David’s choice of Solomon. At David’s command, Zadok anointed Solomon as king at Gihon, 1 Kings 1:38-39. Because of his faithfulness, Solomon later appointed Zadok as the sole high priest, replacing Abiathar. This transition fulfilled a prophecy given years earlier against the house of Eli, 1 Kings 2:27; 1 Kings 2:35.

The "sons of Zadok" are specifically highlighted in the book of Ezekiel. In the prophet's vision of a future temple, the descendants of Zadok are the only Levites permitted to approach the Lord and minister in His presence because they remained faithful when the rest of Israel went astray, Ezekiel 40:46; Ezekiel 44:15.

The life of Zadok and the role of the priesthood point us to our need for a mediator between a holy God and sinful humanity. While Zadok was a faithful servant, even the best human priests could only offer temporary sacrifices for sin. This system of priesthood was designed to lead us to Jesus Christ, the ultimate High Priest.

God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are separated from God and deserve His judgment. However, out of His immense love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now exalted as Lord over all. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we can perform.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How can I know that my future sins are forgiven?

The assurance that your future sins are forgiven rests entirely on the sufficiency and finality of Jesus Christ's sacrifice on the cross. When Jesus died for sins, all of your sins were in the future. Scripture teaches that Christ offered for all time a single sacrifice for sins (Hebrews 10:12). This means His work was not a partial payment that requires your future effort to complete, but a finished work that covers the entirety of your life from beginning to end.

In the eyes of God, your legal standing is changed the moment you place your faith in Christ. You are "justified," which is a legal declaration that you are righteous, not because of your own goodness, but because Christ's righteousness has been credited to you (Romans 5:1; 2 Corinthians 5:21). Because this standing is based on what Jesus did and not what you do, there is now no condemnation for those who are in Christ Jesus (Romans 8:1). Even when you stumble, you have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous, who continues to intercede for you (1 John 2:1).

This deep security is rooted in the Gospel message. God is perfectly holy and cannot tolerate sin, yet all of us have sinned and fall short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Our sin separates us from God, but in His mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to reconcile us to Himself. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, demonstrating His victory over sin and death and confirming that His sacrifice was accepted by the Father (1 Corinthians 15:3–4; Romans 4:25).

Salvation and the forgiveness of all your sins are gifts of God's grace. They are received through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by your own works or religious efforts (Ephesians 2:8–9). Because God is the one who began this good work in you, He is the one who is faithful to bring it to completion (Philippians 1:6). You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, for total forgiveness and eternal life.

What makes it difficult for you to feel secure in God's promise of forgiveness?
Book of Malachi - Bible Survey

The book of Malachi serves as the final prophetic word in the Old Testament, written approximately 430 years before the birth of Christ. At this time, the Jewish people had returned from exile and rebuilt the temple, yet they had fallen into spiritual apathy, ritualism, and moral compromise. Malachi, whose name means "my messenger," delivers a series of six disputes between God and His people to call them back to a sincere relationship with their Creator.

The book begins with an affirmation of God’s sovereign love for His people. Despite their hardships, God reminds them of His choosing of Jacob over Esau (Malachi 1:2; Malachi 1:3). This foundation of love makes their subsequent coldness and disobedience even more tragic. The Lord confronts the priests for offering defiled sacrifices, noting that they showed more respect to human governors than to the King of Kings (Malachi 1:6-8).

Malachi also addresses the social and relational sins of the community. He rebukes the men of Israel for being unfaithful to the wives of their youth and for marrying those who worshiped false gods (Malachi 2:10-16). This unfaithfulness in human relationships mirrored their unfaithfulness to God’s covenant. Furthermore, the people were withholding their tithes and offerings, which the Lord characterized as robbing Him (Malachi 3:8-10). God challenged them to test His faithfulness, promising that obedience would lead to an outpouring of blessing.

Central to Malachi’s message is the announcement of the coming "Day of the Lord." He prophesies of a messenger who would prepare the way before the Lord (Malachi 3:1). The New Testament identifies this messenger as John the Baptist, who preceded the ministry of Jesus Christ (Matthew 11:10; Mark 1:2). Malachi describes the Lord’s coming as a refining fire that will purify His people and judge the wicked (Malachi 3:2-5).

The book concludes with a look toward the future, mentioning the "Sun of Righteousness" who will rise with healing in His wings (Malachi 4:2). Malachi promises that God will send the prophet Elijah before the great and awesome day of the Lord arrives to turn the hearts of fathers to their children and children to their fathers (Malachi 4:5; Malachi 4:6).

Malachi highlights the persistent problem of the human heart, we often drift into formal religion while our hearts remain far from God. This internal brokenness and our inability to keep God’s law perfectly point us to our deep need for the Savior whom Malachi promised. Because of our sin and rebellion, we are naturally separated from a holy and just God. However, in His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfectly righteous life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now exalted as Lord over all. 

Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works or religious rituals we perform. You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

As you study Malachi, let it be an encouragement to examine your own heart. God desires more than outward religious performance; He desires a heart that loves Him and fears His name. If you find yourself in a season of spiritual dryness, remember that God is faithful and invites you to return to Him. Daily prayer, the study of His Word, and fellowship with a local church are vital means of grace that God uses to refine us and keep our hearts warm toward Him.
What are phylacteries?

Phylacteries, also known as tefillin in Hebrew, are small, black leather boxes that contain parchment scrolls with specific verses from the Bible. In Jewish tradition, one box is worn on the forehead and the other is worn on the left arm, close to the heart, during morning prayers on weekdays.

The practice comes from a literal interpretation of four passages in the Old Testament: Exodus 13:1-10; Exodus 13:11-16; Deuteronomy 6:4-9; and Deuteronomy 11:13-21. These scriptures command God's people to bind His words as a sign on their hands and as frontlets between their eyes. The symbolic purpose of wearing them is to remind the believer to love God with all their mind (the head) and all their strength or heart (the arm).

In the New Testament, Jesus mentions phylacteries when He speaks to the crowds and His disciples about the scribes and Pharisees. He says, "They do all their deeds to be seen by others. For they make their phylacteries broad and their fringes long" in Matthew 23:5. 

Jesus was not necessarily condemning the wearing of the boxes themselves, but rather the heart behind it. Some religious leaders were making their phylacteries extra large to signal to everyone around them how holy and devoted they were. This serves as a vital reminder for us today that God is always more concerned with the state of our hearts than our outward religious displays. As it says in 1 Samuel 16:7, "For the Lord sees not as man sees: man looks on the outward appearance, but the Lord looks on the heart."

While these physical reminders were intended to help people keep God's law, the Bible teaches that none of us can perfectly keep the law or earn our own righteousness through rituals or good works. Our sin separates us from a holy and just God, but God in His great mercy sent His Son, Jesus Christ. Jesus lived the perfect life we could not live and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over death and His authority as Lord. 

True transformation does not come from binding verses to our skin, but from having God's Word written on our hearts through the Holy Spirit. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What is your understanding of how a person becomes right with God?
Who are true worshipers (John 4:23)?

True worshipers are those who approach God not through mere external rituals or specific physical locations, but through a heart transformed by the Holy Spirit and a mind aligned with the truth of God's Word. Jesus clarifies this in His conversation with the Samaritan woman, explaining that the Father is seeking people who will worship Him in spirit and truth (John 4:23; John 4:24).

To worship in spirit means that our adoration comes from an inner sincerity and a spirit that has been made alive by God. Without the Holy Spirit, we are spiritually dead and unable to offer the worship God desires (John 3:5; Romans 8:9). True worship involves the whole person, where the Holy Spirit enables us to rejoice in Christ Jesus and put no confidence in our own human efforts or rituals (Philippians 3:3).

To worship in truth means worshiping according to the reality of who God has revealed Himself to be in the Bible. It is worship that is centered on Jesus Christ, who is the Way, the Truth, and the Life (John 14:6). We cannot worship God in truth if we ignore His commands or the testimony of His Son. True worshipers are those who have received the truth of the Gospel and respond with lives of gratitude and obedience.

Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God and unable to worship Him rightly. God is perfectly holy, and our rebellion against Him carries a weight we cannot pay. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life, die a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Through His victory over sin and death, He provides the only way for us to be reconciled to the Father. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own religious works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you to receive forgiveness and the new life required to be a true worshiper. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Book of Obadiah - Bible Survey

The Book of Obadiah is the shortest book in the Old Testament, yet it contains a potent message regarding God's sovereign justice and His faithfulness to His covenant people. Though we know very little about the prophet Obadiah, whose name means servant of Yahweh, his message serves as a stark warning against pride and a profound comfort to those who suffer.

The historical context of the book centers on the relationship between Israel and Edom. The Edomites were the descendants of Esau, while the Israelites were the descendants of his twin brother, Jacob. Despite this familial bond, a long history of enmity existed between the two nations. When Jerusalem was attacked and plundered, the Edomites did not help their brothers. Instead, they stood by, rejoiced in Judah's misfortune, and even captured fugitives to hand them over to the enemy. This betrayal is the primary reason for the divine judgment announced by Obadiah.

The book can be understood through three primary movements:

**The Judgment of Edom**
The prophecy begins by addressing the mountain dwelling Edomites who felt secure in their rocky fortresses. Their pride had deceived them into thinking they were invincible. God declares through the prophet that He will bring them down from their heights. Obadiah 1:3 says, "The pride of your heart has deceived you, you who live in the clefts of the rock, in your lofty dwelling, who say in your heart, 'Who will bring me down to the ground?'" This reminds us that God opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble, as seen in 1 Peter 5:5.

**The Crimes of Edom**
Obadiah lists the specific sins of Edom, highlighting their lack of brotherly compassion. They looked on the day of their brother's misfortune with gloating and participated in the looting of Jerusalem. Obadiah 1:10 states, "Because of the violence done to your brother Jacob, shame shall cover you, and you shall be cut off forever." This section emphasizes that God takes notice of how we treat others, especially those in distress.

**The Day of the Lord and the Kingdom of God**
The focus shifts from a local judgment on Edom to a universal judgment on all nations. The "Day of the Lord" is a recurring biblical theme describing a time when God intervenes in human history to judge sin and establish His rule. Obadiah 1:15 warns, "For the day of the Lord is near upon all the nations. As you have done, it shall be done to you; your deeds shall return on your own head." 

However, the book ends with a note of hope for the house of Jacob. While Edom would face destruction, God's people would find deliverance on Mount Zion. The final verse, Obadiah 1:21, declares, "Saviors shall go up to Mount Zion to rule Mount Esau, and the kingdom shall be the Lord's." This points forward to the ultimate restoration where God's righteous rule is established over all the earth.

As we reflect on Obadiah, we see a God who is holy and just, who will not allow sin and betrayal to go unpunished. Yet, we also see a God who is a refuge for His people. This tension between God's justice and His mercy is perfectly resolved in the person and work of Jesus Christ. 

The Bible teaches that all people have sinned and fallen short of God’s glory, deserving judgment much like the nations described in Obadiah, as noted in Romans 3:23. But because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose from the grave to defeat death. Through His sacrifice, the judgment we deserve was placed on Him, and the righteousness He earned is offered to us as a gift.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. Have you ever considered what it means to find refuge in God's grace rather than your own strength?

If you are walking through a season of trial or feel the weight of injustice, let the message of Obadiah encourage you. God is sovereign over the nations, He sees your struggles, and He is faithful to His promises. I encourage you to spend time in prayer, bringing your heart to Him, and perhaps seek out a local fellowship of believers where you can grow in the knowledge of His Word. Romans 10:13 reminds us that "everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved."
What are the musical terms found in the Bible, and what do they mean?

The Bible contains several musical terms, primarily found in the titles and text of the Psalms. These terms provide insight into how the people of God used music to express their worship, lament, and joy. While some technical meanings have been lost over time, biblical scholars and linguists provide these general understandings based on the Hebrew context.

**Selah**
This is the most common musical term, appearing over seventy times in the Psalms and three times in the book of Habakkuk. It most likely indicates a pause, a musical interlude, or a moment for the congregation to stop and reflect on the words that were just spoken or sung (Psalm 3:2; Psalm 3:4; Habakkuk 3:3; Habakkuk 3:9).

**Maskil**
Found in the titles of thirteen Psalms, this term refers to a contemplative or didactic poem. It suggests a song designed to provide wisdom, insight, or instruction to the listener (Psalm 32:1; Psalm 42:1; Psalm 78:1).

**Miktam**
This term appears in the titles of six Psalms. While its exact meaning is debated, it is often translated as a "golden poem" or a song of deep personal devotion and prayer. It may also refer to a song intended to be engraved or permanently remembered (Psalm 16:1; Psalm 56:1; Psalm 60:1).

**Shiggaion**
This term appears in the title of Psalm 7 and in Habakkuk 3:1. It likely refers to a song of intense emotion, characterized by a wandering or erratic rhythm, conveying great passion or distress (Psalm 7:1; Habakkuk 3:1).

**Neginoth**
This word is a musical direction found in several Psalm titles indicating that the song should be accompanied by stringed instruments, such as the harp or lyre (Psalm 4:1; Psalm 6:1; Psalm 67:1).

**Nehiloth**
Appearing only in the title of Psalm 5, this term refers to wind instruments, most likely pipes or flutes (Psalm 5:1).

**Alamoth**
This term likely refers to high-pitched or soprano voices, as the word itself is related to the Hebrew word for "maidens." It suggests a song meant to be sung in a higher register (1 Chronicles 15:20; Psalm 46:1).

**Sheminith**
Meaning "the eighth," this term likely refers to a lower octave or a bass range. It provides a contrast to Alamoth, indicating that the music should be played or sung by lower voices (1 Chronicles 15:21; Psalm 6:1; Psalm 12:1).

**Gittith**
This term may refer to a specific musical instrument from the city of Gath, or a melody or rhythm associated with the vintage and winepress (Psalm 8:1; Psalm 81:1; Psalm 84:1).

**Mizmor**
Used in many Psalm titles, this is the Hebrew word for "Psalm." It refers to a melody or a song that is specifically accompanied by an instrument (Psalm 3:1; Psalm 4:1; Psalm 5:1).

Music is a beautiful gift that God uses to help us express our praise to Him. However, the most important "song" we can ever know is the message of the Gospel. God is holy and just, but because of our sin, we are separated from Him. In His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was raised bodily on the third day. We are justified by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by any works of our own.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting in Him today?
What does it mean to gain the whole world but lose your soul?

This profound question comes from the words of Jesus during His teachings on the cost of following Him. He asked, "For what will it profit a man if he gains the whole world and forfeits his soul? Or what shall a man give in return for his soul?" in Matthew 16:26. To gain the whole world describes the pursuit of everything this earthly life offers, such as immense wealth, fame, power, and the fulfillment of every physical desire. It represents the pinnacle of worldly success and self-centered achievement.

However, Jesus points out that this gain is temporary and ultimately hollow if it comes at the expense of one's soul. The soul is the eternal part of a person that outlasts physical life. To "lose" or "forfeit" the soul means to be eternally separated from God and to face His righteous judgment for sin. Jesus is teaching that the entire world, with all its riches, is an insufficient payment to buy back a life once it is lost to eternity. No amount of earthly treasure can provide security against the reality of death and the judgment that follows, as noted in Hebrews 9:27.

The reason this trade is so tragic is rooted in our spiritual condition. God is perfectly holy and just, but every human being has sinned and fallen short of His glory, according to Romans 3:23. Because of our sin, we are naturally separated from God. No level of worldly success or moral effort can bridge that gap or save our souls.

The good news of the Gospel is that what we cannot do for ourselves, God has done for us. Out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then willingly died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice, taking the punishment for our sins upon Himself. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death, as recorded in 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Salvation and the preservation of your soul are not earned by your works or your status in this world, but are gifts received through grace alone by faith alone in Jesus Christ alone, as explained in Ephesians 2:8-9. To have eternal life is to know God through His Son, as Jesus says in John 17:3.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

When you consider the temporary nature of things like money or reputation compared to the eternity of your soul, what do you find is the biggest obstacle in trusting Jesus completely?
Why did God send the flood when He knew sin would continue after the flood?

The question of why God would send a global judgment like the flood when He knew sin would persist touches on the very heart of His justice, His holiness, and His plan for redemption. To understand this, we must look at what the Scriptures tell us about God’s character and His purposes in history.

First, the Bible describes the world before the flood as being in a state of total moral collapse. Genesis 6:5 says that "the wickedness of man was great in the earth, and that every intent of the thoughts of his heart was only evil continually." The earth was "filled with violence" according to Genesis 6:11. God, being perfectly holy and just, cannot simply ignore sin. His judgment in the flood was a necessary response to the extreme rebellion and cruelty of that time. It demonstrated that God is the sovereign Judge of all the earth and that sin carries a heavy consequence (Psalm 145:17; Romans 6:23).

Second, the flood was an act of preservation. By saving Noah and his family, God preserved a remnant of humanity. If sin had continued unchecked without this intervention, the line through which the promised Savior would eventually come might have been lost. God had promised in Genesis 3:15 that a descendant of the woman would one day crush the head of the serpent. The flood allowed God to "reset" human history while keeping His redemptive promise alive.

Third, the flood serves as a powerful illustration for all generations. It shows us that while God is patient, His judgment is certain (2 Peter 2:5; 2 Peter 3:5-7). However, it also shows us the way of escape. Just as Noah and his family were saved from the water by being inside the ark, we are told that there is a way to be saved from the coming final judgment.

The fact that sin continued after the flood, as seen in Genesis 8:21, proves that an external washing of the world cannot fix the internal problem of the human heart. This points us to our desperate need for a deeper solution. While the flood judged sin, it did not remove the sinful nature. Only the work of Jesus Christ can do that.

God is perfectly holy, and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). This sin separates us from Him. Because God is just, He must punish sin, but because He is loving, He provided a Substitute. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect, sinless life that we could never live. He then took the punishment we deserved by dying a substitutionary death on the cross. He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Salvation is not something we earn by being good; it is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Jesus Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should a church tithe 10% of the offerings it receives?

The question of whether a local church should tithe ten percent of its income is a matter of wisdom and stewardship rather than an explicit biblical command. While the Bible provides clear instructions for individuals regarding giving, it does not explicitly mandate that a corporate church body must tithe ten percent of the offerings it receives to another organization or ministry.

In the Old Testament, the tithe was a requirement for the people of Israel to support the Levites and the temple (Leviticus 27:30; Numbers 18:21–24). In the New Testament, the emphasis shifts from a specific percentage to the principle of cheerful, sacrificial, and proportional giving. Believers are encouraged to give according to what they have decided in their hearts, for God loves a cheerful giver (2 Corinthians 9:7).

For a local church, the primary responsibility is to be a faithful steward of the resources God provides through the congregation (1 Corinthians 4:2). This includes providing for the ministry of the Word, supporting pastors, caring for the poor, and advancing the Gospel (1 Timothy 5:17–18; Galatians 2:10). Many churches choose to "tithe" or set aside a percentage of their budget for missions, church planting, or benevolence. This is often done to model generosity to the congregation and to express a dependence on God rather than on financial reserves. 

The New Testament records churches supporting one another and sending gifts to help those in need or to support missionary journeys (Philippians 4:15–16; 2 Corinthians 8:1–5). While this demonstrates a spirit of corporate generosity, it is a matter of Christian liberty and local church governance rather than a legalistic requirement. Church leaders should seek the Lord in prayer and look to the needs of their community and the global mission of the church when deciding how to allocate funds.

Ultimately, our giving, whether individual or corporate, is a response to the overwhelming grace of God. The Bible teaches that God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, defeating death (1 Corinthians 15:3–4). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works or financial contributions (Ephesians 2:8–9). 

You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life (Romans 10:9). If you have questions about what it means to follow Jesus or how to trust Him, what is holding you back from placing your faith in Him today?
Easter Bible reading plan

Easter is the cornerstone of the Christian faith, as it celebrates the historical reality of Jesus Christ's victory over sin and death. To help you reflect on these life-changing events, here is an eight day reading plan that follows the journey of Jesus from His triumphal entry into Jerusalem to His glorious resurrection.

**Day 1: The Triumphal Entry**
Read Matthew 21:1-11.
Reflect on how Jesus entered Jerusalem as the promised King, yet He came in humility.

**Day 2: Authority and Cleansing**
Read Mark 11:12-19.
Jesus demonstrates His holiness and authority by cleansing the temple, reminding us that God desires true worship from the heart.

**Day 3: Lessons and Warnings**
Read Matthew 22:15-46.
Jesus engages with the religious leaders, showing His supreme wisdom and confirming that He is the Son of David and the Lord of all.

**Day 4: A Beautiful Act of Devotion**
Read Matthew 26:1-13.
While others plotted His death, a woman anointed Jesus with expensive oil, preparing Him for His burial and showing deep love for her Savior.

**Day 5: The Last Supper and Gethsemane**
Read Luke 22:7-62.
Jesus establishes the New Covenant during the Passover meal and then prays in agony in the garden, submitting Himself to the Father's will for our sake.

**Day 6: The Crucifixion**
Read John 19:1-42.
Jesus, the innocent Lamb of God, suffers and dies on the cross. His sacrificial death is the only sufficient payment to reconcile sinners to a holy God.

**Day 7: The Quiet of the Tomb**
Read Matthew 27:57-66.
As Jesus lay in the tomb, the world seemed dark, but God's plan of redemption was reaching its climax.

**Day 8: The Resurrection**
Read Luke 24:1-12.
The tomb is empty! Christ has risen bodily from the grave, confirming His divinity and securing eternal life for all who believe in Him.

The message of Easter is the heart of the Gospel. It begins with the reality that God is perfectly holy, yet every human being has sinned and is separated from Him (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because of His great mercy and love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose again, demonstrating His lordship and victory over death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). This salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. As you read through these passages, consider what Jesus has done for you. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you are already a believer, I encourage you to use this week to rest in God's grace. You might consider joining a local church for their Easter services or spending time in prayer, thanking God for the "living hope" we have through the resurrection of Jesus Christ (1 Peter 1:3).
What is ignosticism? What is an ignostic?

Ignosticism, also known as igtheism, is the philosophical position that the word "God" does not have a coherent or unambiguous definition. An ignostic argues that until a clear, falsifiable, and verifiable definition of God is provided, any discussion about whether God exists or does not exist is essentially meaningless.

An ignostic is a person who views the term "God" as a word without a specific, agreed-upon meaning. While an atheist might say "God does not exist" and an agnostic might say "I do not know if God exists," an ignostic says "I do not even know what you are talking about when you use the word 'God'." They believe that because different people and religions define the divine in contradictory ways, the word itself has become cognitively empty.

From a Christian perspective, we understand that God is not an abstract concept or a linguistic puzzle for us to solve. Rather, God is the supreme, holy, and sovereign Creator who has chosen to reveal Himself to humanity. We believe that God is one, eternally existing in three persons, the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit (Matthew 28:19; 2 Corinthians 13:14). He is not hidden behind vague definitions, but has spoken clearly through His Word, the Bible, which is our ultimate authority for faith and practice (2 Timothy 3:16-17; Psalm 119:105).

The most significant way God has defined Himself for us is through the person of Jesus Christ. The Bible teaches that Jesus is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). In the incarnation, the invisible God became visible and knowable. Jesus said, "Whoever has seen me has seen the Father" (John 14:9). Through His perfect life, His miracles, and His teaching, Jesus provided the ultimate definition of who God is and what He is like (Colossians 1:15; Hebrews 1:3).

This revelation of God is not just for our intellectual curiosity but for our salvation. Because of our sin, every human being is naturally separated from our holy Creator (Romans 3:23; Isaiah 59:2). However, God is also merciful and loving. He sent Jesus Christ to die a substitutionary, atoning death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. Christ was buried and rose bodily from the dead on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 4:25).

Salvation and a true knowledge of God are not found through human definitions or philosophical arguments, but are secured by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). God invites everyone to move beyond intellectual uncertainty into a personal relationship with Him. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is synthetic parallelism in Hebrew poetry?

Synthetic parallelism is a common feature in the poetry of the Bible, particularly in books like Psalms, Proverbs, and Job. While Hebrew poetry does not typically rely on rhyme or strict meter like much of English poetry, it is built on the foundation of parallelism, which is the balancing of one thought against another.

In synthetic parallelism, the second line of a verse does not repeat the thought of the first line (synonymous parallelism) or contrast with it (antithetic parallelism). Instead, the second line builds upon, completes, or develops the thought introduced in the first line. It is sometimes called constructive parallelism because the lines work together to create a single, unified idea. The second line might provide a reason, a result, or a further description that carries the initial thought to its conclusion.

A clear example can be found in Psalm 19:7, which says, "The law of the LORD is perfect, reviving the soul." The first part of the verse makes a statement about the nature of God's Word, and the second part completes the thought by describing the effect that the Word has on a person. Another example is seen in Psalm 2:6, where God says, "As for me, I have set my King on Zion, my holy hill." Here, the second line provides the specific location that completes the action described in the first line.

This structure reminds us that God's revelation is often layered and progressive. Just as the second line of a poem completes the first, God's plan for humanity is completed in the person and work of Jesus Christ. The Scriptures describe God as holy, supreme, and just, yet we recognize that our own sin has separated us from Him (Isaiah 59:2; Romans 3:23). Because of His great mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross (John 1:1; John 1:14; 1 Peter 2:24). By His bodily resurrection on the third day, He proved His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

We are not saved by our own efforts or by trying to complete our own righteousness, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). This beautiful truth is the "completion" of the story that begins with our need and ends with His sufficiency. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does it mean that Jesus is the Lord of the Sabbath?

When Jesus identifies Himself as the Lord of the Sabbath, He is making a profound claim about His divine authority and His identity as God. This statement is found in the accounts of Matthew 12:8, Mark 2:28, and Luke 6:5.

In the context of these passages, Jesus and His disciples were being criticized by the Pharisees for picking heads of grain to eat as they walked through a field on the Sabbath. The Pharisees had created a complex system of oral traditions and regulations that turned the Sabbath into a day of legalistic burden rather than the day of rest and blessing that God intended. By declaring Himself Lord of the Sabbath, Jesus was teaching several essential truths.

First, He was claiming equality with God. Since God is the one who established the Sabbath at creation and commanded its observance in the Ten Commandments, only God has the authority to define its true meaning and application (Genesis 2:2-3; Exodus 20:8-11). By asserting His Lordship over it, Jesus was revealing that He is the Lawgiver Himself.

Second, Jesus was restoring the Sabbath to its proper purpose. He explained that the Sabbath was made for the benefit of man, not man for the Sabbath (Mark 2:27). He demonstrated that it is always lawful to do good, to show mercy, and to heal on the Sabbath, because the day was intended to reflect God's compassion and provision (Matthew 12:11-12).

Third, Jesus was pointing to Himself as the ultimate source of rest. The physical rest of the Sabbath was a shadow of the spiritual rest that is found only in Christ. He invites all who are weary and burdened to come to Him for rest for their souls (Matthew 11:28-30). This rest is not something we earn through our own efforts or religious observances, but something we receive through His finished work.

This points to the core of the Gospel message. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all sinned and fallen short of His standard, separating ourselves from Him. Jesus Christ, the eternal Son of God, took on human nature to live the perfect life we could not live. He died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. He is now exalted as Lord over all creation. Salvation is a gift of God's grace, received through faith in Jesus Christ alone, not by our own works or law-keeping.

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and eternal life.

What holds you back from trusting Him as your Lord and Savior today?
What does it mean that Jesus Christ conquered death?

Jesus Christ conquering death means that He has triumphed over the ultimate consequence of sin, providing a way for us to be reconciled to God and live forever with Him.

To understand this victory, we must first look at the problem of sin. God is perfectly holy and just, but humanity has turned away from Him. This rebellion, which the Bible calls sin, brought death into the world. Romans 5:12. This death is not just the end of our physical lives, but a spiritual separation from the God who is the source of all life. Romans 6:23.

Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, came to earth to solve this problem. He lived a life of perfect righteousness, never once sinning. In His great love, He went to the cross to serve as our substitute. There, He took the weight of our sin and the judgment we deserved upon Himself. 2 Corinthians 5:21; 1 Peter 2:24. He was buried in a tomb, confirming that He had truly died.

However, the grave could not hold Him. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the dead, demonstrating His power over sin and death. Romans 1:4; Acts 2:24. His resurrection is a historical fact that proves He is the Lord and that His sacrifice was sufficient to pay for our sins. 1 Corinthians 15:3-4.

Because Jesus conquered death, it no longer has the final word for those who belong to Him. He has removed the fear of death by defeating the one who had the power of death, which is the devil. Hebrews 2:14-15. For the believer, death is now a defeated enemy and a doorway into the presence of the Lord. 1 Corinthians 15:54-57. We have the hope of our own future resurrection because Jesus is the "firstfruits" of those who have fallen asleep. 1 Corinthians 15:20.

This salvation is a gift from God. It is secured by Christ’s historical death and resurrection and is received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own good works or efforts. Ephesians 2:8-9. Jesus Himself said, "I am the way, and the truth, and the life. No one comes to the Father except through me." John 14:6.

You can turn to God now. I invite you to repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, to receive forgiveness and new life. 

What holds you back from trusting Him as your Savior today?
What was Nehushtan?

Nehushtan was the name given to the bronze serpent that Moses had crafted in the wilderness, which King Hezekiah eventually destroyed during his reforms.

The origin of this object is found in Numbers 21:4–9. While the Israelites were traveling through the desert, they grew impatient and spoke against God and Moses. In response, God sent venomous snakes among the people, and many died. When the people confessed their sin and asked for mercy, God instructed Moses to make a bronze snake and put it on a pole. Anyone who had been bitten could look at the bronze snake and live. It was a physical symbol of God's provision, healing, and the requirement of faith.

However, over the following centuries, the Israelites began to treat this memorial as an object of worship. By the time of King Hezekiah's reign, the people were offering incense to it. In 2 Kings 18:4, we read that Hezekiah broke it into pieces. He called it Nehushtan, a Hebrew term that means "a piece of bronze" or "merely bronze." By giving it this name, Hezekiah was stripping away the superstitious reverence the people had for it, reminding them that the object itself had no power; only God had the power to save and heal.

This account serves as a solemn reminder that even things God once used for a good purpose can become idols if we fix our eyes on the gift rather than the Giver.

The bronze serpent also points us forward to the work of Jesus Christ. In John 3:14–15, Jesus says, "And as Moses lifted up the serpent in the wilderness, so must the Son of Man be lifted up, that whoever believes in him may have eternal life." 

Just as the Israelites were dying from the venom of snakes, all of humanity is separated from God by the sting of sin, which leads to spiritual death (Romans 3:23; Romans 6:23). Because God is holy and just, He cannot ignore sin, yet because He is merciful and loving, He provided a way for us to be reconciled to Him. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. 

We are not saved by our own works or by religious objects, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone. You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What are the books of the Bible? What does it mean that the Bible is composed of different books?

The Bible is a collection of sixty-six individual books, divided into two major sections: the Old Testament and the New Testament.

The thirty-nine books of the Old Testament are: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy, Joshua, Judges, Ruth, 1 Samuel, 2 Samuel, 1 Kings, 2 Kings, 1 Chronicles, 2 Chronicles, Ezra, Nehemiah, Esther, Job, Psalms, Proverbs, Ecclesiastes, Song of Solomon, Isaiah, Jeremiah, Lamentations, Ezekiel, Daniel, Hosea, Joel, Amos, Obadiah, Jonah, Micah, Nahum, Habakkuk, Zephaniah, Haggai, Zechariah, and Malachi.

The twenty-seven books of the New Testament are: Matthew, Mark, Luke, John, Acts, Romans, 1 Corinthians, 2 Corinthians, Galatians, Ephesians, Philippians, Colossians, 1 Thessalonians, 2 Thessalonians, 1 Timothy, 2 Timothy, Titus, Philemon, Hebrews, James, 1 Peter, 2 Peter, 1 John, 2 John, 3 John, Jude, and Revelation.

That the Bible is composed of different books means it is a diverse library of literature written by approximately forty different human authors over a span of about 1,500 years. These authors came from various backgrounds, including kings, shepherds, fishermen, and a doctor. They wrote in different genres, such as historical narrative, poetry, prophecy, and personal letters.

Despite this diversity, Christians believe the Bible has one ultimate Author. God the Holy Spirit superintended the human writers so that while they used their own styles and personalities, the words they wrote were exactly what God intended (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:20-21). Because of this divine inspiration, the Bible is the infallible and authoritative Word of God.

The most remarkable aspect of the Bible being composed of many books is its unified message. From Genesis to Revelation, the scriptures tell one continuous story of God's relationship with humanity. This story reveals that God is perfectly holy (Isaiah 6:3) and that all human beings have sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). Because of our sin, we are separated from God, but the Bible reveals His plan to rescue us through His Son, Jesus Christ.

The center of the Bible's message is the Gospel: Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died on a cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily from the dead on the third day (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). Through His sacrifice, He offers forgiveness and eternal life to all who believe. Salvation is a gift of God's grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now—repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the creation mandate / cultural mandate?

The creation mandate, often called the cultural mandate, is the first commission given by God to humanity. It is rooted in the account of creation found in the first chapter of the Bible. After creating man and woman in His own image, God blessed them and gave them specific instructions for their life on earth.

In Genesis 1:28, the Scripture says, "And God blessed them. And God said to them, 'Be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth and subdue it, and have dominion over the fish of the sea and over the birds of the heavens and over every living thing that moves on the earth.'" This command establishes that human beings are meant to be God’s representatives and stewards over all that He has made.

There are two primary aspects to this mandate:

1.  **The Social Aspect:** The command to "be fruitful and multiply and fill the earth" highlights the importance of family, procreation, and the building of human society. It suggests that God intends for the earth to be populated by people who reflect His image and live in community with one another.
2.  **The Cultural Aspect:** The command to "subdue it, and have dominion" calls humanity to engage with the natural world. This is not a license for exploitation, but a call to careful stewardship. It involves unfolding the potential of creation through work, science, technology, art, and governance. When we cultivate a garden, write a poem, or develop medicine, we are participating in the cultural mandate by bringing order and beauty to the world God provided.

Even though humanity fell into sin, as described in Genesis 3:1-24, this mandate was not rescinded. However, because of sin, our work is now marked by toil and our relationships by conflict. We often seek to rule for our own glory rather than for the glory of God.

This is why we need the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all turned away from Him and failed in our calling as His stewards. Because of our sin, we are separated from Him and deserve His judgment. But God, in His great love, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live the perfect life we could not live and to die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins. He rose bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death, and He is now the exalted Lord of all creation.

Through Jesus, we are not only forgiven but also restored to a right relationship with God. This restoration allows us to pursue the creation mandate with a new heart, seeking to honor God in our families, our jobs, and our communities. Salvation is a gift of grace that we receive through faith alone in Christ alone, not by our own efforts or cultural achievements.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

If you are already a believer, remember that your daily work and your interactions with others are not secular or meaningless. God refines His people in every calling. Whether you are raising a family, studying, or working in an office, you can do so as an act of worship to the Lord, as encouraged in Colossians 3:23-24. For further study on how we are to live in light of God's Word, you might consider reading through the book of Genesis or the letter to the Colossians.
What does it mean that friendship with the world is enmity with God (James 4:4)?

In the passage of James 4:4, the author uses striking language to describe the spiritual danger of aligning ourselves with the values and priorities of a fallen world. To understand this verse, it is helpful to first define what the Bible means by the world. In this context, it does not refer to the physical earth or the people who live on it, whom we are commanded to love and serve. Instead, the world refers to a spiritual system and a mindset that operates in rebellion against God, characterized by pride, self-sufficiency, and the pursuit of sinful desires.

When James speaks of friendship with the world, he is describing a heart that seeks approval, comfort, and identity from this rebellious system. It involves adopting the world's standards of success, its morality, and its focus on the temporary rather than the eternal. James calls his readers spiritual adulterers because, as believers, our primary loyalty and love are meant to belong to God alone. To cultivate a deep, identifying friendship with a system that rejects God is to place ourselves in opposition to Him. This is why he says that such friendship is enmity, or active hostility, toward God.

The reason for this sharp distinction is that the world’s values are fundamentally at odds with the nature of God. While the world encourages self-exaltation, God calls for humility (James 4:6). While the world pursues the gratification of the flesh, God calls for holiness (1 Peter 1:15-16). You cannot walk in two opposite directions at the same time. As Jesus taught, no one can serve two masters, for he will either hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other (Matthew 6:24).

This warning reveals a deeper reality about our human condition. Because of sin, we are all naturally inclined to seek fulfillment apart from God, which effectively makes us His enemies (Romans 5:10; Ephesians 2:1-3). Our attempts to find peace or meaning in the world's systems only deepen our separation from our holy Creator.

The good news of the Gospel is that while we were still enemies, God acted in love to reconcile us to Himself. God is perfectly holy and must judge sin, yet He is also rich in mercy. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life of total devotion to the Father, never once succumbing to the friendship of the world. Jesus died on the cross as a substitutionary sacrifice, taking the wrath we deserved for our rebellion. He was buried, and on the third day, He rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Through Jesus, the enmity is removed. We are not saved by our ability to keep ourselves unspotted from the world, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9). When we trust in Him, we are justified and given a new heart that desires to please God rather than the world.

If you find your heart pulled toward the world's promises, remember that they are temporary and cannot satisfy. You can turn to God now, repent of sin, and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the biggest challenge in choosing God's ways over the world's influence?

If you are struggling with these things, it is helpful to remember that sanctification is a process. God provides the means of grace to help us grow, such as the regular reading of His Word (Psalm 119:11), persistent prayer (Philippians 4:6-7), and the fellowship of a local church where elders and fellow believers can offer guidance and accountability (Hebrews 10:24-25). He does not leave us to fight worldliness in our own strength, but gives us His Holy Spirit to empower us (Galatians 5:16).
Why did God harden Pharaoh’s heart?

The question of why God hardened Pharaoh’s heart is one that leads us deep into the mystery of God’s sovereignty and human responsibility. In the book of Exodus, we see a complex interaction where the Bible states both that Pharaoh hardened his own heart (Exodus 8:15; Exodus 8:32) and that God hardened Pharaoh’s heart (Exodus 4:21; Exodus 7:3).

One primary reason God hardened Pharaoh’s heart was to demonstrate His supreme power and to ensure that His name would be proclaimed throughout the entire earth. God told Pharaoh directly through Moses, "But for this purpose I have raised you up, to show you my power, so that my name may be proclaimed in all the earth" (Exodus 9:16). By allowing Pharaoh to persist in his rebellion, God multiplied His signs and wonders, proving that He alone is the Lord, far above the false gods of Egypt (Exodus 7:5; Exodus 12:12).

It is also important to recognize that Pharaoh was not an innocent man whom God forced to sin. Pharaoh was a prideful tyrant who had already enslaved the Israelites and ordered the death of their children. When God hardened his heart, it was often a judicial act, meaning God gave Pharaoh over to the stubbornness and wickedness that was already in his heart (Romans 1:24; Romans 9:17-18). This served as a form of divine judgment against his cruelty and his defiance of God’s commands.

Furthermore, this hardening served to strengthen the faith of the Israelites. As they witnessed God’s absolute control over the most powerful ruler on earth, they learned that their deliverance did not depend on Pharaoh’s permission, but on God’s sovereign hand (Exodus 10:1-2).

This reminds us of the holiness of God and the seriousness of our own sin. We are all, by nature, like Pharaoh, prone to hardening our hearts against God's authority. Because God is holy and just, our sin separates us from Him and deserves His righteous judgment. However, the heart of the Gospel is that God did not leave us in our stubbornness. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death.

Through Christ, God offers to take away our "heart of stone" and give us a "heart of flesh" (Ezekiel 36:26). Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you, receiving His forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
How should we treat our enemies according to Proverbs 25:21–22?

Proverbs 25:21–22 instructs us to treat our enemies with unexpected and practical kindness. The passage says, "If your enemy is hungry, give him bread to eat, and if he is thirsty, give him water to drink; for you will heap burning coals on his head, and the Lord will reward you" (Proverbs 25:21; Proverbs 25:22). This means that when someone opposes us or seeks our harm, our response should not be retaliation, but rather a sincere effort to meet their basic needs.

The imagery of heaping "burning coals" on an enemy's head is often understood as the sense of shame or the prick of conscience a person feels when they are met with grace instead of the hostility they expect. By showing mercy, you may lead them to realize the wrongness of their actions and perhaps even bring about a change in their heart. Ultimately, this approach moves the situation out of your hands and into God's, trusting that He is the righteous Judge who rewards those who act in obedience to His Word.

Jesus echoed this principle in the New Testament when He commanded His followers to love their enemies and pray for those who persecute them (Matthew 5:44). The Apostle Paul also quoted this specific proverb to illustrate how believers should overcome evil with good (Romans 12:20; Romans 12:21).

This radical love is only possible because of the grace God has shown to us. The Bible teaches that while we were still sinners and enemies of God, Christ died for us to reconcile us to Himself (Romans 5:8; Romans 5:10). God is holy and just, and our sin separates us from Him, but in His great mercy, He sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works.

You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. Is there someone in your life right now whom you find especially difficult to treat with this kind of biblical kindness?
What does the Bible say about indecision / being indecisive?

The Bible addresses indecision by pointing us toward the character of God, the gift of wisdom, and the necessity of faith. Often, indecisiveness stems from a fear of making the wrong choice or a lack of confidence in God’s providential care. Scripture encourages us to move from a state of wavering to a state of trustful action.

One of the most direct passages regarding indecision is found in the book of James. The author explains that if we lack wisdom, we should ask God, who gives generously to all without finding fault. However, he warns that we must ask in faith, without doubting, because the one who doubts is like a wave of the sea, blown and tossed by the wind. James describes such a person as double-minded and unstable in all they do (James 1:5; James 1:6; James 1:7; James 1:8). This "double-mindedness" refers to a divided heart that hesitates to fully trust God's character or His Word.

The Bible also suggests that indecision can be rooted in a lack of surrender to God’s sovereignty. We are encouraged to trust in the Lord with all our hearts and lean not on our own understanding, but in all our ways acknowledge Him, and He will make our paths straight (Proverbs 3:5; Proverbs 3:6). When we realize that God is holy, sovereign, and good, we can find the courage to make decisions, knowing that His grace is sufficient even when we are imperfect.

In some cases, indecision is a spiritual matter regarding our allegiance. The prophet Elijah challenged the people of Israel by asking how long they would waver between two opinions, urging them to follow the Lord if He is truly God (1 Kings 18:21). Similarly, Joshua called the people to choose this day whom they would serve (Joshua 24:15). 

The most significant decision anyone can face concerns their relationship with God. Because of God's holiness and our own sin, we are naturally separated from Him. However, because of His great love and mercy, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man. Jesus lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary atoning death on the cross to pay for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, demonstrating His victory over sin and death. Salvation is not earned by our works or by being "decisive" enough, but is a gift secured by Christ and received through grace alone through faith alone in Him. 

If you find yourself wavering in your faith or uncertain about your standing with God, the invitation of the Gospel is clear. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?

For those already in Christ, indecision can be met with the peace of the Holy Spirit. God has not given us a spirit of fear, but of power, love, and self-discipline (2 Timothy 1:7). To grow in decisiveness, it is helpful to seek wise counsel from mature believers (Proverbs 11:14), immerse yourself in the Word of God, and remember that even if you make a mistake, God is faithful to refine you and work all things together for your good (Romans 8:28). You are encouraged to pray directly to God for clarity, resting in the fact that He is the author of peace and not of confusion (1 Corinthians 14:33).
What does it mean that God will wipe away every tear (Revelation 21:4)?

This beautiful promise in Revelation 21:4 describes the final and complete restoration of all things when Jesus Christ returns to dwell with His people. It is a moment of profound intimacy where God Himself acts as a tender Father to comfort His children. To say that God will wipe away every tear means that He will remove not only the grief we feel but also the very causes of that grief.

In this future reality, the "former things" have passed away (Revelation 21:4). This refers to the brokenness of our current world, which is marred by the effects of sin. The passage explicitly lists that death, mourning, crying, and pain will no longer exist. This is the fulfillment of the ancient prophecy that the Sovereign Lord will swallow up death forever and wipe away the tears from all faces (Isaiah 25:8). It signifies a state of perfect peace and joy in the presence of God (Revelation 7:17).

This hope is grounded in the character of God, who is merciful, gracious, and loving (Exodus 34:6). While we experience deep sorrow in this life, God is not distant from our pain. He is near to the brokenhearted (Psalm 34:18) and has made a way for us to be reconciled to Him so that we may inhabit this future kingdom.

The reason we can have confidence in this promise is because of the Gospel of Jesus Christ. Because God is holy and just, He cannot simply overlook sin, which is the root cause of all suffering and death (Romans 6:23). We have all sinned and fallen short of His glory (Romans 3:23). However, because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, into the world. Jesus lived a perfect, sinless life and then died a substitutionary death on the cross, taking the punishment for our sins upon Himself (1 Peter 2:24). He was buried and rose bodily from the grave on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). 

Now, Jesus is exalted as Lord, and He offers eternal life and a place in this tearless kingdom to all who believe. Salvation is not something we can earn through our own efforts or good works; it is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What is the meaning of “there but for the grace of God go I”?

The phrase "there but for the grace of God go I" is an expression of deep humility and gratitude. It acknowledges that a person's current well-being, safety, or moral standing is not due to their own inherent strength or superior goodness, but is entirely the result of God’s unmerited favor.

Historically, this sentiment is often attributed to the sixteenth-century English reformer John Bradford. While he was imprisoned in the Tower of London, he reportedly spoke these words as he watched criminals being led to their execution. He recognized that apart from God’s restraining and sustaining grace, his own life could have followed the same path of ruin.

From a biblical perspective, this phrase aligns with the truth that all people share a common fallen nature. The Bible teaches that "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" (Romans 3:23). When we see another person struggling or facing the consequences of sin, this expression reminds us that we are not inherently better than they are. As the Apostle Paul wrote, "For who sees anything different in you? What do you have that you did not receive? If then you received it, why do you boast as if you did not receive it?" (1 Corinthians 4:7).

This realization should lead us to a state of humble dependence on the Lord. Scripture warns those who feel morally superior, stating, "Therefore let anyone who thinks that he stands take heed lest he fall" (1 Corinthians 10:12). It is only by God's power and mercy that we are kept from falling into various temptations or hardships.

The ultimate expression of this grace is found in the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and just, yet we have all rebelled against Him. Because of our sin, we were separated from Him and deserved His judgment. However, out of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life on our behalf. Jesus died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins, was buried, and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now the exalted Lord of all. Salvation is a gift received by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, and not by our own works (Ephesians 2:8-9).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
Who wrote the book of Hosea? Who was the author of Hosea?

The Book of Hosea identifies its author in the very first verse as the prophet Hosea, the son of Beeri (Hosea 1:1). Hosea was a prophet to the northern kingdom of Israel during the eighth century B.C., serving during the reigns of kings Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah of Judah, and King Jeroboam II of Israel (Hosea 1:1).

While Hosea is the primary author who recorded these oracles and experiences, the ultimate author of the book, as with all of Scripture, is God Himself. The Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, where holy men spoke from God as they were carried along by the Holy Spirit (2 Timothy 3:16; 2 Peter 1:21).

The Book of Hosea is deeply moving because God used Hosea’s own marriage to illustrate His relationship with His people. Hosea was commanded to marry an unfaithful woman named Gomer to show how Israel had been spiritually unfaithful to God (Hosea 1:2). Despite her wandering, Hosea was told to go and redeem her again, which serves as a powerful picture of God’s relentless, redeeming love for His people (Hosea 3:1).

This theme of redemption points us to the greatest act of love in history. Though we have all sinned and turned away from God’s holiness (Romans 3:23), God did not abandon us. He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, to live a perfect life and die a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins (John 3:16; 1 Peter 2:24). On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, defeating sin and death forever (1 Corinthians 15:3-4).

Salvation is secured by Christ’s work alone and is received by grace alone through faith alone, not by our own efforts or works (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God today, repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and eternal life. What holds you back from trusting Him?
What were the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place in the temple / tabernacle?

The Holy Place and the Most Holy Place were the two inner chambers of the Tabernacle, and later the Temple, that served as the earthly dwelling place of God’s presence among the Israelites. These rooms were separated by a thick, heavy veil and were designed to illustrate both God’s desire to be with His people and the reality of His absolute holiness, which required a separation from sin.

The Holy Place was the larger, outer room of the sanctuary. Inside this room were three specific items of furniture that the priests tended to daily: the Golden Lampstand, which provided light; the Table of Showbread, which held twelve loaves of bread representing the twelve tribes of Israel; and the Altar of Incense, where sweet-smelling smoke was offered to God as a symbol of prayer. Only the priests were allowed to enter this area to perform their regular duties on behalf of the people. This arrangement is described in Hebrews 9:2 and Exodus 26:33-35.

The Most Holy Place, often called the Holy of Holies, was the innermost chamber located behind the veil. This room contained the Ark of the Covenant, which held the tablets of the Ten Commandments and was covered by the Mercy Seat where the presence of God rested in a cloud of glory. This room was so sacred that only the High Priest could enter, and he could do so only once a year on the Day of Atonement. He would bring the blood of a sacrifice to make atonement for his own sins and the sins of the nation, as noted in Hebrews 9:3-7 and Leviticus 16:2-17.

The separation between these two rooms was a constant reminder that humanity’s sin creates a barrier between us and a holy God. However, the Bible teaches that these physical structures were shadows of a greater reality found in Jesus Christ. When Jesus died on the cross, the veil in the Temple was torn in two from top to bottom, as recorded in Matthew 27:51. This miraculous event signified that through the death of Christ, the barrier of sin has been removed for all who believe.

Because God is perfectly holy and we have all sinned, we are naturally separated from Him. But God, in His great love and mercy, sent His Son, Jesus Christ, to live a perfect life and die as a substitute for sinners. He was buried and rose again on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death. He is now our great High Priest who has entered the true Most Holy Place in heaven, making a way for us to have a direct relationship with the Father.

You can receive this gift of forgiveness and eternal life by turning from your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ alone as your Lord and Savior. He has already done the work required to bring you into God's presence. What is it that might be keeping you from placing your full trust in Him today?
How can we walk in God’s ways (Psalm 128:1)?

To walk in God’s ways, as described in Psalm 128:1, is a life-long journey of faith, reverence, and obedience. It is not a path of perfection earned by our own strength, but a path of grace made possible through a relationship with the Creator.

The foundation for walking in God’s ways is a heart transformed by the Gospel. God is perfectly holy and righteous (Isaiah 6:3; Psalm 145:17), while we have all fallen short of His glory through sin (Romans 3:23). Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man (John 1:1; John 1:14). Jesus lived a sinless life, died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay for our sins, and was raised bodily from the dead on the third day, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). We are reconciled to God by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works we do (Ephesians 2:8-9). You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you.

Once we are unified with Christ, walking in His ways involves several spiritual priorities:

Cultivating the Fear of the Lord
