Salvation is not earned by trying to avoid these seven sins through our own strength; it is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9; Romans 6:23).

You can turn to God now, repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Should pastors be paid a salary?

The Bible provides clear guidance indicating that it is right and biblical for pastors to receive financial support from the congregations they serve. This practice allows those called to ministry to devote their time and energy to the study of the Word, prayer, and the care of the church body.

In 1 Corinthians 9:14, the Apostle Paul writes that the Lord commanded that those who proclaim the gospel should get their living by the gospel. Paul uses several illustrations to show that a worker is entitled to support, comparing a minister to a soldier who does not serve at his own expense, a planter who eats the fruit of his vineyard, and a shepherd who drinks the milk of his flock in 1 Corinthians 9:7.

Furthermore, in 1 Timothy 5:17-18, the Scripture says, "Let the elders who rule well be considered worthy of double honor, especially those who labor in preaching and teaching. For the Scripture says, 'You shall not muzzle an ox when it treads out the grain,' and, 'The laborer deserves his wages.'" In this context, double honor is widely understood by scholars to include both respect and financial remuneration. This principle is also echoed in Galatians 6:6, which instructs those who are taught the Word to share all good things with the one who teaches.

The ultimate purpose of providing a salary for pastors is to ensure that the message of Jesus Christ can be proclaimed without hindrance. The Gospel is the central message of the Christian faith, declaring that while we are all sinners separated from a holy God, He has provided a way of reconciliation. Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and rose bodily from the grave on the third day. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith alone in Christ alone, not by any works of our own. 

You can turn to God now by repenting of your sin and trusting in Jesus Christ, who died and rose for you, for the forgiveness of your sins and the gift of new life.

By supporting their pastors, a congregation participates in the advancement of this message. While some leaders may choose to support themselves through secular work in specific circumstances, as Paul did at times in Acts 18:3, the biblical standard encourages the church to provide for those who labor in the ministry. This allows them to fulfill their calling to watch over souls as those who will give an account, as described in Hebrews 13:17.
What does it mean that man shall not live by bread alone (Deuteronomy 8:3)?

When Moses spoke these words in Deuteronomy 8:3, he was addressing the Israelites at the end of their forty year journey through the wilderness. He reminded them that God had allowed them to hunger and then fed them with manna, a food they had never known before. This physical experience was a spiritual lesson. God wanted His people to understand that while physical food is necessary for the body, it is not enough to truly "live" in the sense that God intended.

To live by "every word that comes from the mouth of the Lord" means that our true source of life, purpose, and strength is found in our relationship with God and our obedience to His will. It is an acknowledgment that we are more than just biological creatures, we are spiritual beings who require the spiritual nourishment of God's truth. Without God's Word, we may exist physically, but we are spiritually malnourished and disconnected from our Creator.

Jesus Christ emphasized this truth during His own time of testing in the wilderness. When the tempter urged Him to turn stones into bread after He had fasted for forty days, Jesus responded by quoting this very passage in Matthew 4:4. He demonstrated that even in the face of extreme physical hunger, the priority of a believer is to remain faithful to God’s Word. This shows us that our dependence on God is even more fundamental than our need for food.

This principle ultimately points us to Jesus Himself. In John 6:35, Jesus says, "I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger, and whoever believes in me shall never thirst." While the manna in the wilderness sustained the Israelites for a time, it could not give them eternal life. Jesus, however, is the Word of God made flesh, as described in John 1:1 and John 1:14. Through His perfect life, His sacrificial death on the cross for our sins, and His bodily resurrection, He provides the only way for us to be reconciled to a holy God.

We all face the temptation to look to material things, success, or relationships to satisfy the deep hunger of our souls, but these things are like "bread alone" and will eventually leave us empty. Because God is holy and we have all sinned and fallen short of His glory, as noted in Romans 3:23, we are naturally separated from the source of life. Yet, in His great mercy, God offers us salvation by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone.

You can turn to God now, repent of your sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What is the significance of Lo Debar in the Bible?

Lo Debar is a place mentioned in the Old Testament, primarily in the story of Mephibosheth, the son of Jonathan and grandson of King Saul. Its name carries deep symbolic weight, as it literally translates from Hebrew to mean "no pasture" or "no word." This suggests a place of barrenness, silence, and obscurity.

The significance of Lo Debar is most clearly seen in 2 Samuel 9. After David was established as king, he sought to show "the kindness of God" to any remaining members of Saul's family because of his covenant with Jonathan (2 Samuel 9:1; 2 Samuel 9:3). He learned from a servant named Ziba that Mephibosheth was living in Lo Debar in the house of Machir (2 Samuel 9:4-5).

Mephibosheth had been living there in hiding and poverty. He was crippled in both feet, having been dropped by his nurse when his family fled following the deaths of Saul and Jonathan (2 Samuel 4:4). To Mephibosheth, Lo Debar was a place of safety from potential political rivals, but it was also a place of shame and brokenness. When David called him out of Lo Debar, Mephibosheth referred to himself as a "dead dog," showing his low self-esteem and sense of unworthiness (2 Samuel 9:8).

Biblically, Lo Debar serves as a powerful illustration of the human condition apart from God's grace. Just as Mephibosheth lived in a barren land, hiding in fear and unable to restore himself, we are often found in a spiritual "Lo Debar" due to sin. Scripture teaches that all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God (Romans 3:23). Sin separates us from God, leaving us in a state of spiritual poverty and spiritual death (Ephesians 2:1).

However, the story of Mephibosheth is ultimately one of restoration. King David brought him out of Lo Debar, restored his ancestral lands, and gave him a permanent place at the king's table (2 Samuel 9:7-13). This points us to the Gospel of Jesus Christ. 

God is holy and just, but He is also rich in mercy. Because of His great love, He sent His Son, Jesus Christ, who lived a perfect life, died a substitutionary death on the cross for our sins, and was buried. On the third day, Jesus rose bodily from the grave, proving His victory over sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4). He is now exalted as Lord. We are not saved by our own works or merit, but by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone (Ephesians 2:8-9).

Just as David sought out Mephibosheth, God seeks us out in our brokenness to bring us into His family. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life.

What holds you back from trusting Him today?
What does the Bible say about parenting?

The Bible views parenting not as a task of behavior management, but as a sacred stewardship where parents are called to reflect the heart of God to their children. Scripture teaches that children are a heritage and a reward from the Lord (Psalm 127:3). This perspective shifts the focus from seeing children as a burden to seeing them as a precious gift to be nurtured for God's glory.

A central theme in biblical parenting is the consistent, daily discipleship of children. In the book of Deuteronomy, God instructs parents to keep His words in their hearts and to teach them diligently to their children. This instruction is meant to happen in the natural rhythms of life: when sitting in the house, when walking along the way, when lying down, and when rising up (Deuteronomy 6:6-7). This suggests that parenting is an immersive process where the faith of the parent is lived out visibly before the child.

The New Testament provides specific guidance on how this discipleship should look. Parents are encouraged to bring their children up in the "discipline and instruction of the Lord" (Ephesians 6:4). This involves both teaching the truths of Scripture and providing gentle correction. However, the Bible also warns parents, specifically fathers, not to provoke their children to anger or discourage them (Ephesians 6:4; Colossians 3:21). This balance of firm boundaries and tender grace mirrors how God treats His own children, as He is patient, kind, and slow to anger (Psalm 103:8-13).

Training a child is often associated with the wisdom found in the book of Proverbs, which says to "train up a child in the way he should go; even when he is old he will not depart from it" (Proverbs 22:6). While this is a general principle rather than a guaranteed promise, it underscores the importance of intentionality. Discipline is viewed as an act of love, modeled after how God disciplines those He loves for their good and their sanctification (Hebrews 12:5-11).

Ultimately, biblical parenting points to the Gospel. Parents are not meant to be perfect, but they are called to point their children to the only One who is perfect: Jesus Christ. Children, like their parents, are born with a nature prone to sin and are in need of the reconciliation that only comes through Christ. The core of the Christian message is that God is holy, yet we have all fallen short of His glory through our sin. Because of His great love, God sent His Son, Jesus, to live a perfect life, die on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rise again to defeat death. Salvation is a gift of grace received through faith in Christ alone, not by our own works or moral efforts (Ephesians 2:8-9).

As you navigate the challenges of parenting, remember that you can turn to God now by repenting of sin and trusting in Jesus Christ who died and rose for you. He provides the grace and wisdom needed for every day. What is the biggest challenge you are currently facing in your journey as a parent?
Who was Jeremiah in the Bible?

Jeremiah was a faithful prophet of God who served during one of the most turbulent and painful periods in the history of Israel and Judah. Often called the "weeping prophet," his life and ministry are recorded in the Old Testament book of Jeremiah and the book of Lamentations.

Jeremiah was the son of Hilkiah, born into a family of priests in the village of Anathoth (Jeremiah 1:1). God called him to his prophetic ministry at a young age, revealing that he had been chosen for this purpose before he was even conceived. God told him, "Before I formed you in the womb I knew you, and before you were born I consecrated you; I appointed you a prophet to the nations" (Jeremiah 1:5). Although Jeremiah initially felt inadequate and hesitant because of his youth, God promised to be with him and to put His words in Jeremiah's mouth (Jeremiah 1:7-9).

His ministry lasted for about forty years, spanning the reigns of the last five kings of Judah before the nation fell to the Babylonian Empire. Jeremiah's primary task was to call the people to repent of their idolatry and social injustice. He warned them that if they did not return to the Lord, Jerusalem and the temple would be destroyed and the people would be carried away into exile (Jeremiah 25:8-11). Because he preached a message of judgment during a time when other "prophets" were falsely promising peace, Jeremiah faced extreme persecution. He was mocked by his neighbors, beaten, put in stocks, and even thrown into a muddy cistern to die (Jeremiah 20:2; Jeremiah 38:6).

Jeremiah is known as the weeping prophet because he felt a deep, agonizing sorrow for the spiritual rebellion of his people and the suffering they would face as a result. He did not take pleasure in the judgment he predicted; instead, he wished his eyes were a fountain of tears so he could weep day and night for his nation (Jeremiah 9:1). His life serves as a profound example of how one can remain faithful to God even when the message is unpopular and the personal cost is high.

Despite the heavy focus on judgment, Jeremiah also provided some of the most beautiful promises of hope in the entire Bible. He looked forward to a day when God would restore His people and make a New Covenant with them. Unlike the old covenant, which the people had broken, this New Covenant would involve God writing His law directly on their hearts and providing total, permanent forgiveness for their sins (Jeremiah 31:31-34).

This promise of a New Covenant finds its perfect fulfillment in the life, death, and resurrection of Jesus Christ. While Jeremiah saw the devastating consequences of sin, the Gospel tells us that God did not leave us in our brokenness. Jesus, the Son of God, lived a perfect life, died on the cross as a substitute for sinners, and rose from the dead on the third day to defeat sin and death (1 Corinthians 15:3-4; Romans 5:8). Through Him, we are not just forgiven, but we are given new hearts that truly know and love God.

Jeremiah's message reminds us that God is holy and takes sin seriously, but He is also full of mercy and invites us into a relationship with Him. You can turn to God now, repent of sin and trust in Jesus Christ alone who died and rose for you for forgiveness and new life. What holds you back from trusting Him today?
Why did Jesus allow the demons to enter the herd of pigs?

The account of Jesus casting the "Legion" of demons into a herd of pigs is a profound demonstration of His divine authority and the value He places on a single human soul. You can find this account in Matthew 8:28–34, Mark 5:1–20, and Luke 8:26–39. While the Bible does not explicitly state every reason why Jesus granted the demons' request, several biblical principles help us understand His actions.

First, this event demonstrates Jesus' absolute sovereignty over the spiritual realm. Even though the demons were numerous and destructive, they could do nothing without His permission (Mark 5:12–13). By allowing them to enter the pigs, Jesus showed that His word is final and that even the most powerful evil forces must submit to His command.

Second, the destruction of the herd served as a vivid, visible confirmation that the demons had truly left the man. For the man who had been tormented, seeing the physical departure of his captors provided a clear sign of his total deliverance. This emphasized the massive spiritual transformation that had occurred, changing him from a man who lived among tombs in chains to one who was "clothed and in his right mind" (Luke 8:35).

Third, Jesus highlighted the infinite worth of a human life compared to material possessions. In that culture, a herd of two thousand pigs represented significant wealth, yet Jesus allowed the loss of the livestock to save one man. This teaches us that in God’s eyes, the restoration of a person created in His image is far more precious than any worldly profit (Matthew 12:12).

Finally, the reaction of the townspeople revealed the state of their hearts. When they saw the power of God, they were afraid and asked Jesus to leave their region because they valued their economic security more than the presence of the Savior (Mark 5:17). In contrast, the healed man became a powerful witness of God's mercy to the entire Decapolis region (Mark 5:19–20).

This story ultimately points to the greater work Jesus came to accomplish. Just as He delivered this man from the power of darkness, He came to deliver all of us from the power of sin and death. God is holy and just, and because of our sin, we are separated from Him. However, out of His great love, Jesus Christ, who is truly God and truly man, lived a perfect life and died a substitutionary death on the cross to pay the penalty for our sins. He was buried and rose bodily on the third day, proving His victory over the grave and His authority as Lord of all.

Through His sacrifice, salvation is offered as a gift of grace to all who believe. You can turn to God now, repent of your sins and trust in Jesus Christ alone, who died and rose for you, for forgiveness and new life. What do you feel is the most challenging part of trusting Jesus with your life today?
